blocks:
Ayahs

الٓمٓ (١)
Alif, Lam, Mim. (1)
ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ (٢)
Allah—there is no god except Him—is the Living One, the All-sustainer. (2)
نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَأَنزَلَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ (٣)
He has sent down to you the Book with the truth, confirming what was [revealed] before it, and He had sent down the Torah and the Evangel (3)
مِن قَبْلُ هُدًى لِّلنَّاسِ وَأَنزَلَ ٱلْفُرْقَانَ ۗ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ ذُو ٱنتِقَامٍ (٤)
before as guidance for mankind, and He has sent down the Criterion. Indeed, there is a severe punishment for those who deny the signs of Allah, and Allah is all-mighty, avenger. (4)

The Virtue of Reciting This Surah

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

من قرء سورة آل عمران اعطى بكل آية منها امانا على جسر جهنم

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Imran, for every verse of it, he shall be granted a guarantee of safety over the bridge of Hell." [^1] And in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

من قرء البقرة و آل عمران جاءا يوم القيامة يظلانه على رأسه مثل الغمامتين

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Baqarah and Al-Imran, they will come on the Day of Resurrection shading his head like two clouds." [^2]

Content of the Surah

According to some famous commentators, it appears that this Surah was revealed during the years between the Battle of Badr and the Battle of Uhud (the second and third years of the Hijrah) and reflects some of the most turbulent periods of the lives of Muslims in the early days of Islam. [^3] Overall, the main axes of the discussions in this Surah are as follows:

1- A significant portion of it discusses Monotheism (Tawhid), the Attributes of Allah, the Hereafter, and Islamic teachings.

2- Another part speaks about Jihad and important, subtle instructions in this field, as well as the lessons to be learned from the two major Islamic battles (Badr and Uhud). It also describes Divine assistance to the believers and the eternal life of the martyrs in the way of Allah.

3- In a section of this Surah, meaningful references are made to a series of Islamic rulings regarding the necessity of unity among the ranks of Muslims, the House of the Kaaba, the obligation of Hajj, enjoining the good and forbidding the evil, Tawalli and Tabarri (befriending the friends of Truth and distancing from the enemies of Truth), the issue of trust (amanah), spending in the way of Allah, abandoning lies, resistance and steadfastness against the enemy, patience and endurance in the face of difficulties and various Divine trials, and the remembrance of Allah in every state.

4- To complete these discussions, a portion of the history of the Prophets is mentioned, including: Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and other Prophets عليهم السلام, as well as the story of Mary and the status of this great woman, and the conspiracies of the rebellious followers of Prophet Moses عليه السلام and the Messiah عليه السلام against Islam.

The contents of this Surah are so interconnected and harmonious that it is as if they were all revealed at once.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

الم (1) اللَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلاَّ هُوَ الْحَيُّ الْقَيُّومُ (2) نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَ أَنْزَلَ التَّوْراةَ وَ الْإِنْجِيلَ (3) مِنْ قَبْلُ هُدىً لِلنَّاسِ وَ أَنْزَلَ الْفُرْقانَ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِ اللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ وَ اللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ ذُو انْتِقامٍ (4)

Occasion of Revelation

Some commentators say: Eighty-odd verses of this Surah were revealed concerning the envoys and Christians of Najran [^1] who had come to Medina as representatives to investigate Islam.

The envoys were sixty people, fourteen of whom were considered among the nobles and prominent figures of Najran. Three of these fourteen held leadership positions, and the Christians of that region referred to them in their affairs and problems. One of them was "Aqib," also called "Abd al-Masih"; he was considered the prince and leader of his people, and his people never opposed his views or opinions. Another was named "Sayyid," also called "Ayham"; he was the supervisor of ceremonies and travel arrangements and was trusted by the Christians. The third person was named "Abu Harithah," a learned and influential man for whom several churches had been built in his name; he had memorized all the religious books of the Christians.

This group of sixty people came to Medina dressed in the attire of the men of the "Bani Ka'b" tribe and entered the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله mosque. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had prayed the Afternoon (Asr) prayer with the Muslims. These sixty people were wearing beautiful, glittering, and attractive clothes, such that one of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله companions said, "We have never seen envoys as beautiful as these!" When they entered the mosque, it was time for their prayer. According to their rituals, they rang the bell and, standing toward the east, began to pray. A group of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله companions wanted to prevent them. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Leave them be!"

[^1]: "Najran" is a place in the northern mountains of Yemen, ten stages away from "Sanaa." Its lands belonged to the Hamdan tribe, and this tribe had an idol in the pre-Islamic era called "Ya'uq." According to Yaqut al-Hamawi in Mu'jam al-Buldan, Najran was the name of several places.

After the prayer, "Aqib" and "Sayyid" came to the Prophet and began to speak with Him. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله proposed to them: "Enter the religion of Islam and submit before Allah."

Aqib and Sayyid said: "We embraced Islam and submitted to Allah before you!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "How can you be on the religion of truth when your actions indicate that you are not submitted to Allah? For you attribute a son to Allah and consider Jesus the son of God, you worship the cross, and you eat pork, while all these matters are contrary to the religion of truth!" Aqib and Sayyid said: "If Jesus is not the son of God, then who was his father?"

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Do you accept that every son bears a resemblance to his father?" They said: "Yes."

He said: "Is it not the case that our God encompasses everything, is the Self-Subsisting (Qayyum), and the sustenance of all beings is with Him?"

They said: "Yes, it is so."

He said: "Did Jesus possess these attributes?"

They said: "No."

He said: "Do you know that nothing in the heavens or the earth is hidden from Allah, and Allah is All-Knowing of them all?"

They said: "Yes, we know."

He said: "Did Jesus know anything of his own accord other than what Allah taught him?"

They said: "No."

He said: "Do you know that our God is the One who fashioned the Messiah in his mother's womb as He willed?"

They said: "It is so."

He said: "Is it not the case that Jesus was carried by his mother in the womb like other children, and then she gave birth to him like other mothers? And after birth, Jesus ate food like other infants."

They said: "Yes, it was so."

He said: "Then how is Jesus the son of God when he bears no resemblance to his Father?!" When the conversation reached this point, they all fell silent. At this time, eighty-odd verses from the beginning of this Surah were revealed to explain the teachings and programs of Islam. [^1]

Commentary

Discovering the Concept of the Disconnected Letters of the Quran via Computer

Once again, at the beginning of this Surah, we encounter the disconnected letters (Hurufe Muqatta'ah): "Alif-Lam-Mim" (الم).

Regarding the disconnected letters of the Quran, the necessary explanations were given at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah, so there is no need to repeat them. The only thing we find necessary to mention here is a theory recently expressed by an Egyptian scholar, the summary of which we bring here due to the importance of the subject.

Of course, judging its validity or falsehood requires extensive investigation, which perhaps lies with future generations, and we mention it only as a "view." [^2]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1-2, p. 406.

[^2]: Unfortunately, this scholar, who lives in America, has recently been so influenced by that polluted environment that he has explicitly risen in opposition to some established Islamic issues and makes false and baseless claims.

Some time ago, the famous Egyptian magazine Akher Sa'a, considered one of the largest illustrated magazines in the Middle East, published a report on the research of an Egyptian Muslim scholar regarding the interpretation of some verses of the Holy Quran with the help of electronic brains (computers), which aroused everyone's wonder in various parts of the world.

This research was the product of three years of persistent effort and continuous work by "Dr. Rashad Khalifa," an Egyptian chemist.

Dr. Rashad conducted this research in America, in the city of St. Louis, Missouri. He currently works as a consultant for a food company in America. To complete his research, he used electronic brains for a long time, the rental of which was $10 per minute, paid with the help of a group of Muslims in that region! All the efforts of the said professor were aimed at discovering the meanings of the disconnected letters of the Quran—letters such as (Qaf, Alif-Lam-Mim, Ya-Sin, etc.). Through complex calculations, he proved that there is a close relationship between these letters and the letters of the Surah at the beginning of which they are placed—pay attention.

Therefore, the electronic brain was used only to perform the calculation of the number of letters in the Surahs and to obtain the ratio (the so-called "percentage") of each of those letters, not that he asked the machine for the interpretation of the Quranic verses.

But certainly, if these machines did not exist, no human would be able to perform these calculations easily with pen and paper!

Now we turn to the description of the said discovery. Dr. Rashad says: We know that the Holy Quran has 114 Surahs, among which 86 Surahs were revealed in Mecca and 28 Surahs in Medina. Among the total Surahs of the Quran, there are 29 Surahs that have "disconnected letters" at their beginning.

Interestingly, these letters collectively form exactly half of the twenty-eight letters of the Arabic alphabet. These disconnected letters are: (ا- ح- ر- س- ص- ط- ع- ق- ك- ل- م- ن- ه- ى), which are sometimes called "Luminous Letters."

He says: For years, I wanted to know what the meaning of these "seemingly disconnected letters" at the beginning of the Quranic Surahs was. No matter how much I referred to the commentaries of great exegetes and the various opinions given in this field, I was not convinced. I sought help from the Great Allah and began studying. Suddenly, the thought occurred to me that perhaps there is a relationship between these letters and the letters of the same Surah at whose beginning they are placed.

However, examining all fourteen luminous letters in the 114 Surahs of the Quran and determining the ratio of each of them, along with many other calculations that had to be done in this field, was not something possible without employing a computer. Therefore, I first determined all the said letters in the 114 Surahs of the Quran separately, as well as the total letters of each Surah precisely, and fed them into the computer (for subsequent calculations) along with the number of each Surah. This work and other preliminaries were realized over a period of two years.

Then, I used this electronic brain for a full year to perform the calculations mentioned. The result of these calculations was very brilliant and, for the first time in Islamic history, unveiled amazing facts that made the miracle of the Quran completely clear from a mathematical perspective (in addition to other aspects).

The electronic brain, through its calculations, made it clear to us what the percentage of each of the fourteen letters is in each of the 114 Surahs of the Quran relative to the total letters of that Surah.

For example, after calculation, we find that the ratio of the letter Qaf, which is one of the luminous letters of the Quran (disconnected letters), has the highest figure in Surah "Al-Falaq" (6.700 percent) and is in the first rank among the Surahs of the Quran (with the exception of Surah "Qaf," of course)! After that is Surah "Al-Qiyamah," whose number of Qafs relative to the letters of the said Surah is (3.907 percent), followed by Surah "Ash-Shams" (3.906 percent).

And as we observe, the difference between Surah "Al-Qiyamah" and "Ash-Shams" is only one-thousandth of a percent! In the same way, we obtain this ratio in all 114 Surahs of the Quran (not only for this one letter but for all fourteen luminous letters), and thus the ratio of the total letters of each Surah to each of these letters becomes clear.

Now, pay attention to the interesting results obtained from these calculations:

1- The ratio of the letter (ق) in Surah "Qaf" is higher than in all Surahs of the Quran without exception. That is, the verses revealed during the 23 years of the Quran's revelation in the other 113 Surahs of the Quran are such that the letter Qaf is used less in them. This is truly astonishing—that a human could monitor the count of each of the letters of his speech over 23 years, while simultaneously expressing his subjects freely without the slightest affectation. Certainly, such a task is beyond a human; even calculating it is impossible for the greatest mathematicians without the help of electronic brains.

All this shows that not only the Surahs and verses of the Quran but also the "letters of the Quran" are based on a specific mathematical account and system that only Allah is capable of maintaining.

Similarly, calculations showed that the letter (ص) in Surah "Sad" also has the same status. That is, its amount in proportion to the total letters of the Surah is higher than in any other Surah of the Quran.

Also, the letter (ن) in Surah "Nun wal-Qalam" has the highest relative figure in the 114 Surahs of the Quran. The only exception in this field is Surah "Al-Hijr," where the relative count of the letter (ن) is higher than in Surah "Nun wal-Qalam." However, what is interesting is that Surah Al-Hijr is one of the Surahs that begins with (الر), and we will see later that these Surahs beginning with (الر) must all be considered as one Surah in ruling; if we do so, the desired result will be obtained—meaning the ratio of the count of (ن) in their total will be less than in Surah "Nun wal-Qalam"!

2- Consider the four letters (المص) at the beginning of Surah Al-A'raf. If we add together the Alifs, Lams, Mims, and Sads present in this Surah and measure their ratio to the letters of this Surah, we will see that it is higher than their total count in any other Surah of the Quran! Similarly, the four letters (المر) at the beginning of Surah "Ar-Ra'd" have the same status, and also the five letters (كهيعص) at the beginning of Surah "Maryam," if calculated together, exceed the total of these five letters in any other Surah of the Quran! Here we encounter a newer face of the issue: not only is a single letter spread throughout this Heavenly Book according to a specific account and order, but its multiple letters also have such a wonderous situation—pay attention.

3- Thus far, the discussion was about letters placed at the beginning of only one Surah of the Quran. However, letters placed at the beginning of several Surahs (such as Alif-Lam-Ra and Alif-Lam-Mim) take another form. That is, according to computer calculations, the total of these three letters, for example (ا- ل- م), if calculated in the total Surahs beginning with "Alif-Lam-Mim" and their ratio to the total letters of these Surahs is obtained, it is higher than their level in any of the other Surahs of the Quran.

Here again, the issue takes a more interesting form: not only are the letters of each Surah of the Quran under a specific regulation and account, but the total letters of similar Surahs also have a single regulation and system.

Incidentally, the point of this subject also becomes clear: why several different Surahs of the Quran began with "Alif-Lam-Mim" or "Alif-Lam-Ra," and this is not a coincidental or groundless matter.

(Dr. Rashad then performed more complex calculations on the Surahs containing "Ha-Mim," which we omit for the sake of brevity).

The said professor, during these studies, also reached other points which we present to the readers along with new points that can be inferred from them:

1- Preserve the Original Script of the Quran

He says all these calculations are correct only if we do not touch the original and ancient script of the Quran (for example, writing Ishaq, Zakat, and Salat in this very form, not as Is-haaq, Zakaah, and Salaah); otherwise, our calculations will be disrupted.

2- Another Proof of the Non-Distortion of the Quran

This research shows that in the Holy Quran, not even a single letter has been added or removed; otherwise, our calculations on the current Quran would certainly not have turned out correct.

3- Meaningful References

In many Surahs of the Quran that begin with disconnected letters, after mentioning these letters, reference is made to the truthfulness and greatness of the Quran, such as:

"الم ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ‌". [^1] and the like. This itself is a subtle reference to the connection of the said letters with the miracle of the Quran.

Conclusion of the Discussion—From the total of the above discussion, we conclude that the letters of the Holy Quran, which were revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله over 23 years, have a very precise and organized account, and each of the letters of the alphabet has a completely precise mathematical ratio with the total letters of each Surah, the maintenance of which is impossible for a human—without the use of electronic brains.

There is no doubt that the investigations of the said scholar, being at the beginning of the path, are not free from deficiencies that must be completed with full patience by other scholars. For this reason, we give no guarantee of its validity or falsehood (correctness or incorrectness) until other studies are conducted on it. Now we proceed with the continuation of the commentary on the verses of the Surah:

In the second verse, He says: "Allah, there is no deity except Him, the Ever-Living, the Self-Subsisting, upon whom everything depends for its existence" (اللَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ الْحَيُّ الْقَيُّومُ‌). The explanation and commentary of this verse passed in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 255.

[^1]: Surah Al-Baqarah, verses 1-2.

In the next verse, addressing the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, He says: The God who is Eternal and Self-Subsisting "sent down the Quran upon you, which is accompanied by signs of truth and completely corresponds with the signs of the previous Heavenly Books [^1]; the same God who sent down the Torah and the Gospel before the Quran for the guidance and direction of mankind" (نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَ أَنْزَلَ التَّوْراةَ وَ الْإِنْجِيلَ مِنْ قَبْلُ هُدىً لِلنَّاسِ‌).

Then He adds: "He also sent down the Criterion (Furqan) which distinguishes truth from falsehood" (وَ أَنْزَلَ الْفُرْقانَ‌).

And after the completion of the proof and the revelation of verses from Allah, and the testimony of fitrah (innate nature) and reason to the truthfulness of the Prophets' call, there is no way but punishment. Therefore, in the above verse—following the discussion regarding the truthfulness of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Holy Quran—He says: "Indeed, those who disbelieved in the verses of Allah will have a severe punishment" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِ اللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ).

And so that it is not imagined that Allah's ability to carry out His threats is in doubt, He adds: "And Allah is All-Mighty, Owner of Retribution" (وَ اللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ ذُو انْتِقامٍ‌). [^2] "Aziz" in the language means anything difficult, impenetrable, and dominant. Therefore, land that is difficult to traverse is called "Azaz," and anything that is difficult to access due to scarcity is called "Aziz." Similarly, powerful and capable individuals whom it is "difficult" or "impossible" to overcome are Aziz. Wherever the word "Aziz" is applied to Allah, it is in this sense—meaning no one is capable of overcoming Him or defeating Him, and all are subject to His Will and Wish.

[^1]: Read the explanation of the phrase "مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ‌" on page 207 of Volume 1, under verse 41 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

[^2]: Some commentators have said that "Dhu" has a more expressive meaning than "Sahib" (owner/possessor), and therefore we do not see the expression Sahib used for the Attributes of Allah in any case, while the expression Dhu is frequent—Al-Bahr al-Muhit, Vol. 2, p. 379.

In the above sentence, so that the disbelievers know this threat is completely serious, He says: Allah is Capable, and for this reason, no one can resist the realization of His threats, because just as He is exceedingly "Merciful and Kind" in His place, He has severe punishment and painful retribution for those who do not deserve mercy.

Of course, "retribution" (intiqam) in today's terminology is mostly used in cases where persons, due to a lack of forgiveness for the wrongdoings or mistakes of others, resort to reciprocal action and do not even consider their own interest in pardon and forgiveness. This attribute is certainly not a praiseworthy one, for a human in many cases should prioritize pardon and forgiveness over retaliation. But "intiqam" in the original language does not have this meaning; rather, it means punishing the sinner. It is certain that punishing rebellious and oppressive sinners is not only a praiseworthy act but overlooking them is contrary to justice and wisdom.

Key Points

1- "Haqq" (Truth) originally means "correspondence and harmony," and for this reason, that which corresponds with existing reality is called Haqq. The reason Allah is called "Haqq" is that His Holy Essence is the greatest undeniable reality in the universe. To put it more clearly: Haqq means a fixed and enduring subject into which falsehood finds no way.

In the verse under discussion, the Ba in "bil-Haqq" is for "accompaniment," meaning: O Prophet! Allah sent down the Quran to you, which is accompanied by signs of reality.

2- "Torah" is originally a Hebrew word meaning "sharia and law," and was subsequently applied to the book revealed by Allah to Moses son of Imran عليه السلام. Sometimes it is applied to the collection of books of the "Old Testament" and sometimes to its "Pentateuch" (five books).

Explanation: The collection of Jewish books called the "Old Testament" is composed of the Torah and several other books. The Torah has five sections named: Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy. This part of the Old Testament books is an account of the creation of the world, man, and other creatures, and a part of the lives of previous Prophets, Moses son of Imran, the Children of Israel, and the rulings of this religion.

Other books in this collection, which are actually the writings of historians after Moses عليه السلام, are accounts of the states of the Prophets, kings, and nations that came into existence after Moses son of Imran.

It goes without saying that other than the five books of the Torah, none of these books are Heavenly Books, and the Jews themselves do not make such a claim. Even the "Zabur" of David, which they call "Psalms," is an account of the supplications and exhortations of David.

As for the five books of the Torah, there are clear indications within them showing that these too are not Heavenly Books, but rather historical books written after Moses son of Imran, because they contain the account of the death of Moses عليه السلام, how he was buried, and some events after the death of Moses. Especially the last chapter of the fifth book (Deuteronomy) clearly proves that this book was written long after the death of Moses son of Imran.

Furthermore, the contents of these books, which are mixed with many superstitions, improper attributions to the Prophets of Allah, and some childish talk, are another witness to their being fabricated.

Historical evidence also shows that the original Torah was lost, and later the followers of Moses son of Imran wrote these books. [^1]

3- "Gospel" (Injil) is originally a Greek word meaning "glad tidings" or "new teaching," and is the name of the book revealed to Prophet Jesus عليه السلام. It is noteworthy that the Quran, in the verse under discussion and wherever it mentions the book of Jesus عليه السلام, uses "Injil" in the singular form and introduces its revelation as being from Allah. Therefore, the many Gospels prevalent among Christians, even the most famous of them, namely the four Gospels (Luke, Mark, Matthew, and John), are not Divine revelation, just as the Christians themselves do not deny that these existing Gospels were all written by the disciples, or the disciples of the disciples, of the Messiah عليه السلام long after him. However, they claim that the disciples of the Messiah wrote these Gospels with Divine inspiration. Here it is appropriate to conduct a brief review of the New Testament and the Gospels and become acquainted with their authors:

The most important religious book of the Christians, which is the mainstay of all Christian sects and upon which they rely as a Heavenly Book, is a collection they call the "New Testament."

The "New Testament," the total of which is not more than one-third of the Old Testament, consists of 27 scattered books and epistles on completely different subjects, as follows:

1- The Gospel of Matthew [^2]: This Gospel was written by "Matthew," one of the twelve disciples of the Messiah عليه السلام, in the year 38 AD, and according to others, between the years 50 to 60 AD. [^1]

2- The Gospel of Mark [^2]: According to the explicit statement of the Bible Dictionary, page 792, Mark was not among the apostles but composed his Gospel under the supervision of "Peter." Mark was killed in the year 68 AD.

3- The Gospel of Luke: "Luke" was a companion and fellow traveler of "Paul" the Apostle. Paul converted to Christianity some time after Jesus and was a fanatical Jew during his time. The death of Luke is recorded around the year 70 AD, and according to the author of the Bible Dictionary (page 772), "the date of the writing of the Gospel of Luke is generally estimated to be 63 AD."

4- The Gospel of John: "John" was among the disciples of the Messiah and a companion and fellow traveler of Paul. According to the aforementioned author, by the testimony of most critics (researchers), its composition is attributed to the end of the first century. [^3] From the contents of these Gospels, which generally describe the story of the crucifixion of the Messiah and the events following it, it is well proven that all these Gospels were written years after the Messiah and none of them is the Heavenly Book revealed to the Messiah عليه السلام.

5- Acts of the Apostles (the deeds of the apostles and missionaries of the first century).

6- 14 Epistles from the letters of Paul to various nations and individuals.

7- The Epistle of James (the twentieth epistle of the twenty-seven books and epistles of the New Testament).

8- The Epistles of Peter (the 21st and 22nd epistles of the New Testament).

9- The Epistles of John (the 23rd, 24th, and 25th epistles of the New Testament).

10- The Epistle of Jude (the 26th epistle of the New Testament).

11- The Revelation of John (the last part of the New Testament).

Therefore, according to the explicit statement of Christian historians and according to the explicit testimony of the Gospels and other books and epistles of the New Testament, none of these are Heavenly Books and are generally books written after the Messiah عليه السلام. From this statement, we conclude that the Gospel, the Heavenly Book of the Messiah, has been lost and is not available today; only parts of it were brought by the disciples of the Messiah in their Gospels, which unfortunately have also been mixed with superstitions.

As for those who say: Muslims should not doubt the authenticity of the existing Gospels and Torah because the Holy Quran has confirmed them and testified to their authenticity, we noted the detailed answer to this in Volume 1, page 210, under the verse "وآمِنُوا بِما أَنْزَلْتُ مُصَدِّقاً لِما مَعَكُمْ‌".

4- After the mention of the Torah and the Gospel, as we saw, the revelation of the Quran is mentioned, and that too as "Furqan." But why is the Quran called "Furqan"? Because "Furqan" in the language means "the means of distinguishing truth from falsehood," and generally anything that specifies truth from falsehood is called "Furqan." Therefore, the day of the Battle of Badr is called in the Quran "يَوْمَ الْفُرْقانِ‌" [^1] because on that day, a small army lacking any military equipment was victorious over a relatively large and powerful army that was superior to it in every respect. Similarly, the ten miracles of Moses عليه السلام are also referred to as "Furqan." [^2]

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَخْفَىٰ عَلَيْهِ شَىْءٌ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ (٥)
Nothing is indeed hidden from Allah in the earth or in the sky. (5)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُصَوِّرُكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْحَامِ كَيْفَ يَشَآءُ ۚ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٦)
It is He who forms you in the wombs [of your mothers] however He wishes. There is no god except Him, the All-mighty, the All-wise. (6)

Commentary

Allah’s Infinite Knowledge and Power

These verses are, in fact, a completion of the preceding verses. In the previous verses, we read that Allah is the Ever-Living and the Sustainer (Al-Hayy al-Qayyum), and the governance of the universe is in His hands. He punishes the stubborn and persistent disbelievers (even if they do not openly manifest their disbelief). Certainly, this task requires extraordinary knowledge and power. For this reason, the first verse under discussion refers to His knowledge, and the second verse refers to His capability.

First, He says: "Indeed, nothing in the earth or in the heaven is hidden from Allah" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَخْفى‌ عَلَيْهِ شَيْ‌ءٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ لا فِي السَّماءِ).

How could anything be hidden from Him when He is present and observant everywhere? By virtue of the fact that His existence is infinite and unlimited in every respect, no place is devoid of Him, and He is closer to us than we are to ourselves. Therefore, while He has no specific location or place, He encompasses everything. This encompassment and His presence regarding everything and in every place means His knowledge and awareness of all things—and that is "Knowledge by Presence" (al-ilm al-huduri), not "Acquired Knowledge" (al-ilm al-husuli). [1]

Then, He points to a corner of His knowledge and power, which is in fact one of the masterpieces of the world of creation and one of the manifest signs of Allah's knowledge and power, saying: "He it is Who shapes you in the wombs (of your mothers) as He wills" (هُوَ الَّذِي يُصَوِّرُكُمْ فِي الْأَرْحامِ كَيْفَ يَشاءُ). "Indeed, there is no deity but He, the Almighty, the All-Wise" (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ‌).

The shaping of a human being in the mother's womb and painting upon water in that dark and gloomy environment—creating such original, wondrous, and successive designs—is truly astonishing; especially with all the diversity that exists in terms of form, appearance, gender, various talents, and different traits and instincts.

And if we see that there is no deity but Him, it is because none is worthy of servitude except His Pure Essence. Therefore, why should created beings like the Messiah عليه السلام be worshipped? It seems this expression refers to the occasion of revelation mentioned at the beginning of the Surah: that the Christians themselves accept that the Messiah was nurtured in the womb of a mother like Mary; thus, he is a creature, not a Creator. Therefore, how can he be an object of worship?

[1]: "Knowledge by Presence" (al-ilm al-huduri) means that the essence of the known is present before the Knower and He encompasses it; the reality of knowledge in its perfection is nothing but this. However, in "Acquired Knowledge" (al-ilm al-husuli), the form and image of the known are present before the knower. For example, our knowledge of our own selves and our mental concepts and judgments is Knowledge by Presence because these are present within us. But regarding external objects and beings, we have Acquired Knowledge because their actual essence is never present in our minds; rather, only their image and form are present.

Key Points

1- Signs of Allah’s Power and Greatness in the Stages of the Embryo

Today, the greatness of the concept of this verse is more apparent than ever before, given the advancements in the science of embryology. The "embryo," which begins as a single-celled organism, shows no initial shape, limbs, organs, or specific systems. Yet, with extraordinary speed in the secret sanctuary of the womb, it takes on a new shape and design every day. It is as if a group of skillful and master painters are sitting beside it, working day and night, and from this insignificant speck, they build a human being in a very short time—one whose exterior is beautifully adorned and within whose being are found extremely delicate, complex, precise, and amazing systems. If the stages of the embryo were photographed (as they have been) and these photos passed before a person's eyes one after another, one would gain a new acquaintance with the greatness of creation and the power of the Creator, and would involuntarily murmur this famous poem: "Worthy of praise is that Creator / Who brings such a delightful design from water and clay." And the wonder is that all these designs are made upon water, which is known for not retaining any design—"Who has performed such portraiture upon water?!"

It is noteworthy that when fertilization occurs and the embryo takes its initial form, it very quickly, through division and exponential increase, takes the shape of a mulberry fruit whose berries are connected, called a "morula." Simultaneous with this progress, a blood clot called the placenta is developing beside it. The placenta is connected on one side to the mother's heart via two arteries and one vein, and on the other side to the embryo via the umbilical cord, and the embryo feeds on all the nutrients present in the placenta's blood.

Gradually, due to nutrition and development, and the cells moving outward, the "morula" becomes hollow, which is called a "blastula." It is not long before the number of cells in the "blastula" increases, forming a double-walled sac, and then an indentation occurs, resulting in the embryo being divided into two regions: "chest" and "abdomen."

Interestingly, until this stage, all cells are similar to one another and have no difference in appearance. But from this stage onward, the "shaping" of the embryo begins, and its components undergo changes in proportion to the tasks they must perform in the future. Different tissues and systems appear, and each group of cells takes charge of building one of the body's systems, such as the nervous system, circulatory system, digestive system, etc. Consequently, after these stages, the embryo is shaped into a balanced human being in the secret sanctuary of the womb. (The explanation of embryonic development and its various stages will be noted, Allah willing, under verse 12 of Surah Al-Mu'minun). [1]

2- "Arham" is the plural of "Rahm"

(Pronounced Rahm) originally means the place in the mother's abdomen where the child is nurtured. Later, it was applied to all relatives who were originally born from one mother. And since there is a bond of affection and friendship among them, this word has been applied to every kind of kindness and affection. Some hold the opposite view, saying its original concept is that very tenderness of heart, affection, and love, and because close relatives have such affection for one another, the place where a child is nurtured is called the rahm (womb).

[1]: Refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 14, page 211 onwards.

Ayah

هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِنْهُ ءَايَـٰتٌ مُّحْكَمَـٰتٌ هُنَّ أُمُّ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَأُخَرُ مُتَشَـٰبِهَـٰتٌ ۖ فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ زَيْغٌ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ مَا تَشَـٰبَهَ مِنْهُ ٱبْتِغَآءَ ٱلْفِتْنَةِ وَٱبْتِغَآءَ تَأْوِيلِهِۦ ۗ وَمَا يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَٱلرَّٰسِخُونَ فِى ٱلْعِلْمِ يَقُولُونَ ءَامَنَّا بِهِۦ كُلٌّ مِّنْ عِندِ رَبِّنَا ۗ وَمَا يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّآ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (٧)
It is He who has sent down to you the Book. Parts of it are definitive verses, which are the mother of the Book, while others are metaphorical. As for those in whose hearts is deviance, they pursue what is metaphorical in it, courting temptation, and seeking its interpretation. But no one knows its interpretation except Allah and those firmly grounded in knowledge; they say, ‘We believe in it; all of it is from our Lord.’ And none takes admonition except those who possess intellect. (7)

Circumstances of Revelation

In Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 1, Page 313, a hadith is narrated from Ma'ani al-Akhbar by Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام to this effect: A group of Jews, accompanied by Huyayy ibn Akhtab and his brother, came to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and used the abbreviated letters (Hurufe Muqatta'ah) "Alif, Lam, Mim" (الم) as a pretext. They said: "According to the Abjad calculation, Alif equals 1, Lam equals 30, and Mim equals 40. Thus, you have informed us that the duration of your nation's survival is no more than seventy-one years!" To prevent their exploitation of the matter, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Why have you calculated only 'Alif, Lam, Mim'? Are there not 'Alif, Lam, Mim, Sad' (المص), 'Alif, Lam, Ra' (الر), and other abbreviated letters in the Quran? If these letters were to indicate the duration of my nation's survival, why do you not calculate them all together?!" (While the purpose of these letters is something else entirely). Then the above verse was revealed.

In the commentary Fi Zilal al-Quran, another circumstance of revelation is mentioned for this verse, which aligns in result with the one above: A group of Christians from Najran came to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and used the Quranic expression regarding Christ—"His Word... and a Spirit from Him" [^1]—as a pretext. They sought to exploit it for the issue of "Trinity" and the "Divinity" of Christ, ignoring all those verses that explicitly negate any partner or likeness to Allah. Consequently, the above verse was revealed and gave them a decisive response.

Commentary

The Muhkam and Mutashabih in the Quran

In the preceding verses, the revelation of the Quran was mentioned as one of the clear proofs of the prophethood of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. In this verse, one of the characteristics of the Quran and the manner in which subjects are expressed in this great Heavenly Book is described. First, He says: "He is the One who has sent down to you the Book; in it are verses that are muhkam (clear and explicit)—they are the foundation of the Book (and they interpret other complex verses)—and others are mutashabih (ambiguous)." These are verses that, due to the high level of the subject matter or other factors, appear complex at first glance.

[^1]: Surah An-Nisa, Verse 171.

(هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ مِنْهُ آياتٌ مُحْكَماتٌ هُنَّ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ وَ أُخَرُ مُتَشابِهاتٌ)

These mutashabih verses serve as a touchstone for testing individuals, separating true scholars from stubborn troublemakers. Therefore, it continues: "As for those in whose hearts is deviation, they follow what is mutashabih of it, seeking discord and seeking an interpretation (an incorrect one according to their own desires) for it (to lead people astray), while no one knows its interpretation except Allah and those firmly rooted in knowledge" (فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ زَيْغٌ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ ما تَشابَهَ مِنْهُ ابْتِغاءَ الْفِتْنَةِ وَ ابْتِغاءَ تَأْوِيلِهِ وَ ما يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُ إِلَّا اللَّهُ وَ الرَّاسِخُونَ فِي الْعِلْمِ). [^1] Then it adds: It is they who, due to a correct understanding of the meaning of the muhkam and mutashabih verses, "say, 'We believe in it; all of it is from our Lord'" (يَقُولُونَ آمَنَّا بِهِ كُلٌّ مِنْ عِنْدِ رَبِّنا).

"(Indeed) none will be reminded except those of understanding" (وَ ما يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّا أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ).

Key Points

In this verse, there are important discussions that must each be addressed independently.

1- What is meant by Muhkam and Mutashabih verses?

The word "muhkam" is originally derived from "ihkam," meaning to prevent or forbid. For this reason, stable and firm entities are called muhkam, as they repel factors of deviation. Likewise, clear and decisive speech that removes any possibility of contradiction is called muhkam. (Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: Hukm (and Hikmah) originally means prevention). Knowledge is called Hikmah (wisdom) because it prevents a person from evils.

Therefore, the meaning of "ayatun muhkamat" refers to verses whose concepts are so clear that there is no room for debate or dispute. Verses such as: "Say, He is Allah, [who is] One" [^1], "There is nothing like unto Him" [^2], "Allah is the Creator of all things" [^3], "For the male, what is equal to the share of two females" [^4], and thousands of similar verses regarding beliefs, rulings, exhortations, and history—all are among the muhkamat.

These verses (muhkamat) are called "Umm al-Kitab" (the Mother of the Book) in the Quran, meaning they are the principle, the reference, the interpreter, and the explainer of other verses.

The word "mutashabih" originally means something whose various parts resemble one another. For this reason, sentences and words whose meanings are complex and sometimes allow for various possibilities are called "mutashabih." This is the meaning of the mutashabihah of the Quran: verses whose meanings are complex at first sight, and initially, multiple possibilities are perceived in them, even though their interpretation becomes clear when considering the muhkam verses.

Although commentators have offered many possibilities regarding muhkam and mutashabih [^5], what

[^1]: "Zaygh" (زَيْغٌ) originally means deviation from a straight state and leaning to one side. Having zaygh in the heart means the deviation of beliefs from the right path toward deviant ways.

we have stated above is most consistent with the original meaning of these two words, the circumstances of revelation, the narrations regarding the verse's interpretation, and the verse under discussion itself.

This is because at the end of the verse, we read that malicious individuals always use the mutashabih verses as their pretext. It is obvious that they exploit verses that, at first glance, are capable of multiple interpretations, and this itself shows that "mutashabih" carries the meaning mentioned above.

As examples of mutashabih verses, one can mention parts of the verses related to the attributes of God and the nature of the Resurrection, such as: "The Hand of Allah is over their hands" [^1], which refers to the power of Allah; "And Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing" [^2], which refers to Allah's knowledge; and "And We place the scales of justice for the Day of Resurrection" [^3], which speaks of the means of weighing deeds.

It is obvious that Allah has neither a hand (in the sense of a specific limb) nor an ear (in that same sense), nor is the scale for weighing deeds similar to our scales; rather, these refer to the general concepts of Power, Knowledge, and the means of evaluation.

It is also necessary to remind that muhkam and mutashabih appear in the Quran with another meaning as well. In the beginning of Surah Hud, we read: "A Book whose verses are perfected (uhkimat)." In this verse, all verses of the Quran are considered "muhkam," referring to the interconnectedness and cohesion of the Quranic verses. And in Verse 23 of Surah Az-Zumar, we read: "A Book that is mutashabih..."

Meaning a book whose verses are all mutashabih; here, "mutashabih" means similar to one another

[^1]: Surah Al-Ikhlas, Verse 1. [^2]: Surah Ash-Shura, Verse 11. [^3]: Surah Az-Zumar, Verse 62 (Note: original text says 26, likely a typo for 62). [^4]: Surah An-Nisa, Verse 11. [^5]: The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan mentioned five interpretations, Fakhr Razi mentioned four, the late Allamah in Al-Mizan mentioned sixteen, and finally, in Al-Bahr al-Muhit, about twenty interpretations are mentioned.

in terms of correctness, truthfulness, and authenticity.

From what we have said about muhkam and mutashabih, it becomes clear that a realistic and truth-seeking person has no way to understand the words of the Lord except by placing all verses side by side to find the truth. If they find ambiguity or complexity in the outward appearance of some verses at first glance, they should resolve that ambiguity by referring to other verses to reach the core truth.

In reality, the "muhkam verses" are like major highways, and the "mutashabih verses" are like side roads. It is clear that if a person occasionally becomes lost on the side roads, they try to reach the first highway and from there correct their path and find the way.

Describing the muhkamat as "Umm al-Kitab" also supports this reality, because the word "Umm" in the language means the root and foundation of anything. If a "mother" is called "Umm," it is because she is the root of the family and the refuge for children during events and difficulties. Thus, the muhkamat are considered the foundation, the root, and the mother of the other verses.

2- Why are some verses of the Quran Mutashabih?

Given that the Quran is light, clarity, and the word of truth and manifest, and has come for the guidance of all people, why does it contain mutashabih verses? Why is the content of some of its verses complex such that it allows for exploitation by troublemakers?

This is a very important subject that deserves careful attention. Generally, the following aspects may constitute the philosophy behind the existence of mutashabih verses in the Quran:

A) The words and expressions used in human dialogue were created only for daily needs. For this reason, as soon as we step outside the circle of

limited material human life—for example, when speaking about the Creator who is infinite in every respect—we clearly see that our words are not suitable molds for those meanings. We are forced to use words that are inadequate in various ways. These inadequacies of words are the source of a significant portion of the mutashabihah in the Quran. Verses like "The Hand of Allah is over their hands" [^1], or "The Most Merciful rose over the Throne" [^2], or "Looking toward their Lord" [^3]—the interpretation of each will come in its place—are of this type. Also, expressions such as "All-Hearing" and "All-Seeing" are all of this kind, which become clearly understood by referring to the muhkam verses.

B) Many truths relate to the other world or the metaphysical world, which is far from our horizon of thought. Because we are limited within the prison of time and space, we are unable to perceive their depth. This inadequacy of our thoughts and the high horizon of those meanings is another reason for the ambiguity of some verses, such as some verses related to the Resurrection and the like.

This is exactly like someone trying to explain the matters of this world to a child living in the womb. If they say nothing, they have fallen short; if they do speak, they are forced to convey the matters in a closed and simplified manner, because the listener in those conditions does not have the capacity or talent for more than that.

C) Another secret of the existence of mutashabih in the Quran is to activate thoughts and intellects and to create an intellectual movement among the people. This is exactly like complex intellectual problems designed to strengthen the minds of thinkers,

[^1]: Surah Al-Fath, Verse 10. [^2]: Surah Ta-Ha, Verse 5. [^3]: Surah Al-Qiyamah, Verse 23.

so that they engage more in reflection, thought, precision, and investigation of issues.

D) Another point in mentioning the mutashabih in the Quran, which the reports of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام confirm, is that the existence of such verses clarifies the people's intense need for Divine leaders, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and his successors. It causes people, by virtue of their scientific need, to seek them out, formally recognize their leadership, and benefit from their other sciences and various guidance. This is exactly like some textbooks where the explanation of certain issues is left to the teacher and professor, so that students do not cut their ties with the teacher and, due to this need, take inspiration from their thoughts in everything. In fact, the Quran is the manifestation of the famous testament of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله who said:

"I am leaving among you two weighty things: the Book of Allah and my progeny (Ahl al-Bayt), and these two shall never separate until they return to me at the Pond (Kawthar)." [^1]

E) The issue of testing individuals and distinguishing troublemakers from true believers is another philosophy mentioned in the verse.

3- What is Ta'wil?

Much has been said about the meaning of "ta'wil." What is closest to the truth is that ta'wil in its original linguistic sense means "returning something." Therefore, any action or speech that we bring to its ultimate goal is called ta'wil. For example, if someone takes an action whose primary goal is not clear, and at the end, they clarify it, this

[^1]: Mustadrak al-Hakim, Volume 3, Page 148 (Beirut edition).

action is called "ta'wil." Just as we read in the story of Musa عليه السلام and that learned man (Khidr), who performed actions during their journey whose purpose was not clear (such as breaking the ship). For this reason, Musa became upset and alarmed. But when he explained his purpose at the end of the task and at the time of parting, saying his intention was to save the ship from the clutches of a usurping and oppressive king, he added:

"That is the ta'wil (interpretation/ultimate goal) of that about which you could not have patience." [^1]

Similarly, if a person sees a dream whose result is not clear, and then by referring to someone or witnessing a scene, they discover the interpretation of that dream, it is called "ta'wil." Just as Yusuf عليه السلام, after his famous dream was realized in reality—or reached its ultimate "return"—said: "This is the ta'wil of my dream of before." [^2]

Also, whenever a person speaks and there are specific concepts and secrets hidden within it that constitute the ultimate goal of that speech, it is called ta'wil.

In the verse under discussion, ta'wil carries this meaning: that in the Quran there are verses with deep secrets and meanings, but individuals whose thoughts are deviant and who have corrupt motives fabricate an incorrect interpretation and meaning for them on their own, relying on it to deceive themselves or others.

Therefore, the meaning of the phrase "seeking its ta'wil" (وَ ابْتِغاءَ تَأْوِيلِهِ) is that they want to reflect the interpretation of the verses in a form other than what it truly is (seeking its interpretation contrary to the truth).

[^1]: Surah Al-Kahf, Verse 82 (Note: original text says 83). [^2]: Surah Yusuf, Verse 100.

Like what we read in the circumstances of revelation, where a group of Jews exploited the abbreviated letters of the Quran, interpreting them to mean the short duration of the religion of Islam, or the Christians who used the word "Spirit from Him" as a pretext to argue for the divinity of Jesus. All of these are considered "interpretation contrary to the truth" and returning the verse to an unrealistic and incorrect goal.

4- Who are "Those Firmly Rooted in Knowledge"?

In the Holy Quran, this expression is used in two places: one here, and the other in Surah An-Nisa, Verse 162, where He says:

"But those firmly rooted in knowledge among them and the believers believe in what has been revealed to you and what was revealed before you."

As understood from the linguistic meaning of this word, it refers to those who are steadfast and possess expert insight in science and knowledge.

Of course, the concept of this word is broad, encompassing all scholars and thinkers. However, among them are distinguished individuals who possess a special brilliance and naturally occupy the first rank among the instances of this word. When this expression is mentioned, all eyes turn toward them first.

And if we observe that in numerous narrations "those firmly rooted in knowledge" (al-rasikhuna fil-ilm) is interpreted as the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of Guidance عليهم السلام, it is based on this view. For we have said many times that the verses and words of the Quran have broad concepts, among whose instances are

exemplary and extraordinary individuals who are sometimes the only ones named in their interpretation.

In Usul al-Kafi, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir or Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said:

"The Messenger of Allah was the greatest of those firmly rooted in knowledge; he knew all that Allah had revealed to him of the interpretation (ta'wil) and revelation (tanzil) of the Quran. Allah never revealed anything to him without teaching him its interpretation, and he and his successors knew all of these."

Many other narrations have been recorded in the book Usul al-Kafi [^1] and other books of hadith in this regard, which the authors of Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn and Tafsir al-Burhan have collected under this verse. As mentioned, interpreting "those firmly rooted in knowledge" as the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of Guidance عليهم السلام does not contradict the breadth of this expression's concept. Thus, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that he used to say: "I am also among those firmly rooted in knowledge," except that everyone becomes aware of the secrets and interpretation of the Quranic verses according to the extent of their knowledge. Those whose knowledge stems from the infinite knowledge of the Lord are naturally acquainted with all the secrets and interpretation of the Quran, while others know only a part of these secrets.

5- "Those Firmly Rooted in Knowledge" are aware of the meaning of the Mutashabihah

There is an important discussion among commentators and scholars here: whether "and those firmly rooted in knowledge" (وَ الرَّاسِخُونَ فِي الْعِلْمِ) begins a new independent sentence or is conjoined (atf) to "except Allah" (إِلَّا اللَّهُ). In other words, does the verse mean: "No one knows the interpretation of the Quran except Allah and those firmly rooted in knowledge," or does it mean: "Only Allah knows the interpretation of the Quran, but those firmly rooted in knowledge say, 'Even though we do not know the interpretation of the mutashabih verses, we submit to all of them, and all are from our Lord'"?

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Volume 1, Page 213.

Proponents of each of these two views have brought evidence to prove their belief. However, what aligns with the existing clues in the verse and the famous narrations is that "and those firmly rooted in knowledge" is conjoined to "Allah," because:

Firstly, it seems very unlikely that there are verses in the Quran whose secrets no one but Allah knows. Were these verses not revealed for the education and guidance of the people? How is it possible that even the Prophet upon whom the Quran was revealed would be unaware of its meaning and interpretation?! This would be exactly like someone writing a book whose sentences no one but himself understands!

Secondly, as the late Tabarsi says in Majma' al-Bayan: It has never been seen among Islamic scholars and Quranic commentators that they refrain from discussing the interpretation of a verse and say, "This verse is among those whose ultimate meaning no one but Allah knows." Rather, they have all constantly strived and made efforts to discover the secrets and meanings of the Quran.

Thirdly, if the intention were that those firmly rooted in knowledge submit to what they do not know, it would have been more appropriate to say: "those firmly rooted in faith" are such, because being "firmly rooted in knowledge" is consistent with knowing the interpretation of the Quran, not with ignorance and mere submission.

Fourthly, the numerous narrations cited in the interpretation of the verse all confirm that those firmly rooted in knowledge know the interpretation of the Quranic verses. Therefore, it must be conjoined to the word "Allah." The only thing that remains here is that a sentence in the "Sermon of the Ghosts" (Khutbat al-Ashbah) from Nahj al-Balagha suggests that those firmly rooted in knowledge do not know the interpretation of the verses and confess their inability:

"Know that those firmly rooted in knowledge are those whom the confession of all that they are ignorant of regarding the interpretation of the veiled unseen has made free from attempting to enter the closed doors set before the unseen..." [^1]

But in addition to the fact that this sentence is inconsistent with some narrations narrated from the Imam himself—which conjoin those firmly rooted in knowledge to "Allah" and introduce them as aware of the Quran's interpretation [^2]—it also does not align with the aforementioned reasons. Therefore, this sentence from the Sermon of the Ghosts must be explained and interpreted in a way that does not contradict the other evidence we have.

6- Conclusion of the Discussion on the Verse's Interpretation

From the sum of what has been said regarding the interpretation of the above verse, the following is concluded:

The verses of the Quran are of two types: the concept of one part of the verses is so clear that there is no room for any denial, justification, or exploitation, and these are called "muhkamat." Another part, due to the high level of the subject matter or discussion about realms beyond our reach—such as the world of the unseen, the world of Resurrection, and the attributes of Allah—is such that their ultimate meaning, secrets, and core reality require a specific scientific capital; these are called "mutashabihat."

Deviant individuals usually try to use these verses as a pretext and fabricate an interpretation contrary to the truth for them, to create discord among the people and lead them away from the path of truth. However, Allah and those firmly rooted in knowledge know the secrets of these verses and explain them to the people. In the light of their vast knowledge, they understand the mutashabih verses just like the

muhkam verses. For this reason, they submit to all of them and say: "All verses are from our Lord," because all of them, both muhkam and mutashabih, are clear in the light of their knowledge, as stated in the text of the verse:

"They say, 'We believe in it; all of it is from our Lord.'" In this way, being firmly rooted in knowledge causes a person to become increasingly aware of the secrets of the Quran. Of course, those who are in the first rank of science and knowledge (such as the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of Guidance عليهم السلام) are aware of all its secrets, while others understand something of it according to the extent of their own knowledge. It is this very reality that sends people, even scholars, to follow the Divine teachers to understand the secrets of the Quran.

7- The phrase "And none will be reminded except those of understanding" (وَ ما يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّا أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ) at the end of the verse indicates that only thinkers know these truths. They are the ones who understand why the Quran must have muhkam and mutashabih verses, and they are the ones who understand that one must place the mutashabih verses alongside the muhkam verses to discover their secrets. Hence, it is narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام that he said:

"Whoever refers the mutashabih of the Quran to its muhkam has been guided to a straight path." [^1]

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 91. [^2]: Refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 1, Page 315. [^1]: Tafsir al-Safi, under the verse discussed.

Ayahs

رَبَّنَا لَا تُزِغْ قُلُوبَنَا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَيْتَنَا وَهَبْ لَنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ رَحْمَةً ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْوَهَّابُ (٨)
[They say,] ‘Our Lord! Do not make our hearts swerve after You have guided us, and bestow Your mercy on us. Indeed, You are the All-munificent. (8)
رَبَّنَآ إِنَّكَ جَامِعُ ٱلنَّاسِ لِيَوْمٍ لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُخْلِفُ ٱلْمِيعَادَ (٩)
Our Lord! You will indeed gather mankind on a day in which there is no doubt. Indeed Allah does not break His promise.’ (9)

Commentary

Deliverance from Slips

Since the Mutashabih (ambiguous) verses and their hidden secrets may become a stumbling block for individuals, causing them to emerge from this trial with blackened faces, the "Firmly Rooted in Knowledge" (Rasikhun fi al-Ilm) and the faithful thinkers—in addition to employing their intellectual assets to understand the meaning of these verses—seek refuge in their Lord. These two verses, spoken from the tongue of the Firmly Rooted in Knowledge, illuminate this reality. They say: "Our Lord, let not our hearts deviate after You have guided us and grant us from Yourself mercy. Indeed, You are the Bestower" (رَبَّنا لا تُزِغْ قُلُوبَنا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَيْتَنا وَ هَبْ لَنا مِنْ لَدُنْكَ رَحْمَةً إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْوَهَّابُ‌).

There are many scholars whom intellectual pride brings to ruin, or whom the whisperings of the devils and carnal desires lead astray into bypasses; it is here that one must entrust oneself to Allah and seek guidance from Him.

It is even mentioned in some narrations that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله himself would entrust himself to Allah and frequently repeat this supplication:

Ya muqallib al-qulub, thabbit qalbi 'ala dinik

"O Turner of hearts! Keep my heart firm upon Your religion." [^1]

And since belief in the Hereafter and mindfulness of the Day of Resurrection is more effective than anything else for controlling desires and whims, the Firmly Rooted in Knowledge remember that day and say: "Our Lord, surely You will gather the people for a Day about which there is no doubt. Indeed, Allah does not fail in His promise" (رَبَّنا إِنَّكَ جامِعُ النَّاسِ لِيَوْمٍ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يُخْلِفُ الْمِيعادَ).

In this way, they distance themselves from whims, desires, and excessive emotions that cause one to slip.

Indeed, it is such individuals who can understand the verses of Allah as they truly are and remain safe from deviation. (In reality, the first verse refers to their perfect faith in the Origin/Source, and the second verse refers to their firm faith in the Resurrection).

[^1]: Tafsir al-Maraghi, Volume 3, Page 102.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَن تُغْنِىَ عَنْهُمْ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمْ وَقُودُ ٱلنَّارِ (١٠)
As for the faithless, neither their wealth nor their children shall avail them anything against Allah; it is they who will be fuel for the Fire; (10)
كَدَأْبِ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (١١)
as in the case of Pharaoh’s clan and those who were before them, who denied Our signs. So Allah seized them for their sins, and Allah is severe in retribution. (11)

Commentary

In the preceding verses, the state of the believers and non-believers regarding the Muhkam (clear) and Mutashabih (ambiguous) verses was explained. Continuing this discussion, the current verse unveils the painful state of the disbelievers on the Day of Resurrection and visualizes the grim consequences of their actions for them, stating: "Indeed, those who disbelieve—neither their wealth nor their children will avail them against Allah in the least (nor will they assist them against Divine punishment), and it is they who are the fuel of the Fire" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَنْ تُغْنِيَ عَنْهُمْ أَمْوالُهُمْ وَ لا أَوْلادُهُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ شَيْئاً وَ أُولئِكَ هُمْ وَقُودُ النَّارِ).

If they imagine that an abundance of wealth, manpower, and children can protect them from Divine punishment in this world or the Hereafter, they are gravely mistaken.

Some commentators have considered this verse to refer to the Jews of Banu Nadir and Banu Qurayza, who took pride in their wealth and children; however, they were certainly just one of the instances of this verse, and its concept is so broad that it encompasses the disbelievers of both the past and the present.

"Waqud"—as previously mentioned—means "fuel" or that which is used to kindle a fire (like firewood), not the igniter (like a match). The expression "وَ أُولئِكَ هُمْ وَقُودُ النَّارِ" (and they are the fuel of the fire of Hell) conveys that the fire of Hell blazes from within their own beings, and it is their very existence that burns them.

Of course, in some verses, we read that the fuel of Hell, in addition to sinners, includes stones, which apparently refers to the idols they fashioned from stone. Therefore, the fire of Hell is ignited from within their own beings, their actions, and their false deities.

Then, it points to a clear example of nations that possessed abundant wealth and manpower, yet these things could not prevent their destruction when the punishment descended, stating: "[Their plight is] like the custom of the people of Pharaoh and those before them. They denied Our signs (and were deluded by the abundance of their wealth and children), so Allah seized them for their sins, and Allah is severe in retribution" (كَدَأْبِ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ وَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا فَأَخَذَهُمُ اللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَ اللَّهُ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ).

"Da’b" originally means the continuation of a journey or movement, and it also means "any continuous work or habit." In the above verse, the state of the disbelievers contemporary to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is likened to the continuous habit and incorrect conduct of the people of Pharaoh and the nations before them, who denied the signs of Allah, and Allah seized them for their sins, and they were afflicted with a severe punishment in this very world.

This is, in fact, a warning to all stubborn disbelievers to remember the fate of the Pharaohs and previous nations and to be mindful of their actions.

It is true that Allah is the Most Merciful of the merciful, but when necessary for the discipline of His servants, He is also severe in retribution, and the vast mercy of the Lord should never cause anyone to become deluded.

Furthermore, from the word "da’b," it is understood that this wrong program—namely, stubbornness against the truth and denying the signs of the Lord—had become their nature and habit, and for this reason, they have been threatened with severe punishment. For as long as "sin and transgression" have not taken the form of a habit, tradition, and lifestyle, turning back from them is easy and the punishment is relatively light. But once it penetrates a person's being, both returning from it is difficult and its punishment is heavy. Thus, it is better for the disbelievers and sinners to return from the wrong path before it is too late.

Ayah

قُل لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ سَتُغْلَبُونَ وَتُحْشَرُونَ إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ ۚ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمِهَادُ (١٢)
Say to the faithless, ‘You shall be overcome and mustered toward hell, and it is an evil resting place.’ (12)

Occasion of Revelation

After the Battle of Badr and the victory of the Muslims, a group of Jews said: "This is the same Unlettered Prophet whose description we have read in our religious book (the Torah), stating that he will not be defeated in war." Others said: "Do not be hasty until another battle and event occurs; only then judge." When the Battle of Uhud took place and ended in the apparent defeat of the Muslims, they said: "No, by God, this is not the prophet who was promised in our book." Following this event, not only did they refuse to become Muslims, but they also increased their hostility and distance from the Prophet and the Muslims. They even violated the non-aggression pact they had with the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله before its expiration. Sixty horsemen, accompanied by Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf, set out for Mecca and formed an alliance with the polytheists to fight against Islam, then returned to Medina. At this time, the above verse was revealed, providing a crushing response to them, telling them to consider the final outcome and to know that they would all soon be defeated. [^1]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1-2, p. 413.

Commentary

False Expectations

In light of the aforementioned occasion of revelation, it becomes clear that the disbelievers, who were arrogant due to their wealth, riches, children, and numbers, were expecting the defeat of Islam. However, the Quran explicitly states in this verse: let them be certain that they will soon be defeated. Addressing the Prophet, He says: "Say to those who disbelieve: 'You will be overcome (humiliated and insignificant in this world) and gathered and driven towards Hell (in the Hereafter)—and what an evil resting place it is!'" (قُلْ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَتُغْلَبُونَ وَ تُحْشَرُونَ إِلى‌ جَهَنَّمَ وَ بِئْسَ الْمِهادُ). [^1]

Key Point: A Clear Prophecy

In the Holy Quran, there are numerous reports of the unseen (ghayb) which serve as evidence of the greatness and miraculous nature (i'jaz) of the Quran, and one example is found in the above verse.

In this verse, Allah explicitly gives glad tidings to His Prophet that he will prevail over all enemies, and He tells the disbelievers that in addition to being defeated and overcome in this world, they will also face a grim fate in the next world.

Considering the occasion of revelation, this verse was revealed after the Battle of Uhud, at a time when the Muslims had outwardly lost their power and influence, and the enemies of Islam had gained significant strength and force by joining together and forming alliances.

[^1]: According to Raghib, "Mihad" means a prepared and ready place, and it is originally derived from the root "Mahd," meaning a cradle and a child's place of rest.

Under such circumstances, an explicit prophecy regarding the near future—as understood from the phrase "ستغلبون" (you will soon be defeated)—is a remarkable matter. Therefore, this verse can be considered among the miraculous verses of the Quran, as it contains explicit news of future events at a time when the signs of the Muslims' victory over the disbelievers and the Jews were not apparent.

It did not take long for the content of the verse to be realized; the Jews of Medina (Bani Qurayza and Bani Nadir) were crushed, and in the Battle of Khaybar, their most important center of power was dismantled, while the polytheists were also permanently defeated during the Conquest of Mecca.

Ayah

قَدْ كَانَ لَكُمْ ءَايَةٌ فِى فِئَتَيْنِ ٱلْتَقَتَا ۖ فِئَةٌ تُقَـٰتِلُ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَأُخْرَىٰ كَافِرَةٌ يَرَوْنَهُم مِّثْلَيْهِمْ رَأْىَ ٱلْعَيْنِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يُؤَيِّدُ بِنَصْرِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ (١٣)
There was certainly a sign for you in the two hosts that met: one host fighting in the way of Allah and the other faithless, who saw them as visibly twice as many. Allah strengthens whomever He wishes with His help. There is indeed a moral in that for those who have insight. (13)

Occasion of Revelation

This verse was revealed regarding the circumstances of the Battle of "Badr," as commentators have stated. In the Battle of Badr, the number of Muslims was 313; 77 of them were from the Muhajirun (Emigrants) and 236 were from the Ansar (Helpers). The banner of the Muhajirun was in the hand of Ali عليه السلام, and Sa'd ibn Ubadah was the standard-bearer of the Ansar. They participated in this great battle possessing only seventy camels, two horses, six suits of armor, and eight swords. This was while the enemy army exceeded one thousand men with sufficient weaponry and one hundred horses. The Muslims, after giving twenty-two martyrs (14 from the Muhajirun and 8 from the Ansar), prevailed over the enemy, who suffered seventy dead and seventy captured, and returned to Medina with a complete victory. This verse recounts a portion of the event of Badr [^1].

[^1]: What is mentioned above is from Majma' al-Bayan, but in Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh by Ibn al-Athir, Vol. 3, p. 136, it states: "The total of those killed from the Muslims at Badr was fourteen men: six from the Muhajirun and eight from the Ansar."

Commentary

The Battle of Badr Was a Clear Example

This verse is, in fact, an explanation of an example of what appeared in the previous verses. It warns the disbelievers not to be deceived by their wealth, riches, and numerical strength, as these will not benefit them. A living witness to this subject is the Battle of Badr, where the enemies of Islam, despite their superior military equipment, numbers, and wealth, suffered a severe defeat. It says: "There was a sign and a lesson for you in the two groups (who) met (on the battlefield of Badr)" (قَدْ كانَ لَكُمْ آيَةٌ فِي فِئَتَيْنِ الْتَقَتا).

"One group was fighting in the way of Allah, and the other was disbelieving" and fighting in the way of Satan and idols (فِئَةٌ تُقاتِلُ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَ أُخْرى‌ كافِرَةٌ).

Why should you not take heed when a small army, almost lacking military equipment but possessing firm and solid faith, triumphed over an army that was several times its size in terms of weaponry and manpower? If numerical superiority and resources alone could be the solution, it should have been manifest in the Battle of Badr, whereas the result was the opposite. Then it adds: "They (the polytheists) saw them (the believers) with their own eyes as twice as many as they were" (يَرَوْنَهُمْ مِثْلَيْهِمْ رَأْيَ الْعَيْنِ‌).

This means that if the believers were 313, they appeared to be more than 600 in the eyes of the disbelievers to increase their terror, and this itself became one of the factors in the defeat of the disbelievers.

In addition to being a form of Divine assistance, this matter also seems natural from one perspective. For when the battle began and the crushing blows of the Muslims—which stemmed from the power of faith—struck the body of the enemy army, they became so intimidated and terrified that they thought another force equal to them had joined them and had taken control of the battlefield with twice the initial power. This is while before the start of the battle, the matter was the opposite; they looked upon the Muslims with such contempt that they imagined their number to be less than it actually was. In other words, Allah willed that before the battle began, the number of Muslims should appear small in their eyes so they would enter the war with pride and negligence, and after the battle started, they should appear as double so that terror and anxiety would overwhelm them and lead to their defeat. Conversely, Allah made the number of the enemy appear small in the eyes of the Muslims to increase their morale and strength.

This is the same thing referred to in verse 44 of Surah Al-Anfal, which says: وَ إِذْ يُرِيكُمُوهُمْ إِذِ الْتَقَيْتُمْ فِي أَعْيُنِكُمْ قَلِيلًا وَ يُقَلِّلُكُمْ فِي أَعْيُنِهِمْ لِيَقْضِيَ اللَّهُ أَمْراً كانَ مَفْعُولًا: "And remember when you met, He showed them to you as few in your eyes (so your morale for battle would not weaken) and He made you appear as few in their eyes (so they would not refrain from starting a war whose end was their defeat), so that Allah might accomplish a matter that was to be done."

Meaning, you should not fear and should enter the battle with full strength, and they too should enter the battle initially with pride and indifference, and then the scene would change, and the Muslims would appear more than they were in the eyes of the enemy, leading to their defeat in this decisive battle [^1]. It is mentioned in some narrations that one of the Muslims said: "Before the Battle of Badr, I said to another: 'Do you think the disbelievers are seventy?' He said: 'I think they are one hundred.'"

[^1]: The commentary provided above is based on the view that in the verse under discussion, the referent of the pronoun in "they saw" (yarawnahum) is the disbelievers, and the referent of the pronoun "them" (hum) is the Muslims. This is the clearest interpretation of the verse and is consistent with verse 44 of Surah Al-Anfal, although commentators have given other possibilities regarding the referents of the aforementioned pronouns—further explanation on this subject will come under verses 41 to 45 of Surah Al-Anfal, Allah willing.

"But when we were victorious in the battle and took many prisoners from them, they informed us that they were a thousand" [^1]. Then it adds: "And Allah supports with His help whom He wills" (وَ اللَّهُ يُؤَيِّدُ بِنَصْرِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ).

As we have said many times, the will and volition of Allah are not without reason and are always intertwined with His wisdom; He does not support and strengthen individuals unless there are merits within them.

It is noteworthy that Allah's support and victory in this historical event for the Muslims were twofold: it was both a "military victory" and a "logical victory." It was a military victory in that a small army without sufficient resources triumphed over an army several times its size that enjoyed abundant resources.

As for the logical victory, it was because Allah had explicitly promised this victory to the Muslims before the start of the war, and this became a proof of the truthfulness of Islam.

At the end of the verse, it says: "Indeed, in that is a lesson for those of vision and insight" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِأُولِي الْأَبْصارِ).

Yes, those who possess the eye of insight and see the truth as it is, take a lesson from this comprehensive victory of the people of faith and know that the primary capital for victory is faith and faith alone [^2].

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 2, p. 1268.

[^2]: "Ibrah" (lesson/example) is originally derived from the root "ubur" (crossing), which means passing from one state to another or from one place to another. A tear is called "abrah" (with the weight of hasrah) because it passes from the eye, and words that pass from tongues and ears are called "ibarah" (expression). Taking a lesson (ibrah) from events is so named because a person passes from what he sees and becomes aware of the realities behind it.

Ayah

زُيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ حُبُّ ٱلشَّهَوَٰتِ مِنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ وَٱلْبَنِينَ وَٱلْقَنَـٰطِيرِ ٱلْمُقَنطَرَةِ مِنَ ٱلذَّهَبِ وَٱلْفِضَّةِ وَٱلْخَيْلِ ٱلْمُسَوَّمَةِ وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ وَٱلْحَرْثِ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَٱللَّهُ عِندَهُۥ حُسْنُ ٱلْمَـَٔابِ (١٤)
The love of [worldly] allures, including women and children, accumulated piles of gold and silver, horses of mark, livestock, and farms has been made to seem decorous to mankind. Those are the wares of the life of this world, but the goodness of one’s ultimate destination lies near Allah. (14)

Commentary

The Attraction of Material Ornaments
