In the preceding verses, the discussion concerned those who relied upon their wealth and children in the life of this world, becoming arrogant and considering themselves independent of God. This verse, in fact, serves as a completion to that discourse, stating: "The things people love—including women, children, heaped-up hoards of gold and silver, branded horses, cattle, and tilled land—have been made fair-seeming to them" so that they may be tested by them (زُيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ حُبُّ الشَّهَواتِ مِنَ النِّساءِ وَ الْبَنِينَ وَ الْقَناطِيرِ الْمُقَنْطَرَةِ مِنَ الذَّهَبِ وَ الْفِضَّةِ وَ الْخَيْلِ الْمُسَوَّمَةِ وَ الْأَنْعامِ وَ الْحَرْثِ‌). [^1] "But these are the provisions of the life of this world (and should never constitute man's ultimate goal), and the excellent destination (and eternal life) is with Allah" (ذلِكَ مَتاعُ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ اللَّهُ عِنْدَهُ حُسْنُ الْمَآبِ‌).

[^1]: "Shahawat" (شهوات) is the plural of "Shahwah" (شهوة), meaning an intense desire for something; however, in the above verse, "Shahawat" is used to mean "Mushtahayat" (the objects of desire).

It is true that one cannot live without these means, and even traversing the path of spirituality and felicity is impossible without material means. However, utilizing them in this path is one matter, while extreme attachment, worshipping them, and making them the ultimate goal is quite another.

Key Points

1- Who has beautified these material matters?

The phrase "زُيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ حُبُّ الشَّهَواتِ" (The love of desires has been made fair-seeming to people), which is mentioned in the passive voice, states that the love for women, children, property, and wealth has been beautified in the eyes of people. Here, the question arises: who is the beautifier?

Some commentators believe these are satanic whims that beautify them in people's eyes, citing verse 24 of Surah An-Naml: "وَ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ أَعْمالَهُمْ‌" (And Satan made their deeds fair-seeming to them) and similar verses. However, this reasoning does not seem correct because the verse under discussion does not speak of "deeds," but rather of wealth, women, and children.

What seems correct in the interpretation of the verse is that the beautifier is Allah, through the system of creation and the innate nature of man.

For it is Allah Who has placed the love for children, property, and wealth within human nature to test him and to advance him on the path of perfection and training, just as the Quran says:

"إِنَّا جَعَلْنا ما عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَها لِنَبْلُوَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا" — "Indeed, We have made that which is on the earth an ornament for it that We may test them [as to] which of them is best in deed." [^1] This means they should utilize this love and affection only in the path of happiness and construction, not in the path of corruption and destruction.

It is interesting that in the verse under discussion, the first subject mentioned is spouses and women. This aligns with what modern psychologists say: "The sexual instinct is among the most powerful human instincts. Both contemporary and past history confirm that the source of many social events has been the storms resulting from this instinct."

It is also necessary to note that the verse under discussion and other similar verses never condemn a moderate affection toward one's spouse, children, property, and wealth, because advancing spiritual goals is impossible without material means. Furthermore, the law of Sharia can never be contrary to the law of creation. What is condemned is excessive love and attachment—in other words, the "worship" of these things.

[^1]: Surah Al-Kahf, Verse 7.

2- What is meant by "الْقَناطِيرِ الْمُقَنْطَرَةِ" and "الْخَيْلِ الْمُسَوَّمَةِ"?

The word "Qanatir" (قناطير) is the plural of "Qintar" (قنطار), meaning something solid and firm, and subsequently, it was applied to a large amount of wealth. If we see that a bridge is called "Qantarah" and intelligent people are called "Qantar," it is because of the firmness in the structure or in their thinking. "Muqantarah" (مقنطره) is the passive participle from the same root, meaning doubled and repeated, and the mention of these two words consecutively is for emphasis, similar to the expression common in Persian today where they say someone possesses "Alaf" and "Uluf," meaning they have great wealth.

Some have defined a specific limit for "Qintar," saying it is seventy thousand gold dinars; some say one hundred thousand, and some twelve thousand dirhams. Others have considered "Qintar" to be a bag full of gold or silver coins.

In a narration from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is mentioned that a Qintar is the amount of gold that would fill a bull's hide. In reality, however, all of these are instances of a broad concept: a large amount of wealth.

The word "Khayl" (خيل) is a collective noun meaning both "horses" and "horsemen." Of course, in the verse under discussion, the first meaning is intended.

The word "Musawwamah" (مسومة) originally means "branded" or "marked." They are marked either because of their distinguished physique and distinct appearance, or because of their training and readiness for riding on the battlefield.

Therefore, the verse under discussion refers to six important assets of life: women, children, cash (gold and silver), distinguished mounts, livestock used in animal husbandry (An'am), and crops (Harth), which constitute the pillars of man's material life.

3- What is meant by the "provision of the life of this world"?

"Mata'" (متاع) refers to something from which man derives benefit, and "al-Hayat al-Dunya" (الحياة الدنيا) means the "lower and base life." Therefore, the meaning of the phrase "ذلِكَ مَتاعُ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا" is that if someone loves these six things only as an ultimate goal and does not utilize them as a ladder on the path of human life, such a person has submitted to a base life.

In fact, the phrase "al-Hayat al-Dunya" (the lower life) refers to the evolutionary trajectory of life, of which the life of this world is considered the first stage. Thus, at the end of the verse, an abbreviated reference is made to that higher life awaiting humanity, stating: "وَ اللَّهُ عِنْدَهُ حُسْنُ الْمَآبِ‌," meaning the excellent destination is with Allah.

4- As mentioned, among material blessings, women are mentioned first because, compared to other blessings, they play a more significant role in attracting the thoughts of world-worshippers and prompting them toward horrific crimes!

Ayahs

۞ قُلْ أَؤُنَبِّئُكُم بِخَيْرٍ مِّن ذَٰلِكُمْ ۚ لِلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا وَأَزْوَٰجٌ مُّطَهَّرَةٌ وَرِضْوَٰنٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ (١٥)
Say, ‘Shall I inform you of something better than that? For those who are Godwary there will be gardens near their Lord, with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever], and chaste mates, and Allah’s pleasure.’ And Allah watches His servants. (15)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ إِنَّنَآ ءَامَنَّا فَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَا وَقِنَا عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ (١٦)
Those who say, ‘Our Lord! Indeed, we have faith. So forgive us our sins, and save us from the punishment of the Fire.’ (16)
ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ وَٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ وَٱلْقَـٰنِتِينَ وَٱلْمُنفِقِينَ وَٱلْمُسْتَغْفِرِينَ بِٱلْأَسْحَارِ (١٧)
[They are] patient and truthful, obedient and charitable, and they plead for [Allah’s] forgiveness at dawns. (17)

Commentary

In light of what was mentioned in the previous verse regarding the things humans love in the material life of this world, here, in a comparison, the verse points to the extraordinary bounties of Allah in the Hereafter and ultimately the ascending arc of human perfection, stating: "Say: Shall I inform you of something better than that (these material assets)?" (قُلْ أَ أُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِخَيْرٍ مِنْ ذلِكُمْ‌).

Then, proceeding to describe it, it adds: "For those who keep their duty (to Allah), with their Lord are Gardens underneath which rivers flow wherein they will abide forever, and pure companions, and (above all) the pleasure of Allah; and Allah is Seer of His servants" (لِلَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّاتٌ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها وَ أَزْواجٌ مُطَهَّرَةٌ وَ رِضْوانٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ اللَّهُ بَصِيرٌ بِالْعِبادِ).

Indeed, unlike most gardens of this world, the flowing water beneath the trees of these heavenly gardens is never cut off. And unlike the material blessings of this world, which are very fleeting and unstable, these are eternal and everlasting.

The companions of that world, unlike many of the beauties of this world, have no dark or negative point in their body or soul and are pure and cleansed from every defect and flaw. All of these are on one side, and the matter of Allah's pleasure (رِضْوانٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ‌), which is the highest of spiritual blessings, is on the other. Yes, this spiritual blessing, which is beyond imagination, also awaits the God-fearing believers.

It is noteworthy that this verse begins with the phrase "أَ أُنَبِّئُكُمْ‌" (Shall I inform you?), which is an interrogative sentence posed to elicit an answer from the awakened fitrah (innate nature) and human intellect, so that its influence on the listener may be deeper. On the other hand, this word is derived from the root "inba’," which is usually used for important and significant news.

In fact, in this verse, the Holy Quran announces to the believers that if they content themselves with the lawful life of this world and refrain from illegitimate pleasures, rebellious desires, and oppression of others, Allah will grant them superior and higher pleasures, both material and spiritual, which are pure and cleansed from any kind of defect or flaw.

Here a question arises: Are there material pleasures in Paradise as well?

Some imagine that material pleasures are limited to this world, and in that world, there is no trace of these pleasures; they claim all the expressions used in the Quranic verses regarding heavenly gardens, various fruits, flowing waters, and good companions are metaphors for a series of spiritual ranks and blessings, expressed in this way based on the principle of "Speak to people according to the capacity of their intellects."

However, against this notion, it must be said: when we accept "Physical Resurrection" (Ma'ad Jismani) according to the explicit text of numerous verses of the Holy Quran, there must be blessings suitable for both parts (body and soul), albeit at a much higher level. Coincidentally, this verse points to both parts: both what is worthy of the physical resurrection and what is worthy of the soul and the resurrection of spirits.

In reality, those who take all the material blessings of that world as metaphors for spiritual blessings have been influenced by eclectic schools of thought. Furthermore, they have unnecessarily interpreted the literal meanings of the Quranic verses and have completely forgotten and denied the physical resurrection and its requirements.

Perhaps the phrase "وَ اللَّهُ بَصِيرٌ بِالْعِبادِ" (And Allah is Seer of His servants) at the end of the verse refers to this very reality; that is, He is the One who knows what the human body and soul each desire in the other world, and He provides these desires in both aspects in the best possible way.

In the following verse, it proceeds to introduce the God-fearing servants mentioned in the previous verse and enumerates six distinguished attributes for them:

1- First: They are turned toward their Lord with all their heart and soul, and faith has illuminated their hearts. For this reason, they feel a heavy sense of responsibility regarding their actions, saying: "Those who say: Our Lord! Lo! we believe. So forgive us our sins and guard us from the punishment of Fire" (الَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنا إِنَّنا آمَنَّا فَاغْفِرْ لَنا ذُنُوبَنا وَ قِنا عَذابَ النَّارِ).

2- "Those who are patient and steadfast," showing patience and standing firm against the difficult events that arise in the path of obeying the Lord, as well as against sins, and during the occurrence of individual and social hardships and calamities (الصَّابِرِينَ‌).

3- "Those who are truthful and upright in conduct," acting outwardly according to what they believe inwardly, and staying far from hypocrisy, lies, deception, and betrayal (وَ الصَّادِقِينَ‌).

4- "Those who are humble and obedient," and are constant in this in the path of servitude and worship of God (وَ الْقانِتِينَ‌). [^1]

5- "Those who spend in the way of Allah," giving to the needy not only from their wealth but from all the material and spiritual blessings at their disposal (وَ الْمُنْفِقِينَ‌).

6- "And those who seek forgiveness at dawn" (وَ الْمُسْتَغْفِرِينَ بِالْأَسْحارِ).

At that time when the eyes of the heedless and unaware are asleep, and the clamor of the material world has subsided—and for this reason, a state of presence of heart and special attention to authentic values is revived in the hearts of the men of God—they rise and prostrate before His Greatness, seeking forgiveness for their sins and becoming absorbed in the lights of His Divine Majesty. Every atom of their being hums the melody of Monotheism (Tawhid). Just as the darkness of night is rolled up with the rising of the morning and the general grace of the Lord settles upon the face of the world, they too, following the whispered morning prayers, find the veils of the darkness of heedlessness and sin removed from their hearts as the lights of Divine mercy, forgiveness, and gnosis (Ma'rifah) descend upon them.

Key Points

1- In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of the last verse, we read that he said: "Whoever says seventy times in the Witr prayer (the last unit of the Night Prayer): 'I seek forgiveness from Allah, my Lord, and I turn to Him in repentance' (Astaghfirullaha Rabbi wa atubu ilayh) and continues this practice for one year, Allah places him among those who seek forgiveness at dawn (al-mustaghfirina bil-ashar) and includes him in His pardon and mercy." [^2]

2- The word "Sahar" (with the vowel pattern of 'bashar') originally means "to be covered and hidden." Since a special concealment reigns over everything in the final hours of the night, it is named Sahar. The word "Sihr" (magic) is also from this root because a magician performs acts whose secrets are covered and hidden from others. The Arabs sometimes call the lungs "Sahar" as well, because of their being hidden inside the body.

Why is only the time of "Sahar" mentioned among all the times of the day and night, while seeking forgiveness and returning to God is desirable at all times? This is because Sahar, due to its peace, silence, the cessation of material activities, and the freshness that comes to a person after rest and sleep, provides a greater readiness for focusing on Allah. This meaning can easily be understood through experience; even many scientists used that time to solve scientific problems, because the lamp of human thought and spirit is brighter and more radiant at that time than at any other. Since the spirit of worship and seeking forgiveness is attention and presence of heart, worship and seeking forgiveness at such an hour will be more precious than at all other hours.

[^1]: "Qanitin" is derived from the root "Qunut," which has been interpreted as both humility before the Lord and constancy in obedience and servitude. [^2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 273.

Ayah

شَهِدَ ٱللَّهُ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَأُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمِ قَآئِمًۢا بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۚ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (١٨)
Allah, maintainer of justice, the Almighty and the All-wise, besides whom there is no god, bears witness that there is no god except Him, and [so do] the angels and those who possess knowledge. (18)

Commentary

All Bear Witness to His Oneness

Following the discussion regarding the true believers in the preceding verses, this verse points to a portion of the proofs for Monotheism (Tawhid) and the knowledge of God, illustrating the clarity of this path. It states: "Allah (through the creation of the magnificent system of the universe) bears witness that there is no deity but Him" (شَهِدَ اللَّهُ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ).

"And also the angels and those possessed of knowledge—the scholars—(each in their own way and by citing a proof or a sign) bear witness to this matter" (وَ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَ أُولُوا الْعِلْمِ‌).

"This is while Allah has established justice in the universe," and this justice is also a clear sign of His existence (قائِماً بِالْقِسْطِ).

Indeed: with these described attributes, "there is no deity but Him, the Almighty, the All-Wise" (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ‌).

Therefore, you too should join your voice with Allah, the angels, and the scholars, and chant the melody of Monotheism.

Key Points

1- How is Allah’s testimony to His own Oneness?

What is meant by Allah’s testimony is a practical and existential testimony, not a verbal one. That is, by bringing into existence the world of creation—where a single system governs, its laws are uniform everywhere, and its program is one, being in fact "a single interconnected unit" and "a unified system"—Allah has practically shown that the Creator and the Deity in the world is no more than One, and all originate from a single source. Therefore, the creation of this unified system is Allah's testimony and witness to the Oneness of His Essence.

However, the testimony of the angels and the scholars is verbal, as each of them, with speech appropriate to their station, confesses to this truth. Such distinctions are frequent in the verses of the Quran; for example, in the verse: "Indeed, Allah and His angels send blessings upon the Prophet" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ وَ مَلائِكَتَهُ يُصَلُّونَ عَلَى النَّبِيِ‌) [1]. The blessing from Allah is one thing, and from the angels, it is another; from Allah, it is the sending of mercy, and from the angels, it is the request for mercy.

Of course, the testimony of the angels and the scholars also has a practical aspect, for they worship only Him and do not bow their heads in submission before any other deity.

2- What is "Establishing Justice"?

The phrase "قائِماً بِالْقِسْطِ" (Establishing Justice) is, in grammatical terms, a hal (circumstantial adverb) for the subject of "witnessed," which is "Allah." This means Allah bears witness to His own Oneness while He is establishing justice in the universe. This phrase is actually a proof for His testimony, because the reality of justice is choosing the middle and straight path and avoiding any form of excess, deficiency, or deviation.

[1]: Surah Al-Ahzab, Verse 56.

And we know that the middle and straight path is always one and no more than one, as we read in Verse 153 of Surah Al-An'am: "And [moreover] this is My path, which is straight, so follow it; and do not follow [other] ways, for you will be separated from His way" (وَ أَنَّ هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ وَ لا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ‌).

In this verse, the "Way of Allah" is introduced as one, while the deviant paths alienated from Allah are numerous and scattered, because "Straight Path" is mentioned in the singular form and "deviant ways" in the plural.

The conclusion is that "justice" is always accompanied by a "unified system," and a unified system is a sign of a "single Origin." Therefore, justice in its true sense in the world of creation will be a proof of the Oneness of the Creator. (Reflect deeply).

3- The Status of Scholars:

In this verse, true scholars are placed in the same rank as the angels, and this itself announces the distinction of scholars over others. It is also understood from the verse that the distinction of scholars lies in the fact that, in the light of their knowledge, they have become aware of the truths and confess to the Oneness of Allah, which is the greatest truth.

It is clear that the verse includes all scholars, and if in some narrations mentioned regarding this verse, "those possessed of knowledge" (Ulul-Ilm) is interpreted as the Pure Imams علیهم السلام, it is because they are the most manifest examples of "those possessed of knowledge."

The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, while interpreting the verse, narrates from Jabir ibn Abdullah al-Ansari from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله who said:

"An hour of a scholar leaning on his bed, reflecting upon his knowledge, is better than seventy years of worship by a worshiper." (ساعة من عالم يتكى على فراشه ينظر فى علمه خير من عبادة العابد سبعين عاما)

At the end of the verse, the phrase "لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ" (There is no deity but Him) is repeated. This repetition seems to indicate that just as the testimony of Allah, the angels, and the scholars was mentioned at the beginning of the verse, whoever hears this testimony must also join their voices with theirs and bear witness to the Oneness of the Deity.

And since the phrase "لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ" is for fulfilling the right of magnifying Allah and declaring Monotheism, it concludes with two attributes: "Almighty" and "All-Wise" (عزيز and حكيم). This is because establishing justice requires power and wisdom; only the God who is capable of everything and aware of everything can establish justice in the universe.

This verse is among those that were always of interest to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, and He would frequently recite it on various occasions. Zubayr ibn al-Awwam says: "On the night of 'Arafah, I was in the service of the Messenger of Allah, and I heard Him repeatedly reciting this verse." [1]

[1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Volume 2, Page 421.

Ayah

إِنَّ ٱلدِّينَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْإِسْلَـٰمُ ۗ وَمَا ٱخْتَلَفَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ ۗ وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (١٩)
Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam, and those who were given the Book did not differ except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves. And whoever denies Allah’s signs [should know that] Allah is swift at reckoning. (19)

Commentary

The Essence of Religion is Submission to the Truth!

After establishing the oneness of the Object of Worship, the verse proceeds to the oneness of religion, stating: "Indeed, the religion in the sight of Allah is Islam" (إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ الْإِسْلامُ‌).

The word "Din" (religion) linguistically originally means recompense and reward, and it has also been used to mean obedience and following a command. In religious terminology, it refers to the collection of rules, laws, and etiquettes under whose shadow a human being can draw near to God, attain happiness in both worlds, and tread the correct path from a moral and educational perspective.

The word "Islam" means submission. Therefore, the meaning of the sentence "Indeed, the religion in the sight of Allah is Islam" is that the true creed in the presence of God is submission to His command. In reality, the spirit of religion in every age and time has been and will be nothing but submission to the Truth. However, since the creed of the Prophet of Islam is the final and most superior of creeds, the name "Islam" was chosen for it; otherwise, from one perspective, all divine religions are Islam.

As previously mentioned, the principles of heavenly religions are one, although as human society evolved, Allah sent more complete religions for them until they reached the final stage of evolution, which is the religion of the Seal of the Prophets, the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family).

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), in a saying narrated from him in the short maxims of Nahj al-Balagha, clarified this reality through a profound explanation:

"I shall define Islam with a definition that no one before me has given: Islam is submission, and submission is certainty, and certainty is affirmation, and affirmation is acknowledgment, and acknowledgment is performance, and performance is action."

In this statement, Imam (peace be upon him) first says: "I wish to interpret Islam in such a way that no one has done before," then he describes six stages for Islam.

First, he says: Islam is submission to the Truth. Then he adds that "submission" is impossible without certainty (because submission without certainty is blind submission, not scholarly). Next, he says: Certainty is affirmation (meaning that mere knowledge and wisdom are not enough; rather, they must be followed by belief and heart-felt affirmation). Then he says: Affirmation is acknowledgment (meaning it is not enough for faith to remain only in the region of the human heart and soul; rather, it must be declared with courage and strength). Then he adds: Acknowledgment is the performance of duty (meaning acknowledgment is not just verbal talk, but a commitment and acceptance of responsibility). Finally, he says: Performing responsibility is action (acting upon God's command and implementing divine programs), because commitment and responsibility can be nothing other than action. Those who spend their energy in talks, plans, meetings, and associations and only speak have neither accepted a commitment nor a responsibility, nor are they aware of the spirit of Islam. [^1] This is the clearest interpretation that can be given for Islam in all its aspects.

Then, the verse proceeds to explain the source of religious differences that arose despite the true unity of divine religion, stating: "And those who were given the Scripture did not differ except after knowledge had come to them—out of envy among themselves" (وَ مَا اخْتَلَفَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ‌).

Therefore, the emergence of differences was, firstly, after knowledge and awareness, and secondly, it had no motive other than rebellion, injustice, and envy.

The Jews fell into dispute and conflict regarding the successor of Moses bin Imran and shed much blood. The Christians pursued the path of disagreement regarding the matter of monotheism, tainting it with polytheism and the Trinity. Both sowed the seeds of discord regarding the proofs of the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) which were mentioned in their books; one group accepted, and another group denied.

In short: heavenly religions have always been accompanied by clear evidence and the miracles of the prophets, leaving no ambiguity for truth-seekers. For example, the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), in addition to clear miracles including the Holy Quran and the lucid proofs within the text of this creed, had his descriptions and characteristics mentioned in the previous heavenly books, parts of which were in the hands of the Jews and Christians. For this reason, their scholars, before his appearance, used to give glad tidings of his coming with great longing and emphasis. However, as soon as he was commissioned, because they saw their interests at risk, they ignored everything out of rebellion, injustice, and envy.

For this reason, at the end of the verse, it describes their fate and those like them, saying:

"And whoever disbelieves in the verses of Allah, then indeed, Allah is swift in account" (وَ مَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِآياتِ اللَّهِ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ‌).

Yes, those who make the divine verses a plaything for their desires will see the result of their work in this world and the hereafter. Allah swiftly settles the account of their deeds and gives each the appropriate recompense and punishment (we have provided a sufficient discussion on the interpretation of the phrase "Swift in Account" under verse 202 of Surah Al-Baqarah).

The meaning of "Verses of Allah" (آيات اللَّه) here includes all divine signs, His proofs, and the heavenly books, and it likely even includes the creative signs of Allah in the universe. There is no evidence for the interpretation of some commentators who restricted it specifically to the Torah or the Gospel or the like.

Key Point: The Source of Religious Differences

An interesting subject derived from the verse is that the source of religious differences and conflicts usually does not stem from ignorance and lack of awareness, but rather from rebellion, injustice, deviation from the truth, and the imposition of personal views. If people, especially the scholarly class, were to set aside prejudice, malice, narrow-mindedness, personal interests, and the transgression of their limits and rights, and were to examine God's laws with realism and a spirit of seeking justice, the path of truth would be very clear and differences would be resolved quickly.

This verse is, in fact, a crushing response to those who say: "Religion has created division among mankind and brought about much bloodshed throughout history." These critics have confused "religion" with "religious prejudices" and deviant thoughts. When we examine the instructions of religions, we see they all pursue one goal and all came for human happiness, even if they evolved over time. In reality, heavenly religions are like raindrops falling from the sky. Raindrops are all life-giving, but when they fall on contaminated lands—one salty and another bitter—they take on different colors and tastes. These differences do not belong to the rain but to these contaminated lands. However, based on the principle of evolution, the last of them is the most complete of them.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Maxims, Maxim 125; and Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 45, with slight variations.

Ayah

فَإِنْ حَآجُّوكَ فَقُلْ أَسْلَمْتُ وَجْهِىَ لِلَّهِ وَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَنِ ۗ وَقُل لِّلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْأُمِّيِّـۧنَ ءَأَسْلَمْتُمْ ۚ فَإِنْ أَسْلَمُوا۟ فَقَدِ ٱهْتَدَوا۟ ۖ وَّإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّمَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ (٢٠)
If they argue with you, say, ‘I have submitted my will to Allah, and [so has] he who follows me.’ And say to those who were given the Book and the uninstructed ones, ‘Do you submit?’ If they submit, they will certainly be guided; but if they turn away, then your duty is only to communicate, and Allah watches His servants. (20)

Commentary

Avoid Dispute and Contention

Following the explanation of the roots of religious differences, this verse points to a specific instance of these disputes—namely, the arguments and contentions of the Jews and Christians with the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. It states: "So if they argue with you, say: 'I have submitted my whole self to Allah, and so have those who follow me'" (فَإِنْ حَاجُّوكَ فَقُلْ أَسْلَمْتُ وَجْهِيَ لِلَّهِ وَ مَنِ اتَّبَعَنِ‌).

"حاجوك" (Hājjūka) is derived from the root "محاجه" (Muhājjah), which literally means discussion, debate, reasoning, and defending a belief or an issue.

It is natural for the followers of any faith to stand up in defense of their beliefs and present themselves as being on the side of truth. Therefore, the Quran tells the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله:

It is possible that the People of the Book (Jews and Christians) will debate with you and claim that they are submitted to the truth, and they may even persist in this claim, just as the Christians of Najran did in the presence of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله.

In this verse, Allah commands His Prophet to avoid further debate and contention with them, and instead, for the sake of guidance and to end the hostility, "say to those who were given the Scripture (Jews and Christians) and to the unlearned (the polytheists): 'Have you [also] submitted yourselves?'" (just as I have submitted to the command of truth) (وَ قُلْ لِلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ وَ الْأُمِّيِّينَ أَ أَسْلَمْتُمْ‌).

"If they truly submit, they are rightly guided; but if they turn away and refuse, then your duty is only to convey [the message]" and you are not responsible for their actions (فَإِنْ أَسْلَمُوا فَقَدِ اهْتَدَوْا وَ إِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَإِنَّما عَلَيْكَ الْبَلاغُ‌).

It is evident that the intention is not a mere verbal or claimed submission, but rather a true and practical submission to the truth. If they bow their heads in submission to the words of truth—considering that your call is manifest and coupled with logic and clear evidence—they will certainly believe. If they do not believe, they are not submitted to the truth and only claim to be in Islam and submission to the command of the Truth.

In short: your duty is to convey the message, accompanied by proof and reasoning. If the spirit of seeking the truth exists within them, they will accept it; and if they do not, you have fulfilled your duty.

At the end of the verse, He says: "And Allah is All-Seeing of [His] servants" (وَ اللَّهُ بَصِيرٌ بِالْعِبادِ).

He distinguishes the false claimants of submission from the truthful ones and knows the intentions of the disputants—for what purpose they debate and discuss. He sees the deeds of all, whether good or bad, and will grant everyone the appropriate recompense.

Key Points

1- It is implicitly understood from this verse that one must avoid continuing debates and contentions with stubborn people who do not submit to sound logic.

2- The term "Ummiyyīn" (the unlearned), referring to those who do not know how to read or write, refers in this verse to the polytheists. The reason the polytheists are described by this name in contrast to the People of the Book (Jews and Christians) is that the polytheists did not possess a Divine Book that would necessitate learning, reading, and writing.

3- It is clearly evident from this verse that the method of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was never to impose thoughts or beliefs. Rather, he strove and exerted effort so that the truths would become clear to the people, and then he would leave them to themselves to make the necessary decision in following the truth.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَقْتُلُونَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ وَيَقْتُلُونَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ فَبَشِّرْهُم بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٢١)
Those who deny Allah’s signs and kill the prophets unjustly and kill those who call for justice from among the people, inform them of a painful punishment. (21)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ (٢٢)
They are the ones whose works have failed in this world and the Hereafter, and they will have no helpers. (22)

Commentary

Signs of Their Rebellion

Following the previous verse, which implicitly showed that the Jews, Christians, and polytheists who engaged in debate and contention with the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) were not submissive to the Truth, this verse points to some of the signs of this issue, stating: "Indeed, those who disbelieve in the signs of Allah and kill the prophets without right and kill those who enjoin justice among the people—give them tidings of a painful punishment" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِآياتِ اللَّهِ وَ يَقْتُلُونَ النَّبِيِّينَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ وَ يَقْتُلُونَ الَّذِينَ يَأْمُرُونَ بِالْقِسْطِ مِنَ النَّاسِ فَبَشِّرْهُمْ بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ).

In this verse, three of their major sins are first mentioned: (disbelieving in the signs of the Lord, killing the prophets without right, and killing those among the people who defended the programs of the prophets and invited people to justice). Each of these sins alone was sufficient to prove that they are not submissive to the command of Truth; rather, they stifle the voices of those who speak the Truth.

The use of the terms "yakfurun" (they disbelieve) and "yaqtulun" (they kill) in the "present/future tense" (mudaari') indicates that disbelieving and killing the prophets and those who enjoin justice had become, as it were, a part of their life's program, which they performed and continue to perform persistently (note that the present tense verb denotes continuity).

Of course, these actions were primarily the method of the Jews, which continues today in other forms, but this does not prevent the concept of the verse from being general.

Then, in the continuation of this verse and the following verse, it points to three punishments and their grim fate: First was the "painful punishment" mentioned above.

Second, He says: "They are those whose deeds have become worthless in this world and the Hereafter," and if they have performed any good deeds, those deeds have lost their effect under the influence of their major sins (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمالُهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيا وَ الْآخِرَةِ).

Third, "they will have no helpers (or intercessors) against the severe punishments of Allah" (وَ ما لَهُمْ مِنْ ناصِرِينَ).

As stated under verse 61 of Surah Al-Baqarah, the eventful history of the Jews shows that, in addition to denying divine signs, they were exceptionally audacious in killing prophets and heralds of Truth, putting to the sword the mujahideen who rose to support them. It is certain that the three aforementioned punishments described regarding them apply to all those who commit similar acts.

Key Points

  1. In the first verse, those who enjoin justice and invite to what is right and true are placed in the same rank as the prophets. Disbelieving in Allah, killing the prophets, and killing such individuals are placed on the same level, and this clarifies the utmost importance Islam places on the issue of spreading justice in society. From the second verse, the severity of the punishment for those who attempt to kill such righteous people becomes clearly evident, because as we have previously stated, "habt" (nullification of deeds) does not apply to all sins, but rather to severe sins that destroy good deeds as well [1]. Furthermore, the denial of intercession for these individuals is another sign of the gravity of their sin.

  2. The phrase "without right" (بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ) does not mean that prophets could ever be killed "with right"; rather, it means that the killing of prophets has always been without right and oppressive. In technical terms, "without right" is an explanatory qualifier (qayd tawdihi) used for emphasis.

  3. From the phrase "give them tidings of a painful punishment" (فَبَشِّرْهُمْ بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ), it is understood that the disbelievers contemporary with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) were also included in this verse, even though we know they were not the actual killers of any prophets. This is because anyone who is pleased with the program, school of thought, and actions of a group shares in their good and bad deeds. Since this group of disbelievers (especially the Jews) were strictly loyal to the programs of their predecessors and their deviant acts, they will share their fate.

  4. The word "bisharat" (glad tidings) originally means news that brings joy, the effect of which appears on the "bishrah" (skin/face) of a human. Using the word "bisharat" regarding punishment in this verse and some other verses of the Quran is, in fact, a type of threat and mockery of the thoughts of the sinners. This is similar to a common expression among us where, if someone does something bad, we say to them in a threatening and mocking tone, "We will give you your reward and prize!"

  5. In a hadith from Abu Ubaidah al-Jarrah, he says: I asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), "Which of the people will have the most severe punishment on the Day of Resurrection?" He replied: "One who kills a prophet or a man who enjoins what is right and forbids what is wrong." Then he recited this verse: "and kill the prophets without right and kill those who enjoin justice among the people." Then he added: "O Abu Ubaidah! The Children of Israel killed 43 prophets at the beginning of the day in a single hour. At that moment, 112 of the worshippers among the Children of Israel rose up and enjoined the killers to do right and forbade them from wrong; they too were killed on that same day. And this is what Allah says: 'So give them tidings of a painful punishment.'" [1]

[1]: For an explanation and analysis regarding the issue of "habt," refer to the commentary under verse 217 of Surah Al-Baqarah. [1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 423.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ نَصِيبًا مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَىٰ كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّىٰ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُمْ وَهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٢٣)
Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book, who are summoned to the Book of Allah in order that it may judge between them, whereat a part of them refuse to comply and they are disregardful? (23)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا۟ لَن تَمَسَّنَا ٱلنَّارُ إِلَّآ أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ ۖ وَغَرَّهُمْ فِى دِينِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٢٤)
That is because they say, ‘The Fire shall not touch us except for a number of days,’ and they have been misled in their religion by what they used to fabricate. (24)
فَكَيْفَ إِذَا جَمَعْنَـٰهُمْ لِيَوْمٍ لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهِ وَوُفِّيَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا كَسَبَتْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (٢٥)
But how will it be [with them] when We gather them on a day in which there is no doubt, and every soul shall be recompensed fully for what it has earned, and they will not be wronged? (25)

Occasion of Revelation

In the commentary Majma' al-Bayan, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that during the era of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), a Jewish man and woman from Khaybar committed adultery while married (zina al-muhsana). Although the Torah commands the punishment of stoning for such individuals, because they belonged to the aristocratic class, the Jews refused to carry out this command. It was suggested that they refer to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) to seek judgment, hoping that he might determine a lighter punishment for them. When they approached the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), he said: "This very Torah currently with you shall judge between me and you."

They accepted, and they invited "Ibn Suriya," who was one of their scholars, from Fadak to Medina. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) recognized him and asked: "Are you Ibn Suriya?" He replied: "Yes." The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) asked: "Are you the most learned of the Jewish scholars?" He said: "So they believe!" The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) ordered that the portion of the Torah containing the verse of Rajm (stoning) be placed before him. Having been informed of the situation beforehand, when Ibn Suriya reached this section, he placed his hand over it and read the subsequent sentences.

Abdullah ibn Salam, who had previously been a Jewish scholar and had later embraced Islam, was present. He immediately noticed Ibn Suriya's concealment, stood up, removed Ibn Suriya's hand from the sentence, and read it from the text of the Torah. He said: "The Torah says it is obligatory upon the Jews that whenever a married man and woman commit adultery and there is sufficient evidence of their crime, they must be stoned." Subsequently, the Prophet ordered the aforementioned punishment to be carried out on the two criminals according to their own creed. A group of Jews became enraged, and this verse was revealed regarding their situation. [^1]

[^1]: In the current Torah, in Leviticus, Chapter 20, Verse 10, we read: "And the man that committeth adultery with another man's wife, even he that committeth adultery with his neighbour's wife, the adulterer and the adulteress shall surely be put to death." Although the punishment of stoning is not explicitly mentioned in the above phrase, the principle of the death penalty is stated. It is possible that the copies of the Torah existing during the era of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) contained this specific phrasing as well.

Commentary

Why Do They Not Submit to the Truth?

Following the previous verses which spoke of the arguments and stubborn debates of a group of the People of the Book, these verses clarify that they were not submissive to logical proposals and recount the motives for this behavior and its consequences.

In the first verse, He says: "Have you not seen those who were given a portion of the Scripture? They are invited to the Book of Allah that it may arbitrate between them; then a party of them turns away, and they are averse" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا نَصِيباً مِنَ الْكِتابِ يُدْعَوْنَ إِلى‌ كِتابِ اللَّهِ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّى فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ وَ هُمْ مُعْرِضُونَ‌). This was while their own Heavenly Book had expressed the Divine decree and they were aware of it.

Indeed, they would not even submit to the existing decree in their own religious book and, through excuse-making and baseless claims, they disobeyed the execution of Divine limits.

Furthermore, from the phrase "given a portion of the Scripture" (أُوتُوا نَصِيباً مِنَ الْكِتابِ), it is understood that the Torah (and Gospel) in the hands of the Jews and Christians at that time was not the entirety of the original Torah and Gospel, but only a part of it. Likely, the greater part of these two Heavenly Books had been lost or distorted. Other verses of the Holy Quran also confirm this meaning, and historical evidence and signs support it, though this is not the place for its detailed explanation.

In the next verse, He explains the reason for their opposition and disobedience: that they believed, based on a false notion, that they were a superior race (just as they think today, and their writings and actions bear witness to their intense racism).

They believed they had a special connection with the Lord, to the extent that they called themselves the children of God and said: "We are the children of Allah and His beloved ones" (نَحْنُ أَبْناءُ اللَّهِ وَ أَحِبَّاؤُهُ) [^1]. For this reason, they claimed for themselves immunity against Divine punishment. Therefore, the Quran says in this verse that these actions and behaviors are because they said: "The Fire shall not touch us except for a few numbered days" (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قالُوا لَنْ تَمَسَّنَا النَّارُ إِلَّا أَيَّاماً مَعْدُوداتٍ‌). If we are to have any punishment, it will be very limited.

"Numbered" (ma'dud) means countable, and is usually applied to a small number of things, as large quantities are either uncountable or difficult to count.

The meaning of "a few numbered days" (أَيَّاماً مَعْدُوداتٍ) is either those forty days during which they worshipped the calf in the absence of Moses (عليه السلام)—which was a great sin that even they themselves could not deny—or it refers to the limited days of their lives in which they committed exceptionally explicit sins, to the point that they were unable to deny, justify, or conceal them.

Then He adds: They attributed these false privileges to Allah, "and that which they used to forge has deceived them in their religion" (وَ غَرَّهُمْ فِي دِينِهِمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ‌).

In the third and final verse under discussion, the Holy Quran strikes a line of cancellation through all these vain claims and false imaginations, saying: "How then will it be when We gather them together on a Day about which there is no doubt, and every soul shall be paid in full what it has earned, and they shall not be wronged?" (فَكَيْفَ إِذا جَمَعْناهُمْ لِيَوْمٍ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ وَ وُفِّيَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما كَسَبَتْ وَ هُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ‌).

Yes, on that Day, everyone will be present in the court of Divine Justice, and everyone will reap what they have sown. The deeds of every person will be handed over to them, and whatever their punishment may be, it will never be unjust because it is the product of their own actions. It is on that Day that they will realize they have no privilege over others and that Divine Justice encompasses everyone.

Furthermore, from the phrase "what it has earned" (ما كَسَبَتْ), it is clearly understood that the reward and punishment of the Resurrection, and salvation or misery in the other world, depend solely on the actions of the human being themselves, not on vain notions and false imaginations.

Key Points

  1. Is it possible for a person to tell a lie or forge a fabrication against Allah and then later be influenced by it and become deceived by it, as the Quran narrates regarding the Jews in the above verses? Is this believable?

The answer to this question is not very difficult, because the issue of "self-deception" is among the established matters of modern psychology. Sometimes the apparatus of thought and reflection seeks to neglect the conscience and alters the face of reality in the view of one's own conscience. We have often seen individuals tainted with great sins such as murder, theft, or various addictions who, despite clearly perceiving the ugliness of their actions, strive to make people appear deserving of such acts in order to gain a false peace of conscience. Or, to justify their harmful addictions, they use life's troubles and overwhelming social problems as an excuse to justify their refuge in narcotics. Additionally, these types of fabrications and false privileges were created by previous generations of the People of the Book and gradually became an established belief for future generations who had little knowledge of the reality (and did not trouble themselves to discover the truth).

  1. It might be said that the belief in "limited punishment" also exists among us Muslims, as we believe that Muslims will not remain in Divine punishment forever and that ultimately their faith will lead to their salvation.

However, one must pay attention to this point: we never believe that a sinful Muslim tainted with all kinds of crimes will only see Divine punishment for a few days. Rather, we believe they will be caught in punishment for long years, the duration of which no one knows but Allah. However, their punishment will not be eternal and everlasting because of their faith. If there are indeed individuals among Muslims who claim such a privilege for themselves and believe that under the protection of the name of Islam and faith in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Pure Imams (علیهم السلام) they are permitted to commit any sin and their punishment will be no more than a few days, they are gravely mistaken and are far removed from the spirit of Islam and its teachings.

Furthermore, we do not claim a privilege in this matter specifically for Muslims; rather, we believe that the followers of every nation in their own era who believed in the Prophet of their time and committed a sin are subject to this law, regardless of their race. In contrast, the Jews claimed this privilege specifically for the race of Israel and not for other peoples.

The Quran has answered this false privilege of theirs, and in Surah Al-Ma'idah, Verse 18, it says: "Nay, you are but human beings from among those He has created" (بَلْ أَنْتُمْ بَشَرٌ مِمَّنْ خَلَقَ‌).

Ayahs

قُلِ ٱللَّهُمَّ مَـٰلِكَ ٱلْمُلْكِ تُؤْتِى ٱلْمُلْكَ مَن تَشَآءُ وَتَنزِعُ ٱلْمُلْكَ مِمَّن تَشَآءُ وَتُعِزُّ مَن تَشَآءُ وَتُذِلُّ مَن تَشَآءُ ۖ بِيَدِكَ ٱلْخَيْرُ ۖ إِنَّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٢٦)
Say, ‘O Allah, Master of all sovereignty! You give sovereignty to whomever You wish, and strip of sovereignty whomever You wish; You make mighty whomever You wish, and You degrade whomever You wish; all choice is in Your hand. Indeed You have power over all things.’ (26)
تُولِجُ ٱلَّيْلَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَتُولِجُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ ۖ وَتُخْرِجُ ٱلْحَىَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَيِّتِ وَتُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَيِّتَ مِنَ ٱلْحَىِّ ۖ وَتَرْزُقُ مَن تَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ (٢٧)
‘You make the night pass into the day and You make the day pass into the night. You bring forth the living from the dead and You bring forth the dead from the living, and You provide for whomever You wish without any reckoning.’ (27)

Occasion of Revelation

The famous commentator "Tabarsi" in Majma' al-Bayan has mentioned two occasions of revelation for the first verse, both of which pursue the same reality:

  1. When the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) conquered Makkah, he gave glad tidings to the Muslims that soon the empires of Persia and Rome would also come under the banner of Islam. The hypocrites, whose hearts had not been illuminated by the light of faith and who had not understood the spirit of Islam, considered this statement exaggerated and said with astonishment: "Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) is not content with Madinah and Makkah and has greed for the conquest of Persia and Rome!" At this moment, the first verse was revealed.

  2. While the Noble Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) along with the Muslims was busy digging the trench around Madinah, and the Muslims were working in organized groups with speed and diligence to complete this defensive means before the enemy army arrived, they suddenly encountered a large, hard white rock in the trench that the Muslims were unable to break or move. Salman (عليه السلام) came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and reported the matter. The Prophet entered the trench, took the pickaxe from Salman, and struck the rock firmly. From the impact of the pickaxe on the rock, a spark flew out. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) shouted the Takbir of victory, and the Muslims joined him, their voices echoing everywhere. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) struck the rock a second time; again a spark flew, a part of the rock broke, and the sound of the Takbir of victory from the Prophet and the Muslims filled the surrounding air. For the third time, he raised the pickaxe and struck the remainder of the rock firmly; once more, a spark flew from the impact, illuminating the surroundings, and the rest of the rock was shattered, and for the third time, the sound of Takbir echoed in the trench.

Salman said: "Today I observed a strange state from you!" The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) replied: "In the spark that flew the first time, I saw the palaces of Al-Hirah and Al-Mada'in, and my brother Gabriel gave me the glad tidings that they would come under the banner of Islam! In the second spark, I saw the palaces of Rome, and he informed me that they would be at the disposal of my followers. In the third spark, I saw the palaces of Sana'a and the land of Yemen, and he gave me the glad tidings that the Muslims would prevail over them. In that state, I shouted the Takbir of victory. O Muslims, glad tidings to you!..."

The true Muslims were overjoyed and thanked Allah. However, the hypocrites frowned and said with distress and protest:

"What a vain hope and what an impossible promise?! While they have taken a defensive posture out of fear for their lives and are busy digging a trench, unable to fight even that limited enemy, they harbor dreams of conquering the great countries of the world!" At this time, the verses under discussion were revealed and answered them.

Commentary

Everything is in His Hand!

In the preceding verses, there was mention of the privileges that the People of the Book (Jews and Christians) claimed for themselves, imagining themselves to be the chosen ones of Allah (furthermore, they claimed that sovereignty and ownership belonged to them). In these two verses, Allah rejects their false claim with this beautiful statement, saying: "Say: O Allah! Owner of Sovereignty, You give sovereignty to whom You will and whom You deem worthy, and You take sovereignty away from whom You will" (قُلِ اللَّهُمَّ مالِكَ الْمُلْكِ تُؤْتِي الْمُلْكَ مَنْ تَشاءُ وَ تَنْزِعُ الْمُلْكَ مِمَّنْ تَشاءُ).

"You exalt whom You will upon the throne of honor, and You abase whom You intend upon the dust of humiliation" (وَ تُعِزُّ مَنْ تَشاءُ وَ تُذِلُّ مَنْ تَشاءُ).

And in one sentence: "In Your powerful hand is all good, for You are over all things Competent" (بِيَدِكَ الْخَيْرُ إِنَّكَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

It goes without saying that the meaning of Divine Will and Providence in this verse is not that He grants something to someone or takes it away without account or reason; rather, His Will stems from Wisdom and the observance of the order, interest, and wisdom of the world of creation and humanity. Sometimes these governments are due to merit, and sometimes the rule of tyrants is in harmony with the unworthiness of nations.

It is also noteworthy that the word "Khayr" (good) is a superlative (better), but in many cases, it is also used to mean "good" (without the comparative sense), and it appears that in the verse under discussion, it carries the second meaning. That is, the source of all good is from Him and in His hand.

The phrase "بِيَدِكَ الْخَيْرُ" (In Your hand is all good), considering the definite article "Al" (denoting totality) in "Al-Khayr" and the advancement of the predicate over the subject (as it says Biyadikal-Khayr and not Al-Khayru Biyadik), indicates that all goodness, blessings, and virtues are in the hand of Allah. Additionally, it is inferred from this phrase that both honor is good and humiliation is good, and both granting sovereignty and taking it away are each good in their proper place and according to the law of justice; there is no evil. For criminals and evildoers, their "good" lies in being in prison, and for the faithful righteous, their "good" lies in freedom.

In other words, there is no evil in the universe of existence; it is we who transform goodness into evil. If He says "good is only in Your hand" and does not mention evil, it is because no evil emanates from His Pure Essence.

The phrase "إِنَّكَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ" (Indeed, You are over all things Competent) is actually a proof for everything mentioned in this verse, for when He is capable of all things, sovereignty, honor, and all goodness are in His hand.

Question

An important question arises here: some might conclude from the above verse that whoever attains power or falls from power, it was the will of Allah, and the result of this statement would be the endorsement of all the tyrannical and oppressive governments in history, such as the rule of Genghis Khan, Hitler, etc. Incidentally, we read in history that Yazid ibn Mu'awiyah also argued with this verse to justify his disgraceful and oppressive rule [1]. For this reason, explanations are found in the words of commentators to resolve this problem, such as: the verse is specific to Divine governments, or specific to the government of the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the ending of the rule of the tyrants of Quraysh.

However, the truth is that the verse has a general and universal concept according to which all governments, good and bad, are according to Allah's will, but with this explanation: Allah has created a series of factors and causes for progress and victory in this world, and the utilization of the effects of these causes is the very Will of Allah.

Therefore, Allah's Will means the effects created within those causes and factors. Now, if oppressive and unfit individuals (such as Genghis, Yazid, Pharaoh, and the like) utilized those means, and weak, abject, and cowardly nations submitted to them and tolerated their disgraceful rule, this is the result of their own actions, as it is said: "Every nation deserves the government it has!" But if nations were aware and took those factors and causes out of the hands of tyrants and gave them to the righteous, creating just governments, that too is the result of their actions, which depends on how they utilized Divine factors and causes.

In reality, the verse is a wake-up call for all individuals and human societies to be alert and utilize the means of victory, and to strive to occupy the strongholds before unfit individuals overcome them and seize the sensitive positions of society from them.

[1]: Irshad of Al-Mufid, as cited in Al-Mizan, under the verse in discussion.

In short: Allah's Will is that He created causes in the world so that we may see how we utilize the world of causes.

In the next verse, to complete this meaning and show Allah's sovereignty over the entire universe, it adds: "You cause the night to enter the day, and You cause the day to enter the night; and You bring the living out of the dead, and You bring the dead out of the living; and You provide for whom You will without account" (تُولِجُ اللَّيْلَ فِي النَّهارِ وَ تُولِجُ النَّهارِ فِي اللَّيْلِ وَ تُخْرِجُ الْحَيَّ مِنَ الْمَيِّتِ وَ تُخْرِجُ الْمَيِّتَ مِنَ الْحَيِّ وَ تَرْزُقُ مَنْ تَشاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ‌).

Thus, the issue of the gradual change of night and day—the shortening of the night in half of the year, which is expressed as the night entering the day, and the lengthening of the nights in the other half (the day entering the night)—as well as the emergence of living beings from the dead and dead matter from the living, and the abundant provisions bestowed upon some individuals, each is a clear sign of His absolute power.

Key Points

  1. "Wuluj" linguistically means "entry." This verse says: Allah causes the night to enter the day and the day to enter the night (this meaning is also mentioned in eight other places in the Quran).

The meaning of this phrase is that same perceptible gradual change in night and day throughout the year. This change is due to the tilt of the Earth's axis relative to its orbit, which is slightly more than 23 degrees, and the difference in the angle of sunlight. Thus, we see that in northern lands (points above the equator), at the beginning of winter, the days gradually lengthen and the nights shorten until the beginning of summer; then, conversely, the nights lengthen and the days shorten until the beginning of winter. In southern lands (points below the equator), it is exactly the opposite.

Therefore, Allah constantly causes the night and day to enter one another, meaning He subtracts from one to add to the other.

It might be said that at the true equator and also at the actual North and South Poles, night and day are equal throughout the year and no change occurs. At the equator, night and day are each twelve hours all year round, and at the poles, there is one six-month night and one day of the same length; therefore, the verse is not universal.

In response to this question, it must be said: the true equator is nothing more than an imaginary line, and the actual life of people is always either on this side of the equator or that side. Similarly, the pole is nothing more than a very small point, and the life of the inhabitants of polar regions (if there are any) is certainly in a place wider than the actual point of the pole; therefore, both groups experience a difference in night and day.

The verse may, in addition to the above meaning, encompass another meaning: that night and day on Earth do not occur suddenly because of the atmospheric layers around this globe; rather, the day begins gradually from dawn (Fajr) and spreads, and the night begins from the twilight (Shafaq) and the redness of the eastern side at sunset, and gradually darkness covers everywhere.

The gradual nature of the change of night and day, in whatever sense, has beneficial effects on the lives of humans and the creatures of the Earth, because the growth of plants and many animals takes place under the light and gradual heat of the sun. This means that from the beginning of spring, as light and heat increase day by day, plants and many animals undergo a new stage of their evolution every day. Since they require more light and heat as time passes, and this is provided through the gradual changes of night and day, they can reach the final point of their perfection.

If night and day were always the same, the growth and development of many plants and animals would be disrupted, and the four seasons—which are a consequence of the "difference in night and day" and the "angle of sunlight"—would disappear, and naturally, humans would be deprived of the benefits of the changing seasons.

Similarly, if we consider the second meaning in the interpretation of the verse—that the beginning of night and day is gradual and not sudden, and certainly twilight and the period between dawn and sunrise are between the two—it becomes clear that this gradual nature of night and day is a great blessing for the inhabitants of the Earth, as they gradually become accustomed to darkness or light, and their physical strength and social life adapt to it. Otherwise, discomforts would certainly arise.

  1. The phrase "تُخْرِجُ الْحَيَّ مِنَ الْمَيِّتِ وَ تُخْرِجُ الْمَيِّتَ مِنَ الْحَيِ‌" and its like appear in several verses of the Holy Quran, stating: "Allah brings the living out of the dead and the dead out of the living."

The meaning of bringing the "living" out of the "dead" is the emergence of life from inanimate matter. We know that on the day the Earth became ready to accept life, living beings emerged from inanimate materials. Furthermore, in our bodies and all living beings of the world, inanimate materials constantly become part of cells and are transformed into living matter.

The emergence of the dead from living beings is also constantly manifested before our eyes.

In reality, the verse refers to the law of the constant exchange of death and life, which is the most universal, complex, and yet most interesting law governing us.

There is another interpretation for this verse which does not contradict the previous one, and that is the issue of spiritual life and death. We see that sometimes faithful individuals, who are the "truly living," are born from faithless individuals, who are considered the "truly dead," and sometimes conversely, faithless individuals are born from faithful ones.

The Quran has referred to spiritual life and death as "faith" and "disbelief" in numerous verses.

According to this interpretation, the Quran announces the disruption of the law of "heredity," which some scientists consider one of the definitive laws of nature. This is because a human being, due to having free will, is not like the inanimate objects of nature that are under the compulsory influence of various factors. This itself is one of the manifestations of Allah's power: He washes away the effects of disbelief from the existence of the children of disbelievers (those who truly want to be believers) and removes the effects of faith from the existence of the children of believers (those who truly want to be disbelievers). This independence of will, which can even prevail over favorable or unfavorable hereditary backgrounds, is from Him.

This same meaning has reached us in a narration from the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), as cited in the commentary Al-Durr al-Manthur from Salman al-Farsi, that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) said in interpreting the verse "تُخْرِجُ الْحَيَّ مِنَ الْمَيِّتِ...": "It means He brings the believer out of the loins of the disbeliever and the disbeliever out of the loins of the believer."

  1. The phrase "وَ تَرْزُقُ مَنْ تَشاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ‌" is, so to speak, of the type of mentioning the "general" after the "specific," because in the preceding sentences, examples of Allah's provisions to His servants were stated, and in this sentence, the matter is mentioned in a general and more universal form regarding all blessings and provisions. That is, not only are honor, sovereignty, life, and death in the hand of Allah, but every type of provision and blessing is from Him.

The phrase "بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ‌" (without account) indicates that the sea of Divine blessings is so vast and expansive that no matter how much He grants to anyone, it does not have the slightest effect on Him and does not require "keeping an account." For accounts are kept by those who have limited capital and fear the exhaustion or shortage of their capital; such people are constantly calculating their gifts lest their capital be lost. But Allah, who is the infinite sea of existence and perfections, neither fears shortage, nor does anyone take an account from Him, nor does He need an account.

From what has been said, it becomes clear that this phrase does not contradict the verses that state Divine decree, measurement, the merit and worthiness of individuals, and the wisdom and management of creation.

  1. Another question arises here: from the perspective of the law of creation, the judgment of reason, and the call of the Prophets, every person is autonomous and free in attaining prosperity, happiness, honor, and humiliation, and in striving to earn their own provision. So how is it that in the above verse, all of these are attributed to Allah?!

The answer to this question is that the primary source of the world of creation and all the blessings and powers that individuals possess is Allah. It is He who has placed all the possibilities for attaining honor and happiness at the disposal of His servants, and it is He who has established laws in this world such that if they are ignored, the result is humiliation. For this reason, all of these can be attributed to Him, but this attribution never contradicts human free will, for it is the human who makes good use or ill use of these laws, blessings, powers, and forces.

In short: Allah's Will is that He created causes in the world so that we may see how we utilize the world of causes.

Ayah

لَّا يَتَّخِذِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۖ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ فَلَيْسَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ فِى شَىْءٍ إِلَّآ أَن تَتَّقُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ تُقَىٰةً ۗ وَيُحَذِّرُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسَهُۥ ۗ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٢٨)
The faithful should not take the faithless for allies instead of the faithful, and Allah will have nothing to do with those who do that, except when you are wary of them, out of caution. Allah warns you to beware of [disobeying] Him, and toward Allah is the return. (28)

Commentary

Do Not Befriend the Enemies

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on the fact that honor, humiliation, and all goodness are in the hands of Allah. In this verse, following that theme, the believers are strictly forbidden from befriending disbelievers; for if these friendships are sought for the sake of gaining power, wealth, or honor, all of these belong to Allah.

It states: "The believers must not take the disbelievers as their allies, friends, or supporters instead of the believers" (لا يَتَّخِذِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الْكافِرِينَ أَوْلِياءَ مِنْ دُونِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌). [^1] "And whoever does that has no connection with Allah whatsoever," and has completely severed his bond with his Lord (وَ مَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ فَلَيْسَ مِنَ اللَّهِ فِي شَيْ‌ءٍ).

This verse was revealed at a time when certain relationships existed between Muslims and the polytheists, as well as with the Jews and Christians. Since the continuation of these ties was detrimental to the Muslims, they were forbidden from such actions. In reality, this verse provides an important socio-political lesson to Muslims: never accept outsiders as intimate friends, protectors, or supporters, and do not be deceived by their attractive speech and seemingly sincere displays of affection. Indeed, many of the heavy blows dealt throughout history to people of faith and purpose have occurred through this very channel.

The history of colonialism tells us that exploitative oppressors have always appeared in the guise of friendship, sympathy, and "development" (civilization-building). It should not be forgotten that the term "colonialism" (استعمار - isti‘mār, which literally means "seeking to develop or cultivate") is derived from this very concept. They would enter under the pretext of development and cultivation, but once they secured their foothold, they would ruthlessly assault that society and plunder everything they possessed.

The phrase "مِنْ دُونِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌" (instead of the believers) indicates that in social life, everyone needs friends and helpers; however, people of faith must choose their allies from among the believers. With them available, what need is there for the ruthless and oppressive disbelievers? Furthermore, the emphasis on the descriptions of "faith" and "disbelief" suggests that these two are alien to one another and irreconcilable.

The phrase "فَلَيْسَ مِنَ اللَّهِ فِي شَيْ‌ءٍ" (he is not from Allah in anything) indicates that those who establish bonds of friendship and cooperation with the enemies of God will find their connection with Allah and the God-fearing severed.

Then, as an exception to this general rule, He says: "Unless you [do so] to protect yourselves against them" and practice Taqiyyah (إِلَّا أَنْ تَتَّقُوا مِنْهُمْ تُقاةً).

This is the same Taqiyyah (precautionary dissimulation) intended for preserving strength, preventing the waste of forces and resources, and ultimately achieving victory over the enemy. In such cases, it is permissible for Muslims to express friendship toward faithless individuals for the sake of protecting their lives and the like.

At the end of the verse, a warning is given to all Muslims, stating: "And Allah warns you of Himself (His disobedience), and to Allah is the final return" (وَ يُحَذِّرُكُمُ اللَّهُ نَفْسَهُ وَ إِلَى اللَّهِ الْمَصِيرُ).

These two phrases emphasize the prohibition of befriending the enemies of God. On one hand, it says to beware of Allah's punishment and wrath, and on the other, it states: "If you oppose [this command], the return of all of you is toward Him, and you shall receive the consequences of your actions."

[^1]: Awliyā’ is the plural of Walī, and here it means friend, protector, ally, and helper.

Key Points

1. Taqiyyah is a Defensive Shield

It is true that sometimes a person is willing to sacrifice his precious life for higher goals, for the preservation of honor, and for the strengthening of truth and the crushing of falsehood. However, can any rational person say it is permissible for a human to endanger his life without a significant objective? Islam explicitly permits a person to temporarily refrain from declaring the truth and to fulfill his duty in secret in cases where his life, property, or honor is at risk and declaring the truth would yield no significant result or benefit. As the Quran points out in the above verse, and expresses in another way in Surah An-Nahl, verse 106: "إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ وَ قَلْبُهُ مُطْمَئِنٌّ بِالْإِيمانِ‌" (except one who is compelled while his heart is content with faith).

Islamic history and books of Hadith have not forgotten the story of ‘Ammar and his parents, who were captured by the idolaters and tortured to renounce Islam. ‘Ammar’s parents refused and were killed by the polytheists, but ‘Ammar verbally expressed what they wanted and then rushed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله weeping out of fear of the Great Allah. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to him: "If they return [to torture you], return to what you said," [^1] and thereby calmed his anxiety, terror, and weeping.

A point that must be carefully noted is that Taqiyyah does not have a single ruling in all circumstances; rather, it is sometimes obligatory, sometimes forbidden, and sometimes permissible.

Taqiyyah is obligatory when a person's life is endangered without any significant benefit. However, in cases where Taqiyyah leads to the promotion of falsehood, the misguidance of people, or the strengthening of tyranny and oppression, it is forbidden and prohibited.

Based on this, all objections raised in this regard are answered. In fact, if the critics had researched this matter, they would have realized that the Shia are not alone in this belief; rather, the issue of Taqiyyah is a definitive rational principle and in accordance with human nature. [^2] For all the wise and rational people of the world, when they find themselves at a crossroads where they must either refrain from expressing their inner belief or endanger their life, property, and reputation by expressing it, they investigate: if expressing the belief is in a case that is worth sacrificing life, property, and reputation, they consider such a sacrifice correct. But if they see no significant effect in it, they refrain from expressing that belief.

[^1]: Majma‘ al-Bayan, Vol. 5 & 6, p. 388. [^2]: Adapted from the book A’in-e Ma (Our Creed), p. 364.

2. Taqiyyah: A Change in the Form of Struggle

In the history of religious, social, and political struggles, times arise when the defenders of truth—if they were to engage in open combat—would see both themselves and their school of thought destroyed, or at least placed in grave danger. Such was the situation of the followers of Ali عليه السلام during the era of the usurping Umayyad government. In such instances, the correct and rational path is not to waste one's forces, but to engage in indirect or secret struggles to advance holy objectives. In reality, for such schools and their followers, Taqiyyah at such moments is considered a change in the form of struggle that can save them from annihilation and lead them to victory in the continuation of their endeavors. It is unclear what plan those who dismiss Taqiyyah entirely have for such cases. Is it better to be destroyed, or to continue the struggle in a correct and logical manner? The second path is Taqiyyah, and the first is something no one can prescribe.

From what we have said, it is clear that Taqiyyah is an established Quranic principle, but only in specific and calculated cases whose criteria were mentioned above. The fact that we see some uninformed individuals considering Taqiyyah an innovation of the followers of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام is evidence that they are not very familiar with the verses of the Quran.

Ayah

قُلْ إِن تُخْفُوا۟ مَا فِى صُدُورِكُمْ أَوْ تُبْدُوهُ يَعْلَمْهُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٢٩)
Say, ‘Whether you hide what is in your hearts or disclose it, Allah knows it, and He knows whatever there is in the heavens and whatever there is in the earth; and Allah has power over all things.’ (29)

Commentary

He is Aware of Your Inner Secrets

In the previous verse, friendship and cooperation with disbelievers and the enemies of God, as well as relying upon them, were strictly prohibited, except in cases of Taqiyyah (precautionary dissimulation). Since some might use this exception as an excuse to choose disbelievers as friends, submit to their guardianship and support, and establish relations with the enemies of Islam by exploiting the title of Taqiyyah, this verse warns them, saying: "Say: Whether you conceal what is in your hearts or reveal it, Allah knows it" (قُلْ إِنْ تُخْفُوا ما فِي صُدُورِكُمْ أَوْ تُبْدُوهُ يَعْلَمْهُ اللَّهُ‌).

Not only does He know your inner secrets, but "He knows whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth; (and in addition to this vast knowledge) Allah has power over all things" (وَ يَعْلَمُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

Therefore, with His infinite knowledge that encompasses the expanse of the earth and the heavens, He is aware of all your intentions, and He also possesses the power to punish the sinners.

Ayah

يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُّحْضَرًا وَمَا عَمِلَتْ مِن سُوٓءٍ تَوَدُّ لَوْ أَنَّ بَيْنَهَا وَبَيْنَهُۥٓ أَمَدًۢا بَعِيدًا ۗ وَيُحَذِّرُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسَهُۥ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ رَءُوفٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ (٣٠)
The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil, it has done, it will wish there were a far distance between it and itself. Allah warns you to beware of [disobeying] Him, and Allah is most kind to [His] servants. (30)

Commentary

The Presence of Human Deeds on the Day of Resurrection

This verse serves as a completion to what was mentioned in the previous verse, unveiling the presence of good and evil deeds on the Day of Resurrection.
