It states: "Remember the day when every soul will find what it has done of good present [before it] and what it has done of evil" (يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُحْضَراً وَ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ سُوءٍ). [^1] "While it wishes there were a great distance of time between itself and those [evil] deeds" (تَوَدُّ لَوْ أَنَّ بَيْنَها وَ بَيْنَهُ أَمَداً بَعِيداً).

It does not say that the soul wishes its evil deeds were annihilated, for it knows that nothing in the universe is annihilated; rather, it wishes to be distanced from them.

The word "Amad" (أمد) linguistically refers to a limited period of time. Its difference from "Abad" (أبد) is that "Abad" refers to unlimited time, whereas "Amad" usually focuses on the end of a time period or a deadline, although it is also used to mean a limited duration (as opposed to unlimited).

Indeed, both the sinners and the righteous will see their deeds present on that day, with the difference being that the righteous will be delighted and joyful at the sight of their deeds, while the wrongdoers will be so overcome with horror at the sight of theirs that they will wish to be distanced from them—not just a spatial distance, but a long temporal distance, which is more expressive of intense loathing. This is because in a spatial distance, there remains the possibility of being present near it, but in a temporal distance, it is by no means possible. For example, someone living in a remote region during a World War still felt some degree of anxiety and concern because the scope of the war might reach there. However, we who are now separated from that war by decades feel no anxiety whatsoever that those specific battles will encompass us (another world war might occur, but the spread of those past wars to the present is meaningless).

Some commentators have suggested that "Amad" here might mean spatial distance (as Majma' al-Bayan narrates from some), but this word apparently does not appear in the language except to mean time or a temporal deadline.

At the end of the verse, to emphasize further, it says: "And Allah warns you of Himself, and Allah is Kind to [His] servants" (وَ يُحَذِّرُكُمُ اللَّهُ نَفْسَهُ وَ اللَّهُ رَؤُفٌ بِالْعِبادِ).

In reality, this sentence is a blend of fear and hope; on one hand, it issues a danger signal and warns, and on the other, it makes the servants hopeful of His grace, so that a balance is established between fear and hope—which is a vital factor in human upbringing. It is also possible that these two phrases emphasize one another, similar to someone saying to another: "I warn you against this dangerous act, and I am kind to you for issuing this warning."

The Presence of Deeds and Their Embodiment in the Resurrection

The Holy Quran, in the verse under discussion and many other verses, unveils the reality that on the Day of Resurrection, the good and evil deeds of man will each be embodied in a specific form and appear in the arena of the Gathering.

The word "Tajid" (تجد) comes from the root "Wujdan" (finding), which is the opposite of loss and non-existence. The words "Khayr" (good) and "Su'" (evil) appear in the indefinite form here to denote generality; meaning every human on that day will find all their good and bad deeds—no matter how small—present before them.

Although a group of commentators insists on interpreting this verse and similar ones metaphorically, saying that the "presence of deeds" means the presence of their reward and punishment, or the presence of the Record of Deeds in which all human actions are recorded.

However, it is evident that these interpretations do not align with the literal meaning of the verse, for this verse clearly states that on the Day of Resurrection, man finds "the deed itself." In the latter part of the verse, we read that the sinner wishes for a separation between himself and the ugly deed he committed; here too, the deed itself is the subject of the verse, not the Record of Deeds, nor the punishment or reward.

Another proof is that in the verse under discussion, we read: the sinner wishes there were a great distance between him and his deed, and he never wishes for the annihilation of his deed. This indicates that the annihilation of deeds is impossible, and for this reason, he does not even wish for it.

Numerous other verses also confirm this matter, such as verse 49 of Surah Al-Kahf: "وَ وَجَدُوا ما عَمِلُوا حاضِراً وَ لا يَظْلِمُ رَبُّكَ أَحَداً" (And they will find what they did present [before them]. And your Lord does injustice to no one).

And verses 7 and 8 of Surah Az-Zalzalah: "فَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْراً يَرَهُ وَ مَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُ" (So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it).

As we mentioned, some commentators have occasionally assumed the word "Jaza'" (reward/punishment) is implied in these verses, but this contradicts the apparent meaning of the verses.

From some verses, it is understood that this world is the field for the next world, and human action is like a seed that a farmer sows in the heart of the earth; then that same seed grows and develops, and he harvests that same seed in much larger quantities. Human deeds also return to the person with more transformations and changes necessitated by the Abode of Resurrection, as Allah says in Surah Ash-Shura, verse 20:

"مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ حَرْثَ الْآخِرَةِ نَزِدْ لَهُ فِي حَرْثِهِ" (Whoever desires the harvest of the Hereafter - We increase for him in his harvest).

From other verses, it is understood that the good deeds of this world turn into light and radiance in the other world. The hypocrites will demand this light from the believers, saying: "انْظُرُونا نَقْتَبِسْ مِنْ نُورِكُمْ" (Wait for us that we may acquire some of your light).

And they will be told: "ارْجِعُوا وَراءَكُمْ فَالْتَمِسُوا نُوراً" (Go back behind you and seek light [from the world]). [^2] These verses and dozens of others indicate that on the Day of Resurrection, we find the very deed in a more complete form, and this is the "Embodiment of Deeds" (Tajassum al-A'mal) that Islamic scholars believe in.

[^1]: The word "Yawm" (Day) in the above sentence is the object of an omitted verb such as "Udhkuru" (Remember) or "Ihdharu" (Beware). Other possibilities have been suggested but seem unlikely. [^2]: Surah Al-Hadid, verse 13.

Many narrations from the great leaders of Islam also testify to this matter, of which we suffice with one example:

The Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله said to one who requested an exhortation:

"O Qays! You must have a companion who will be buried with you while he is alive, and you will be buried with him while you are dead. If he is noble, he will honor you, and if he is base, he will abandon you. Then he will not be gathered except with you, and you will not be gathered except with him. You will not be questioned except about him. So make him righteous, for if he is righteous, you will find comfort in him, and if he is corrupt, you will feel horror from none but him; and he is your 'deed'." [^1]

To clarify this discussion, it is necessary to first examine the nature of the reward and punishment of deeds.

Views of Scholars on the Nature of Divine Reward and Punishment

Scholars have various opinions regarding the reward and punishment of deeds:

  1. A group believes that the recompense of deeds, like the rewards and punishments of this world, is based on convention. That is, just as in this world a punishment is determined by legislators for every bad act, the Great God has determined a specific punishment or reward for every deed. This theory is the theory of "wages and penalties" by regulation.

  2. Another group believes that all punishments and rewards are created by the human soul and spirit, which the human spirit involuntarily creates in that world. The explanation is that good and evil deeds in this world create good and bad "dispositions" (malakat) in the human soul, and these dispositions become the essence of man and part of his nature. Each of these dispositions creates a form suitable to itself, consisting of blessing or torment. People of good inner nature in this world deal with a series of good thoughts and conceptions, while impure individuals are occupied in sleep and wakefulness with their false thoughts and bad conceptions.

These dispositions, on the Day of Resurrection, are the creators of blessings, tortures, pains, peace, and torment. In other words, what we read about the blessings of Paradise and the punishments of Hell are the creations of man's own good and bad qualities, and nothing else.

  1. Another group of great Islamic scholars has chosen a different path and brought many evidences from verses and narrations for it. Its summary is: every action of ours, whether good or bad, has a worldly form that we observe, and an otherworldly form that is currently hidden within that action. On the Day of Resurrection, after the transformations that occur within it, it loses its worldly form and manifests in its Resurrection form, causing peace and comfort to the doer or causing him pain.

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, (Company edition) Vol. 3, p. 257; New edition, Vol. 7, p. 228; and Ma'ani al-Akhbar, p. 233 with slight variation.

Among the above views, the latter view perfectly aligns with the literal meaning of many Quranic verses. Therefore, human deeds, which are various forms of energy, according to the law of conservation of "matter and energy," never perish and will always remain in this world, even though through superficial study we might imagine they have vanished.

The survival of these deeds and their eternity, on one hand, causes everyone to see all their deeds during the reckoning of the Resurrection, leaving no room for denial. On the other hand, it causes one to live among their own deeds in the Resurrection, suffering or finding peace. Although human instruments are not yet capable of uncovering past events except those related to a few moments ago, [^1] it is certain that if a more perfect device were created or if we had a more perfect "vision" and "perception," we could perceive and understand everything that happened in the past (of course, there is no obstacle to some rewards and punishments also having a conventional aspect).

Embodiment of Deeds from the Perspective of Modern Science

To prove the possibility of the embodiment of past deeds, one can use the established principles of modern "physics." From the perspective of physics, it is certain that matter converts into energy (body converts into force). The latest theory regarding matter and energy is that "matter" and "force" are two manifestations of one reality, and matter is concentrated and compressed energy that converts into force under certain conditions. Sometimes the energy hidden in one gram of matter is equivalent to the explosive power of over thirty thousand tons of dynamite.

The result is that matter and energy are manifestations of one reality. Given the non-annihilation of energy and matter, there will never be an obstacle for the dispersed forces and energies to once again become concentrated and take on a mass and physical state. The forces used in the path of reform and righteousness, or in the path of tyranny and oppression, could become compressed and be embodied in a specific physical form on the Day of Resurrection. If they are good deeds, they manifest as attractive and beautiful material blessings, and if they are bad and evil deeds, they manifest in the form of instruments of torture and torment.

[^1]: Recently, scientists have invented a camera called "Infrared" that can take pictures of the past (from a few moments ago). This new device works with a thermal system, meaning it absorbs waves emitted from objects and, through a special thermogram, divides them into cold and hot types and then turns them into light or dark images (quoted from Kayhan, No. 7888, year 1348 [1969]). By this means, one can discover the occurrence and nature of a crime, placing the image of the criminals' past actions before their eyes and exposing their lies.

Ayahs

قُلْ إِن كُنتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ ٱللَّهَ فَٱتَّبِعُونِى يُحْبِبْكُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٣١)
Say, ‘If you love Allah, then follow me; Allah will love you and forgive you your sins, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful.’ (31)
قُلْ أَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلرَّسُولَ ۖ فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٣٢)
Say, ‘Obey Allah and the Apostle.’ But if they turn away, indeed Allah does not like the faithless. (32)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the above verses, two occasions of revelation have been mentioned in the commentaries of Majma' al-Bayan and Al-Manar: First, a group of people in the presence of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله claimed to love the Lord, while "action" according to Divine programs was rarely seen in them; thus, the above verses were revealed and provided them with an answer.

Second, a group of Christians from "Najran" came to Medina to meet the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and, during their conversation, stated: "If we hold Christ عليه السلام in such extraordinary honor, it is because of the love we have for God." The above verses were revealed to respond to them.

Commentary

This is True Love!

As we read in the occasion of revelation, there were groups who claimed to love the Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله or other prophets. The above verses define the concept of true love and clarify the difference between it and false or hollow affection.

First, it says: "Say: If you love Allah, then follow me; Allah will love you and forgive you your sins, and Allah is Forgiving, Merciful" (قُلْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ اللَّهَ فَاتَّبِعُونِي يُحْبِبْكُمُ اللَّهُ وَ يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ وَ اللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌).

This means that love is not merely a weak internal feeling devoid of any effect; rather, its effects must be reflected in a person's actions. For one who claims to love and have affection for the Lord, the first sign is that he follows His Prophet and messenger.

In reality, this is a natural effect of love that draws a person toward the "Beloved" and His desires. Of course, weak forms of love may exist whose radiance does not extend beyond the heart, but such love is so insignificant that it can hardly be called love. A fundamental love certainly has practical effects; it inevitably connects the lover to the Beloved and compels him toward fruitful effort in the path of the Beloved's wishes.

The reason for this is clear, because a person's love and affection for something is certainly because he has found a perfection in it. A human never falls in love with a being that possesses no point of strength. Therefore, man's love for Allah is because He is the primary source and origin of every kind of perfection. Certainly, such a Being's programs and commands are also perfect. In this state, how is it possible for a person who is in love with perfection and progress to turn away from those programs? And if he does turn away, is it not a sign of the lack of reality in his love and affection?

This verse not only answers the Christians of Najran or those claiming love for the Lord in the era of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, but it is a general principle in the logic of Islam for all ages and centuries. Those who day and night speak of love for the Lord or love and affection for the leaders of Islam, the mujahideen in the way of God, and the righteous and good, yet in practice bear the slightest resemblance to them, are nothing more than false claimants.

Those who are polluted with sin from head to toe, yet consider their hearts full of love for Allah, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام, and the great leaders—or believe that faith, love, and affection belong only to the heart and have no connection to action—are completely alienated from the logic of Islam.

In Ma'ani al-Akhbar, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: Ma ahabba Allaha man 'asahu "He who sins does not love Allah."

Then he recited this famous poem: You disobey the God while you manifest His love, By my life, this is a strange thing among deeds! If your love were sincere, you would have obeyed Him, For surely the lover is submissive to the one he loves.

The Quran, in the phrase "Allah will love you and forgive you your sins, and Allah is Forgiving, Merciful" (يُحْبِبْكُمُ اللَّهُ وَ يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ وَ اللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌), says: If you have love for Allah and its effects become manifest in your actions and life, Allah will also love you, and following this love, its effects will appear in His relationship with you: He will forgive your sins and encompass you in His mercy.

The reason for Allah's reciprocal love is also clear, for He is a Being perfect and infinite in every respect, and He will have a bond of love with any being that steps onto the path of perfection due to their shared affinity.

From this verse, it also becomes clear that one-sided love cannot exist, because every love invites its possessor to practically step into the path of the Beloved's true desires, and in such a state, the "Beloved" will certainly develop an affection for him as well.

Question

A question may be raised here: if the "lover" always obeys the command of the Beloved, no sin would remain for him to be forgiven; thus, the phrase "and forgive you your sins" (يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ‌) would have no subject.

Answer

In response, it must be said: Firstly, this phrase may refer to the forgiveness of past sins. Secondly, the lover does not persist in disobeying the Beloved, but it is possible that due to an outburst or the overwhelming of desires, a slip may occasionally occur, which will be forgiven in the light of his continuous obedience.

Key Point: Religion and Love

In numerous narrations from the leaders of Islam, it is stated that religion is nothing but love. Among these, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام in the books Al-Khisal and Al-Kafi that he said: "Is religion anything but love? Then he recited this verse: 'If you love Allah, then follow me' (إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ اللَّهَ فَاتَّبِعُونِي‌)."

The meaning of these narrations is that the spirit and reality of religion is that very faith and love for Allah—a faith and love whose radiance illuminates the entire existence of man, and all members and systems of the body come under its influence, and its most prominent and clear effect is following the command of Allah.

In the next verse, the discussion from the previous verse is followed up, stating: "Say: Obey Allah and the Messenger" (قُلْ أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَ الرَّسُولَ‌). Therefore, since you claim to love Him, you must prove this love practically by obeying His command and His Prophet.

Then it adds: "But if they turn away, then surely Allah does not love the disbelievers" (فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يُحِبُّ الْكافِرِينَ‌). Their turning away shows that they do not have love for Allah; therefore, Allah does not love them either, because one-sided love is meaningless.

Furthermore, from the phrase "Obey Allah and the Messenger" (أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَ الرَّسُولَ‌), it is understood that obedience to Allah and the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله are inseparable. For this reason, in the previous verse, only the following and obedience of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله was mentioned, while here both are mentioned.

Ayahs

۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱصْطَفَىٰٓ ءَادَمَ وَنُوحًا وَءَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَءَالَ عِمْرَٰنَ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٣٣)
Indeed Allah chose Adam and Noah, and the progeny of Abraham and the progeny of Imran above all the nations; (33)
ذُرِّيَّةًۢ بَعْضُهَا مِنۢ بَعْضٍ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٣٤)
some of them are descendants of the others, and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (34)

Commentary

The Ancestors of Maryam

These verses serve as an introduction to the life story of Maryam (Mary) and an allusion to the high status of her ancestors. They provide a clear example of true love for the Lord and the manifestation of the effects of this love in action, which was referenced in the preceding verses.

First, He says: "Indeed, Allah chose Adam and Noah and the family of Abraham and the family of 'Imran over the worlds" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ اصْطَفى‌ آدَمَ وَ نُوحاً وَ آلَ إِبْراهِيمَ وَ آلَ عِمْرانَ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ‌).

"Istafa" (اصطفى) is derived from the root "Safw" (صفو), meaning the purification of something. "Safwah" refers to the pure essence of anything. In the Arabic language, a smooth, clear stone is called "Safa" because of its purity and clarity. Therefore, Istifa means selecting the pure part of something.

The above verse states: "We chose Adam, Noah, the family of Abraham, and the family of 'Imran."

This selection may be "ontological" (takwini) or "legislative" (tashri'i). This means that Allah made their creation distinguished from the beginning. However, despite having a distinguished creation, they were never forced to choose the path of truth; rather, they traversed this path by their own will and choice. Subsequently, due to their obedience to Allah's command, their piety, and their efforts in guiding humanity, they acquired new merits that blended with their inherent distinctions, making them truly chosen human beings.

In the following verse, He adds: "They were descendants, some of them from others" (ذُرِّيَّةً بَعْضُها مِنْ بَعْضٍ‌). [^1] These divine elect were similar to one another in terms of submission (Islam), purity, piety, and struggle for the guidance of mankind—like multiple copies of a single book, each transcribed from the other.

At the end of the verse, He points to the reality that Allah was observant of their efforts and struggles, heard their words, and was aware of their deeds, saying:

"And Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing" (وَ اللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ‌).

In the verses above, in addition to Adam, all the "Arch-Prophets" (Ulul-'Azm) are alluded to. The name of Noah is explicitly mentioned, and the "family of Abraham" includes Abraham himself, as well as Moses, Jesus, and the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. The mention of the "family of 'Imran" is a further reference to Maryam and Prophet Jesus عليه السلام, and its repetition serves as a prelude to the detailed account of their lives in the upcoming verses.

[^1]: "Dhurriyyah" (ذُرِّيَّةً) originally refers to small children, but it is sometimes applied to all offspring, whether small or large, direct or indirect. This word is derived from the root "Dhar'" (ذرء), meaning creation and origination.

Key Points

1. The Distinctions of the Prophets

A question arises here: if this inherent distinction did not force them to follow the path of truth and did not conflict with the issue of free will, does it not still constitute a form of discrimination?

In response, it must be said: a creation integrated into a precise system necessitates such differences (reflect on this). For example, the human body is an organized creation, and to maintain this system, there must be differences among the organs. If all the cells of the human body were as delicate as the cells of the retina, or as strong and powerful as the bone cells of the leg, or as sensitive as brain cells, or as mobile as heart cells, the organization of the body would surely collapse. Rather, there must be cells like the brain to take charge of leading the muscles and organs, strong bone cells to maintain the body's stability, delicate and sensitive cells to become aware of the smallest events, and mobile cells to create movement.

No one can ask why the entire body is not a brain, or why in a plant all cells are not as delicate and beautiful as the petals; for such a condition would completely expose the structure of the plant to destruction and non-existence.

However, the noteworthy point here is that this inherent distinction—which is absolutely necessary for creating an organized system—is not simple; rather, it is accompanied by a great responsibility commensurate with that "distinction." The existence of this heavy responsibility ensures the balance of the scales of their creation. That is, to the same extent that the prophets and leaders of humanity have distinctions, they also have responsibilities, while others who have different distinctions will have less responsibility.

Beyond this, inherent distinctions are never sufficient for a human's proximity to God; they must be accompanied by acquired merits.

  1. The verse does not intend to list all of God's chosen ones; rather, it only refers to a group of them. If some prophets who are not from these lineages are not mentioned, it is not evidence of them not being chosen. Additionally, it should be noted that the "family of Abraham" includes Moses son of 'Imran, the Prophet of Islam, and the chosen ones of his household, as they are all from the lineage of Abraham.

  2. According to Raghib in the book Al-Mufradat, the word "Al" (آل) is derived from "Ahl" (اهل). The only difference is that "Al" is usually used for the relatives of great and noble individuals, whereas "Ahl" has a broad meaning and is applied to everyone. Furthermore, "Al" is attributed to human individuals, but the word "Ahl" can be attributed to time, place, and anything else. For example, they say "Ahl of such-and-such city," but they do not say "Al of such-and-such city."

  3. It goes without saying that the meaning of the chosen ones of the family of Abraham and the family of 'Imran is not that all the children of Abraham and 'Imran are among the chosen. There may even be disbelievers among them. Rather, the meaning is that a group from their "descendants" has been chosen.

  4. "'Imran" in the above verse is the father of Maryam, not the father of Moses. Wherever the name 'Imran is mentioned in the Quran, it refers to the father of Maryam, and the subsequent verses describing Maryam's life are also evidence of this.

  5. In numerous narrations reached to us through the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, this verse has been used to argue for the infallibility ('Ismah) of the Prophets and Imams. This is because Allah never chooses individuals who are sinful or tainted by polytheism, disbelief, or wickedness; rather, He chooses those who are free from impurities and are infallible (of course, certain levels of infallibility can be inferred from the verse).

  6. Some recent writers have used this verse to argue for the theory of the evolution of species. They believe the verse indicates that "Adam" was not the first human, but that many humans existed during Adam's time, and Allah chose Adam from among them and brought forth a distinguished generation from his children.

They consider the expression "over the worlds" (عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ) in the above verse as evidence for this, saying: in the era of Adam, "the worlds"—meaning "human society"—existed. Therefore, there is no obstacle to the first human, who came into existence millions of years ago, having evolved from other animals, while "Adam" was merely a chosen human! However, in response to this, it must be said that there is no evidence that "the worlds" here refers to the contemporaries of Adam. Rather, it may refer to the entirety of human society throughout history. Thus, the meaning of the verse would be: "Allah chose from the entire human society throughout history certain individuals, the first of whom was Adam, then Noah, the family of Abraham, and the family of 'Imran." Since these chosen ones each lived in a specific era and time, we understand that "the worlds" refers to the entire human society across all ages and centuries. Therefore, it is not necessary to believe that many humans existed in Adam's era from whom Adam was chosen—reflect on this!

Ayahs

إِذْ قَالَتِ ٱمْرَأَتُ عِمْرَٰنَ رَبِّ إِنِّى نَذَرْتُ لَكَ مَا فِى بَطْنِى مُحَرَّرًا فَتَقَبَّلْ مِنِّىٓ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٣٥)
When the wife of Imran said, ‘My Lord, I dedicate to You in consecration what is in my belly. Accept it from me; indeed You are the All-hearing, the All-knowing.’ (35)
فَلَمَّا وَضَعَتْهَا قَالَتْ رَبِّ إِنِّى وَضَعْتُهَآ أُنثَىٰ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا وَضَعَتْ وَلَيْسَ ٱلذَّكَرُ كَٱلْأُنثَىٰ ۖ وَإِنِّى سَمَّيْتُهَا مَرْيَمَ وَإِنِّىٓ أُعِيذُهَا بِكَ وَذُرِّيَّتَهَا مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ٱلرَّجِيمِ (٣٦)
When she bore her, she said, ‘My Lord, I have borne a female [child]’—and Allah knew better what she had borne, and the male [child she expected] was no match for the female [child she had borne] —‘and I have named her Mary, and I commend her and her offspring to Your care against [the evil of] the outcast Satan.’ (36)

Commentary

Imran and His Daughter Maryam

Following the reference to the greatness of the Family of Imran in the preceding verses, these verses speak of Imran and his daughter Maryam, concisely recounting the circumstances of her birth, upbringing, and some significant events in the life of this great lady.

To elaborate: It is understood from history, Islamic reports, and the statements of commentators that "Hannah" and "Eshya" (Elizabeth) were two sisters. The former married "Imran," who was one of the prominent figures of the Children of Israel, while the latter was chosen as a wife by the Prophet Zakariya (Zachariah) عليه السلام. [^1] Years passed for Hannah, the wife of Imran, without a child being born to her. One day, while sitting under a tree, she saw a bird feeding its chicks. Observing this maternal affection kindled the fire of longing for a child in her heart. She sincerely petitioned Allah for a child from the depths of her soul, and it was not long before this sincere prayer reached the point of acceptance, and she became pregnant.

It is understood from some narrations that Allah had sent a revelation to Imran that He would grant him a blessed son who could heal incurable diseases and bring the dead to life by Allah's command, and who would be sent as a prophet to the Children of Israel.

He shared this matter with his wife, Hannah. Therefore, when she became pregnant, she assumed that the child was the very one she carried in her womb, unaware that the one in her womb was the mother of that child (Maryam). For this reason, she vowed to make the boy a servant of the House of God, "Bayt al-Maqdis." However, upon giving birth, she saw that it was a girl. At this moment, she became concerned about what to do in this situation, as the servants of Bayt al-Maqdis were chosen from among boys, and there was no precedent for a girl being chosen for such a role.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 2, p. 523

With the above explanation, we return to the commentary of the verses:

In the first verse, He says: Remember "when the wife of Imran said: 'My Lord! I have vowed to You what is in my womb, to be dedicated (and free for the service of Your House), so accept it from me; indeed, You are the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing'" (إِذْ قالَتِ امْرَأَتُ عِمْرانَ رَبِّ إِنِّي نَذَرْتُ لَكَ ما فِي بَطْنِي مُحَرَّراً فَتَقَبَّلْ مِنِّي إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ). [^1] This verse refers to the vow made by Imran's wife during her pregnancy. She assumed her child would be a boy (based on the glad tidings from Allah), and thus she used the masculine term muharraran (or its equivalent in another language) rather than muharraratan, asking Allah to accept her vow.

The word muharraran is derived from the root tahrir, which means "to set free." In the terminology of that time, it referred to children who were dedicated to the service of the temple and the House of God to handle cleaning and other services, and to engage in the worship of the Lord during their free time. Since they were free from any service to their parents, they were called muharrar (liberated), or perhaps they were called so because they were purely devoted, free from any worldly striving or effort.

Some have said that these children performed their duties under the supervision of their parents from the time they were capable of such service until the age of maturity. After reaching maturity, their destiny was in their own hands; if they wished to end their service at the temple and leave, they could, and if they preferred to stay, they stayed.

Some have suggested that the action of Imran's wife in making the vow is evidence that Imran had passed away during her pregnancy; otherwise, it would be unlikely for her to make such a vow independently.

[^1]: It is understood from some narrations that "Imran" was also a prophet and received revelation. It should be noted that this Imran is different from Imran the father of Musa عليه السلام, and there is a gap of 1800 years between them—Majma' al-Bayan and Tafsir al-Maraghi under the verse in question.

[^1]: Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Taqabbul" is the acceptance of something in a way that entails a reward and merit (thus it differs from the root qabul).

Then He adds: "So when she gave birth to her (and found her to be a girl), she said: 'My Lord! I have given birth to a female'" (فَلَمَّا وَضَعَتْها قالَتْ رَبِّ إِنِّي وَضَعْتُها أُنْثى‌).

Of course, "Allah knew better what she had brought forth" (وَ اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما وَضَعَتْ‌).

Then she added: "You know that the female and the male (for the purpose I vowed) are not the same" (وَ لَيْسَ الذَّكَرُ كَالْأُنْثى‌).

A girl, after reaching maturity, has her monthly cycle and cannot remain in the mosque; furthermore, their physical strength is not the same, and issues related to hijab, pregnancy, and childbirth make the continuation of this service difficult for a girl. Therefore, boys were always the ones vowed.

From the context of the verse and the narrations regarding its commentary, it is understood that the phrase "and the male is not like the female" is spoken by Maryam's mother, although some have considered the possibility that it is the word of Allah, but that seems unlikely.

Here a question arises: Maryam's mother should normally have said: "and the female is not like the male" (wa laysat al-untha kad-dhakar), because she had brought forth a girl, not a boy. Thus, it has been said that the sentence contains a transposition (advancing one term over another), as is common in many Arabic expressions. Perhaps the sudden distress she felt at the time of delivery caused her to speak this way, for she longed to have a son to be a servant of Bayt al-Maqdis; this very longing caused her to involuntarily mention the name of the "male" first while speaking.

Then she added: "And I have named her Maryam, and I seek refuge for her and her offspring with You from Satan, the rejected (and driven away from Allah's presence)" (وَ إِنِّي سَمَّيْتُها مَرْيَمَ وَ إِنِّي أُعِيذُها بِكَ وَ ذُرِّيَّتَها مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ الرَّجِيمِ‌).

"Maryam" literally means a "worshipping woman" and a "servant." Since this naming was done by her mother after delivery, it demonstrates the ultimate love and devotion of this faithful mother to dedicate her child to the path of servitude and worship of Allah. It was also for this reason that after naming her, she entrusted her newborn and the children who would be born from her in the future to Allah against satanic whisperings.

Ayah

فَتَقَبَّلَهَا رَبُّهَا بِقَبُولٍ حَسَنٍ وَأَنۢبَتَهَا نَبَاتًا حَسَنًا وَكَفَّلَهَا زَكَرِيَّا ۖ كُلَّمَا دَخَلَ عَلَيْهَا زَكَرِيَّا ٱلْمِحْرَابَ وَجَدَ عِندَهَا رِزْقًا ۖ قَالَ يَـٰمَرْيَمُ أَنَّىٰ لَكِ هَـٰذَا ۖ قَالَتْ هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ (٣٧)
Thereupon her Lord accepted her with a gracious acceptance, and made her grow up in a worthy fashion, and He charged Zechariah with her care. Whenever Zechariah visited her in the sanctuary, he would find provisions with her. He said, ‘O Mary, from where does this come for you?’ She said, ‘It comes from Allah. Allah provides whomever He wishes without any reckoning.’ (37)

Commentary

Mary’s Upbringing Under the Shadow of Divine Grace

This verse continues the discussion from the previous verse regarding the story of Mary. It states:

"So her Lord accepted her with a gracious acceptance and caused her to grow in a goodly manner" (فَتَقَبَّلَها رَبُّها بِقَبُولٍ حَسَنٍ وَ أَنْبَتَها نَباتاً حَسَناً).

Mary’s mother did not believe that she would be accepted as a servant of the House of Allah (Bayt al-Maqdis); for this reason, she had wished for her child to be a boy, as it was unprecedented for a girl to be chosen for such a task. However, according to the above verse, Allah accepted this pure girl for the first time for this spiritual and mystical service.

Some commentators have said: The sign of her acceptance was that after reaching maturity during her period of service at Bayt al-Maqdis, Mary never experienced menstruation so that she would not be forced to distance herself from this spiritual center. Alternatively, the presence of heavenly food before her prayer niche (Mihrab) was evidence of this acceptance.

It is also possible that the acceptance of this vow and the acceptance of Mary were announced to her mother through inspiration.

The expression "anbataha" (caused her to grow), derived from the root "inbat" (to cause to grow), regarding Mary’s upbringing, refers to the aspects of her spiritual, mystical, and moral perfection.

Furthermore, this phrase points to a subtle point: that Allah’s work is "inbat" (growth). Just as within the seeds of flowers and plants there are latent potentials that develop and manifest under the supervision of a gardener, within the human existence and the depths of his soul and primordial nature (Fitrah), all kinds of sublime potentials are hidden. If a person places himself under the training of Divine educators—who are the gardeners of the orchard of humanity—he will develop rapidly, and those God-given potentials will manifest, and "inbat" in the true sense of the word will take place.

Then it adds: "and He placed her in the care of Zechariah" (وَ كَفَّلَها زَكَرِيَّا).

"Kaffalaha" is derived from the root "kafalat," which originally means "joining one thing to another." For this reason, individuals who take responsibility for the care of a child are called "kafil" or "kafeel," because they essentially join the child to their own existence.

When this root is used in the simple trilithic form (kafula), it means to undertake guardianship and care. When used in the augmented form (kaffala), it means to select and appoint a guardian for someone else.

It is recorded in history that Mary’s father (Imran) passed away before her birth. After her birth, her mother brought her to Bayt al-Maqdis to the scholars and rabbis of the Jews and said: "This child is a gift to Bayt al-Maqdis; let one of you take charge of her care." Since signs of greatness were apparent in her face and she was born into a noble family, a dispute arose among the scholars of the Children of Israel, each wanting the honor of Mary’s guardianship to fall to him. Finally, through a special ceremony—the details of which will come in the commentary of verse 44 of this same Surah—Zechariah took charge of her care.

As Mary grew older, the signs of greatness and majesty became more apparent in her, reaching a point where the Quran says about her in the continuation of this verse: "Whenever Zechariah entered her prayer niche, he found with her a special provision" (كُلَّما دَخَلَ عَلَيْها زَكَرِيَّا الْمِحْرابَ وَجَدَ عِنْدَها رِزْقاً).

"Mihrab" (prayer niche) is a special place designated in a temple for the Imam of that temple or for specific individuals. Several reasons have been mentioned for naming it so, the best of which are the following three:

First, it is derived from the root "harb," meaning war, because believers rise up in this place to battle against Satan and rebellious desires. Second, "Mihrab" originally means the upper part of a gathering, and since the location of the Mihrab is placed at the top/front of the temple, it is called by this name. It should also be noted that the state of the Mihrab among the Children of Israel, as stated, differed from our Mihrabs; they built the Mihrab higher than the ground level so that it required several steps, and its surroundings were protected like the walls of a room, such that those inside the Mihrab were less visible from the outside.

Third: Mihrab means the entire temple, which was the place of struggle against the whims of the self and Satan.

Mary grew up under the care of Zechariah and was so immersed in worship and servitude to Allah that, according to Ibn Abbas, when she reached nine years of age, she would fast during the days and spend the nights in worship. She advanced so far in piety and the gnosis (Ma'rifah) of her Lord that she surpassed the rabbis and pious scholars of that time [1]. Whenever Zechariah stood by her Mihrab and came to visit her, he would observe special foods by her Mihrab that would cause him wonder. One day "he said: O Mary, from where did this come to you?" (قالَ يا مَرْيَمُ أَنَّى لَكِ هذا).

In response, Mary "said: It is from Allah; surely Allah provides for whomever He wills without account" (قالَتْ هُوَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَرْزُقُ مَنْ يَشاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ).

What kind of food this was and how it came to Mary is not explained in the verse. However, from numerous narrations found in Tafsir al-Ayyashi and other books of both the "Shia" and "Sunni" traditions, it is understood that it was a type of heavenly fruit that appeared by Mary’s Mihrab by the command of the Lord out of season. It is not surprising that Allah would host His pious servant in such a manner.

The fact that "rizqan" (provision) refers to heavenly food can also be inferred from clues found throughout the verse. Firstly, the word "rizqan" in its indefinite form is a sign that it was a special and unknown provision to Zechariah. Secondly, Mary’s answer that "it is from Allah" is another sign of this. Thirdly, Zechariah’s excitement and his request for a child from the Lord, mentioned in the following verse, is considered another clue for this meaning.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 2, p. 436.

However, some commentators (such as the author of Al-Manar) believe that "rizqan" refers to ordinary worldly food. It is narrated from Ibn Jarir that the Children of Israel were afflicted by famine and Zechariah was unable to provide for Mary’s life. At this time, they cast lots, and it fell to a carpenter who proudly prepared Mary’s food from his earnings. When Zechariah stood by her Mihrab, he would wonder at the existence of such food under those difficult conditions, and Mary, in response to his question, would say: "It is from Allah," meaning that Allah made a man of faith interested in this service under those harsh conditions.

But as we said, this interpretation is neither consistent with the clues in the verse nor with the narrations that have been transmitted regarding the verse. Among them, in Tafsir al-Ayyashi, a narration is quoted from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, the summary of which is as follows: "One day the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله came to the house of Fatima al-Zahra عليها السلام while no food had been seen in her house for several days. Suddenly, he observed abundant special food with her and asked her: From where is this food?

Fatima عليها السلام said: It is from Allah, for He grants provision to whomever He wills without account. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: This event is just like the event of Zechariah when he came to Mary’s Mihrab and saw special food there and asked her: O Mary! From where is this food? She said: It is from Allah" [1]. Regarding the phrase "without account" (بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ), we have discussed it under verse 202 of Surah Al-Baqarah and under verse 27 of this Surah.

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 333 from Tafsir al-Ayyashi, Vol. 1, p. 171.

Ayahs

هُنَالِكَ دَعَا زَكَرِيَّا رَبَّهُۥ ۖ قَالَ رَبِّ هَبْ لِى مِن لَّدُنكَ ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً ۖ إِنَّكَ سَمِيعُ ٱلدُّعَآءِ (٣٨)
Thereat Zechariah supplicated his Lord. He said, ‘My Lord! Grant me a good offspring from You! Indeed You hear all supplications.’ (38)
فَنَادَتْهُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَهُوَ قَآئِمٌ يُصَلِّى فِى ٱلْمِحْرَابِ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكَ بِيَحْيَىٰ مُصَدِّقًۢا بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَسَيِّدًا وَحَصُورًا وَنَبِيًّا مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٣٩)
Then, as he stood praying in the sanctuary the angels called out to him: ‘Allah gives you the good news of John, as a confirmer of a Word of Allah, eminent and chaste, a prophet, and one of the righteous.’ (39)
قَالَ رَبِّ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِى غُلَـٰمٌ وَقَدْ بَلَغَنِىَ ٱلْكِبَرُ وَٱمْرَأَتِى عَاقِرٌ ۖ قَالَ كَذَٰلِكَ ٱللَّهُ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَآءُ (٤٠)
He said, ‘My Lord, how shall I have a son while old age has overtaken me and my wife is barren?’ Said He, ‘So it is that Allah does whatever He wishes.’ (40)

Commentary

Zechariah and Mary

These verses describe a portion of the life of the divine Prophet, Zechariah (peace be upon him), in connection with the story of Mary.

As previously mentioned, the wife of Zechariah and the mother of Mary were sisters, and by chance, both were initially barren and sterile. When Mary’s mother, by the grace of the Lord, was blessed with such a worthy child, and Zechariah witnessed her sincerity and other wondrous qualities, he longed to also have a pure and God-fearing child like Mary—a child whose very countenance would be a sign of Allah’s greatness. Although many years of his and his wife's lives had passed, and by natural standards it seemed highly unlikely for them to have a child, his faith in the power of the Lord and the sight of fresh fruits out of season in Mary’s prayer niche (Mihrab) filled his heart with hope. He hoped that in the season of old age, the fruit of a child might appear on the branch of his existence. For this reason, while he was engaged in supplication, he asked Allah for a child. As the Quran states in the first verse above: "At that time, Zechariah called upon his Lord and said: 'My Lord! Grant me from Yourself a pure offspring; indeed, You are the Hearer of supplication'" and the Granter of it (هُنالِكَ دَعا زَكَرِيَّا رَبَّهُ قالَ رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً إِنَّكَ سَمِيعُ الدُّعاءِ). ^1

"Then the angels called out to him while he stood praying in the sanctuary: 'Allah gives you glad tidings of John (Yahya), confirming a word from Allah (Jesus), and [he will be] a leader, chaste, and a prophet from among the righteous.'"

(فَنادَتْهُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَ هُوَ قائِمٌ يُصَلِّي فِي الْمِحْرابِ أَنَّ اللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكَ بِيَحْيى‌ مُصَدِّقاً بِكَلِمَةٍ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ سَيِّداً وَ حَصُوراً وَ نَبِيًّا مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ‌).

Not only did Allah announce the acceptance of his prayer through the angels, but He also described five characteristics of this pure child: First, he would believe in "a word from Allah" (بِكَلِمَةٍ مِنَ اللَّهِ), referring to Prophet Jesus عليه السلام, and by believing in and supporting him, he would strengthen Jesus عليه السلام. (Note that "Word" here refers to Prophet Jesus عليه السلام, as evidenced by verse 45 of this same Surah and verse 171 of Surah An-Nisa; the reason for this expression will soon be clarified).

As recorded in history, Yahya was six months older than Jesus عليه السلام and was the first person to testify to his prophethood and call people toward him. Since Yahya was widely renowned among the people for his asceticism and purity, his inclination toward Jesus عليه السلام had a profound impact on the people's attention toward him.

Second, he would hold the status of "Sayyid" (mastery and leadership) in terms of knowledge and action. Third, he would protect himself from rebellious desires and the contamination of worldliness; this meaning is derived from the word "Hasur."

The word "Hasur" is derived from "Hasr," meaning to withhold or confine. Here, it refers to someone who restrains himself from whims and desires. This word is also sometimes used to mean someone who abstains from marriage; for this reason, a group of commentators have interpreted it this way, and it has been mentioned in some narrations as well.

Fourth and fifth, he would be a great Prophet (note that "Nabiyyan" is used in the indefinite form, which here denotes greatness) and would be among the righteous and worthy.

Upon hearing this glad tiding from the angels, Zechariah was overwhelmed with joy and delight, yet he could not hide his amazement at such a matter. He said:

"My Lord! How can I have a son when old age has reached me and my wife is barren?" (قالَ رَبِّ أَنَّى يَكُونُ لِي غُلامٌ وَ قَدْ بَلَغَنِيَ الْكِبَرُ وَ امْرَأَتِي عاقِرٌ).

Here, Allah answered him and "said: 'Thus Allah does what He wills'" (قالَ كَذلِكَ اللَّهُ يَفْعَلُ ما يَشاءُ).

With this brief response, which relied on the influence of the Divine Will and Decree, Zechariah was satisfied.

Key Points

1- Is Abstaining from Marriage a Virtue?

The first question that arises here is: if "Hasur" means "one who abstains from marriage," is this act considered a merit for a human being, as it is mentioned regarding "Yahya"?

In response, it must be said: Firstly, there is no definitive proof that "Hasur" in the verse means one who abstains from marriage. The narrations cited in this regard are not certain in terms of their chain of transmission. It is not at all unlikely that "Hasur" in the verse means one who abandons lusts, desires, and worldliness—a quality similar to asceticism (Zuhd).

Secondly, it is possible that Yahya, like Jesus, was forced to live a celibate life due to specific life circumstances and the necessity of numerous travels to propagate God's religion.

This cannot be a general law for everyone. If Allah praises him with this attribute, it is because he did not marry due to specific circumstances, yet he was able to protect himself from sin and remain entirely untainted. Generally, the law of marriage is an innate (Fitri) law, and in no religion is it possible for a ruling to be legislated contrary to this innate law. Therefore, neither in Islam nor in any other religion has abstaining from marriage been considered a good deed in itself.

2- Yahya and Jesus عليه السلام

The word "Yahya" is derived from the root of "Hayat" (life), meaning "he shall live," which was chosen as the name for this great Prophet. The intended "life" is both material and spiritual life under the light of faith, the status of prophethood, and connection with God. As understood from this verse and verse 7 of Surah Maryam, Allah chose this name for him before his birth:

"O Zechariah, indeed We give you glad tidings of a boy whose name is Yahya. We have not assigned to anyone before [him] this namesake." (يا زَكَرِيَّا إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ بِغُلامٍ اسْمُهُ يَحْيى‌ لَمْ نَجْعَلْ لَهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ سَمِيًّا).

Furthermore, the latter phrase indicates that this name was unprecedented.

As understood from the preceding verses, the request for Yahya's birth was made by Zechariah after witnessing Mary's rapid spiritual progress. Interestingly, as a result of this prayer, Allah gave Zechariah a child who resembled Mary’s son, "Jesus," in many ways: in terms of prophethood in childhood, the meaning of their names (both Jesus and Yahya etymologically mean "he shall live"), and in terms of Allah’s salutations upon them in the three stages of "birth, death, and resurrection," among other aspects.

3- In the above verses, when describing his old age, Zechariah says: وَ قَدْ بَلَغَنِيَ الْكِبَرُ (Old age has reached me), but in verse 8 of Surah Maryam, we read his words as: وَ قَدْ بَلَغْتُ مِنَ الْكِبَرِ عِتِيًّا (I have reached the extreme stage of old age).

This difference in expression is because, just as a person moves toward old age, it is as if old age and death are also coming toward him from the opposite direction, as Ali عليه السلام says:

"When you are in retreat [from life] and death is approaching, how quickly the meeting will be!" ^1

4- "Ghulam" linguistically means a young boy. "Aqir" (barren) is from the word "Aqr," meaning "root and foundation" or "confinement." The reason barren women are called "Aqir" is either because their [reproductive] work has ended or because they are confined from giving birth to a child.

5- A question arises here: why was Zechariah surprised and amazed, given the infinite power of the Lord?

However, considering other verses of the Quran, the answer is clear. He wanted to know how a child could be born from a barren woman who had even passed her menstrual years long ago. What changes would occur in her being? Would she once again experience menstruation like young and middle-aged women, or would she be prepared to nurture a child in another way?

Moreover, faith in Allah's power is different from witnessing (Shuhud). In reality, he wanted his faith to reach the stage of witnessing. Just as Abraham had faith in the Resurrection but requested to witness it, Zechariah wanted to attain such a level of certainty. It is natural for any human being, when faced with a matter contrary to the laws of nature, to fall into thought and desire to find a sensory sign for it.

^1: Nahj al-Balagha, Wisdom 29.

Ayah

قَالَ رَبِّ ٱجْعَل لِّىٓ ءَايَةً ۖ قَالَ ءَايَتُكَ أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ ٱلنَّاسَ ثَلَـٰثَةَ أَيَّامٍ إِلَّا رَمْزًا ۗ وَٱذْكُر رَّبَّكَ كَثِيرًا وَسَبِّحْ بِٱلْعَشِىِّ وَٱلْإِبْكَـٰرِ (٤١)
He said, ‘My Lord, grant me a sign.’ Said He, ‘Your sign is that you will not speak to people for three days except in gestures. Remember Your Lord much, and glorify Him morning and evening.’ (41)

Commentary

The Glad Tidings of Yahya's Birth

The discussion was regarding Zechariah عليه السلام, his request for a child from the presence of Allah, and the glad tidings of the birth of Yahya عليه السلام.

At this point, Zechariah asks Allah for a sign regarding this great news. As we have mentioned, this was not due to a lack of trust in the divine promises, but rather for further emphasis and so that his faith in this matter might become a faith of witnessing (intuitive certainty), so his heart would be overflowing with tranquility—just as Ibrahim the Friend (Khalil) requested to witness the scene of the Resurrection to grant his heart even greater peace.

Zechariah pleaded: "My Lord! Appoint for me a sign" (قالَ رَبِّ اجْعَلْ لِي آيَةً).

In response, Allah "said: Your sign is that you shall not speak to people for three days except by gesture and signal," and your tongue, without any defect or illness, will cease to function for conversing with people (قالَ آيَتُكَ أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ النَّاسَ ثَلاثَةَ أَيَّامٍ إِلَّا رَمْزاً).

"But remember your Lord much (in gratitude for this blessing) and glorify Him in the evening and the morning" (وَ اذْكُرْ رَبَّكَ كَثِيراً وَ سَبِّحْ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَ الْإِبْكارِ).

From this sentence, it is understood that while his tongue was temporarily disabled from speaking to people, he was capable of glorifying and remembering Allah. This was so he could fulfill the duty of gratitude for this immense gift that Allah had bestowed upon him, and also to serve as a clear sign from the Great Lord regarding the opening of closed matters. In other words, detaching from people and connecting to the Truth during these three days would bring him closer to Allah and serve as a clear sign and miracle of Allah's power.

"Ramz" (رمز) originally refers to signaling with the lips; it is also used for speaking in a low voice. In common usage, this word has acquired a broader meaning, referring to any speech, gesture, or sign that conveys a message secretly or indirectly.

In any case, Allah accepted Zechariah's request, and for three days and nights, his tongue was prevented from speaking to people without any natural cause, while he continued to chant the remembrance of Allah. This extraordinary state was a sign of the Lord's power over all things. The God who can open a closed tongue for His remembrance is capable of bringing forth a faithful child—who is a manifestation of the Lord's remembrance—from a sterile and closed womb. From here, the connection between this sign and what Zechariah requested becomes clear.

Some commentators have said: It is possible that this refraining from speaking to people was voluntary, and Zechariah was commanded by Allah not to open his tongue during these three days except for divine praise; in other words, he was commanded to observe a "fast of silence," which existed among some previous nations.

Fakhr al-Razi narrates this view from Abu Muslim and considers it a beautiful and reasonable interpretation [^1]. However, it is clear that this interpretation is not consistent with the context of the verse, because Zechariah requested a sign and a miracle for the divine glad tidings, and a voluntary silence cannot serve as a proof for this meaning.

The word "Ashiyy" (عشى) usually refers to the final hours of the day, just as "Ibkar" (ابكار) refers to the early hours of the day. Some believe that from the beginning of noon until sunset is called "Ashiyy," and from the beginning of dawn until noon is "Ibkar" [^1]. However, Raghib Isfahani in the book Al-Mufradat says: "Ashiyy" is from the time of noon until the next morning, and "Ibkar" is from the break of dawn until noon. Therefore, "Ashiyy" and "Ibkar" would encompass the entire twenty-four-hour period. But as we said, these two words are usually used in the first two meanings.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kabir, Vol. 8, p. 41. [^1]: Al-Mushaf al-Mufassar, written by Farid Wajdi (under the verse in question).

Key Point

A question arises here: Is the binding of a Prophet's tongue consistent with the status of Prophethood and his missionary duty?

The answer to this question is not very difficult, because this matter would only be inconsistent with the duty of Prophethood if it were prolonged. However, for a short period—a period in which the Prophet can be absent from his people and community to engage in the worship of Allah—it poses no obstacle. Furthermore, during this same period, he was able to convey necessary matters to them through signs and gestures, or to make them understand truths by reciting the verses of the Lord, which is considered the remembrance of Allah. Incidentally, he did exactly this and guided the people to the remembrance of Allah through gestures!

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قَالَتِ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَـٰمَرْيَمُ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱصْطَفَىٰكِ وَطَهَّرَكِ وَٱصْطَفَىٰكِ عَلَىٰ نِسَآءِ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٤٢)
And when the angels said, ‘O Mary, Allah has chosen you and purified you, and He has chosen you above the world’s women. (42)
يَـٰمَرْيَمُ ٱقْنُتِى لِرَبِّكِ وَٱسْجُدِى وَٱرْكَعِى مَعَ ٱلرَّٰكِعِينَ (٤٣)
O Mary, be obedient to your Lord and prostrate and bow down with those who bow [in worship].’ (43)

Commentary

Mary, the Divinely Chosen Lady

These verses return once again to the story of Mary, speaking of the period of her flourishing and maturity, and enumerating her lofty stations.

First, it discusses the conversation of the angels with Mary, stating: And remember "when the angels said: O Mary! Allah has chosen you and purified you and chosen you above all the women of the worlds" (وَ إِذْ قالَتِ الْمَلائِكَةُ يا مَرْيَمُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ اصْطَفاكِ وَ طَهَّرَكِ وَ اصْطَفاكِ عَلى‌ نِساءِ الْعالَمِينَ‌). [^1] What honor could be greater than for a human being to be addressed by angels—a discourse that brings glad tidings of her being chosen by Allah, her purity, and her superiority over all the women of the world? This was achieved only under the shadow of her piety, God-wariness, faith, and worship. Indeed, she was chosen to give birth to a Prophet such as Jesus Christ.

It is noteworthy that the phrase "He chose you" (اصطفاكِ) is repeated in the verse: once to express her being chosen in an absolute sense, and the second time for her being chosen over all the women of the world.

This verse testifies that Mary was the greatest female personality in her own world. This does not contradict what has been narrated regarding the Great Lady of Islam, Fatimah عليه السلام, that she is the most superior lady of the world; for it has been narrated in numerous traditions from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that:

"As for Mary, she was the leader of the women of her time, but Fatimah is the leader of all the women of the world, from the first to the last." [^1]

The word "al-Alamin" (the worlds) does not contradict this statement at all, because this word in the Quran and in common usage is often applied to the people living in a particular age and time. Just as we read regarding the Children of Israel: "And that I preferred you over the worlds" (وَ أَنِّي فَضَّلْتُكُمْ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ‌) [^2]. It is obvious that the meaning is the superiority of the believers of the Children of Israel over the people of their own era.

[^1]: The meaning of Mary's purity is either purity from the menstrual cycle, which allowed her to continuously serve the Holy House (Bayt al-Maqdis), or purity from any kind of moral and spiritual pollution and sin.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 336; and Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 10, p. 24. [^2]: Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 47.

In the next verse, there is mention of another address from the angels to Mary; they say: "O Mary! (In gratitude for these great blessings—that you have been chosen by Allah, have gained superiority over the women of the world of your time, and have become pure and refined in every respect) prostrate before your Lord and bow down with those who bow" (يا مَرْيَمُ اقْنُتِي لِرَبِّكِ وَ اسْجُدِي وَ ارْكَعِي مَعَ الرَّاكِعِينَ‌).

Here, three commands are given by the angels to Mary:

First, "Qunut" before the Lord; and this term, as we previously mentioned, means humility and constancy in obedience. Second, "Sujud" (prostration), which is also a form of "complete humility" before Allah. And third, "Ruku" (bowing), which is yet another form of humility and modesty.

The phrase "And bow with those who bow" (وَ ارْكَعِي مَعَ الرَّاكِعِينَ‌) might be an allusion to congregational prayer, and it could also be an allusion to joining the ranks of the worshippers and those humble before Allah; meaning, just as other sincere servants of Allah perform Ruku for Him, you too should perform Ruku.

In this verse, prostration (Sajdah) is mentioned first and then bowing (Ruku). This is not because in their prayer prostration was performed before bowing; rather, the intention is the performance of both acts of worship, without regard for their sequence. It is like saying, "Pray and perform ablution and be pure," meaning perform all these duties, as the conjunction "and" (waw) does not necessarily imply sequence. Furthermore, Ruku and Sujud fundamentally mean modesty and humility, and the conventional bowing and prostration are considered among their manifestations.

Ayah

ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ ۚ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ يُلْقُونَ أَقْلَـٰمَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ يَكْفُلُ مَرْيَمَ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ (٤٤)
These accounts are from the Unseen, which We reveal to you, and you were not with them when they were casting lots [to see] which of them would take charge of Mary’s care, nor were you with them when they were contending. (44)

Commentary

The Guardianship of Maryam

This verse refers to another aspect of the story of Maryam and states: "What We have related to you regarding the life of Maryam and Zakariyya is from the news of the unseen which We reveal to you" (ذلِكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ‌).

This is because these stories, in this form (accurate and free from any superstition), do not exist in any of the previous scriptures that have undergone corruption, and their only source is the divine revelation of the Quran.

Then, continuing this discourse, it says: "You were not present with them when they cast their pens (for drawing lots) to determine who should take charge of Maryam, nor were you present when they (the scholars of the Children of Israel, seeking the honor of her guardianship) were disputing with one another," and We told you all of this through revelation (وَ ما كُنْتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ يُلْقُونَ أَقْلامَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ يَكْفُلُ مَرْيَمَ وَ ما كُنْتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ‌).

As mentioned in the commentary of the preceding verses, after giving birth, the mother of Maryam wrapped her newborn in a cloth and brought her to the Temple. She addressed the scholars and elders of the Children of Israel, saying that this child had been vowed to the service of the House of Allah, and asked them to take charge of her guardianship. Since Maryam belonged to a great family known for purity and righteousness (the family of Imran), the worshippers of the Children of Israel vied with one another for her guardianship. Consequently, they found no choice but to draw lots. They came to the side of a river and prepared the pens or wooden sticks they used for casting lots, writing each person's name on one of them. Any pen that sank into the water was not the winner; the only pen that remained floating on the surface was the one with the name of Zakariyya عليه السلام written on it. Thus, Zakariyya's guardianship over Maryam was established. In reality, he was the most deserving of all, for in addition to holding the station of Prophethood, he was the husband of Maryam's maternal aunt.

Drawing Lots: The Final Solution to Disputes

From this verse and the verses in Surah As-Saffat regarding Yunus عليه السلام, it is understood that to solve a problem or during a dispute and conflict—when a complete deadlock is reached and no other way is seen to end the contention—one may seek assistance through drawing lots (qur'ah). These verses, combined with the narrations of the leaders of Islam, have caused the "Rule of Drawing Lots" to be recognized and discussed as one of the jurisprudential rules in Islamic texts. However, as noted above, drawing lots is conditional upon the existence of a complete deadlock; therefore, whenever another way is found to solve the problem, drawing lots cannot be used.

The method of drawing lots in Islam does not have a specific form; rather, one can use wooden arrows, pebbles, paper, or the like, in a manner that precludes collusion or manipulation. It is clear that in Islam, one cannot engage in gambling or betting through drawing lots, because such matters are not "problems" that require lots for a solution, and such income is not legitimate.

It is also necessary to mention that drawing lots is not exclusive to disputes and conflicts between people; rather, other deadlocks can also be resolved by it. For example, as mentioned in the Hadiths, if a deviant person commits bestiality with a sheep and then releases it into a flock such that it cannot be identified, one must be selected by lot to be removed and its meat avoided. This is because discarding the entire flock would be a great loss, and consuming the meat of all of them is not permissible. In such a case, drawing lots solves the problem.

Ayahs

إِذْ قَالَتِ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَـٰمَرْيَمُ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكِ بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنْهُ ٱسْمُهُ ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ وَجِيهًا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَمِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ (٤٥)
When the angels said, ‘O Mary, Allah gives you the good news of a Word from Him whose name is Messiah, Jesus son of Mary, distinguished in the world and the Hereafter and one of those brought near [to Allah]. (45)
وَيُكَلِّمُ ٱلنَّاسَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ وَكَهْلًا وَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٤٦)
He will speak to the people in the cradle and in adulthood, and will be one of the righteous.’ (46)

Commentary

The Glad Tidings of the Birth of the Messiah

From this verse onward, the discourse turns to another significant part of Maryam’s life—the events surrounding the birth of her son, the Messiah (peace be upon him)—and explains important points regarding this matter. First, it says: Remember "when the angels said: 'O Maryam! Indeed, Allah gives you glad tidings of a word (a magnificent being) from Him, whose name is the Messiah, Jesus, son of Maryam'" (إِذْ قالَتِ الْمَلائِكَةُ يا مَرْيَمُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكِ بِكَلِمَةٍ مِنْهُ اسْمُهُ الْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ‌). [^1]

[^1]: It is noteworthy that the pronoun in "اسمه" (his name) refers back to "كلمة" (word); however, it appears in the masculine form because it regards the meaning and the person it represents, who was the Messiah (peace be upon him).

"While he will be honored and distinguished in this world and the Hereafter, and will be among those brought near (to Allah's presence)" (وَجِيهاً فِي الدُّنْيا وَ الْآخِرَةِ وَ مِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ‌).

Key Points

1- In this verse and two other verses, the "Messiah" is referred to as a "Word" (Kalimah). This expression is also found in the books of the New Testament.

There is much discussion among commentators as to why Jesus is called a "Word," but it seems most likely that the reason is the extraordinary birth of the Messiah, which falls under the scope of: "His command, when He intends a thing, is only that He says to it, 'Be,' and it is" (إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ‌). [^1] Or, it may be because before his birth, Allah had given the glad tidings of him to his mother through a word (speech).

Furthermore, the reason for this expression might be that "Word" is used in Quranic terminology to mean "creature," as in: "Say, 'If the sea were ink for [writing] the words of my Lord, the sea would be exhausted before the words of my Lord were exhausted, even if We brought the like of it as a supplement'" (قُلْ لَوْ كانَ الْبَحْرُ مِداداً لِكَلِماتِ رَبِّي لَنَفِدَ الْبَحْرُ قَبْلَ أَنْ تَنْفَدَ كَلِماتُ رَبِّي وَ لَوْ جِئْنا بِمِثْلِهِ مَدَداً). [^2] In this verse, "the words of Allah" refers to His creatures. Since the Messiah (peace be upon him) was one of the great creatures of Allah, the term "Word" was applied to him, which also serves as a response to those who claim the divinity of Jesus.

2- The application of "Messiah" (Al-Masih)—meaning "the Anointer" or "the Anointed"—to Jesus (peace be upon him) may be because he would heal incurable patients by stroking his hand over their bodies by Allah's command. Since this honor was preordained for him from the beginning, Allah named

[^1]: Surah Ya-Sin, Verse 82.

[^2]: Surah Al-Kahf, Verse 109.

him "Messiah" before his birth.

Alternatively, it may be because Allah "anointed" him from impurity and sin, making him pure.

3- In this verse and numerous other verses, the Quran explicitly introduces Jesus as the "son of Maryam" to provide a response to those who claim his divinity. For how can one who is born of a mother and is subject to all the changes of the embryonic period and the transformations of the material world be God? A God who is removed from all changes and alterations.

In the next verse, it points to one of the virtues and miracles of the Messiah (peace be upon him), saying: "He will speak to the people in the cradle and in maturity (middle age), and he is of the righteous" (وَ يُكَلِّمُ النَّاسَ فِي الْمَهْدِ وَ كَهْلًا وَ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ‌).

As will be mentioned in Surah Maryam, to clear herself of the accusation of giving birth to a child without a father, Maryam—by Allah's command—pointed to her newborn infant, Jesus (peace be upon him). At that moment, he began to speak and, with an eloquent and clear tongue, revealed his status as a servant before Allah, as well as his status of prophethood. Since it is impossible for such a magnificent prophet to emerge from a defiled womb, he proved his mother's chastity through this miracle.

It should be noted that the word "Mahd" (cradle) refers to a place prepared for an infant's sleep and rest. It is close to the meaning of "gahvareh" in Persian, with the difference that "gahvareh" implies the concept of swinging and movement, while "Mahd" has a general meaning and includes any place prepared for an infant.

The apparent meaning of the verses in Surah Maryam is that he began to speak in the very first days of his birth—an act that is normally impossible for any infant and was itself a great miracle.

However, speaking in middle age and maturity [^1] is a completely normal matter. Mentioning these two together in the above verse may be an indication that he spoke in the cradle just as he did upon reaching the perfection of his life—measured, meaningful, and calculated words, not childish talk.

There is also the possibility that this expression refers to the fact that the Messiah (peace be upon him), from his birth until he reached maturity, always spoke the truth and took steps in the path of guiding and preaching to the people.

Furthermore, this expression regarding Jesus (peace be upon him) is seemingly a type of prophecy and an indication of his future life. For we know according to history that the Messiah (peace be upon him) never reached old age in this world among the people; rather, he departed from among the people at the age of 33, and Allah raised him to the heavens. According to numerous narrations, he will return among the people during the era of the appearance of Imam al-Mahdi (may Allah hasten his reappearance) (and will speak to them just as he spoke at the beginning of his life).

The expression "of the righteous" (مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ‌) shows that being righteous and worthy is among the greatest honors bestowed upon a human being, as if all human values are gathered within it.

[^1]: "Kahl" refers to a person or thing that has reached the limit of perfection. Since a human reaches the stage of perfection between the ages of thirty to forty or fifty, these years are called the years of "Kuhulah" (maturity). Ages less than that are called "Shabab" (youth) and more than that are called "Shayb" (old age).

Ayah

قَالَتْ رَبِّ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِى وَلَدٌ وَلَمْ يَمْسَسْنِى بَشَرٌ ۖ قَالَ كَذَٰلِكِ ٱللَّهُ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ (٤٧)
She said, ‘My Lord, how shall I have a child seeing that no human has ever touched me?’ He said, ‘So it is that Allah creates whatever He wishes. When He decides on a matter He just says to it ‘‘Be!’’ and it is. (47)

Commentary

How Can I Have a Child Without a Spouse?!

In this verse, the story of Maryam عليه السلام continues. When she heard the glad tidings of the birth of 'Isa عليه السلام, she "said: My Lord! How shall I have a child while no man has touched me" and I have never had a spouse? (قالَتْ رَبِّ أَنَّى يَكُونُ لِي وَلَدٌ وَ لَمْ يَمْسَسْنِي بَشَرٌ).

We know that this world is a world of causes, and Allah has established creation such that every being enters the realm of existence following a series of factors and causes. For example, for the birth of a child, sexual intercourse, marriage, and the union of "sperm" and "ovum" are necessary. Therefore, it is not surprising that Maryam was struck with wonder upon hearing the glad tidings that she would soon have a child.

However, Allah put an end to this wonder and "said: Even so; Allah creates what He wills" (قالَ كَذلِكِ اللَّهُ يَخْلُقُ ما يَشاءُ).

The system of the natural world is the creation of Allah and is subject to His command. Whenever He wills, He can alter this system and create beings through extraordinary causes and factors.

Then, to complete this statement, He says: "When He decrees a matter (and issues the command for its existence), He only says to it: 'Be,' and it is" (إِذا قَضى‌ أَمْراً فَإِنَّما يَقُولُ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ‌).

It is evident that the expression "Be" (کن) is, in reality, an expression of the definitive Will of Allah; otherwise, there is no need for speech. This means that as soon as His Will attaches to something and the command for creation is issued, it is immediately clothed in the garment of existence.

It is noteworthy that regarding the creation of 'Isa, the word "yakhluqu" (He creates) is used in this verse, whereas regarding the creation of Yahya a few verses prior, the expression "yaf'alu" (He does) was used. Perhaps this difference in expression is an allusion to the difference in the creation of these two prophets—one came into being through the ordinary channel and the other through an extraordinary one.

Another point worthy of attention is that at the beginning of these verses, Maryam speaks with the angels, but here we see her speaking with her Lord and hearing the response from Him. It is as if she became so captivated by the Pure Essence of Truth that the intermediaries were removed, and she became entirely connected to the Source of the Universe, saying what she desired and hearing what was necessary without any intermediary. Of course, there is no issue with non-prophets speaking with Allah as long as it is not in the form of the revelation of prophethood (Wahy al-Nubuwwah).

Ayahs

وَيُعَلِّمُهُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ (٤٨)
He will teach him the Book and wisdom, the Torah and the Evangel, (48)
وَرَسُولًا إِلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ أَنِّى قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِـَٔايَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ أَنِّىٓ أَخْلُقُ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلطِّينِ كَهَيْـَٔةِ ٱلطَّيْرِ فَأَنفُخُ فِيهِ فَيَكُونُ طَيْرًۢا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُبْرِئُ ٱلْأَكْمَهَ وَٱلْأَبْرَصَ وَأُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا تَأْكُلُونَ وَمَا تَدَّخِرُونَ فِى بُيُوتِكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٤٩)
and [he will be] an apostle to the Children of Israel, [and he will declare,] “I have certainly brought you a sign from your Lord: I will create for you the form of a bird out of clay, then I will breathe into it, and it will become a bird by Allah’s leave. I heal the blind and the leper and I revive the dead by Allah’s leave. I will tell you what you have eaten and what you have stored in your houses. There is indeed a sign in that for you, should you be faithful. (49)

Commentary

Other Attributes of the Messiah

Following the four attributes mentioned in the previous verses for Prophet Christ عليه السلام (being honored in this world and the Hereafter, being among those brought near to Allah, speaking in the cradle, and being among the righteous), the text points to two other descriptions of this great Prophet, each of which is a combination of a set of important qualities.

First, It says: "And He will teach him the Book and wisdom and the Torah and the Gospel" (وَيُعَلِّمُهُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَالتَّوْرَاةَ وَالْإِنْجِيلَ).

It first refers to the teaching of the Book and wisdom and knowledge in general, and then mentions two clear instances of this Book and wisdom, namely the Torah and the Gospel.

It is evident that individuals appointed by God as leaders of human society must, in the first instance, possess sufficient knowledge and insight and bring with them vibrant and constructive laws and systems. In the second instance, they must present clear evidence and documents of their connection with God, and through these two means, complete and establish their mission of guiding the people. In the above verses, these two meanings are addressed: the first verse spoke of the knowledge, insight, and the Heavenly Book of Prophet Christ عليه السلام, and the second verse refers to his numerous miracles.

It continues: "And [Allah will make him] a messenger to the Children of Israel" (وَرَسُولًا إِلَىٰ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ). [^1] At first glance, it might seem from this sentence that the mission of Prophet Christ عليه السلام was solely to invite the Children of Israel—those who at that time were entangled in various superstitions, moral and ideological corruptions, and severe disputes.
