This does not contradict Christ عليه السلام being among the Ulul-'Azm (Arch-Prophets), because an Ulul-'Azm Prophet is one who possesses a new Divine law, whether his mission is universal or not (in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, a narration is also cited regarding the mission of Prophet Christ عليه السلام being restricted to the Children of Israel). [^1] However, some commentators have stated that the invitation of Prophet Christ عليه السلام was universal and not limited to the Children of Israel, even though the Children of Israel were in the first rank of those he was commissioned to guide. The late Allamah Majlisi in Bihar al-Anwar cites reports in the commentary of Ulul-'Azm whose implication is the universality of the invitation of these Prophets. [^2] Then it adds: He was commanded to tell them: "I have come to you with a sign from your Lord" (أَنِّي قَدْ جِئْتُكُمْ بِآيَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ). [^3] Not just one sign, but multiple signs (therefore, the tanwin [nunation] here is to express the greatness of this sign, not its singularity).

"I design for you from clay that which is like the shape of a bird, then I breathe into it and it becomes a bird by Allah's permission" (أَنِّي أَخْلُقُ لَكُمْ مِنَ الطِّينِ كَهَيْئَةِ الطَّيْرِ فَأَنْفُخُ فِيهِ فَيَكُونُ طَيْرًا بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ).

Since the invitation of the Prophets is in truth toward real life and vitality, the above verse, while describing the miracles of Prophet Christ عليه السلام, first refers to the creation of life in inanimate objects by Allah's command.

The issue of creating life in the entities of the world is not strange if it occurs gradually, for we know that all current living beings have emerged from this very water and soil. The miracle is that Allah gathers those same factors that occurred over thousands or millions of years into one place, and rapidly, a small statue in the shape of a bird is transformed into a living being. This can be a sign of the truthfulness of the claim of the one who brings it regarding his connection with the supernatural world and the infinite power of the Lord.

Then it proceeds to state the second miracle, which is the treatment of incurable diseases or those untreatable through ordinary means: "And I heal the blind and the leper" (وَأُبْرِئُ الْأَكْمَهَ وَالْأَبْرَصَ). [^1] There is no doubt that these matters, especially for the physicians and scholars of 그 time, were undeniable miracles.

In the third stage, it refers to another miracle: "And I give life to the dead by Allah's permission" (وَأُحْيِي الْمَوْتَىٰ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ).

Something that in every age and time is considered among miracles and extraordinary acts. And in the fourth stage, it raises the subject of informing people of their hidden secrets, because every person usually has secrets in their individual and private life that others are unaware of. If someone, without any prior background, precisely reports, for example, the foods people have eaten or what they have stored, it is proof that he has received inspiration from a Divine source of the Unseen. Christ says: "And I inform you of what you eat and what you store in your houses" (وَأُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِمَا تَأْكُلُونَ وَمَا تَدَّخِرُونَ فِي بُيُوتِكُمْ).

And at the end, referring to all these four miracles, it says: "Indeed in that is a sign for you, if you are believers" and are in search of the truth (إِنَّ فِي ذَٰلِكَ لَآيَةً لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ).

[^1]: In this sentence, a verb is implied, such as "He makes him"—other possibilities have also been suggested for this ellipsis. [^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 343. [^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 11, p. 32. [^3]: Here too, a phrase is omitted and is implied as "He spoke to them that I...". [^1]: Some have interpreted "akmah" as blind, and some as a person who cannot see at night, but most commentators and linguists have said it means one born blind, and some have gone further to mean one whose eye sockets are completely smooth and devoid of any trace of an eye.

Key Points

1- Are the Miracles of Christ Strange?

Some commentators (such as the author of Al-Manar) insist on justifying the aforementioned miraculous acts that the Quran explicitly mentions for Christ in some way—for example, by saying that Jesus only claimed he could perform such acts by Allah's command but never actually did them. However, even if this possibility were debatable in this verse, it is entirely unacceptable in verse 110 of Surah Al-Ma'idah: "...and when you designed from clay what was like the shape of a bird..." because this verse explicitly states that one of Allah's favors upon you (Jesus) was that you used to make a bird from clay and breathe into it, and it would become alive by Allah's permission.

Furthermore, insisting on such justifications has no basis or reason, because if the intention is to deny the extraordinary acts of the Prophets, the Quran has explicitly stated this in many instances. Even if we justify one or a few cases, what will happen to the rest?

Beyond this, when we consider Allah as the Ruler over the laws of nature and not subject to them, what obstacle is there for the ordinary laws of nature to change form by His command in exceptional cases and for events to occur through extraordinary means? And if they imagine that this subject is inconsistent with the "Unity of Actions" (Tawhid al-Af'ali) of Allah and His Creatorship and the negation of partners, the Quran has provided the answer, for in all places it conditions the occurrence of these events upon "Allah's permission." This means no one can engage in such acts relying on their own power unless it is by Allah's command and by seeking help from His infinite power, and this is the essence of Monotheism (Tawhid), not polytheism (Shirk).

2- Generative Authority (Wilayah Takwini):

From the content of the above verse and similar verses—which we will refer to under each, God willing—it is understood that the messengers and saints of Allah can, by His command and permission, intervene in the generative world (Takwin) and creation when necessary, and bring about events contrary to habit and the natural flow. This is because the phrases "I heal" (أُبْرِئُ) and "I give life to the dead" (أُحْيِي الْمَوْتَىٰ) and the like, which are mentioned in the first-person verb form, are evidence of these types of acts originating from the Prophets themselves. Interpreting these expressions as the Prophets merely praying, and that their work was only to pray for the realization of these matters and nothing else, is an interpretation without evidence. Rather, the literal meaning of these expressions is that they intervened in the generative world and brought these events into existence.

However, so that no one imagines that the Prophets and saints of Allah have independence of their own or have established a system in opposition to the system of creation, and also to remove the possibility of any polytheism or dualism in creation, the phrase "by Allah's permission" (بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ) and its like have been emphasized in several instances in these verses (in the verse under discussion it is repeated twice, and in verse 110 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, the word "by My permission" is repeated four times). The meaning of "Generative Authority" (Wilayah Takwini) is nothing other than this: that Prophets or Imams, at times of necessity and urgency, perform interventions in the world of creation by the permission of the Lord. This is something higher than "Legislative Authority" (Wilayah Tashri'i), which is the guardianship of people in terms of governance, the dissemination of laws, and invitation and guidance to the straight path.

From what has been said, the answer to those who deny the Generative Authority of the men of God and consider it a type of polytheism becomes clearly evident. No one posits an independent system for the Prophets or Imams against Allah; they perform all these acts by His command and permission. But the deniers of Generative Authority say the work of the Prophets is exclusively the propagation of rulings and invitation toward Allah, and occasionally they use prayer for the occurrence of some generative matters, and beyond this, nothing is within their power. Meanwhile, the above verse and similar verses say otherwise.

Additionally, it is understood from the above verse that at least many of the miracles of the Prophets are acts performed by them themselves, although by Allah's command and by seeking help from Divine power. In fact, it can be said that a miracle is both the work of the Prophets (because it is performed by them) and the work of Allah (because it is performed by seeking help from the Lord's power and His permission).

3- It is noteworthy that the emphasis on Allah's permission is repeated in this verse so that no excuse remains for those who claim divinity for Christ, and people do not mistake him for God. If it was not repeated in the matter of informing of the Unseen, it is due to its clarity.

Ayahs

وَمُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَىَّ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَلِأُحِلَّ لَكُم بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى حُرِّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَجِئْتُكُم بِـَٔايَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (٥٠)
And [I come] to confirm [the truth of] that which is before me of the Torah, and to make lawful for you some of the things that were forbidden you. I have brought you a sign from your Lord; so be wary of Allah and obey me. (50)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۗ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ (٥١)
Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord; so worship Him. This is a straight path.” ’ (51)

Commentary

This is the Straight Path

These verses continue the discourse of Prophet Jesus عليه السلام, and in fact, explain a portion of the objectives of his mission. He says: "I have come to confirm the Torah and to strengthen its foundations and principles" (وَ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيَّ مِنَ‌ التورية).

"And I have also come to make lawful for you some of the things that were forbidden to you (due to injustice and sin), such as the prohibition of camel meat, certain animal fats, and some birds and fish" (وَ لِأُحِلَّ لَكُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي حُرِّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ‌).

This sentence refers to what is mentioned in verse 160 of Surah An-Nisa, where He says: فَبِظُلْمٍ مِنَ الَّذِينَ هادُوا حَرَّمْنا عَلَيْهِمْ طَيِّباتٍ أُحِلَّتْ لَهُمْ‌: "For the wrongdoing of the Jews, We made unlawful for them certain good things which had been lawful for them." However, with the advent of Prophet Jesus عليه السلام and as a reward for believing in this great Prophet, those prohibitions were lifted.

He then adds: "I have brought you a sign from your Lord" (وَ جِئْتُكُمْ بِآيَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ‌).

This is an emphasis on what we read in the previous verse from the tongue of Prophet Jesus عليه السلام regarding his miracles, and it is a summary of that detailed account.

At the end of the verse, he draws this conclusion: "Therefore, fear (disobeying) Allah and obey me" (فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ أَطِيعُونِ‌).

In the next verse, to remove any ambiguity or mistake, and so that his exceptional birth would not be used as a pretext for his divinity, the Quran quotes Prophet Jesus عليه السلام as saying: "Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him (not me, nor anything else); this is the straight path"—the path of Monotheism and the worship of the One, not the path of polytheism or the worship of two or more gods (إِنَّ اللَّهَ رَبِّي وَ رَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُوهُ هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ‌).

In other verses of the Quran, we repeatedly read that Prophet Jesus عليه السلام emphasized his own servitude and status as a servant before Allah. Contrary to what is quoted from Jesus عليه السلام in the current distorted Gospels—where he often uses the word "Father" for God—the Holy Quran quotes him using the word "Rabb" (Lord) and the like. This is evidence of his utmost attention to combating polytheism or the claim of his own divinity. Therefore, as long as Prophet Jesus عليه السلام was among the people, no one dared to introduce him as one of the gods. Even the effects of Jesus's عليه السلام teachings regarding Monotheism did not allow deviants to manifest their polytheistic beliefs until two centuries after him. According to the admission of Christian researchers, the issue of the Trinity and belief in three gods emerged from the third century AD. (Further explanation on this matter will be provided in Volume 4 under verse 171 of Surah An-Nisa).

Ayahs

۞ فَلَمَّآ أَحَسَّ عِيسَىٰ مِنْهُمُ ٱلْكُفْرَ قَالَ مَنْ أَنصَارِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالَ ٱلْحَوَارِيُّونَ نَحْنُ أَنصَارُ ٱللَّهِ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَٱشْهَدْ بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ (٥٢)
When Jesus sensed their faithlessness, he said, ‘Who will be my helpers [on the path] toward Allah?’ The Disciples said, ‘We will be helpers of Allah. We have faith in Allah, and you be witness that we have submitted [to Him]. (52)
رَبَّنَآ ءَامَنَّا بِمَآ أَنزَلْتَ وَٱتَّبَعْنَا ٱلرَّسُولَ فَٱكْتُبْنَا مَعَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ (٥٣)
Our Lord, we believe in what You have sent down, and we follow the apostle, so write us among the witnesses.’ (53)
وَمَكَرُوا۟ وَمَكَرَ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ خَيْرُ ٱلْمَـٰكِرِينَ (٥٤)
Then they plotted [against Jesus], and Allah also devised, and Allah is the best of devisers. (54)

Commentary

The Steadfastness of Christ’s Disciples (peace be upon him)

These verses continue the discussion regarding the mission of Christ (peace be upon him) and the events of his life.

According to the prophecies and glad tidings of Moses (peace be upon him), the Jewish people were awaiting the appearance of Jesus (peace be upon him) before his arrival. However, when he appeared and the illicit interests of a group of deviant Israelites were endangered, only a limited group gathered around Christ (peace be upon him). Those who suspected that following his faith would jeopardize their status, position, and interests refused to accept it.

The first verse refers to this very meaning, stating: "When Jesus (peace be upon him) sensed disbelief (and opposition) from them, he said: 'Who will be my helpers toward Allah (for the propagation of His religion)?'" (فَلَمَّا أَحَسَّ عِيسى‌ مِنْهُمُ الْكُفْرَ قالَ مَنْ أَنْصارِي إِلَى اللَّهِ‌). [^1] Here, only a small group responded positively to this call; these were the pure individuals whom the Quran refers to as the "Hawariyyun" (Disciples). The Hawariyyun (the special students of Christ) said: "We are the helpers of (the religion of) Allah; we have believed in Allah, and bear witness that we have submitted (become Muslims) and surrendered to the religion of truth" (قالَ الْحَوارِيُّونَ نَحْنُ أَنْصارُ اللَّهِ آمَنَّا بِاللَّهِ وَ اشْهَدْ بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ‌).

It is noteworthy that in response to Jesus (peace be upon him), the Disciples did not say "we are your helpers," but rather, to demonstrate the ultimate level of monotheism and sincerity—and so that their words would not carry even a hint of polytheism—they said: "We are the helpers of Allah and we support His religion, and we take you as a witness to this fact." It is as if they also sensed that in the future, deviant individuals would claim divinity for Christ (peace be upon him), and they wanted to provide them with no pretext.

Furthermore, the use of the term "Islam" in the above verse is evidence that Islam has been the religion of all the Prophets.

It was at this point that Christ (peace be upon him) separated the ranks of his sincere friends from the enemies and hypocrites, so that his planning could be precise and cohesive—the same action that the Prophet of Islam performed during the Allegiance of Aqaba.

[^1]: The use of the term "sensed" (احساس), despite disbelief being an internal matter, may be because they were so persistent in expressing their disbelief that it became as if it were a sensory matter—Al-Mizan, under the verse in question.

In the following verse, phrases are quoted that express the ultimate sincerity of the Disciples. After accepting the call of Christ (peace be upon him) and declaring their readiness to cooperate and assist him, they presented their faith to the presence of Allah and said: "Our Lord! We have believed in what You have sent down and we have followed the Messenger (Christ), so write us down among the witnesses" (رَبَّنا آمَنَّا بِما أَنْزَلْتَ وَ اتَّبَعْنَا الرَّسُولَ فَاكْتُبْنا مَعَ الشَّاهِدِينَ‌).

They first declared their faith in what had been revealed, but since faith alone was not sufficient, they brought forward the matter of acting upon the heavenly commands and following Christ (peace be upon him), which was a living testimony of their firm faith. For when faith penetrates the soul and spirit of a human being, it must inevitably be reflected in their actions; without action, it may only be an imaginary faith rather than a real one. Then they requested Allah to record their names among the witnesses who hold the rank of leading the nations in this world and the rank of intercession and witnessing over deeds in the next world.

After describing the faith of the Disciples, the third verse refers to the satanic plots of the Jews, saying: "And they (the Jews and other enemies of Christ, to destroy him and his religion) plotted, and Allah planned (to preserve him and His religion), and Allah is the best of planners" (وَ مَكَرُوا وَ مَكَرَ اللَّهُ وَ اللَّهُ خَيْرُ الْماكِرِينَ‌). It is obvious that Allah's plans precede the plans of everyone else, for they have meager and limited knowledge while Allah's knowledge is infinite; they have negligible power to implement their designs while His power is boundless.

Key Points

1- Who were the Hawariyyun (Disciples)?

"Hawariyyun" is the plural of "Hawari," derived from the root "Haur," meaning to wash and whiten. Sometimes it is applied to anything white; hence, the Arabs call white foods "Hawari," and the maidens of Paradise are called "Huri" because they are fair-skinned or because the whites of their eyes are brilliant (and the pupils are intensely black).

Regarding the reason for naming the students of Christ (peace be upon him) by this name, several possibilities have been suggested. However, what seems closest to the truth and is mentioned in the traditions of the great religious leaders is that, in addition to having pure hearts and serene souls, they strove greatly to purify and enlighten the thoughts of others and to wash people from pollution and sin.

In Uyun Akhbar al-Rida, it is narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (peace be upon him) that he was asked: "Why were the Hawariyyun named by this name?" The Imam (peace be upon him) replied: "A group of people imagine that they were clothes-washers by profession, but according to us, the reason was that they had purified themselves from the pollution of sin and also strove to purify others." [^1]

2- The Disciples in the View of the Quran and the Gospel

The Quran speaks about the "Hawariyyun" in Surah As-Saff, verse 14, and mentions their faith. However, from the sentences found in the Gospels regarding the "Hawariyyun," it is understood that they all had lapses regarding Christ.

In the Gospels of Matthew and Luke (Chapter 6), the names of the Disciples are given as follows:

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 285.

1- Peter 2- Andrew 3- James 4- John 5- Philip 6- Bartholomew 7- Thomas 8- Matthew 9- James son of Alphaeus 10- Simon (called the Zealot) 11- Jude brother of James 12- Judas Iscariot, who betrayed Christ.

The famous commentator, the late Tabarsi, narrates in Majma' al-Bayan [^1] that the "Hawariyyun" used to travel with Jesus, and whenever they became thirsty or hungry, food and water would be prepared for them by Allah's command. They considered this a great honor for themselves and asked Christ if anyone superior to them could be found. He said: "Yes, 'Superior to you is one who works with his hands and eats from his own earning'" (افضل منكم من يعمل بيده و ياكل من كسبه). Following this event, they engaged in washing clothes and taking wages for it (and practically gave a lesson to all people that work and effort are not a cause of shame).

3- What is meant by "Divine Makr" (Planning/Plotting)?

The word "Makr" in the Arabic language differs greatly from what we understand from it in modern Persian. In modern Persian, "Makr" refers to satanic and harmful plots, whereas in the Arabic language, any kind of devising a solution or planning is called "Makr," which is sometimes good and sometimes harmful.

In Raghib's Mufradat, we read: "Al-Makr is to turn another away from what he intends" (whether his intention is good or bad).

In the Holy Quran, "Makir" is sometimes mentioned with the word "Khayr" (Best), such as: "وَ اللَّهُ خَيْرُ الْماكِرِينَ‌" (And Allah is the best of planners), and sometimes "Makr" is mentioned with the word "Sayyi'" (Evil), such as: "وَ لا يَحِيقُ الْمَكْرُ السَّيِّئُ إِلَّا بِأَهْلِهِ‌" (And the evil plot encompasses none but its authors). [^1] Therefore, the meaning of the verse under discussion and several other verses that attribute "Makr" to Allah is that the enemies of Christ wanted to prevent this divine call with their satanic plans, but Allah devised a plan to preserve the life of His Prophet and the progress of His religion, and their plots were neutralized; and likewise in other cases.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1-2, p. 448. [^1]: Surah Fatir, verse 43.

Ayah

إِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَـٰعِيسَىٰٓ إِنِّى مُتَوَفِّيكَ وَرَافِعُكَ إِلَىَّ وَمُطَهِّرُكَ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَجَاعِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوكَ فَوْقَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ فِيمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ (٥٥)
When Allah said, ‘O Jesus, I shall take you[r soul], and I shall raise you up toward Myself, and I shall clear you of [the calumnies of] the faithless, and I shall set those who follow you above the faithless until the Day of Resurrection. Then to Me will be your return, whereat I will judge between you concerning that about which you used to differ. (55)

Commentary

The Return of the Messiah to Allah

This verse continues the verses related to the life of Prophet Jesus عليه السلام. Based on verse 157 of Surah An-Nisa, it is well-known among Islamic commentators that Jesus عليه السلام was never killed (and he escaped the conspiracy plotted against him by the Jews with the cooperation of certain treacherous Christians) and that Allah took him up to heaven. Although Christians, according to the current Gospels, say that Jesus was killed and buried, and then rose from the dead, remained on earth for a short period, and then ascended to heaven [1], the above verse refers to this same meaning, stating: Remember "when Allah said: 'O Jesus, I will take you and raise you to Myself...'" (إِذْ قالَ اللَّهُ يا عِيسى‌ إِنِّي مُتَوَفِّيكَ وَ رافِعُكَ إِلَيَ‌).

Some have imagined that the word "mutawaffika" (متوفيك) is derived from the root "wafāt" (وفات) meaning "death"; for this reason, they think it contradicts the well-known belief among Muslims regarding the non-death of Prophet Jesus and his being alive.

However, this is not the case, because the root "fawt" (فوت) means "to pass away" or "to be lost," but "tawaffi" (توفى) is from the root "wafa" (وفى) meaning "to complete something." The fulfillment of a covenant or promise is called "wafa" because of its completion and execution. For the same reason, if someone receives their debt in full from another, the Arabs say: "tawaffa daynahu" (توفى دينه), meaning "he received his debt in full."

In the verses of the Quran, "tawaffi" has been repeatedly used to mean "taking" or "receiving," such as: "And He it is who takes your souls (yatawaffākum) by night and knows what you have committed by day" [1]. In this verse, the issue of sleep is mentioned as the "tawaffi" of the soul. This same meaning appears in verse 42 of Surah Az-Zumar and other verses of the Quran. It is true that the word "tawaffi" is sometimes used to mean death, and "mutawaffa" to mean the deceased, but even in such cases, it does not literally mean death, but rather the "taking delivery" of the soul. Fundamentally, the concept of death is not inherent in the meaning of "tawaffi," and the root "fawt" is entirely distinct from the root "wafa."

Considering what has been said, the meaning of the verse under discussion becomes clear: Allah says, "O Jesus! I will take you and bring you toward Myself" (of course, if "tawaffi" only meant taking the soul, its implication would be the death of the body).

Then He adds: "and purify you from those who disbelieve" (وَ مُطَهِّرُكَ مِنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا).

The meaning of this purification is either his rescue from the clutches of filthy and faithless individuals, or from false accusations and treacherous conspiracies, which was achieved under the shadow of the victory of his religion. As we read regarding the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله in Surah Al-Fath: "Indeed, We have granted you a clear victory, so that Allah may forgive you your sins of the past and the future."

This means to purify him from the sins that were attributed to him in the past and for which they had prepared the ground for the future.

It is also possible that the meaning of purifying him is the removal of the Messiah عليه السلام from that polluted environment.

Then He adds: "and make those who follow you superior to those who disbelieve until the Day of Resurrection" (وَ جاعِلُ الَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوكَ فَوْقَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلى‌ يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ).

This is a glad tiding that Allah gave to the Messiah and his followers to encourage them in the path they had chosen.

This verse is one of the miraculous verses and the prophecies and news of the unseen in the Quran, stating that the followers of the Messiah عليه السلام will always have superiority over the Jews who opposed the Messiah عليه السلام.

In today's world, we see this reality with our own eyes: that the Jews and Zionists cannot continue their political and social life for even a single day without dependence on and reliance upon Christians. It is clear that "those who disbelieve" (الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا) refers to the group of Jews who disbelieved in the Messiah عليه السلام.

And at the end of the verse, He says: "Then to Me is your return, and I will judge between you concerning that over which you used to differ" (ثُمَّ إِلَيَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ فِيما كُنْتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ‌).

This means that the victories mentioned pertain to this world; the final trial and receiving the results of deeds is something that will come in the Hereafter.

[^1]: Gospel of Mark, Chapter 16; Gospel of Matthew, Chapter 28; Gospel of Luke, Chapter 24; Gospel of John, Chapter 31. [^1]: Surah Al-An'am, Verse 60.

Key Point: Will the religion of the Messiah عليه السلام remain until the end of the world?

A question arises here: according to this verse, Jews and Christians will exist in the world until the brink of the Resurrection, and these two religions will always continue their existence, whereas in the reports and narrations regarding the appearance of Imam Mahdi (may Allah hasten his reappearance), we read that he will bring all religions under his influence. The answer to this question becomes clear by carefully examining the aforementioned narrations. In the narrations regarding the appearance of Imam Mahdi (may Allah hasten his reappearance), we read that no house will remain in the city or desert except that Monotheism (Tawhid) will penetrate it. This means that Islam will encompass the entire world as an official religion, and the government will become an Islamic government, and nothing but the laws of Islam will rule the world. However, there is no obstacle to a minority of Jews and Christians existing under the protection of the government of Imam Mahdi (may Allah hasten his reappearance) under the conditions of "Ahl al-Dhimmah" (protected minorities).

For we know that Imam Mahdi (may Allah hasten his reappearance) does not draw people to Islam through compulsion, but rather proceeds with logic. His recourse to power and military force is for the expansion of justice, the overthrow of oppressive governments, and placing the world under the banner of Islamic justice, not for forcing the acceptance of this religion; otherwise, freedom and choice would have no meaning.

Ayahs

فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَأُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ (٥٦)
As for the faithless, I will punish them with a severe punishment in the world and the Hereafter; and they will have no helpers.’ (56)
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٥٧)
But as for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, He will pay them in full their rewards, and Allah does not like the wrongdoers. (57)
ذَٰلِكَ نَتْلُوهُ عَلَيْكَ مِنَ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ وَٱلذِّكْرِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (٥٨)
These that We recite to you are from the signs and the Wise Reminder. (58)

Commentary

The Fate of the Followers and Opponents of Christ عليه السلام

The first and second verses are a continuation of the address to Prophet Jesus عليه السلام regarding what was mentioned in the previous verse concerning his followers and opponents, while the third verse is addressed to the person of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله.

In the first verse, it states: After the people return to Allah and He judges between them, the ranks will be separated: "As for those who disbelieved (and recognized the truth yet denied it), I will punish them with a severe punishment in this world and the Hereafter, and they will have no helpers" (فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَأُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذاباً شَدِيداً فِي الدُّنْيا وَ الْآخِرَةِ وَ ما لَهُمْ مِنْ ناصِرِينَ).

In this verse, in addition to the punishment of the Hereafter—which is the result of the Lord’s judgment on the Day of Resurrection—reference is also made to a severe punishment in this world that will afflict the disbelievers and the opponents of truth and justice, while no one will have the power to support them.

Then, referring to the second group, He says: "But as for those who believed and did righteous deeds, He will give them their rewards in full" (وَ أَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ). And He emphasizes again: "And Allah does not love the wrongdoers" (وَ اللَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الظَّالِمِينَ).

Placing the fate of the disbelievers before the believers is because those who disbelieved in Christ عليه السلام were the majority.

Furthermore, from the fact that the first verse mentions the punishment of this world, it is clearly understood that the disbelievers (referring here to the Jews) will be afflicted with painful punishments in this very world as well, and the history of the Jewish nation bears witness to this claim.

It is interesting to note that in the first verse, the emphasis is solely on disbelief (kufr), but in the second verse, faith and righteous deeds are mentioned together. This indicates that disbelief alone can be the source of Divine punishment, but faith alone is not sufficient for salvation; rather, it requires righteous deeds as well.

Additionally, the phrase "And Allah does not love the wrongdoers" (وَ اللَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الظَّالِمِينَ) seemingly points to the fact that all branches of disbelief and evil deeds are summarized in "oppression" (zulm) in its broad sense. It is certain that the God who does not love the wrongdoers will never commit injustice against His servants and will grant them their reward in full.

In the final verse, after explaining the story of Christ عليه السلام and a portion of his eventful history in the preceding verses, the address turns to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "This which We recite to you is of the signs [of your truthfulness] and the Wise Reminder" which has been revealed to you in the form of Quranic verses and is free from any falsehood or superstition (ذلِكَ نَتْلُوهُ عَلَيْكَ مِنَ الْآياتِ وَ الذِّكْرِ الْحَكِيمِ).

This is while others have tainted the life story of this great Prophet with a thousand types of false legends, superstitions, and innovations.

Ayahs

إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسَىٰ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ ءَادَمَ ۖ خَلَقَهُۥ مِن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ قَالَ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ (٥٩)
Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was. (59)
ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ فَلَا تَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْمُمْتَرِينَ (٦٠)
This is the truth from your Lord, so do not be among the skeptics. (60)

Occasion of Revelation

As explained in detail at the beginning of the Surah, a large portion of the verses in this Surah were revealed in response to the dialogues with the Christians of Najran. This occurred when they arrived in Medina as a delegation of sixty people, accompanied by several of their leaders and elders, to engage in discussions with the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله.

Among the issues raised in this dialogue was their question to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "To what do you invite us?"

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied: "To the One God, and that I hold the mission from Him to the creation; and that the Messiah عليه السلام is a servant among His servants, who possessed human qualities and ate food like others."

They did not accept this statement and pointed to the birth of Jesus عليه السلام without a father, citing it as evidence of his divinity. The above verses were revealed to answer them, and when they refused to accept the response, He invited them to Mubahala (mutual imprecation), the details of which will follow shortly.

Commentary

Negating the Divinity of the Messiah

As mentioned in the occasion of revelation, these verses refer to those who took the birth of Prophet Jesus عليه السلام without a father as evidence of his sonship to God or his divinity. The first verse says: "Indeed, the example of Jesus to Allah is like that of Adam. He created him from dust; then He said to him, 'Be,' and he was!" (إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسى‌ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ قالَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ‌).

With this short and clear argument, He responds to their claim: if Jesus عليه السلام was born without a father, it is no cause for wonder, nor is it evidence of being the son of God or God Himself. This is because the creation of Adam عليه السلام was even more amazing than this; he was born without both a father and a mother. Then, He makes the heedless understand that every task is easy before the Will of the Truth; it is sufficient for Him to say, "Be," and it becomes.

Fundamentally, "difficult" and "easy" are relative terms for creatures who have limited power. However, for Him whose power is unlimited, the categorization of tasks into difficult and easy has no meaning. For Him, creating a single leaf or creating a forest spanning thousands of kilometers is the same, and the creation of a speck of dust is equal to the creation of the solar system.

In the second verse, to emphasize what was mentioned in the previous verses, He says: "This (which We recite to you regarding Prophet Jesus عليه السلام, the manner of his birth, and his status) is the truth from your Lord, and since it is the truth, never be among the doubters" (الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ فَلا تَكُنْ مِنَ الْمُمْتَرِينَ‌).

Regarding the phrase "الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ" (The truth is from your Lord), commentators have offered two possibilities. First, that the sentence is composed of a subject and a predicate ("The Truth" is the subject, "from your Lord" is the predicate). Thus, the meaning would be that "Truth always originates from the Lord," because truth means reality, and reality is the essence of existence, and all existences spring from His Being. Falsehood is non-existence and void, and is alien to His Essence.

The second possibility is that the aforementioned phrase is the predicate of an omitted subject, which would be "This news." That is: "These reports given to you are all truths from your Lord." Both meanings are consistent with the verse.

Ayah

فَمَنْ حَآجَّكَ فِيهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ فَقُلْ تَعَالَوْا۟ نَدْعُ أَبْنَآءَنَا وَأَبْنَآءَكُمْ وَنِسَآءَنَا وَنِسَآءَكُمْ وَأَنفُسَنَا وَأَنفُسَكُمْ ثُمَّ نَبْتَهِلْ فَنَجْعَل لَّعْنَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٦١)
Should anyone argue with you concerning him, after the knowledge that has come to you, say, ‘Come! Let us call our sons and your sons, our women and your women, our souls and your souls, then let us pray earnestly, and call down Allah’s curse upon the liars.’ (61)

Circumstances of Revelation

It has been stated that this verse and the verses preceding it were revealed concerning the delegation from Najran, consisting of "Aqib," "Sayyid," and a group accompanying them. They came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and asked: "Have you ever seen a child born without a father?" At that moment, the verse "Indeed, the likeness of Jesus near Allah..." was revealed. When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) invited them to Mubahalah [^1], they requested a respite until the following day. After returning to their leaders, the Bishop (their high priest) told them: "Tomorrow, watch Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله); if he comes for the Mubahalah with his children and family, fear engaging in it with him. But if he comes with his companions, then proceed, for he has nothing of substance."

[^1]: "Mubahalah" originally stems from the root "Bahl" (rhyming with Ahl), meaning to release or to remove a bond or constraint from something. For this reason, when an animal is left to itself and its udder is not placed in a bag so that its newborn can drink milk freely, it is called "Bahil." In supplication, "Ibtihal" means entreaty and entrusting the matter to God.

If it is sometimes interpreted as destruction, cursing, or distance from God, it is because leaving and abandoning a servant to their own state leads to these consequences; this is the linguistic root of Mubahalah. In the common conceptual usage derived from the above verse, it means two parties cursing one another. This occurs when individuals debating an important religious issue gather in one place, entreating the threshold of God and asking Him to disgrace and punish the liar.

When the next day arrived, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) came while holding the hand of Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام), with Hasan and Husayn (عليهما السلام) walking in front of him, and Fatimah (عليها السلام) walking behind him. The Christians also came out, led by their Bishop. When he looked and saw the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) arriving with those few individuals, he inquired about them. He was told: "This is his cousin and son-in-law, the most beloved of God's creation to him; these two boys are his daughter's sons by Ali (عليه السلام); and this young lady is his daughter Fatimah (عليها السلام), the dearest of people to him and the closest to his heart..."

Sayyid said to the Bishop: "Step forward for the Mubahalah."

He replied: "No, I see a man who steps into this Mubahalah with total courage, and I fear he is truthful. If he is truthful, by God, not a year will pass while there remains a single Christian on earth who drinks water."

The Bishop said to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله): "O Abu al-Qasim! We will not engage in Mubahalah with you, but rather we will make peace. Settle with us." The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) made peace with them on the condition of two thousand Hullahs (a suit of fine fabric), with the minimum value of each being forty dirhams, and the loaning of thirty suits of armor, thirty spears, and thirty horses, in the event of a conspiracy against the Muslims in the land of Yemen. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) would be the guarantor for these loans until they were returned. A treaty was written to this effect.

In another narration, it is stated that the Christian Bishop told them: "I see faces such that if they were to ask God to uproot the mountains, He would do so. Never engage in Mubahalah with them, or you will perish, and not a single Christian will remain on the face of the earth until the Day of Resurrection." [^1]

Commentary

Mubahalah with the Christians of Najran

Following the previous verses and the arguments presented therein to negate the divinity of Christ (عليه السلام), this verse commands the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "Then whoever argues with you about it after [this] knowledge has come to you - say, 'Come, let us call our sons and your sons, our women and your women, ourselves and yourselves, then supplicate earnestly [together] and invoke the curse of Allah upon the liars.'" (فَمَنْ حَاجَّكَ فِيهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَكَ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ فَقُلْ تَعالَوْا نَدْعُ أَبْناءَنا وَ أَبْناءَكُمْ وَ نِساءَنا وَ نِساءَكُمْ وَ أَنْفُسَنا وَ أَنْفُسَكُمْ ثُمَّ نَبْتَهِلْ فَنَجْعَلْ لَعْنَتَ اللَّهِ عَلَى الْكاذِبِينَ).

It is self-evident that the purpose of "Mubahalah" is not merely for these individuals to gather, curse, and then disperse, as such an act alone would be of no benefit. Rather, the intent is for this curse to be effective, and with the manifestation of its effect, the liars would be afflicted by punishment and thus identified.

In other words, although the effect and result of the Mubahalah are not explicitly stated in this verse, since this act was used as the "final weapon" after "logic and reasoning" failed to have an effect, it proves that the intent was the manifestation of an external consequence of this curse, not just a simple verbal invocation.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, with slight abridgment. This circumstance of revelation appears with minor variations in other commentaries such as Abu al-Futuh Razi, Tafsir al-Kabir, and others. Fakhr Razi claims this narration is agreed upon among scholars of Tafsir and Hadith.

Key Points

1. The Invitation to Mubahalah: A Clear Proof of the Prophet's Truthfulness

In the above verse, Allah commands His Prophet that if anyone disputes with him regarding Jesus after the previous clear arguments and resorts to contention, he should propose "Mubahalah": to bring his children and women, and they should bring theirs, and pray so that Allah may disgrace the liar.

The issue of "Mubahalah" in this form was perhaps unprecedented among the Arabs until then, and it was a path that indicated 100% faith and the sincerity of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) call.

How is it possible for someone who does not believe with his entire being in his connection with the Lord to enter such an arena? To invite his opponents, saying: "Come, let us go together to the threshold of God and ask Him to disgrace the liar, and you will quickly see the result—how Allah punishes the liars." Certainly, entering such a field is extremely dangerous, for if his prayer is not answered and no sign of punishment appears for the opponents, it would result in nothing but the disgrace of the inviter. How could a wise and sensible person take such a step without being certain of the outcome? It is from here that it is said the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) invitation to Mubahalah is one of the signs of the truthfulness of his mission and his decisive faith, regardless of the results that were later obtained from the Mubahalah.

It is recorded in Islamic narrations that when the matter of Mubahalah was raised, the representatives of the Christians of Najran asked the Prophet for time to think about it and to consult with their elders. The result of their consultation, which stemmed from a psychological insight, was that they instructed their people: "If you observe Muhammad coming to the Mubahalah with noise, a large crowd, and commotion, then engage in Mubahalah with him and do not fear, for there is no reality in his work if he resorts to commotion. But if he comes to the meeting place with a very limited number of his closest elite and his young children, know that he is a Prophet of God and avoid Mubahalah with him, for it is dangerous!"

According to the prior arrangement, they went to the meeting place and suddenly saw the Prophet with his children Hasan and Husayn (عليهما السلام) in front of him, and Ali (عليه السلام) and Fatimah (عليها السلام) accompanying him, and he was instructing them: "Whenever I pray, you say 'Amen'." When the Christians witnessed this scene, they were struck with intense terror, refrained from the Mubahalah, and agreed to "peace," submitting to the conditions of Dhimmah and paying the tax (Jizyah).

2. "Mubahalah": A Living Document of the Greatness of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام)

The majority of commentators and traditionists, both Shi'a and Sunni, have explicitly stated that the Verse of Mubahalah was revealed concerning the Ahl al-Bayt of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). The Prophet took only his sons Hasan and Husayn (عليهما السلام), his daughter Fatimah (عليها السلام), and Ali (عليه السلام) to the meeting place. Therefore, the term "Abna'ana" (our sons) in the verse refers exclusively to Hasan and Husayn (عليهما السلام), just as "Nisa'ana" (our women) refers to Fatimah (عليها السلام), and "Anfusana" (ourselves) refers only to Ali (عليه السلام). Numerous hadiths have been narrated in this regard.

However, some Sunni commentators, who are in a clear minority, have attempted to deny the transmission of hadiths in this field. For instance, the author of Tafsir al-Manar says under this verse: "These narrations are all from Shi'a routes, and their goal is clear; they have strived so much in spreading and promoting these hadiths that they have confused the matter even for many Sunni scholars"!!

However, a review of authentic Sunni sources shows that, contrary to the prejudiced notions of the author of al-Manar, many of the chains of these hadiths never end with "Shi'a or Shi'a books." If we were to deny the transmission of these hadiths from Sunni routes, their other hadiths and books would also lose their credibility.

To clarify this reality, we mention a portion of their narrations on this subject with their references:

"Qadi Nurullah Shushtari" in the third volume of the exquisite book Ihqaq al-Haqq (new edition, page 46) states: "Commentators are in consensus on this issue that 'Abna'ana' in the above verse refers to Hasan and Husayn (عليه السلام), 'Nisa'ana' refers to Fatimah (عليه السلام), and 'Anfusana' refers to Ali (عليه السلام)."

Then (in the footnotes of the said book), about sixty "Sunni luminaries" are mentioned who have explicitly stated that the Verse of Mubahalah was revealed concerning the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), and their names and the details of their books are provided in detail from page 46 to 76.

Among the prominent figures from whom this has been narrated are:
