In the book Ghayat al-Maram, it is narrated from Sahih Muslim in the chapter on the "Virtues of Ali ibn Abi Talib" that: One day Mu'awiyah said to Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas: "Why do you not revile and curse Abu Turab (Ali عليه السلام)?!" He replied: "From the moment I remembered three things the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said regarding Ali (عليه السلام), I refrained from this... (one of them was that) when the Verse of Mubahalah was revealed, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) invited only Fatimah, Hasan, Husayn, and Ali (عليه السلام) and then said: Allahumma ha'ula'i ahli: 'O Allah! These are my close elite.'"

The author of Tafsir al-Kashshaf, who is among the great Sunni scholars, says under the verse: "This verse is the strongest proof establishing the virtue of the People of the Cloak (Ahl al-Kisa)."

Shi'a commentators, traditionists, and historians are also universally in consensus regarding the revelation of this verse concerning the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام). In Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, numerous narrations are cited in this regard.

Among them, citing the book Uyun Akhbar al-Rida regarding a debate session organized by Ma'mun in his court, it is written: Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام) said: "Allah has identified the pure ones among His servants in the Verse of Mubahalah and commanded His Prophet: 'Then whoever argues with you about it after [this] knowledge has come to you - say, "Come, let us call our sons..."' and following the revelation of this verse, the Prophet took Ali, Fatimah, Hasan, and Husayn (عليه السلام) with him for Mubahalah... This is a distinction in which no one has preceded the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), a virtue that no human has reached, and an honor that no one possessed before it." [^1]

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 349; Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 289; Tafsir al-Ayyashi, Vol. 1, p. 177; Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 20 (new edition), p. 52 and Vol. 6, p. 652.

3. Answer to a Question

There is a well-known question raised by Fakhr Razi and some others regarding the revelation of the verse for the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام): How is it possible that "Abna'ana" (our sons) refers to Hasan and Husayn (عليه السلام) when "Abna" is a plural form and plural is not used for two people? And how can "Nisa'ana" (our women), which has a plural meaning, be applied only to the Lady of Islam, Fatimah (عليها السلام)? And if "Anfusana" (ourselves) refers only to Ali (عليه السلام), why is it in the plural form?!

Answer:

Firstly—As previously mentioned in detail, the consensus of Islamic scholars and the numerous hadiths that have reached us in many famous and reliable Islamic sources, both Shi'a and Sunni, regarding the revelation of this verse for the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام)—explicitly stating that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) brought no one to the Mubahalah except Ali (عليه السلام), Fatimah (عليها السلام), Hasan, and Husayn (عليه السلام)—serve as a clear context for interpreting the verse. We know that among the contexts that interpret Quranic verses are the Sunnah and the definitive circumstances of revelation.

Therefore, the aforementioned objection does not only target the Shi'a; rather, all Islamic scholars must answer it.

Secondly—Applying the "plural form" to a "singular" or "dual" is not new, and this usage is frequent in the Quran and elsewhere in Arabic literature, and even in non-Arabic languages.

To explain: It often happens that when stating a law or drafting a treaty, the ruling is presented in a general form and in the plural. For example, they might write in a treaty that "the responsibility for its execution lies with the signatories and their children," while one of the parties might have only one or two children. This in no way contradicts drafting the law or treaty in the "plural" form.

In summary: We have two stages: the "stage of the agreement" and the "stage of execution." In the stage of agreement, words are sometimes mentioned in the plural to apply to all instances, but in the stage of execution, the instance might be limited to a single individual. This limitation in the instance does not contradict the generality of the issue.

In other words, the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was obliged, according to the agreement he made with the Christians of Najran, to bring all the specific children and women of his household and all those who were like his own soul with him to the Mubahalah. However, these had no instances other than two sons, one woman, and one man (note carefully).

In addition to this, we have several cases in the Quranic verses where the expression is in the plural form, but its instance, for one reason or another, was limited to a single individual. For example, in this same Surah, verse 173, we read: "Those to whom the people said, 'Indeed, the people have gathered against you, so fear them.'" (الَّذِينَ قالَ لَهُمُ النَّاسُ إِنَّ النَّاسَ قَدْ جَمَعُوا لَكُمْ فَاخْشَوْهُمْ).

In this verse, the word "al-Nas" (the people) refers, according to the explicit statement of a group of commentators, to Nu'aym ibn Mas'ud, who had taken money from Abu Sufyan to frighten the Muslims with the power of the polytheists! Similarly, in verse 181, we read: "Allah has certainly heard the statement of those who said, 'Indeed, Allah is poor, while we are rich.'" (لَقَدْ سَمِعَ اللَّهُ قَوْلَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ فَقِيرٌ وَ نَحْنُ أَغْنِياءُ).

The word "al-ladhina" (those who) in the verse refers, according to a group of commentators, to Huyayy ibn Akhtab or Finhas.

Sometimes the application of a plural word to a singular is also seen as a form of honoring, just as we read concerning Abraham: "Indeed, Abraham was an Ummah (a nation), obedient to Allah..." (إِنَّ إِبْراهِيمَ كانَ أُمَّةً قانِتاً لِلَّهِ) [^1]. Here, the word "Ummah," which is a collective noun, is applied to an individual.

4. Maternal Grandsons are Our Children

Furthermore, it is understood from the Verse of Mubahalah that the children of a daughter are also truly called "Ibn" (son/child), contrary to what was customary in the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), where they considered only the children of sons as their own children, saying: "Our sons are the sons of our sons; but our daughters—their sons are the sons of distant men." Meaning, our children are only our grandsons through our sons, but our grandsons through our daughters are considered the children of strangers, not our own! This way of thinking was the product of the same false tradition in the Arab Jahiliyyah where they did not consider daughters and women as primary members of human society and viewed them as mere vessels for carrying sons! As their poet says: "Mothers of people are but vessels—entrusted [with offspring]—and for lineages, there are only fathers!" But Islam shattered this way of thinking and applied the rulings of "child" equally to both paternal and maternal grandchildren.

In Surah al-An'am, verses 84-85, we read concerning the descendants of Abraham: "And of his descendants, David and Solomon and Job and Joseph and Moses and Aaron. Thus do We reward the doers of good. And Zechariah and John and Jesus and Elias. And all were of the righteous."

In this verse, Prophet Jesus is counted among the descendants of Abraham, even though he was a maternal descendant and essentially had no father.

In the narrations transmitted through both Shi'a and Sunni routes concerning Imam Hasan and Imam Husayn (عليه السلام), the application of the term "Ibn Rasul Allah" (Son of the Messenger of Allah) is frequently seen.

In the verses regarding women with whom marriage is forbidden, we read: "And [prohibited are] the wives of your sons..." (وَ حَلائِلُ أَبْنائِكُمُ) [^1]. Among Islamic jurists, it is an established matter that the wives of sons and grandsons—whether through a daughter or a son—are forbidden to a person and are included in the above verse.

5. Is Mubahalah a General Ruling?

There is no doubt that the above verse does not give a general command for inviting to Mubahalah to all Muslims; rather, the address in it is only to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله). However, this does not prevent Mubahalah from being a general ruling against opponents. Faithful individuals who possess complete piety and devotion to God may, when their arguments against enemies fail due to the enemies' stubbornness, invite them to Mubahalah.

The generality of this ruling is also understood from the narrations cited in Islamic sources. In Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 351, a hadith is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) who said: "If the opponents do not accept your truthful words, invite them to Mubahalah."

[^1]: Surah an-Nahl, verse 120. [^1]: Surah an-Nisa, verse 23.

The narrator says: I asked, "How should I perform Mubahalah?"

He replied: "Refine your character for three days," and I believe he said: "Fast and perform a ritual bath (Ghusl), and go out to the desert with the one you wish to engage in Mubahalah with. Then, interlace the fingers of your right hand with his right fingers and begin with yourself and say: 'O Allah! You are the Lord of the seven heavens and the seven earths, and the Knower of hidden secrets, and the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. If my opponent has denied a truth and claimed a falsehood, send a calamity from heaven upon him and afflict him with a painful punishment!' Then repeat this prayer again and say: 'If this person has denied the truth and claims a falsehood, send a calamity from heaven upon him and afflict him with a punishment!'" Then he said: "It will not be long before the result of this prayer becomes manifest. By Allah, I never found anyone willing to engage in Mubahalah with me in this manner."

Additionally, it is clear from this verse that, contrary to the indiscriminate attacks of those who say "Islam is practically a religion for men and women are not taken into account in it," women stood alongside men against the enemy at sensitive moments, contributing their share to the advancement of Islamic goals. The brilliant pages of the life of Fatimah, the Lady of Islam (عليها السلام), and her daughter Zaynab al-Kubra (عليها السلام), and other women who followed in their footsteps in Islamic history, bear witness to this reality.

Ayahs

إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ ٱلْقَصَصُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۚ وَمَا مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٦٢)
This is indeed the true account, for sure. There is no god but Allah, and indeed Allah is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (62)
فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٦٣)
But if they turn away, indeed Allah knows best the agents of corruption. (63)

Commentary

True Stories

In the aforementioned verses, after explaining the life of Christ, it states as a further emphasis: "Indeed, this is the true narrative of Christ," not claims such as the divinity of Christ or him being the son of God (إِنَّ هذا لَهُوَ الْقَصَصُ الْحَقُّ).

Neither those who claimed his divinity spoke the truth, nor those who—God forbid—called him an illegitimate child. The truth is what you have brought and what you have said: he was a servant of Allah and a Prophet, who was born through a divine miracle from a pure mother without a father.

Again, for further emphasis, He adds: "And there is no deity except Allah, the One" (وَ ما مِنْ إِلهٍ إِلَّا اللَّهُ).

"And indeed, Allah is the Almighty, the All-Powerful, and the All-Wise," and the birth of a child without a father is not a significant matter in the face of His power (وَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ).

Yes, such a Being is worthy of worship, not anyone else.

The word "qasas" (قصص) is singular and means "story" or "narrative." Originally, it is derived from the root "qass" (قص) (rhyming with saff), meaning to search for something. For example, in the story of Moses bin Imran, we read: وَ قالَتْ لِأُخْتِهِ قُصِّيهِ, "The mother of Moses said to his sister: 'Search for him [Moses]'" [1]. The reason blood-retaliation is called "qisas" (قصاص) is that it involves searching for the right of the victim's family. The chronicles and history of predecessors are also called "qissah" (story) because they search for the conditions and events of their lives.

From what has been stated above, it becomes clear that the referent of "hadha" (this) is the narrative of Christ, not the entire Glorious Quran or the collective history of the Prophets.

In the next verse, He threatens those who refuse to accept these truths, saying: "If they turn away (despite all these clear proofs and evidences, then know that they are not seekers of truth and are corrupt and mischief-makers), for indeed, Allah is aware of the mischief-makers" (فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ بِالْمُفْسِدِينَ).

It is certain that a group who, after all these logical arguments of the Quran regarding Christ and also their retreat from the invitation to Mubahalah (mutual imprecation), does not submit to the truth and continues their stubborn disputes, are not "truth-seekers." Rather, they are "mischief-makers" whose goal is to numb the correct beliefs of the people. Certainly, Allah knows them, is aware of their intentions, and will punish them in due time.

[^1]: Surah Al-Qasas, Verse 11.

Ayah

قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ تَعَالَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ كَلِمَةٍ سَوَآءٍۭ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمْ أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ وَلَا نُشْرِكَ بِهِۦ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنَا بَعْضًا أَرْبَابًا مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَقُولُوا۟ ٱشْهَدُوا۟ بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ (٦٤)
Say, ‘O People of the Book! Come to a common word between us and you: that we will worship no one but Allah, that we will not ascribe any partner to Him, and that some of us will not take some others as lords besides Allah.’ But if they turn away, say, ‘Be witnesses that we have submitted [to Allah].’ (64)

Commentary

An Invitation to Unity

In the preceding verses, the Quran first invited the Christians to logical reasoning, and following their opposition, it invited them to Mubahalah (mutual imprecation). Since the invitation to Mubahalah significantly impacted their morale—evidenced by their refusal to engage in it and their acceptance of the conditions of Dhimmah (protected minority status)—the Quran once again utilizes this psychological readiness to resume logical argumentation. However, this reasoning differs greatly from the previous instances.

In the prior verses, the invitation was toward Islam with all its specific tenets, but in this verse, the invitation is toward the common ground between Islam and the creeds of the People of the Book. Addressing the Prophet, it says: "Say: 'O People of the Book! Come to a word that is common between us and you: that we worship none but Allah, and that we associate nothing with Him, and that none of us shall take others as lords besides Allah.'" (قُلْ يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ تَعالَوْا إِلى‌ كَلِمَةٍ سَواءٍ بَيْنَنا وَ بَيْنَكُمْ أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ إِلَّا اللَّهَ وَ لا نُشْرِكَ بِهِ شَيْئاً وَ لا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنا بَعْضاً أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ).

In reality, through this method of reasoning, the Quran teaches us that if some people are not ready to cooperate with you in all sacred goals, strive at least to attract their cooperation in important common objectives and use that as a foundation to advance your holy aims.

The above verse is a call to unity addressed to all heavenly religions. It says to the Christians: You claim "Monotheism" (Tawhid) and even assert that the issue of the "Trinity" (belief in three gods) does not contradict "Monotheism," and thus you believe in unity within the Trinity.

Similarly, the Jews, despite polytheistic statements such as considering "Ezra" (Uzayr) to be the son of God, claimed and continue to claim Monotheism.

The Quran announces to all of them: We and you share the principle of Monotheism; let us join hands to revive this shared principle without any embellishments and refrain from misplaced interpretations that result in polytheism and distance from pure Monotheism.

It is noteworthy that in this verse, the oneness of God is emphasized through three different expressions: first, with the phrase "أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ إِلَّا اللَّهَ" (that we worship none but Allah); second, with the phrase "لا نُشْرِكَ بِهِ شَيْئاً" (that we associate nothing with Him); and for the third time, with the phrase "وَ لا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنا بَعْضاً أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ" (that none of us shall take others as lords besides Allah).

Furthermore, the final phrase is a subtle allusion to the fact that Christ is a human being and of our own kind; he should not be recognized as a deity.

There is also the possibility that some deviant scholars among the People of the Book abused their positions and altered God's lawful (Halal) and prohibited (Haram) matters according to their own desires, and others followed them.

To explain: it is understood from the Quranic verses that among the scholars of the People of the Book, there were those who distorted God's laws according to their "interests" or "prejudices." From the perspective of Islamic logic, anyone who knowingly and unconditionally follows such individuals has performed a type of servitude and worship toward them.

The reason for this stance is clear: legislation and the determination of what is lawful and prohibited belong to Allah. Anyone who considers another person to have authority in this matter has associated them as a partner with Allah.

Commentators have narrated under this verse that "Adi bin Hatim," who was previously a Christian and later embraced Islam, understood the word "Arbaba" (lords) upon the revelation of this verse to mean that the Quran says the People of the Book worship some of their scholars. Thus, he said to the Prophet: "In the past, we never worshipped our scholars!" The Prophet replied: "Did you not know that they would change God's laws according to their desires and you would follow them?"

Adi said: "Yes."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "That is exactly the worship and servitude!" [1] In truth, Islam considers intellectual slavery and colonialism as a form of "servitude and worship," and it fights against intellectual colonialism—which resembles idolatry—with the same intensity that it fights against polytheism and idol worship.

However, it should be noted that "Arbab" is a plural form; therefore, one cannot use this verse alone to prohibit the worship of Jesus. Nevertheless, it is possible that the verse intends to prohibit both the servitude to Christ and the servitude to deviant scholars. Then, at the end of the verse, it states: "If they (after this logical invitation toward the common ground of Monotheism) turn away, then say: 'Bear witness that we are Muslims'" and we are submissive to the Truth while you are not (فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَقُولُوا اشْهَدُوا بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ).

Therefore, your distance from the Truth will not have the slightest effect on our spirit, and we shall continue on our path—the path of Islam. We worship only Allah, we recognize only His laws, and the worship of humans in any shape or form shall not exist among us.

Key Point: The Prophet's Letters to World Leaders

It is gathered from Islamic history that when Islam gained sufficient influence in the land of Hijaz (especially after the revelation of the above verse and the invitation to cooperate in the matter of Monotheism, which is the common denominator of all heavenly religions), the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent numerous letters to the great rulers of that era. In parts of these letters, he specifically emphasized the above verse. Some of these letters are mentioned below due to the importance of the subject and the manner of invitation to this shared principle.

1- Letter to the Muqawqis [1]

"In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. From Muhammad, son of Abdullah, to the Muqawqis, the leader of the Copts. Peace be upon him who follows the guidance. To proceed: I invite you with the call of Islam. Embrace Islam and you will be safe; Allah will grant you your reward twofold. But if you turn away, then upon you is the sin of the Copts. 'O People of the Book! Come to a word that is common between us and you: that we worship none but Allah, and that we associate nothing with Him, and that none of us shall take others as lords besides Allah. But if they turn away, then say: Bear witness that we are Muslims.'"

[1]: "Muqawqis" was the governor of Egypt on behalf of "Heraclius," the Roman Emperor.

"In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. From Muhammad, son of Abdullah, to the Muqawqis, the leader of the Copts. Peace be upon the followers of the Truth. I invite you toward Islam; embrace Islam so that you may remain safe. Allah shall reward you twice (once for your own faith, and another reward for those who follow you and believe). And if you refuse to accept Islam, the sin of the Copts [1] will be upon you. 'O People of the Book! We invite you to a common principle: that we worship none but the one Allah, and that we associate no one with Him, and that none of us shall take others as lords. And whenever they turn away from the religion of Truth, say: Bear witness that we are Muslims.'" [2]

[1]: The Copts were an ethnic group living in Egypt. [2]: Makatib al-Rasul, Vol. 1, p. 97.

When the Muqawqis held the governorship of Egypt and the Prophet of Islam was sending letters to the rulers and dignitaries of the world, inviting them to Islam, he commissioned "Hatib bin Abi Balta'ah" to deliver a letter to the Muqawqis, the leader of Egypt.

The Prophet's envoy set out for Egypt and learned that the governor of Egypt was in Alexandria. The Prophet's envoy صلى الله عليه وآله entered Alexandria using the travel means of that time, reached the palace of the Muqawqis, and delivered the letter to him. The Muqawqis opened the letter, read it, thought for a while, and then said: "If Muhammad is truly the messenger of God, why were his opponents able to expel him from his birthplace, forcing him to reside in Medina? Why did he not curse them so they would be destroyed?"

The Prophet's envoy replied: "Jesus was the messenger of God, and you also testify to his truthfulness. When the Children of Israel plotted to kill him, why did he not curse them so that God would destroy them?!"

The Muqawqis began to praise this logic and said: "Ahsant, anta hakimun min 'indi hakim" ("Well done! You are a wise man coming from a wise person").

Hatib then added: "Before you, someone (meaning Pharaoh) ruled this country who for a long time claimed divinity over the people. God destroyed him so that his life would be a lesson for you; but you should strive so that your life does not become a lesson for others! The Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله invited us to a pure religion. The Quraysh fought him stubbornly, the Jewish community opposed him with specific malice, and the closest people to Islam are the Christians. By my life, just as Moses gave glad tidings of the prophethood of Christ, Christ also gave glad tidings of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله. We invite you toward Islam just as you invited the followers of the Torah to the Gospel. Any nation that hears the call of a true prophet must follow him. I have brought the call of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله to your land; it is fitting that you and the nation of Egypt respond to this call." Hatib bin Abi Balta'ah stayed for a while to receive the reply to the Messenger of Allah's صلى الله عليه وآله letter.

A few days passed. One day, the Muqawqis summoned Hatib to his palace and asked him to provide more explanation about Islam.

Hatib replied: "Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله invites us to worship the one God, and commands the people to connect closely with their Lord five times a day and night, to perform prayers, to fast for one month a year, to visit the House of God (the center of Monotheism), to be faithful to their covenants, and to avoid consuming blood and carrion." He also explained some characteristics of the life of the Prophet of Islam to him.

The Muqawqis said: "These are good signs. I imagined that the Seal of the Prophets would appear from the land of Sham (Levant), which is the land of prophets. Now it has become clear to me that he has been raised from the land of Hijaz."

Then he ordered his scribe to write a letter in Arabic to the Prophet with this content: "To Muhammad, son of Abdullah, from the Muqawqis, the leader of the Copts. Peace be upon you. I have read your letter and understood your purpose, and I have grasped the reality of your invitation. I knew that a prophet would appear, but I imagined he would be raised from the region of Sham. I have honored your envoy." Then, in the letter, he mentioned the gifts he had sent to the Prophet and concluded the letter with the phrase "Peace be upon you." [1] It is recorded in history that the Muqawqis sent about eleven types of gifts to the Prophet, the details of which are recorded in Islamic history.

Among them, he also sent a physician to the Prophet to treat the sick among the Muslims.

The Prophet accepted the gifts but did not accept the physician, saying: "We are a people who do not eat until we are hungry, and we stop eating before we are full, and this is sufficient for our health and hygiene." (And perhaps, in addition to this great hygienic instruction, the Prophet did not feel safe with the physician, who was likely a devout Christian, and did not want to entrust his life and the lives of the Muslims to him).

The fact that the Muqawqis honored the Prophet's envoy, sent gifts to him, and placed the name of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله before his own name in the letter, all indicate that he had inwardly accepted the Messenger of Allah's call, or at least felt an inclination toward Islam, but refrained from openly declaring it so that his position would not be destabilized.

[1]: Makatib al-Rasul, p. 100.

2- Letter to the Caesar of Rome:

"In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. From Muhammad, son of Abdullah, to Heraclius, the leader of Rome. Peace be upon him who follows the guidance. To proceed: I invite you with the call of Islam. Embrace Islam and you will be safe; Allah will grant you your reward twofold. But if you turn away, then upon you is the sin of the Arisiyyin (the Roman peasants/workers). 'O People of the Book! Come to a word that is common between us and you: that we worship none but Allah, and that we associate nothing with Him, and that none of us shall take others as lords besides Allah. But if they turn away, then say: Bear witness that we are Muslims.'"

From Muhammad, son of Abdullah, to Heraclius, the great leader and King of Rome. Peace be upon those who follow the Truth. I invite you to Islam; embrace Islam so that you may be in safety and health, and Allah will give you two rewards.

(One reward for your own faith and another for those who believe by following you). If you turn away from the religion of Islam, the sin of the Arisiyyin (the Roman race and the population of workers) will also be upon you. "O People of the Book! We invite you to a common principle: that we worship none but God, that we associate no one with Him, and that none of us shall take others as lords. Whenever they turn away from the religion of Truth, say: 'Bear witness that we are Muslims.'"

The officer commissioned to deliver the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله message to "Caesar" was a man named "Dihyah al-Kalbi." The Prophet's envoy set out for Rome, but before reaching Constantinople, the center of Caesar's government, he learned that Caesar had left Constantinople for a pilgrimage to Jerusalem (Bayt al-Maqdis). Therefore, he contacted the governor of "Busra," "Harith bin Abi Shamir," and explained his mission to him.

Apparently, the Prophet had also permitted Dihyah to give the letter to the governor of Busra so that he might deliver it to Caesar.

After the Prophet's envoy contacted the governor, the governor summoned "Adi bin Hatim" and commissioned him to go with Dihyah to Jerusalem and deliver the letter to Caesar.

The meeting between the envoy and Caesar took place in the city of "Homs." However, before the meeting occurred, the court officials said: "You must prostrate before Caesar, otherwise he will pay no attention to you." Dihyah, that vigilant man, said: "I have come all this way to strike down these misplaced traditions. I have come from the author of this letter to inform Caesar that the worship of humans must be abolished and none but the one God should be worshipped. With such a belief, how is it possible for me to prostrate to other than God?!" The powerful logic of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله envoy amazed them. One of the courtiers said: "In that case, you can place the letter on the Sultan's special table and leave; no one but Caesar touches the letters on the table." Dihyah thanked him, placed the letter on the table, and returned.

Caesar opened the letter. The beginning of the letter, which started with Bismillah, caught his attention, and he said: "I have not seen such a letter since the letter of Solomon!" Then he called for his translator to read and translate the letter.

The ruler of Rome suspected that the author of the letter might be the same prophet promised in the Gospel and the Torah. He sought to obtain information about his life's characteristics. He ordered a search throughout Sham to find relatives of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله or someone who had information about his affairs. Coincidentally, during those days, Abu Sufyan and a group from the Quraysh had come to Sham (which was part of Eastern Rome) for trade. Caesar's officer contacted them and took them to Jerusalem. Caesar asked them: "Is there anyone among you who has a kinship bond with Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله?"

Abu Sufyan said: "I and Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله are from the same tribe and meet at the fourth ancestor." Then Caesar asked him questions, and he answered accordingly:

1- What is the lineage and status of Muhammad? Abu Sufyan said: "He is from a noble and honorable family."

2- Was there anyone among his ancestors who ruled over the people? - No.

3- Did he avoid lying before he claimed prophethood? - Yes, Muhammad was a truthful man.

4- Which class opposes him and which group supports him? - The noble class opposes him; the ordinary and middle-class people desire him.

5- Has anyone among his followers apostatized and returned from his religion? - No.

6- Are his followers increasing? - Yes.

Then Caesar said to Abu Sufyan and his companions: "If these reports are correct, he is certainly the promised prophet. I knew that such a prophet would appear, but I did not know he would be from the Quraysh. I am ready to humble myself before him and, as a sign of respect, wash his feet (one of the honors common at that time)."

"I predict that his religion and government will take over the land of Rome."

Caesar summoned Dihyah, honored him, wrote a reply to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله letter, and sent a gift along with it to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, reflecting his interest in the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله in that letter. [1]

[1]: Makatib al-Rasul, Vol. 1, p. 109.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِمَ تُحَآجُّونَ فِىٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَتِ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةُ وَٱلْإِنجِيلُ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦٓ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (٦٥)
O People of the Book! Why do you argue concerning Abraham? Neither the Torah nor the Evangel were sent down until [long] after him. Do you not exercise your reason? (65)
هَـٰٓأَنتُمْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ حَـٰجَجْتُمْ فِيمَا لَكُم بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ فَلِمَ تُحَآجُّونَ فِيمَا لَيْسَ لَكُم بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٦٦)
Ah! You are the very ones who argue about that of which you have knowledge. But why do you argue about that of which you have no knowledge? And Allah knows and you do not know. (66)
مَا كَانَ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ يَهُودِيًّا وَلَا نَصْرَانِيًّا وَلَـٰكِن كَانَ حَنِيفًا مُّسْلِمًا وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (٦٧)
Abraham was neither a Jew nor a Christian. Rather, he was a Hanif, a Muslim, and he was not one of the polytheists. (67)
إِنَّ أَوْلَى ٱلنَّاسِ بِإِبْرَٰهِيمَ لَلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوهُ وَهَـٰذَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ وَلِىُّ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٦٨)
Indeed the nearest of all people to Abraham are those who follow him and this prophet and those who have faith, and Allah is the guardian of the faithful. (68)

Circumstances of Revelation

It is mentioned in Islamic narrations that the scholars of the Jews and the Christians of Najran stood before the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to argue and dispute regarding Prophet Abraham (عليه السلام). The Jews claimed: "He was only a Jew," while the Christians claimed: "He was only a Christian." (In this way, each claimed him as their own to establish a great distinction for themselves, as Abraham, the great Prophet of God, was recognized for his greatness among the followers of all religions). The above verses were revealed and refuted them in these baseless claims.

Commentary

The Dispute of the People of the Book Regarding Abraham (عليه السلام)

Continuing the discussions related to the People of the Book, these verses address them directly, stating: "O People of the Book! Why do you argue and dispute concerning Abraham (and each of you claim him as your own), while the Torah and the Gospel were not revealed until after him (and his era was before Moses (عليه السلام) and Christ (عليه السلام))? Do you not reason?" (يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لِمَ تُحَاجُّونَ فِي إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمَا أُنْزِلَتِ التَّوْرَاةُ وَالْإِنْجِيلُ إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ).

Is it reasonable that a preceding Prophet would be a follower of religions that came after him?

In the next verse, He rebukes them through another way, saying: "You are those who have disputed about that of which you had knowledge, but why do you dispute about that of which you have no knowledge?" (هَا أَنْتُمْ هَؤُلَاءِ حَاجَجْتُمْ فِيمَا لَكُمْ بِهِ عِلْمٌ فَلِمَ تُحَاجُّونَ فِيمَا لَيْسَ لَكُمْ بِهِ عِلْمٌ). This points out that you argued regarding matters related to your own religion of which you had knowledge, and you saw what great mistakes you fell into even in those discussions and how far you strayed from the truth (in reality, your knowledge was "compounded ignorance"). Despite this, how can you argue about something you have no information about, and consequently say something that is inconsistent with any history?

Then, to emphasize the previous points and prepare for the upcoming discussion, He says:

"Allah knows and you do not know" (وَاللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنْتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ).

Yes, He knows at what date He revealed His religion to Abraham, not you who came into existence in later times and judge this matter without information or evidence.

Then, He responds to these claimants with total clarity: "Abraham was neither a Jew nor a Christian, but he was a pure monotheist and a Muslim (pure-hearted)" (مَا كَانَ إِبْرَاهِيمُ يَهُودِيًّا وَلَا نَصْرَانِيًّا وَلَكِنْ كَانَ حَنِيفًا مُسْلِمًا). It should be noted that the word "Hanif" (حنيف) comes from the root "Hanaf" (حنف), meaning a person or thing that has inclined toward a certain direction. In the language of the Quran, it refers to someone who has inclined away from false religions toward the true religion.

Here, Allah describes Abraham (عليه السلام) as "Hanif" because it was he who tore through the veils of imitation and prejudice, and in a time and environment drowned in idolatry, he never prostrated before an idol.

However, since the idolaters of the pre-Islamic era of ignorance (Jahiliyyah) also presented themselves as being on the "Hanif" religion of Abraham (عليه السلام), and this claim had become so widespread that the Jews and Christians called them "Hunafa" (thus "Hanif" had acquired a meaning exactly opposite to its original meaning and had become synonymous with idolatry), Allah, after describing Abraham (عليه السلام) as "Hanif" and "Muslim," says: "And he was never of the polytheists" (وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ).

This is to negate any connection between Abraham and the Arab idolaters.

Question

Here it might be said: If we cannot introduce Abraham as a follower of the religion of Moses (عليه السلام) and Christ (عليه السلام), then by greater reason we cannot consider him a Muslim, because he lived thousands of years before the emergence of Islam and the Noble Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)? So why does the Quran introduce him as a Muslim?

Answer

The answer to this question becomes clear from a point we have previously mentioned: "Muslim" in Quranic terminology does not specifically mean the followers of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله). Rather, Islam in the broad sense of the word is submission to the command of Truth and complete monotheism, free from any kind of polytheism and idolatry, of which Abraham (عليه السلام) was the standard-bearer.

Based on what has been said, it is clear that Abraham was not a follower of any of these religions. The only thing that remains here is: who can prove their relationship and bond with the school of Abraham (عليه السلام)—as a badge of honor—or in other words, how can one consider oneself a follower of this great Prophet for whom all followers of divine religions hold greatness? In the last verse under discussion, He addresses this meaning and says: "Indeed, the people most worthy of Abraham are those who followed him and this Prophet (the Prophet of Islam) and those who have believed" (إِنَّ أَوْلَى النَّاسِ بِإِبْرَاهِيمَ لَلَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُ وَهَذَا النَّبِيُّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا).

Therefore, the issue of kinship and lineage, or the issue of race, which followers of various religions mentioned to prove their bond with Abraham, has no value. Guardianship (Wilayah) and connection with the Prophets is only through pure faith in Allah and following their school of thought. In this way, it is proven that the real bond is the ideological bond, whether it be those who lived in his time and followed him (لَلَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُ), or those who showed loyalty to his school and program after him, such as "this Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and his followers" (وَهَذَا النَّبِيُّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا).

How can the People of the Book, with their polytheistic beliefs that have trampled the most fundamental principle of Abraham's (عليه السلام) call—namely monotheism—or the Arab idolaters who stand exactly at the opposite point of Abraham's (عليه السلام) religion, consider themselves followers of Abraham and on his path? Yes, we must confess that the closest people to Abraham are the Prophet of Islam and his followers, who remained loyal to him in the principles and branches of Islam.

And at the end of the verse, He gives glad tidings to those who were true followers of the school of the great Prophets of God that: "And Allah is the Guardian of the believers" (وَاللَّهُ وَلِيُّ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

Key Point: The Most Important Bond is the Ideological Bond

In the above verse, this truth is implicitly stated: that no relationship is higher than the ideological relationship; rather, the connection with the men of God and the Friends of Allah (Awliya Allah) is only through this path.

Therefore, no one can claim a connection with the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Infallible Imams (عليه السلام) except through this path.

In Islamic narrations, this subject is explicitly emphasized. Among them, we read in a Hadith from Ali (عليه السلام):

"Indeed, the people most worthy of the Prophets are those who act most upon what they brought." Then he recited this verse (إِنَّ أَوْلَى النَّاسِ بِإِبْرَاهِيمَ...) and added: "The friend of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) is he who obeys Allah, even if his lineage is distant; and the enemy of Muhammad is he who disobeys Allah, even if his kinship is near." [1]

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 458 (In some editions "A'lamuhum" [the most knowledgeable] and in others "A'maluhum" [those who act most] is narrated; in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, which narrates from Majma', it appears as "A'maluhum" - Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 353).

Ayah

وَدَّت طَّآئِفَةٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَوْ يُضِلُّونَكُمْ وَمَا يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّآ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٦٩)
A group of the People of the Book were eager to lead you astray; yet they lead no one astray except themselves, but they are not aware. (69)

Occasion of Revelation

Some commentators have narrated that a group of Jews were striving to invite prominent and valiant individuals among the pure-hearted Muslims, such as "Mu'adh" and "Ammar" and others, toward their own faith, attempting to turn them back from Islam through satanic whisperings. The above verse was revealed and warned all Muslims regarding this matter [^1]!

Commentary

The Whisperings of the Jews

As mentioned in the occasion of revelation, the enemies of Islam, especially the Jews, spared no effort to distance the new Muslims from Islam. They even harbored such ambitions regarding the special companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, hoping they could turn them away from Islam. Undoubtedly, if they could have influenced one or several of the Prophet's close companions, it would have dealt a great blow to Islam and prepared the ground for the wavering of others.

The above verse, while exposing this plot of the enemies, reminds them to cease their futile efforts, stating: "A group of the People of the Book wished they could lead you astray" (وَدَّتْ طائِفَةٌ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ لَوْ يُضِلُّونَكُمْ) ^1. They were unaware that the training of the Muslims in the school of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was so calculated and conscious that the possibility of apostasy did not exist. They had perceived Islam with their entire being and were enamored by it; therefore, the enemies could not lead them astray.

Rather, as the Quran continues in this verse: "They lead none astray but themselves, and they perceive it not" (وَ ما يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَ ما يَشْعُرُونَ).

This is because by casting doubts and attributing falsehoods to Islam and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, they were nurturing a spirit of pessimism within their own souls. Indeed, one who is intent on fault-finding and nitpicking fails to see the points of strength, and sometimes, due to prejudice and stubbornness, luminous and strong points appear dark and negative in his eyes. For this reason, he distances himself further from the Truth day by day.

The phrase "وَ ما يَشْعُرُونَ" (and they perceive it not) likely refers to this very psychological point: that a person is unconsciously influenced by his own words. When one tries to mislead others through sophistry, lies, and slander, he himself will not remain immune to their effects. These falsehoods gradually impact his soul and spirit to such an extent that they turn into a firm belief; he begins to believe them and becomes lost forever.

[^1]: This occasion of revelation has been mentioned with slight variations in the Tafsir of Abu al-Futuh, Ruh al-Ma'ani, Tafsir al-Kabir, and others.

[^1]: "لو" in the phrase "لَوْ يُضِلُّونَكُمْ" is in the sense of the infinitive "ان" (an), and since "لو" conveys the meaning of wishing in such cases, it is used in its place to be more expressive.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِمَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَأَنتُمْ تَشْهَدُونَ (٧٠)
O People of the Book! Why do you deny Allah’s signs while you testify [to their truth]? (70)
يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِمَ تَلْبِسُونَ ٱلْحَقَّ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ وَتَكْتُمُونَ ٱلْحَقَّ وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٧١)
O People of the Book! Why do you mix the truth with falsehood, and conceal the truth while you know [it]? (71)

Commentary

Why Do You Conceal the Truth?

In continuation of the discussion regarding the destructive activities of the People of the Book mentioned in the previous verse, these two verses address them directly, severely rebuking them for concealing the truth and refusing to submit to it.

First, He says: "O People of the Book! Why do you disbelieve in the verses of Allah while you witness (to their accuracy and truth)?" (يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ لِمَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِآياتِ اللَّهِ وَ أَنْتُمْ تَشْهَدُونَ‌) [^1]. You have read the signs of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله in the Torah and the Gospel and you possess knowledge regarding them; so why do you choose the path of denial?

[^1]: The phrase "تشهدون" (you witness), according to the interpretation mentioned above, means knowledge and awareness—as stated in Majma' al-Bayan and elsewhere. This knowledge and awareness stemmed from the mention of the descriptions of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله in the Torah and the Gospel. However, some have said it refers to the knowledge and awareness of the sufficiency of miracles to prove the prophethood of the Prophet of Islam, and others have said it means that you deny it outwardly but in private gatherings, you bear witness to the truthfulness of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله mission and his legitimacy.

In the second verse, He addresses them once more, saying: "O People of the Book! Why do you clothe the truth with falsehood and obscure it? (so that you may lead people astray and become astray yourselves) and why do you conceal the truth while you know?" (يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ لِمَ تَلْبِسُونَ الْحَقَّ بِالْباطِلِ وَ تَكْتُمُونَ الْحَقَّ وَ أَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ‌).

In reality, in the previous verse, He reprimands them for deviating from the path of truth despite their knowledge and awareness, and in the second verse, for leading others astray.

Under the commentary of verse 42 of Surah Al-Baqarah, which contained the same theme, further discussions on this subject were presented.

Ayahs

وَقَالَت طَّآئِفَةٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَجْهَ ٱلنَّهَارِ وَٱكْفُرُوٓا۟ ءَاخِرَهُۥ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٧٢)
A group of the People of the Book say, ‘Believe in what has been sent down to the faithful at the beginning of the day, and disbelieve at its end, so that they may turn back [from their religion].’ (72)
وَلَا تُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ إِلَّا لِمَن تَبِعَ دِينَكُمْ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلْهُدَىٰ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ أَن يُؤْتَىٰٓ أَحَدٌ مِّثْلَ مَآ أُوتِيتُمْ أَوْ يُحَآجُّوكُمْ عِندَ رَبِّكُمْ ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلْفَضْلَ بِيَدِ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٧٣)
And [they say,] ‘Do not believe anyone except him who follows your religion.’ Say, ‘Indeed [true] guidance is the guidance of Allah.’ [They also say.] ‘[Do not believe] that anyone may be given the like of what you were given, or that he may argue with you before your Lord.’ Say, ‘Indeed all grace is in Allah’s hand; He grants it to whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing. (73)
يَخْتَصُّ بِرَحْمَتِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٧٤)
He singles out for His mercy whomever He wishes, and Allah is dispenser of a great grace.’ (74)

Circumstances of Revelation

Some early commentators have narrated that twelve Jewish scholars from Khaybar and other regions devised a skillful plot to unsettle the faith of some believers. They conspired with one another to visit the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the morning, ostensibly embracing Islam and becoming Muslims, only to turn back from this religion at the end of the day. When questioned why they did so, they were to say: "We observed the qualities of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله up close, but when we referred to our religious books or consulted our religious scholars, we saw that his attributes and way of life do not correspond with what is in our books; therefore, we returned." Their aim was to make some people say: "These individuals are more knowledgeable about the Heavenly Books than us; surely what they say is true," and thereby cause them to waver.

Another circumstance of revelation has also been narrated regarding this verse, but the one mentioned above is closer to the meaning of the verse.

Commentary

A Dangerous Conspiracy!

The above verses unveil another of the destructive plots of the Jews and show that they used every means—military, political, economic, and cultural—to shake the faith of the Muslims. The above verses refer to a part of their cultural invasion.

It says: "A group of the People of the Book said: '(Go and outwardly) believe in what has been revealed to the believers at the beginning of the day and disbelieve at its end (and manifest your disbelief), so that perhaps they—the believers—may also waver and turn back'" (وَ قالَتْ طائِفَةٌ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ آمِنُوا بِالَّذِي أُنْزِلَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَجْهَ النَّهارِ وَ اكْفُرُوا آخِرَهُ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ‌).

Perhaps the purpose of "the beginning and end of the day" is that the interval between your faith and disbelief should be short. This short interval would cause people to say that they thought Islam was something significant, but upon closer inspection, they found it to be something else, and thus they quickly turned away from it.

This conspiracy would have a significant effect on weak-willed individuals, especially since the aforementioned group were Jewish scholars, and everyone knew they possessed complete knowledge of the Heavenly Books and the signs of the Final Prophet. This would, at the very least, shake the foundations of the faith of new Muslims. The phrase "so that perhaps they may turn back" (لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ‌) shows that they were hopeful about the effectiveness of this plan.

However, to ensure they did not lose their own followers, they emphasized that your faith must only be formal: "And do not (truly) believe except in him who follows your religion" (وَ لا تُؤْمِنُوا إِلَّا لِمَنْ تَبِعَ دِينَكُمْ‌). It appears from some commentaries that the "Jews of Khaybar" gave this advice to the "Jews of Medina," lest those who were closer to the Prophet be influenced by him and embrace faith, for a group of them believed that prophethood would only remain within the Jewish race, and if a prophet were to appear, he must be from the Jews.

Some commentators have taken the phrase "do not believe" (لا تُؤْمِنُوا) from the root iman in the sense of "trust" and "confidence" (as this root also carries this meaning). Therefore, the meaning of the above phrase is that this conspiracy must be kept completely confidential and should not be disclosed to anyone other than Jews—not even to the polytheists—lest this secret be revealed and the plan be thwarted. But Allah, the All-Knowing and Aware, pulled back the curtain from their secret and disgraced them, so that it might be a lesson for the believers and a means of guidance for the disbelievers.

Then, in a parenthetical statement which is the word of Allah, He says: "Say: Indeed, guidance is only the guidance of Allah," and these conspiracies of yours are ineffective against it (قُلْ إِنَّ الْهُدى‌ هُدَى اللَّهِ‌).

In this so-called parenthetical sentence placed amidst the words of the Jews, Allah gives them a meaningful and concise response: firstly, guidance is from Allah and is not the monopoly of any specific race or people, and there is no necessity for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to be only from the Jews; and secondly, those who have been included in Divine guidance will not be shaken by these conspiracies.

Returning once more to the continuation of the Jews' words, it says they said: "Never believe that anyone will be given the like of what you were given (of the Heavenly Book), (rather, prophethood is exclusive to you), nor that they can dispute with you before your Lord" (أَنْ يُؤْتى‌ أَحَدٌ مِثْلَ ما أُوتِيتُمْ أَوْ يُحاجُّوكُمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّكُمْ‌). [^1] In this way, it becomes clear that they were afflicted with a strange sense of self-superiority; they considered themselves the best of the world's races and thought that prophethood, as well as intellect, wisdom, logic, and reasoning, belonged solely to them. With this hollow logic, they wanted to claim merit for themselves over others in both aspects.

At the end of the verse, Allah gives them a firm reply and, ignoring them, addresses the Prophet, saying: "Say: Indeed, bounty and grace are in the hand of Allah; He gives it to whom He wills and deems worthy, and Allah is All-Encompassing (possessing vast bounties) and All-Knowing (of those who are worthy)" (قُلْ إِنَّ الْفَضْلَ بِيَدِ اللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ اللَّهُ واسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ‌).

That is, say: Divine bounties, including the high station of prophethood as well as the gift of intellect, logic, and other honors, are all from Him, and He grants them to the deserving. No one has taken a covenant or promise from Him, no one has a kinship or relationship with Him, and He has never restricted His bounties to a specific group.

And in the final verse, for further emphasis, He adds: "Allah singles out for His mercy whom He wills (and deems worthy), and Allah is the possessor of great bounty," and no one can limit His gifts (يَخْتَصُّ بِرَحْمَتِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ اللَّهُ ذُو الْفَضْلِ الْعَظِيمِ‌). [^1] Incidentally, it is understood from this phrase that if Divine grace and bounty include some and not others, it is not because it is limited, but because of the difference in worthiness.

Key Point: Ancient Conspiracies!

The above verses, which were in fact among the miraculous verses of the Quran, unveiled the secrets of the Jews and the enemies of Islam, exposing their skillful plan to unsettle the Muslims of the early era. They became vigilant in light of this and were cautioned against the deceptive whispers of the enemy. However, if we look closely, we see that in our own age and time, the same schemes are implemented in other forms. The enemy's propaganda machines, which are among the most equipped and powerful in the world, are employed in this area, attempting to destroy the foundations of Islamic beliefs in the minds of Muslims, especially the younger generation.

[^1]: At the beginning of this phrase, a clause is implied, which was "and do not believe/accept" (و لا تصدقوا) or "and do not have faith" (و لا تؤمنوا); meaning: do not believe that a Heavenly Book like yours has been given to anyone.

[^1]: "Fadl" (Bounty) refers to anything that is in excess of the necessary amount of blessings and favors, and this has a positive and valuable meaning. However, sometimes it has a censured and rebuked meaning, referring to exceeding the limit of moderation and going toward excess, and it is often used in the form of fudul (plural of fadl), such as "fudul al-kalam," meaning excessive talk.

They use every means and every person in the guise of "scholars, orientalists, historians, natural scientists, journalists, and even cinema actors."

They do not hide the fact that their goal in this propaganda is not to convert Muslims to Christianity or Judaism, but rather their goal is to shake the foundations of Islamic beliefs in the minds of the youth and to make them indifferent toward the glories of their religion and tradition. Today, the Quran still warns Muslims against this current.

Ayahs

۞ وَمِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مَنْ إِن تَأْمَنْهُ بِقِنطَارٍ يُؤَدِّهِۦٓ إِلَيْكَ وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ إِن تَأْمَنْهُ بِدِينَارٍ لَّا يُؤَدِّهِۦٓ إِلَيْكَ إِلَّا مَا دُمْتَ عَلَيْهِ قَآئِمًا ۗ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا۟ لَيْسَ عَلَيْنَا فِى ٱلْأُمِّيِّـۧنَ سَبِيلٌ وَيَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (٧٥)
Among the People of the Book is he who if you entrust him with a quintal will repay it to you, and among them is he who, if you entrust him with a dinar will not repay it to you unless you stand persistently over him. That is because they say, ‘We have no obligation to the non-Jews.’ But they attribute lies to Allah, and they know [it]. (75)
بَلَىٰ مَنْ أَوْفَىٰ بِعَهْدِهِۦ وَٱتَّقَىٰ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٧٦)
Yes, whoever fulfills his commitments and is wary of Allah—Allah indeed loves the Godwary. (76)

Circumstances of Revelation

This verse was revealed concerning two individuals from the Jews: one was trustworthy and righteous, while the other was treacherous and base. The first person was "Abdullah bin Salam," with whom a wealthy man entrusted 1,200 uqiyahs ^1 of gold. Abdullah returned all of it to its owner at the appropriate time, and because of his trustworthiness, Allah praises him in the above verse. The second person is "Phineas bin Azura," to whom a man from the Quraysh entrusted a single dinar. Phineas betrayed that trust. Allah

rebukes him for this breach of trust.

Some have said that the first phrase refers to a group of Christians, while those who committed the breach of trust were the Jews. There is no objection if both are intended, for we know that although most verses of the Quran were revealed regarding specific cases, they possess a general application and, as the saying goes, the specific instance does not restrict the ruling.

Commentary

The Treacherous and the Trustworthy Among the People of the Book

The above verses identify another facet of the People of the Book, for a group of Jews believed they were not responsible for safeguarding the trusts of others; they even felt they had the right to misappropriate them. Their logic was: "We are the People of the Book, and the Prophet of God and His Heavenly Book have been among us; therefore, the property of others holds no sanctity for us." However, not all the People of the Book agreed with this inhumane way of thinking; rather, a group of them considered themselves duty-bound to fulfill the rights of others.

In the first verse under discussion, the Quran refers to both groups, giving each their due. It says: "Among the People of the Book are those who, if you entrust them with a great amount of wealth, they will return it to you; and among them are those who, if you entrust them with a single dinar, they will not return it to you unless you stand over them (and maintain authority over them)" (وَمِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ مَنْ إِنْ تَأْمَنْهُ بِقِنْطَارٍ يُؤَدِّهِ إِلَيْكَ وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ إِنْ تَأْمَنْهُ بِدِينَارٍ لَا يُؤَدِّهِ إِلَيْكَ إِلَّا مَا دُمْتَ عَلَيْهِ قَائِمًا).

"Qintar," as mentioned in the commentary of verse 14 of this same Surah, originally means something solid and firm; subsequently, it was applied to a large amount of wealth. A bridge is called qantarah because of its firmness, and intelligent people are called "qantar" because they possess firm thinking.

The meaning of "qintar" in this verse is abundant wealth, and the meaning of "dinar" is a small amount of wealth.

In any case, the Holy Quran does not condemn all of them because of the wrongdoing of a group among them, and this is an important moral lesson for all Muslims.

Furthermore, it shows that the group who considered themselves permitted and authorized to seize and usurp the property of others accepts no logic except the logic of force and dominance. We see an extensive example of this in today's world with the Zionists. Indeed, this group of Jews is such that they recognize no principle in fulfilling the rights of others except the principle of power. International resolutions, world public opinion, and concepts such as right and justice have no meaning for them. This is, in fact, one of the interesting matters predicted in the Holy Quran in the above verse; for this reason, Muslims have no way to reclaim their rights from them except by acquiring power.

Then, in the continuation of this verse, it explains the logic of this group regarding the usurpation of others' property, saying: "This is because they say: 'We have no obligation toward the Ummiyyun (non-People of the Book)'" (ذَلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا لَيْسَ عَلَيْنَا فِي الْأُمِّيِّينَ سَبِيلٌ).

"Ummiyyun" means unlettered and illiterate individuals, but they meant the Arab polytheists and the Arabs who were generally unaware of reading and writing, or they meant all those who did not benefit from reading the Torah and the Gospel.

Indeed, with this self-superiority and false privilege, they gave themselves the right to misappropriate the property of others under any name or title.

Undoubtedly, this logic was far worse and more dangerous than the act of betrayal itself; for if treacherous individuals consider their action wrong, they have committed at least one sin, but if they consider themselves entitled to do so, they have committed a greater sin.

The Holy Quran, in response to them at the end of this verse, explicitly states: "And they speak a lie against Allah while they know" (وَيَقُولُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ).

They knew well that in their Heavenly Books, they were by no means permitted to betray the trusts of others, yet they fabricated such lies and attributed them to Allah to justify their disgraceful actions.

The next verse, while negating the claim of the People of the Book who said: "لَيْسَ عَلَيْنَا فِي الْأُمِّيِّينَ سَبِيلٌ" (Consuming the property of non-People of the Book is not forbidden for us)—and for this reason, they allowed themselves freedom of action, the same freedom we see today in the actions of many of them who permit themselves any transgression or violation of the rights of others—states: "Nay, but whoever fulfills his covenant and fears Allah (Allah loves him because) indeed, Allah loves the God-fearing" (بَلَى مَنْ أَوْفَى بِعَهْدِهِ وَاتَّقَى فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُتَّقِينَ).

That is to say, the criterion for human superiority and the measure of a person's character and value is the fulfillment of covenants, the absence of betrayal of trust, and piety and God-wariness in general. Indeed, Allah loves such people, not the treacherous liars who permit themselves any usurpation of others' rights and even attribute it to Allah.

Key Points

1- Question:

An objection might be raised here that in Islam, the same ruling is seen regarding the property of foreigners, as Islam permits Muslims to misappropriate their property.

Answer

Attributing such a thing to Islam is undoubtedly a "slander," for among the definitive rulings of Islam mentioned in numerous narrations is that betrayal of trust is not permissible, whether the trust belongs to Muslims or non-Muslims, and even polytheists and idolaters. In a famous hadith from Imam Sajjad عليه السلام, it is narrated that he said:

"You must return the trust; for by the One Who sent Muhammad ﷺ as a Prophet in truth, if the killer of my father, Al-Husayn bin Ali bin Abi Talib عليه السلام, were to entrust me with the sword with which he killed him, I would return it to him." ^1

In another narration from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام, it is narrated that he said:

"Allah never sent a Prophet except that (truthfulness) and (returning the trust) were part of his program, which must be observed toward both the righteous and the wicked." [^2]

Therefore, what was said in the above verse regarding the Jews' action of betraying trust and their logic to justify this betrayal is by no means permitted for Muslims, and they are duty-bound not to betray the trusts of people—without any exception.

says: "Am I not your Lord?" (أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ) "They said: 'Yes (bala)'" ^1. Just as the word "na'am" (نعم) is mentioned in response to an affirmative question, such as: "Have you found what your Lord promised you to be true? They said: 'Yes (na'am)'" [^2].

Ayah

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَشْتَرُونَ بِعَهْدِ ٱللَّهِ وَأَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَا خَلَـٰقَ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَلَا يُكَلِّمُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَلَا يَنظُرُ إِلَيْهِمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وَلَا يُزَكِّيهِمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٧٧)
There shall be no share in the Hereafter for those who sell Allah’s covenant and their oaths for a paltry gain, and on the Day of Resurrection Allah will not speak to them nor will He [so much as] look at them, nor will He purify them, and there is a painful punishment for them. (77)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of Jewish scholars, such as "Abi Rafi," "Hayy bin Akhtab," and "Ka'b bin al-Ashraf," when they saw their social status among the Jews in jeopardy, attempted to distort the signs regarding the final Prophet that existed in the Torah—signs they had personally transcribed in copies of the Torah with their own hands. They even went as far as swearing oaths that those distorted sentences were from Allah! Consequently, the above verse was revealed, issuing a severe warning to them.

A group of commentators has also stated that this verse was revealed concerning "Ash'ath bin Qays," who falsely intended to seize someone else's land. When he prepared to take an oath to support his claim, the above verse was revealed. At that moment, Ash'ath bin Qays became fearful, confessed to the truth, and returned the land to its rightful owner. [^1]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed.

Commentary

The Distorters of Truth

Continuing the discussions regarding the wrongdoings of the Jews and the People of the Book, this verse points to another part of their transgressions, stating: "Indeed, those who exchange the covenant of Allah and their oaths (taken in His Holy Name) for a small price will have no share in the Hereafter" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَشْتَرُونَ بِعَهْدِ اللَّهِ وَ أَيْمانِهِمْ ثَمَناً قَلِيلًا أُولئِكَ لا خَلاقَ لَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ).

Of course, the verse is mentioned in a general sense, even though its occasion of revelation concerns a group of scholars from the People of the Book who exchanged divine covenants and their oaths for a meager material income. In this verse, the Quran mentions five punishments for them. The first is what was mentioned above: that they will have no share of the infinite blessings of the next world. [^1] The second is that "Allah will not speak to them on the Day of Resurrection" (و لا يكلمهم اللَّه).

Furthermore, "He will withhold His look of mercy from them on 그날 and will not look at them" (وَ لا يَنْظُرُ إِلَيْهِمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ). From these expressions, it becomes clear that on that Day, Allah will speak to His believing servants (either directly or through angels)—words that are a source of joy and happiness for them and evidence of His attention and regard for them. Similarly, Allah "looking" at them refers to His special attention and favor, not a look with physical eyes,

[^1]: "Khalaq" (خلاق) is derived from the root "Khalq" (خلق), meaning a portion or share, because a person attains it through their "Akhlaq" (character/morals). This is an allusion to the fact that they possessed no noble character that could secure a share for them.

as some uninformed individuals have imagined.

However, those who exchange divine verses for a material price are neither included in this favor nor addressed by those words.

It is also clear that by Allah "speaking," it does not mean speaking with a tongue, for Allah is pure and sanctified from a body and physical attributes. Rather, it means speaking through heart-felt inspiration or by creating sound waves in space, similar to the words that Musa عليه السلام heard from the tree at Mount Tur.

The expression "a small price" (ثَمَناً قَلِيلًا) does not imply that if they exchanged the divine covenant for a high price, it would be a good deed. Rather, it means that any material price obtained in exchange for these great sins is "small" and insignificant, even if it were a vast kingdom or sovereignty.

Finally, their fourth and fifth punishments are: "Allah will not purify them (from sin), and for them is a painful punishment" (وَ لا يُزَكِّيهِمْ وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

From this, it becomes clear how grave the sin of concealing divine verses, breaking His covenant, and using false oaths is, such that it is threatened with all these spiritual and physical punishments and a total deprivation of divine favors and graces.

Key Point

A point that must be noted here is that the five ominous consequences mentioned in this verse for "breaking covenants" and "false oaths" seemingly refer to the gradual stages of "proximity to" and "distance from" Allah.

One who draws near to Allah and steps upon the carpet of His proximity first becomes the recipient of a series of spiritual blessings. When he gets closer, Allah speaks to him. Getting closer still, Allah casts a look of mercy upon him. And when he is even closer, He purifies him from the effects of the lapses of sin, and as a result, he is saved from painful punishment and immersed in His bounties. However, those who set themselves on the path of breaking covenants and making improper use of the Lord's Name are deprived of all these blessings and graces, retreating stage by stage. Under the commentary of verse 174 of Surah Al-Baqarah, which is similar to this verse in several aspects, further explanations regarding the meaning of the verse have also been provided.

Ayah

وَإِنَّ مِنْهُمْ لَفَرِيقًا يَلْوُۥنَ أَلْسِنَتَهُم بِٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِتَحْسَبُوهُ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَمَا هُوَ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَيَقُولُونَ هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (٧٨)
There is a group of them who alter their voice while reading out a text [that they have themselves authored], so that you may suppose it to be from the Book, though it is not from the Book, and they say, ‘It is from Allah,’ though it is not from Allah, and they attribute lies to Allah, and they know [it]. (78)

Occasion of Revelation

The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan narrates from some sources that this verse was also revealed regarding a group of Jewish scholars who wrote things with their own hands contrary to what was in the Torah concerning the attributes of the Prophet of Islam and attributed them to Allah (and with their tongues, they distorted the truths of the Torah). Abu al-Futuh Razi specifically mentions the names of Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf, Huyayy ibn Akhtab, and some other scholars of theirs in this context.

Commentary

The Scandal of the Distorters

Once again, this verse speaks of another part of the wrongdoings of some of the scholars of the People of the Book. It says: "And indeed, there is among them a party who alter their tongues while reciting the Book so that you may think it is from the Book, while it is not from the Book" (وَ إِنَّ مِنْهُمْ لَفَرِيقاً يَلْوُونَ أَلْسِنَتَهُمْ بِالْكِتابِ لِتَحْسَبُوهُ مِنَ الْكِتابِ وَ ما هُوَ مِنَ الْكِتابِ‌).

The word yalwuna is derived from the root layy (rhyming with hayy), meaning to twist or bend. This expression here is an interesting metaphor for the distortion of Divine words. It is as if, while reciting the Torah, when they reached the attributes of the Prophet of Islam—whose advent was promised in the verses of the Torah—they would change them at that very moment. This act was performed so skillfully that the listener would imagine what they were hearing was the actual text of the Divine verses.

They did not stop at this; rather, they would explicitly say: "This is from Allah, while it is not from Allah" (وَ يَقُولُونَ هُوَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ وَ ما هُوَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ‌).

Then the Quran emphasizes that this was not because they had fallen into an error, but rather "they speak a lie against Allah while they know [it]" (وَ يَقُولُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ وَ هُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

All of this was because they saw their material interests at risk, and for that sake, they committed every sin. Whereas, if they had elevated their horizon of thought and shown sincerity of intention, Allah the Almighty was capable of providing for both their spiritual life and their material needs.

Furthermore, from this verse and the preceding verses, the grave danger of deviant scholars and intellectuals for a nation and a people becomes clear; for this intensification of punishments is evidence of the importance of the position of scholars and the dangers that arise from their deviation.

Ayahs

مَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَن يُؤْتِيَهُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحُكْمَ وَٱلنُّبُوَّةَ ثُمَّ يَقُولَ لِلنَّاسِ كُونُوا۟ عِبَادًا لِّى مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَـٰكِن كُونُوا۟ رَبَّـٰنِيِّـۧنَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تُعَلِّمُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَبِمَا كُنتُمْ تَدْرُسُونَ (٧٩)
It does not behoove any human that Allah should give him the Book, judgement and prophethood, and then he should say to the people, ‘Be my servants instead of Allah.’ Rather [he would say], ‘Be a godly people, because of your teaching the Book and because of your studying it.’ (79)
وَلَا يَأْمُرَكُمْ أَن تَتَّخِذُوا۟ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ وَٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ أَرْبَابًا ۗ أَيَأْمُرُكُم بِٱلْكُفْرِ بَعْدَ إِذْ أَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (٨٠)
And he would not command you to take the angels and the prophets for lords. Would he call you to unfaith after you have submitted [to Allah]? (80)

Circumstances of Revelation

Two accounts have been mentioned regarding the circumstances of revelation for these two verses:

First, a person came to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and stated: "We greet you with 'Salam' just as we do others, but in our view, such respect is not sufficient. We request that you allow us to grant you a special distinction and prostrate to you!" The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) replied: "Prostration is not permissible for anyone other than Allah; respect your Prophet only as a human being, but recognize his right and follow him!"

Second, a Jew named "Abu Rafi," along with the head of the delegation sent from "Najran," came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) one day in Medina and asked: "Do you wish for us to worship you and attribute the status of divinity to you?!" (Perhaps they imagined that the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) opposition to the divinity of Christ was because he himself had no share in this matter; therefore, if they accepted him as a divine figure like Christ, he would cease his opposition. Or perhaps this proposal was a conspiracy to defame the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and divert public opinion from him). However, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "Ma'adh Allah" (Allah forbid) that I should allow anyone other than the One Lord to be worshipped; Allah has never sent me for such a purpose! [^1]

Commentary

Invitation to Worship Other Than Allah is Impossible

These verses continue to negate and correct the false ideas of a group of the People of the Book. Specifically, they remind the Christians that Christ (عليه السلام) never claimed divinity, and whatever has been said about him in this regard are all false attributions given to him later. It also explicitly answers the request of those who wanted to repeat such claims regarding the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) (for the reasons mentioned in the circumstances of revelation). It states: "It is not for a human that Allah should give him the Scripture and authority and prophethood and then he would say to the people, 'Be servants to me rather than Allah'" (مَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَنْ يُؤْتِيَهُ اللَّهُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ ثُمَّ يَقُولَ لِلنَّاسِ كُونُوا عِبَادًا لِي مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ).

Neither the Prophet of Islam nor any other prophet has the right to say such a thing. These types of attributions given to the prophets are all fabricated by ignorant individuals who are far from their teachings. How is it possible for them to receive these great statuses from Allah and yet simultaneously invite people toward polytheism (shirk)?

Then it adds: Rather (what befits his status is to say), be godly people as you have been taught the Scripture and as you have studied, and never worship other than Allah (وَلَكِنْ كُونُوا رَبَّانِيِّينَ بِمَا كُنْتُمْ تُعَلِّمُونَ الْكِتَابَ وَبِمَا كُنْتُمْ تَدْرُسُونَ).

Indeed, the divine messengers never exceeded the stage of servitude and devotion, and they were always more humble before Allah than anyone else. Therefore, it is impossible for them to deviate from the path of monotheism (Tawhid) and invite people to polytheism.

"Rabbaniyyin" is the plural of "Rabbani," referring to someone whose connection with the "Rabb" (Lord) is strong. [^1] Since the word "Rabb" is also applied to one who reforms and nurtures others, the concept of this word in the above verse is that it is never befitting for prophets to invite people to worship them. What is befitting for them is to transform people into divine and godly scholars in the light of teaching divine verses and studying religious truths—individuals who worship none but Allah and invite to nothing but knowledge and wisdom.

From the aforementioned phrase, it is understood that the goal of the prophets was not merely to nurture individuals, but rather to train teachers, mentors, and leaders for the people—those who could illuminate an environment with their knowledge and faith.

In the above verse, the issue of "Ta'lim" (teaching) is mentioned first, followed by the issue of "Ta'allum" (learning) and "studying." The difference between these two words is that "Ta'lim" has a broad and extensive meaning, encompassing any kind of teaching through word and deed to both literate and illiterate individuals. However, "studying" (dars) refers to learning that is done from a book or record; in technical terms, the relationship between the two is "general and specific absolute."

Thus, the goal of the prophets was to nurture godly scholars, social reformers, and knowledgeable, aware, and capable managers and administrators.

The next verse is a completion of what appeared in the previous verse. It says: Just as the prophets did not invite people to worship themselves, they also did not invite them to worship angels or other prophets. It states: "Nor [is it for him] to order you to take the angels and the prophets as lords" (وَلَا يَأْمُرَكُمْ أَنْ تَتَّخِذُوا الْمَلَائِكَةَ وَالنَّبِيِّينَ أَرْبَابًا).

This sentence is, on one hand, a response to the Arab polytheists who imagined the angels to be the daughters of God and attributed a form of lordship to them, while still introducing themselves as followers of the religion of Ibrahim (عليه السلام). On the other hand, it is a response to the Sabians who considered themselves followers of Yahya (عليه السلام) but elevated the status of angels to the point of worship.

It is also a response to the Jews and Christians who introduced Uzair (عليه السلام) or Christ (عليه السلام) as the son of God and attributed a share of lordship to them.

The verse says in response to all of them: It is never possible for a prophet, with that divine knowledge and awareness, to invite people to the lordship of other than Allah.

At the end of the verse, for further emphasis, it adds: "Would he order you to disbelief after you had been Muslims?" (أَيَأْمُرُكُمْ بِالْكُفْرِ بَعْدَ إِذْ أَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ).

Is it possible for a prophet of God to invite to disbelief and allow you to worship him? It is evident that "Islam" here, as in many other cases, is used in its broad sense, meaning submission to the command of God, faith, and monotheism. That is, how is it possible for a prophet to appear and first invite people to faith and monotheism, and then show them the way of polytheism? Or how is it possible for a prophet to blow away the results of the efforts of other prophets who invited to Islam and turn them toward disbelief and polytheism?! The verse also implicitly alludes to the infallibility (Ismah) of the prophets and their lack of deviation from the path of God's command. [^1]

Key Point: Worship of Humans is Forbidden

The above verses, with complete clarity, condemn any form of worship of other than Allah, especially the worship of humans, and nurture the spirit of liberty and independence of personality in man—the very spirit without which one would not be worthy of the name "human."

Throughout history, we know many individuals who, before reaching power, had an innocent appearance and invited people to truth, justice, liberty, and faith.
