However, once the foundations of their power were solidified in society, they gradually changed their path and leaned toward personality cults and inviting people to themselves.

In truth, one of the ways to distinguish "callers to truth" from "callers to falsehood" is this: the callers to truth, at the head of whom were the prophets and Imams, on the day they held the greatest powers, invited people toward sacred religious and human goals—including monotheism and liberty—just as they did in the first days of their mission. But the callers to falsehood, upon reaching power and victory, the first thing that sprouts in their minds is inviting to themselves and encouraging people toward a type of servitude. The flattery and sycophancy of shallow individuals who usually surround them, combined with their own pride and lack of capacity, bring about such a phenomenon.

An interesting Hadith narrated from Ali (عليه السلام) clarifies the true and spiritual character of that Hazrat and serves as evidence for this discussion:

The day the Imam (عليه السلام) reached the land of "Anbar" (one of the border towns of Iraq), a group of peasants, according to a custom they were used to, dismounted from their steeds and rushed toward him (and perhaps prostrated). The Imam (عليه السلام) not only did not approve of this act but became severely upset and shouted at them:

"What is this that you have done?" They replied: "It is a custom of ours by which we honor our leaders." The Imam said: "By Allah! Your leaders do not benefit from this, and you only distress yourselves in your world and bring misery upon yourselves in your hereafter! How loss-making is the hardship followed by Allah's punishment, and how profitable is the ease accompanied by safety from the Fire!" [^1]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1-2, p. 466. [^1]: When the "Ya" of attribution is joined to "Rabb," an "Alif" is added to it, and it is said as "Rabbani." [^1]: In the famous recitation upon which the current text of the Quran is based, the phrase "وَلَا يَأْمُرَكُمْ" is read in the accusative case (with a Fatha on the Ra) and is actually conjoined to the phrase "يُؤْتِيَهُ اللَّهُ" in the previous verse, and "La" is mentioned as an emphasis of the negative "Ma" in the previous verse. Therefore, the sentence becomes: "And it was not for a human... that he should order you to take the angels and the prophets as lords." [^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 37.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ أَخَذَ ٱللَّهُ مِيثَـٰقَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ لَمَآ ءَاتَيْتُكُم مِّن كِتَـٰبٍ وَحِكْمَةٍ ثُمَّ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مُّصَدِّقٌ لِّمَا مَعَكُمْ لَتُؤْمِنُنَّ بِهِۦ وَلَتَنصُرُنَّهُۥ ۚ قَالَ ءَأَقْرَرْتُمْ وَأَخَذْتُمْ عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكُمْ إِصْرِى ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ أَقْرَرْنَا ۚ قَالَ فَٱشْهَدُوا۟ وَأَنَا۠ مَعَكُم مِّنَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ (٨١)
When Allah took a compact concerning the prophets, [He said,] ‘Inasmuch as I have given you [the knowledge] of the Book and wisdom, should an apostle come to you thereafter confirming what is with you, you shall believe in him and help him.’ He said, ‘Do you pledge and accept My covenant on this condition?’ They said, ‘We pledge.’ He said, ‘Then be witnesses, and I, too, am among the witnesses along with you.’ (81)
فَمَن تَوَلَّىٰ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (٨٢)
Then whoever turns away after that—it is they who are the transgressors. (82)

Commentary

The Sacred Covenant

Following the references in the preceding verses regarding the existence of clear signs of the Prophet of Islam in the books of previous prophets, these verses point to a general principle in this regard: that the previous prophets (and subsequently their followers) had entered into a covenant with Allah to humble themselves and submit before the prophets who would come after them. He says: "And (remember) when Allah took a firm covenant from the prophets (and their followers) that: 'Whatever I give you of the Book and wisdom, then there comes to you a messenger confirming what is with you (and whose signs agree with what you possess), you must believe in him and you must help him'" (وَ إِذْ أَخَذَ اللَّهُ مِيثاقَ النَّبِيِّينَ لَما آتَيْتُكُمْ مِنْ كِتابٍ وَ حِكْمَةٍ ثُمَّ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مُصَدِّقٌ لِما مَعَكُمْ لَتُؤْمِنُنَّ بِهِ وَ لَتَنْصُرُنَّهُ‌) [1]. In reality, just as the later prophets and nations are duty-bound to respect the past prophets and their religions and honor their principles, the previous prophets and nations also had such a duty toward the prophets coming after them. In the verses of the Quran, the unity of purpose among God's prophets is repeatedly mentioned, and this verse is a living example of that.

Let us not forget that "Mithaq" (covenant) is originally derived from the root "Wuthuq," meaning trust and confidence in someone; for this reason, firm promises that are a source of confidence are called Mithaq. Of course, taking a covenant from the prophets is coupled with taking a covenant from their followers. The subject of the covenant was that if a prophet comes whose call is harmonious with theirs and whose signs match what is in their heavenly books (thereby proving his truthfulness), they must not only believe in him but also rise to assist him.

Then, for emphasis, He adds: Allah said to them, "Do you acknowledge this? And do you take My firm covenant (burden) upon that?" They said: "We acknowledge it." He said: "Then bear witness, and I am with you among the witnesses."

[1]: Regarding the structure of the phrase "لَما آتَيْتُكُمْ...", some consider "ma" to be a relative pronoun and the subject (mubtada), the "lam" to be for the preparation of an oath (muwatti'ah lil-qasam), and the phrase "لَتُؤْمِنُنَّ بِهِ وَ لَتَنْصُرُنَّهُ‌" to be the predicate (khabar). Others consider "ma" to be a "temporal conditional" (shartiyyah zamaniyyah) and its result (jaza) to be "لَتُؤْمِنُنَّ بِهِ وَ لَتَنْصُرُنَّهُ‌". The second possibility is more compatible with the meaning of the verse and more consistent with what is commonly seen where an oath appears in the result of a condition (the commentary mentioned above is based on this).

(قالَ أَ أَقْرَرْتُمْ وَ أَخَذْتُمْ عَلى‌ ذلِكُمْ إِصْرِي قالُوا أَقْرَرْنا قالَ فَاشْهَدُوا وَ أَنَا مَعَكُمْ مِنَ الشَّاهِدِينَ‌) [1]

In the following verse, the Holy Quran censures and threatens the covenant-breakers, saying: "Then whoever turns away after that (after all these firm covenants and strong pledges) and refuses (to believe in a prophet like the Prophet of Islam whose glad tidings and signs appeared in previous books), they are the defiant (fasiqun) and outside the obedience of Allah's command" (فَمَنْ تَوَلَّى بعد ذلِكَ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ‌). And we know that Allah does not guide such stubborn and prejudiced defiant ones, as stated in verse 80 of Surah At-Tawbah: وَ اللَّهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ‌ ("And Allah does not guide the defiant people"). Whoever is not included in divine guidance, his destiny is Hell and severe divine punishment.

Key Points

1- Is the above verse only about the glad tidings of previous prophets and their covenant regarding the Prophet of Islam? Or does it encompass every prophet sent after another? The appearance of the verse's expressions suggests a general and universal matter, although the Seal of the Prophets is its most prominent instance. This broad and expansive meaning is also consistent with the spirit of Quranic concepts. Therefore, if we see reports explicitly stating that it refers to the Noble Prophet of Islam, it is considered a commentary on the verse and its application to a clear instance, rather than

[1]: "Isr" originally means tying something or holding it with force and dominance. Matters that hinder a person from important tasks are called "Isr," and for this reason, the term is applied to a firm covenant because breaking it deprives and prevents a person from rewards and blessings.

implying exclusivity. Fakhr al-Razi in his commentary narrates from Imam Ali عليه السلام: "When Allah created Adam and the other prophets, He took a covenant from them that whenever Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله was sent, they would believe in him and help him." [1]

2- Given the content of the verse, a question arises: Is it possible for an Ulu al-'Azm (Arch-Prophet) to be sent during the time of another Ulu al-'Azm prophet such that he is duty-bound to follow him? In response to this question, it must be said: As mentioned in the commentary of the verse, the covenant was not taken from the prophets alone, but also from their followers. In reality, the purpose of taking a covenant from the prophets is taking a covenant from their nations and the generations that come after them who witness the era of the next prophet. Furthermore, the prophets themselves, if (hypothetically) they were to witness the future prophets, would believe; meaning that God's prophets are never separate from one another in their goals and calls, and they have no conflict or strife with each other.

3- Another point regarding the verse is that although it is about prophets, it is obviously also true for their successors (awsiya), because their true successors are not separate from them and all pursue one goal. Prophets have always introduced their successors, given glad tidings regarding them, and invited people to believe in and help them. If we see in narrations cited under this verse in our books of commentary or Hadith that the phrase "وَ لَتَنْصُرُنَّهُ‌" (and you must help him) is interpreted regarding Ali عليه السلام and the issue of Wilayah (guardianship) is included in it, it is actually a reference to this very meaning. It should not go unmentioned that the grammatical structure of the above verse has been a subject of discussion among commentators and linguists.

[1]: Tafsir al-Kabir, Vol. 8, p. 115.

4- Obstructive Prejudices

History has shown that followers of a religion are not easily willing to let go of their faith and submit to new prophets sent by Allah. Instead, they stand by the old religion with a particular rigidity and stubbornness and defend it, as if they belong to it and it belongs to them, perceiving its abandonment as the destruction of their identity. Consequently, they accept a new religion only with great difficulty. The source of many religious wars that have occurred throughout history—among the most painful and bitter events in history—has been this dry prejudice and rigidity toward ancient religions.

However, the law of evolution dictates that religions must come one after another to advance humanity on the path of God-consciousness, truth, justice, faith, ethics, humanity, and virtue until they reach the final religion, which is the "Seal of Religions." This is like a child who passes through educational levels one after another until graduation. Obviously, if primary school students become so attached and prejudiced toward the school environment that they refuse to attend high school, the result will be nothing but stagnation and falling behind the caravan of progress. The insistence and emphasis in the above verse regarding taking a firm covenant from the prophets and previous nations concerning future prophets was seemingly to avoid and prevent these very prejudices, rigidities, and stubbornness. Unfortunately, after all this emphasis, we still see that followers of ancient religions do not easily submit to new truths. (Incidentally, regarding why and how Islam is the Seal of Religions and the final faith, we will speak in detail under verse 40 of Surah Al-Ahzab, God willing).

Ayahs

أَفَغَيْرَ دِينِ ٱللَّهِ يَبْغُونَ وَلَهُۥٓ أَسْلَمَ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ طَوْعًا وَكَرْهًا وَإِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُونَ (٨٣)
Do they seek a religion other than that of Allah, while to Him submits whoever there is in the heavens and the earth, willingly or unwillingly, and to Him they will be brought back? (83)
قُلْ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا وَمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَٱلْأَسْبَاطِ وَمَآ أُوتِىَ مُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ وَٱلنَّبِيُّونَ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُمْ وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ (٨٤)
Say, ‘We have faith in Allah and in what has been sent down to us, and what was sent down to Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob and the Tribes, and that which Moses, Jesus and the prophets were given by their Lord. We make no distinction between any of them, and to Him do we submit.’ (84)
وَمَن يَبْتَغِ غَيْرَ ٱلْإِسْلَـٰمِ دِينًا فَلَن يُقْبَلَ مِنْهُ وَهُوَ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٨٥)
Should anyone follow a religion other than Islam, it shall never be accepted from him, and he will be among the losers in the Hereafter. (85)

Commentary

Islam is the Supreme Divine Religion

Up to this point, detailed discussions have been presented in the previous verses regarding former religions. In these verses, the discussion about Islam begins, drawing the attention of the People of the Book and the followers of past religions toward it.

In the first verse, He says: "Do they seek other than the religion of Allah?" while His religion is this very Islam (أَ فَغَيْرَ دِينِ اللَّهِ يَبْغُونَ‌).

He then adds: "While to Him submitted all those within the heavens and the earth, willingly or by compulsion (and they are surrendered to His command), and to Him they shall be returned." Therefore, Islam is the religion of the entire existence and the world of creation (وَ لَهُ أَسْلَمَ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ طَوْعاً وَ كَرْهاً وَ إِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُونَ‌).

Here, the Holy Quran interprets Islam in a broad sense, stating that all those in the heavens and the earth, and all beings existing within them, are Muslims—meaning they are submitted to His command. This is because the spirit of Islam is submission to the Truth; however, one group submits to His "legislative laws" (Tashri'i) by choice (taw'an), while another group submits to His "creative laws" (Takwini) without choice (karhan).

To explain: Allah has two types of commands in the universe. One set of His commands consists of natural and supernatural laws that govern the various beings of this world; all of them are compelled to kneel before them and cannot deviate from these laws for a single moment, and if they were to hypothetically deviate, they might be obliterated. This is one type of "Islam and submission" to Allah's command. Thus, the sunbeams that shine upon the seas, the water vapor that rises from the ocean, the patches of clouds that join together, the raindrops that fall from the sky, the trees that grow because of it, and the flowers that bloom in its light—all are Muslims, because each is submitted to the law that Creation has designated for it.

There is another type of Allah's command called the legislative command, meaning the laws present in divine legislation and the teachings of the Prophets. Submission to these has an "optional" aspect, and only the people of faith, by virtue of their submission to them, are worthy of the name "Muslim." Of course, deviating from these laws ultimately results in no less than deviating from the laws of creation, as this too leads to degradation, backwardness, or destruction.

Since the word "aslama" (submitted) in the above verse refers to the broad meaning of Islam which encompasses both parts, He says: a group submits by choice (taw'an), like the believers, and a group submits by compulsion (karhan), like the disbelievers regarding the creative laws. Therefore, the meaning of the verse is that although the disbelievers have refused to accept Islam regarding one part of Allah's commands, they have been forced to accept the other part; so why do they not submit all at once to all divine laws and the religion of Truth?

Another possibility in the interpretation of the verse, mentioned by many commentators and not contradicting what was said above, is that people of faith turn toward Allah out of desire and choice during times of prosperity and peace, whereas faithless individuals rush toward Him and call upon Him only during times of affliction and overwhelming hardship. Although they may associate partners with Him in normal circumstances, in those difficult and sensitive moments, they recognize and call upon none but Him.

From what has been said, it becomes clear that "man" (whoever) in the phrase "مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ" (whoever is in the heavens and the earth) includes both rational and non-rational beings (and technically, this word, which is specific to rational beings, is applied in a broader sense by way of taghlib [predominance]). "Taw'an" refers to rational and believing beings, and "karhan" refers to disbelievers and non-rational beings.

In the next verse, Allah commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (and all his followers) to have faith in all the teachings of the previous Prophets and messengers, in addition to what was revealed to the Prophet of Islam. He says: "Say, 'We have believed in Allah and in what was revealed to us and what was revealed to Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, and the Descendants (the Prophets of the tribes of Israel), and in what was given to Moses and Jesus and the Prophets from their Lord. We make no distinction between any of them, and we are Muslims [submitting] to Him'" (قُلْ آمَنَّا بِاللَّهِ وَ ما أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْنا وَ ما أُنْزِلَ عَلى‌ إِبْراهِيمَ وَ إِسْماعِيلَ وَ إِسْحاقَ وَ يَعْقُوبَ وَ الْأَسْباطِ وَ ما أُوتِيَ مُوسى‌ وَ عِيسى‌ وَ النَّبِيُّونَ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ لا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِنْهُمْ وَ نَحْنُ لَهُ مُسْلِمُونَ‌).

Yes, we make no distinction between them in terms of their truthfulness; we recognize them all. They were all divine leaders and were all sent for the guidance of creation, and we are submitted to Allah's command in every respect. Thus, we cut off the hands of the dividers by this means.

Finally, in the last verse, as a general conclusion, He says: "And whoever desires other than Islam as religion—never will it be accepted from him, and he, in the Hereafter, will be among the losers" (وَ مَنْ يَبْتَغِ غَيْرَ الْإِسْلامِ دِيناً فَلَنْ يُقْبَلَ مِنْهُ وَ هُوَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ‌). The word "yabtaghi" is from the root "ibtigha," meaning effort and striving, which is used for both worthy and unworthy matters, and in each case, it follows the context present there.

In this way, the transition is made from the general concept of Islam as a universal principle—which is submission to the Truth—to its specific concept, namely the religion of Islam, which is its most complete and perfect example. It states: today, nothing is accepted from anyone except the religion of Islam. While respecting all divine religions, the program for today is Islam. Just as doctoral students, while respecting all the lessons they studied in various educational stages like elementary, middle school, high school, and undergraduate levels, must only follow the high-level lessons of their final stage, and attending to anything else would result in nothing but loss and waste. Those who turn their backs on this religion through misplaced imitation, ignorant prejudice, racial issues, and self-made superstitions will undoubtedly suffer loss and ruin, gaining nothing but regret and remorse for the capital of their lives and existence that they have squandered.

A group of commentators has said that this verse was revealed concerning twelve hypocrites who had declared faith and then apostatized and returned from Medina to Mecca. The verse was revealed to announce to them that whoever accepts other than Islam is a loser. [1] In a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said: "On the Day of Resurrection, deeds will be brought one by one (to the Divine Court). Each will introduce itself; one will say, 'I am Prayer,' another will say, 'I am Fasting,' and so on. Then Islam will be brought and it will say: 'O Lord! You are Peace (Salam) and I am Islam.' Allah will say: '...Today, by means of (opposition to) you, I shall take to account, and by you, I shall reward.' Allah says in His Book: وَ مَنْ يَبْتَغِ غَيْرَ الْإِسْلامِ دِيناً فَلَنْ يُقْبَلَ مِنْهُ وَ هُوَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ." [2] Regarding the difference between Islam and Iman (faith), the characteristics of each, and the reconciliation between this verse and the verses related to faith in Surah Al-Hujurat under verse 14, it will be discussed, Allah willing.

[^1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh al-Razi, Vol. 3, p. 100. [^2]: Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur, under the verse in question (summarized), Vol. 2, p. 48.

Ayahs

كَيْفَ يَهْدِى ٱللَّهُ قَوْمًا كَفَرُوا۟ بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِهِمْ وَشَهِدُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱلرَّسُولَ حَقٌّ وَجَآءَهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٨٦)
How shall Allah guide a people who have disbelieved after their faith and [after] bearing witness that the Apostle is true, and [after] manifest proofs had come to them? Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (86)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ جَزَآؤُهُمْ أَنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ لَعْنَةَ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ وَٱلنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ (٨٧)
Their requital is that there shall be upon them the curse of Allah, the angels, and all mankind. (87)
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا لَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ (٨٨)
They will remain in it [forever], and their punishment will not be lightened, nor will they be granted any respite, (88)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٨٩)
except such as repent after that and make amends, for Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (89)

Occasion of Revelation

One of the Ansar (the Muslims of Medina) named "Harith bin Suwayd" stained his hands with the blood of an innocent man named "Muhadhdhir bin Ziyad." Fearing punishment, he apostatized from Islam and fled to Mecca (and eleven of his followers who had become Muslims also apostatized). After entering Mecca, he became deeply regretful of his action and fell into thought about what to do regarding this situation. Finally, it occurred to him to send someone to his relatives in Medina to ask the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله whether there was a way for him to return or not.

The above verses were revealed, announcing the acceptance of his repentance under specific conditions. Harith bin Suwayd came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, embraced Islam again, and remained loyal to Islam until his last breath (however, the other eleven followers who had turned back from Islam remained in their state). [^1] In the commentary Al-Durr Al-Manthur and some other commentaries, other occasions of revelation have been narrated for the above verses, which do not differ significantly from what we have narrated.

Commentary

The Penalty for Apostasy

In the previous verses, the discussion was about the religion of Islam, which is the only acceptable religion before Allah. In these verses, the discussion is about those who accepted Islam and then turned away from it, who are termed "apostates" (murtad) in technical language.

He says: "How shall Allah guide a people who disbelieved after their belief and [after] they had borne witness that the Messenger is true and clear signs had come to them? And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people." (كَيْفَ يَهْدِي اللَّهُ قَوْماً كَفَرُوا بَعْدَ إِيمانِهِمْ وَ شَهِدُوا أَنَّ الرَّسُولَ حَقٌّ وَ جاءَهُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ وَ اللَّهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ‌).

Why does Allah not guide them? The reason is clear: they recognized the Prophet through clear signs and bore witness to his messengership; therefore, in returning and deviating from Islam, they are in fact wrongdoers and oppressors (zalim). One who knowingly commits oppression and wrongdoing is not worthy of Divine guidance; he has destroyed the grounds for guidance within himself.

The meaning of "clear signs" (bayyinat) in the above verse refers to the Noble Quran and the other miracles of the Prophet of Islam.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1-2, p. 471.

And the meaning of "wrongdoer" (zalim) here is one who, in the first instance, has wronged himself by choosing the path of apostasy, and in the second instance, has become the cause of misguiding others.

Then He adds: "Those—their recompense is that upon them is the curse of Allah and the angels and the people, all together." (أُولئِكَ جَزاؤُهُمْ أَنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ لَعْنَةَ اللَّهِ وَ الْمَلائِكَةِ وَ النَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ‌). "Curse" (la'n) originally means expulsion and driving away accompanied by anger and wrath. In the case of Allah, it means distancing someone from His mercy. As for the angels and the people, it means anger, hatred, and spiritual rejection, or requesting Allah to distance such individuals from His mercy.

Indeed, these wrongdoing apostates who have knowingly trodden this path become so immersed in corruption and sin that they become the object of hatred for all rational and purposeful beings in the universe, including humans and angels; rather, even higher than that, they become the object of the wrath and anger of the Lord.

In the next verse, He adds: "Abiding eternally therein. The punishment will not be lightened for them, nor will they be reprieved." (خالِدِينَ فِيها لا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ الْعَذابُ وَ لا هُمْ يُنْظَرُونَ‌). In fact, if this curse and expulsion were not eternal, or if it were eternal but gradually lightened, or if at least a respite were given to them, it would be easier to bear. But none of these apply to them; their punishment is painful, eternal, non-reducible, and without any respite. In reality, it is neither cut off from the beginning, nor from the end, nor does its intensity decrease.

In the final verse, He opens the way of return to these individuals and allows them to repent, because the goal of the Quran everywhere is reform and education. One of the most important means of reform and education is opening the way of return for the wicked and the tainted. He says: "Except for those who repent after that and correct themselves [and seek to compensate] (for their repentance is accepted), for indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful." (إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تابُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ وَ أَصْلَحُوا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌).

This verse, like many other verses of the Quran, after raising the issue of repentance with the phrase "and correct themselves" (wa aslahu), conveys the truth that repentance is not merely regret for the past and a decision to abandon sin in the future. Rather, the condition for the acceptance of repentance is that one compensates for previous ugly deeds with good deeds in the future. Therefore, in some verses of the Quran, after mentioning repentance, faith and righteous deeds are mentioned, such as the above verse and verse 60 of Surah Maryam which says: إِلَّا مَنْ تابَ وَ آمَنَ وَ عَمِلَ صالِحاً: "Except for him who repents, believes, and does righteous work." Otherwise, the repentance is not a complete repentance.

In fact, it can be inferred from this expression that sin creates a deficiency in a person's faith, which after repentance must be remedied by renewing faith so that this deficiency is removed.

Key Point: Is the Repentance of an "Apostate" Accepted?

An "apostate" (murtad)—meaning someone who accepted Islam and then turned back from it—is of two types: the "innate apostate" (murtad fitri) and the "national apostate" (murtad milli). An innate apostate refers to someone born to a Muslim father or mother, or according to some, someone whose father or mother was Muslim at the time of their conception, and who then accepted Islam and later turned away from it. However, a national apostate refers to someone not born to Muslim parents, but who accepted Islam themselves after reaching maturity and then turned away from it.

The repentance of a national apostate is accepted, and in truth, his punishment is light because he was not born into a Muslim family. But in the case of the "innate apostate," the ruling is more severe and harsher. Although his repentance is accepted in reality and before Allah, if his status is proven in an Islamic court, he will be sentenced to death, his property will pass as inheritance to his Muslim heirs, and his wife will be separated from him. Even his repentance cannot prevent these severe rulings! However, as we mentioned, this strictness is only regarding the innate apostate, and only if he is a man.

Some might be surprised by this strictness and consider it a kind of severe, inflexible violence that is incompatible with the spirit of Islam.

But in reality, this ruling has a fundamental philosophy: the preservation of the internal front of the Islamic country and the prevention of its disintegration and the infiltration of foreigners and hypocrites. For apostasy is, in fact, a kind of uprising against the regime of the Islamic country, the punishment for which in many laws of today's world is also death. If individuals were allowed to declare themselves Muslim whenever they wished and resign whenever they did not, the internal front of Islam would soon disintegrate, the way for the infiltration of enemies and their agents and hands would be opened, and severe chaos would emerge throughout the Islamic society. Therefore, the aforementioned ruling is actually a political ruling necessary for the preservation of the government and the Islamic society and for combating foreign hands and agents.

Furthermore, someone who abandons a religion like Islam after research and acceptance and goes toward other religions usually has no correct or justified motive, and therefore deserves heavy punishments. And if we see that this ruling is lighter regarding women, it is because all punishments are lightened in their case.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِهِمْ ثُمَّ ٱزْدَادُوا۟ كُفْرًا لَّن تُقْبَلَ تَوْبَتُهُمْ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلضَّآلُّونَ (٩٠)
Indeed those who turn faithless after their faith, and then advance in faithlessness, their repentance will never be accepted, and it is they who are the astray. (90)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَمَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ فَلَن يُقْبَلَ مِنْ أَحَدِهِم مِّلْءُ ٱلْأَرْضِ ذَهَبًا وَلَوِ ٱفْتَدَىٰ بِهِۦٓ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ (٩١)
Indeed those who turn faithless and die while they are faithless, a world of gold will not be accepted from any of them should he offer it for ransom. For such there will be a painful punishment, and they will have no helpers. (91)

Occasion of Revelation

Some have stated that the first verse was revealed concerning the People of the Book who had believed in the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) before his mission but disbelieved in him after he was sent. Others have said it was revealed regarding "Harith bin Suwayd" and eleven of his companions who had apostatized from Islam. However, Harith felt regret and repented, while—as mentioned in the occasion of revelation for the previous verse—his eleven companions remained in their state and did not return. In response to Harith's invitation, they told him: "We will stay in Mecca and continue our activities against Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله), waiting for his defeat. If we achieve our goal, all the better; otherwise, the path of repentance is open, and whenever we return, (he will accept us and) that which was revealed regarding Harith will be revealed regarding us." When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) conquered Mecca, some of them entered Islam and their repentance was accepted. However, regarding those who died in a state of disbelief, the second verse was revealed. [^1]

Commentary

Futile Repentance

In the preceding verses, the discussion was about those who regretted their deviant path and performed sincere repentance, and thus their repentance was accepted. However, this verse speaks of those whose repentance is not accepted. These are the people who first believed, then disbelieved, and persisted and insisted upon their disbelief. For this reason, they are never ready to follow the commands of Truth unless matters become difficult for them and they see no way out except obedience, repentance, and submission. Certainly, Allah will not accept the repentance of such individuals.

He says: "Indeed, those who disbelieved after their belief and then increased in disbelief (and persisted in this path), never will their repentance be accepted (because it is done out of necessity), and they are the ones astray," for they have lost both the way to God and the way to repentance (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بَعْدَ إِيمانِهِمْ ثُمَّ ازْدادُوا كُفْراً لَنْ تُقْبَلَ تَوْبَتُهُمْ وَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الضَّالُّونَ‌).

Their repentance is merely superficial, for when they see the victory of the supporters of Truth, they express regret and repentance out of compulsion. Naturally, such a repentance is not accepted.

Another possibility mentioned in the commentary of this verse is that such persons regret and repent only when they find themselves on the threshold of death and the end of their lives.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1-2, page 472, under the verse in question.

And we know that at that hour, the doors of repentance are closed, and such a repentance will not be accepted. In fact, this verse is similar to what appears in Surah An-Nisa, verse 18, which states: "And of no effect is the repentance of those who continue to do evil deeds until death faces one of them and he says: 'Now I repent.'"

A third interpretation mentioned for the verse is that it refers to repenting from ordinary sins while remaining in a state of disbelief. For example, if someone persists in his disbelief but repents from sins such as injustice, backbiting, and immorality, his repentance is useless. This is because washing superficial pollutions from the soul and heart while severe and deep pollutions remain will not be effective.

It is worth noting that the three aforementioned interpretations are not contradictory, and it is possible that the verse refers to all such futile repentances, although the first interpretation is more consistent with the preceding verses and the occasion of revelation.

In the following verse, following the reference to futile repentances in the previous verse, it speaks of futile ransom. It says: "Indeed, those who disbelieved and died while they were disbelievers, never would the (whole) capacity of the earth in gold be accepted from any one of them if he would (seek to) ransom himself with it" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ ماتُوا وَ هُمْ كُفَّارٌ فَلَنْ يُقْبَلَ مِنْ أَحَدِهِمْ مِلْ‌ءُ الْأَرْضِ ذَهَباً وَ لَوِ افْتَدى‌ بِهِ‌). It is clear that disbelief blows away all of a person's good deeds; even if the entire earth were full of gold and they spent it in the way of Allah, it would not be accepted. And most certainly, if such a thing were in their possession on the Day of Resurrection and they offered it, it would not be accepted.

Obviously, the meaning of this expression (the whole earth being full of gold and they spend it) is that no matter how great their spending might be, it is useless in the presence of the pollution of the soul and spirit and enmity toward the Truth. Otherwise, it is clear that if the entire earth were filled with gold, at that time, the value of gold would be equal to dust. Therefore, the above sentence is a metaphor to express the vast extent of spending and giving.

Regarding whether this spending refers to this world or the next, commentators have offered two possibilities. However, it is possible that the verse refers to both: whether they spend the entire world during their lifetime or offer it as a ransom in the Hereafter, it is not accepted. Although the literal appearance of the verse indicates it pertains to the next world, as it says: "those who disbelieved and died while they were disbelievers," meaning that after death in a state of disbelief, if hypothetically the greatest wealth of the world were at their disposal and they imagined they could use wealth to ward off Divine punishment as they do in this world, they are gravely mistaken. This financial penalty and ransom will have no effect whatsoever on their punishment. In fact, the theme of the verse is similar to what is stated in Surah Al-Hadid, verse 15:

فَالْيَوْمَ لا يُؤْخَذُ مِنْكُمْ فِدْيَةٌ وَ لا مِنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا: "So today no ransom shall be taken from you (hypocrites) nor from those who disbelieved."

At the end of the verse, He points to another note, saying: "Those will have a painful punishment, and they will have no helpers" (أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ وَ ما لَهُمْ مِنْ ناصِرِينَ‌).

This means that not only do ransom and spending not benefit them, but the intercession of intercessors will also not include them. This is because intercession has conditions, the most important of which is faith in Allah. For this reason, if all the intercessors of the Day of Resurrection gathered to intercede for a faithless person, it would not be accepted. Fundamentally, since intercession is by the permission of Allah, they would never intercede for such unworthy individuals, as intercession itself requires a level of worthiness, and Divine permission does not extend to the unworthy.

O Lord! Grant us the success to repent and return to You before the doors of repentance are closed.

O God! Grant us the worthiness to be included in the intercession of the pure and the righteous.

O God! Do not deprive us of a good end, which is among Your greatest blessings.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds.

Ayah

لَن تَنَالُوا۟ ٱلْبِرَّ حَتَّىٰ تُنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا تُحِبُّونَ ۚ وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِن شَىْءٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِهِۦ عَلِيمٌ (٩٢)
You will never attain piety until you spend out of what you hold dear, and whatever you may spend of anything, Allah indeed knows it. (92)

Commentary

A Sign of Faith

لَنْ تَنالُوا الْبِرَّ حَتَّى تُنْفِقُوا مِمَّا تُحِبُّونَ The word "birr" (righteousness/goodness) originally means "vastness," which is why vast deserts are called "barr" (with a fatha on the 'b'). Consequently, good deeds whose results are extensive and reach others are called "birr" (with a kasra on the 'b'). The linguistic difference between "birr" and "khayr" (good) in Arabic is that "birr" refers to an act of goodness performed with attention, intention, and choice, whereas "khayr" is applied to any type of good done to another, even if it is without specific attention.

The above verse states: "You shall never attain the reality of righteousness (birr) unless you spend in the way of Allah from that which you love."

Regarding what is meant by the word "birr" here, commentators have much discussion. Some have interpreted it as Paradise, some as piety and God-wariness (taqwa), and others as a goodly reward. However, what can be inferred from the verses of the Quran is that "birr" has a broad meaning, encompassing all goodness, including faith and pure deeds. As understood from Verse 177 of Surah Al-Baqarah, "faith in Allah, the Last Day, the Prophets, helping the needy, prayer, fasting, fulfilling covenants, and steadfastness in the face of hardships and events" are all considered branches of "birr."

Therefore, reaching the station of the truly righteous has many conditions, one of which is spending from the wealth that a person cherishes. This is because true love for Allah, and respect for the principles of humanity and morality, become clear when a person stands at a crossroads: on one side lies wealth, status, or a position that he dearly loves, and on the other side lie Allah, truth, human emotions, and righteousness. If he bypasses the former for the sake of the latter, it becomes evident that he is sincere in his love. If he is only willing to sacrifice minor things in this path, it shows that his spiritual love is of the same low level. This is a scale for measuring faith and character.

The Influence of Quranic Verses on the Hearts of Muslims

The influence of Quranic verses on the hearts of Muslims was so swift and deep that the effects would manifest immediately after their revelation. As an example regarding the above verse, we read the following in Islamic histories and commentaries:

  1. One of the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله named Abu Talhah al-Ansari owned a date grove and garden in Medina that was very lush and beautiful, so much so that everyone in Medina spoke of it. In it was a spring of clear water; whenever the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله visited that garden, He would drink from that water and perform ablution. Furthermore, that garden provided a good income for Abu Talhah. After the revelation of the above verse, he came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "You know that the most beloved of my properties is this garden, and I wish to spend it in the way of Allah so that it may be a treasure for my Resurrection." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: بخ بخ ذلك مال رابح لك "Bravo! Bravo! This is a wealth that will be profitable for you." Then He said: "I deem it appropriate that you give it to your needy relatives." Abu Talhah acted upon the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله instruction and distributed it among his kinsmen. [1]

  2. One day, a guest arrived at the home of Abu Dharr. Abu Dharr, who lived a simple life, apologized to the guest, saying: "Due to certain preoccupations, I cannot personally attend to you. I have some camels in such-and-such a place; please take the trouble to go and bring the best of them (so I may sacrifice it for you)." The guest went and brought back a lean camel. Abu Dharr said to him: "You have betrayed me; why did you bring such a camel?" He replied: "I thought that one day you would be in need of the other camels." Abu Dharr said: "The day of my need is the time I close my eyes to this world (how much better to store it for that day)." Allah says: لَنْ تَنالُوا الْبِرَّ حَتَّى تُنْفِقُوا مِمَّا تُحِبُّونَ [2]

  3. Zubaydah, the wife of Harun al-Rashid, possessed a very expensive Quran decorated with gold and jewels, which she loved dearly. One day, while reciting from that very Quran, she reached the verse: لَنْ تَنالُوا الْبِرَّ حَتَّى تُنْفِقُوا مِمَّا تُحِبُّونَ. Upon reading the verse, she fell into deep thought and said to herself: "Nothing is more beloved to me than this Quran, and I must spend it in the way of Allah." She sent for jewelers, sold its decorations and jewels, and spent the proceeds in the deserts of Hijaz to provide water for the desert-dwellers. It is said that even today, the remains of those wells exist and are named after her.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Sahih Muslim, and Bukhari. [2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 2, p. 474.

وَ ما تُنْفِقُوا مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ بِهِ عَلِيمٌ At the end of the verse, to draw the attention of those who spend, He says: Whatever you spend in the way of Allah—be it little or much, from cherished wealth or otherwise—Allah is fully aware of it all. Therefore, it will never be lost, nor will its quality be hidden from Him.

Ayahs

۞ كُلُّ ٱلطَّعَامِ كَانَ حِلًّا لِّبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ إِلَّا مَا حَرَّمَ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ مِن قَبْلِ أَن تُنَزَّلَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةُ ۗ قُلْ فَأْتُوا۟ بِٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ فَٱتْلُوهَآ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٩٣)
All food was lawful to the Children of Israel except what Israel had forbidden himself before the Torah was sent down. Say, ‘Bring the Torah, and read it, if you are truthful.’ (93)
فَمَنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٩٤)
So whoever fabricates a lie against Allah after that—it is they who are the wrongdoers. (94)
قُلْ صَدَقَ ٱللَّهُ ۗ فَٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ حَنِيفًا وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (٩٥)
Say, ‘Allah has spoken the truth; so follow the creed of Abraham, a Hanif, and he was not one of the polytheists. (95)

Occasion of Revelation

It is understood from narrations and the accounts of commentators that the Jews raised two other objections in their discussions with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. First, how could the Prophet of Islam consider camel meat and milk permissible (halal) while they were supposedly forbidden (haram) in the creed of Abraham عليه السلام? For this reason, the Jews, following Abraham, considered them forbidden for themselves. They claimed that not only Abraham but also Noah had prohibited these things; thus, how could someone who does not consider them forbidden claim to follow the creed of Abraham?!

The second objection was: how could the Prophet of Islam consider himself faithful to the creed of the great Prophets of God, especially Abraham عليه السلام, while all the Prophets from the lineage of Isaac, the son of Abraham, honored "Bayt al-Maqdis" (Jerusalem) and prayed toward it, yet the Prophet of Islam turned away from that Qiblah and chose the Kaaba as his direction of prayer?! The above verses respond to the first objection and expose their lies, while the upcoming verses will address the second objection.

Commentary

كُلُّ الطَّعامِ كانَ حِلًّا لِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ إِلَّا ما حَرَّمَ إِسْرایلُ عَلى‌ نَفْسِهِ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ تُنَزَّلَ التَّوْراةُ:

This verse, with complete clarity, rejects the slanders of the Jews regarding the prohibition of certain pure foods (such as camel milk and meat) and states: In the beginning, all these foods were permissible for the Children of Israel, except what Israel (Israel is another name for Jacob) had forbidden for himself.

Regarding what type of food Jacob had forbidden for himself and the reason for that prohibition, no explanation is mentioned in the verse. However, it appears from Islamic narrations that whenever Jacob ate camel meat, the pain of sciatica [1] would intensify for him.

Therefore, he decided to abstain from eating it forever. His followers

[1]: "Irq al-Nisa" (Sciatica) is a type of nerve ailment known today as "sciatica," which causes severe discomfort in the nerves of the lower back and legs, sometimes depriving a person of the ability to move.

followed his example in this matter, and gradually the issue became confused for some. They imagined this was a Divine prohibition and attributed it to Allah as a religious decree. In the above verse, the Quran explains the cause of their mistake and makes it clear that attributing this matter to Allah is a slander.

Therefore, before the revelation of the Torah, none of the pure foods were forbidden to the Children of Israel, as indicated by the phrase "before the Torah was revealed" (مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ تُنَزَّلَ التَّوْراةُ) in the verse above. Although, after the revelation of the Torah and the coming of Moses son of Amram, certain pure foods were forbidden to them as a punishment due to the injustice and transgression of the Jews.

قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِالتَّوْراةِ فَاتْلُوها إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ In this phrase, Allah commands His Prophet to invite the Jews to bring the very Torah present with them and read it, so it may become clear that their claim regarding the prohibition of foods is incorrect. However, they were not willing to do this because they knew such a thing did not exist in the Torah.

فَمَنِ افْتَرى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ Now that they have refused to bring the Torah and their fabrication against Allah has been proven, they must know that those who forge lies against Allah are wrongdoers. They wrong themselves by subjecting themselves to Divine punishment and retribution, and they wrong others by diverting them from the right path through lies and deception.

The Current Torah and the Prohibition of Certain Meats

In the current Torah, in the Book of "Leviticus," Chapter 11, while describing forbidden and permissible meats, we read: "Nevertheless these shall ye not eat of them that chew the cud, or of them that divide the hoof: as the camel, because he cheweth the cud, but divideth not the hoof; he is unclean unto you."

From the above sentences, it is understood that the Jews considered camel meat and other non-cloven-hoofed animals

forbidden, but there is no indication whatsoever of their prohibition in the creed of Abraham and Noah. It is possible that this part was among those things forbidden to the Jews as a punishment.

قُلْ صَدَقَ اللَّهُ فَاتَّبِعُوا مِلَّةَ إِبْراهِيمَ حَنِيفاً وَ ما كانَ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ Now that you see I am truthful and sincere in my call, follow my creed, which is the same pure and unadulterated creed of Abraham, for he was "Hanif"—meaning he inclined from false religions toward the Truth—and in his instructions, even regarding pure foods, there was no deviant ruling or baseless prohibition. He was never among the polytheists, and the claim of the Arab polytheists that they follow his creed is completely meaningless; where stands the "idol-worshipper" and where stands the "idol-breaker"?

It is noteworthy that the Quran repeatedly emphasizes the phrase that Abraham was not among the polytheists [1]. This is because, as we previously mentioned, the polytheists of the Age of Ignorance claimed to follow the creed of Abraham and had gone so far in this claim that others identified them as "Hunafa" (followers of Abraham)! Therefore, the Quran repeatedly negates this notion.

[1]: The expression وَ ما كانَ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ is seen in Surah Al-Imran 67-95, Al-An'am 161, Al-Nahl 124, and Al-Baqarah 135.

Ayahs

إِنَّ أَوَّلَ بَيْتٍ وُضِعَ لِلنَّاسِ لَلَّذِى بِبَكَّةَ مُبَارَكًا وَهُدًى لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٩٦)
Indeed the first house to be set up for mankind is the one at Bakkah, blessed and a guidance for all nations. (96)
فِيهِ ءَايَـٰتٌۢ بَيِّنَـٰتٌ مَّقَامُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ۖ وَمَن دَخَلَهُۥ كَانَ ءَامِنًا ۗ وَلِلَّهِ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ حِجُّ ٱلْبَيْتِ مَنِ ٱسْتَطَاعَ إِلَيْهِ سَبِيلًا ۚ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَنِىٌّ عَنِ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٩٧)
In it are manifest signs [and] Abraham’s Station, and whoever enters it shall be secure. And it is the duty of mankind toward Allah to make pilgrimage to the House—for those who can afford the journey to it—and should anyone renege [on his obligation], Allah is indeed without need of the creatures. (97)

Commentary

The First House of the People

إِنَّ أَوَّلَ بَيْتٍ وُضِعَ لِلنَّاسِ لَلَّذِي بِبَكَّةَ مُبارَكاً As mentioned under the previous verses, the Jews raised two objections against the Prophet of Islam. The response to the first objection was given in the preceding verses, and the response to the second objection—concerning the virtue of Jerusalem (Bayt al-Maqdis) and its alleged superiority over the Kaaba—is provided in these verses.

In the above verse, the Quran states that if the Kaaba has been chosen as the Qibla for Muslims, it is not surprising, for it is the first house of Monotheism and the most ancient temple existing on earth. No center prior to it was a place for the supplication and worship of the Lord. It is a house established for the people and for the benefit of human society in a location that is a center of gathering and a blessed place.

History and Islamic sources also tell us that the House of the Kaaba was built by Adam عليه السلام, was later damaged during the Flood of Noah, and was reconstructed by Abraham the Friend (Khalil) [^1]. Therefore, choosing the most ancient house of Monotheism as the Qibla is more fitting than any other point.

It is noteworthy that in this verse, the House of the Kaaba, whose other name is "Bayt Allah" (the House of Allah), is introduced as the "House of the People." This expression conveys the reality that whatever is named after Allah and is for Allah must be in the service of the people and His servants, and whatever is in the service of the people and the servants of Allah is considered to be for Allah.

Furthermore, this verse clarifies the importance of having a "precedent" (seniority) in Divine and constructive paths. Thus, the first virtue mentioned for the House of the Kaaba is its long and extended history. From here, the answer to the objection regarding the veneration of the "Black Stone" (Hajar al-Aswad) also becomes clear. Some ask: what value or importance does a piece of stone have that millions of people every year compete to touch it (Istilam), and why is it included as a highly recommended (Mustahabb Mu'akkad) act in the program of the pilgrims of the House of Allah?

However, paying attention to the history of this specific stone shows that it possesses a distinction that cannot be found in any other stone in the world: it is the most ancient object used as building material in a center for the worship and adoration of Allah. We know that all temples on earth, and even the Kaaba itself—which is the first place of worship—have been rebuilt many times, and the materials used in their construction have changed. Only this piece of stone has remained as a constant material in this ancient temple through the passage of thousands of years. Therefore, its importance lies in its precedent in the path of Allah and service to the people.

[^1]: For more explanation regarding the evidence for this subject from verses and narrations, refer to Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 330.

Moreover, this stone is a silent history of numerous generations of believers across different centuries and eras. This stone revives the memory of the Istilam of great Prophets and special servants of Allah who stood beside it to supplicate to the Lord.

Another point requiring attention here is that the verse says: "This was the first house built for the people." It is clear that the meaning is "the first house of worship and adoration." Therefore, there is no obstacle to the existence of other residential houses on earth before it. This expression is a clear response to those (such as the author of Tafsir al-Manar) who claim the Kaaba was first built by Abraham and dismiss its construction by Adam as a legend.

In reality, temples and places of worship certainly existed in the world before Abraham, and previous Prophets like Noah utilized them. Therefore, how could it be possible that the House of the Kaaba, which is the first temple in the world, was built [for the first time] by Abraham?

What is the Meaning of Bakkah?

"Bakkah" is originally derived from the root "Bakk" (rhyming with Fakk), meaning crowding and gathering. The reason the House of the Kaaba, or the land upon which it is built, is called "Bakkah" is due to the crowding and gathering of people there. It is not unlikely that this name was not attached to it from the beginning but was given to it after it became officially recognized for worship.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is mentioned that "Makkah" is the name of the entire city, while "Bakkah" is the name of the specific location where the House of the Kaaba is built.

Some commentators have also suggested that "Bakkah" is the same as "Makkah," where the "M" has been transformed into "B," similar to "Lazim" and "Lazib," which both have the same meaning in the Arabic language.

Another reason given for naming the House of the Kaaba and its location "Bakkah" is that the root also means the elimination of arrogance and pride. Since in this great center, all forms of discrimination are abolished, and the tyrants and the arrogant must stand in prayer just like ordinary people—thereby breaking their pride—it has been called Bakkah.

The Expansion of Masjid al-Haram

From the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله onwards, as the number of Muslims increased and consequently the pilgrims of the House of Allah grew, the Masjid al-Haram was expanded by the caliphs of the time.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, it is narrated that during the time of Mansur, the Abbasid caliph, due to the large number of pilgrims, they wanted to expand the Masjid al-Haram once again. Mansur summoned the people who had houses around the mosque to purchase their homes, but they refused to sell at any price. Mansur found himself in a difficult deadlock (for on one hand, he did not want to demolish their houses by force as it would create a bad impression, and on the other hand, they were unwilling to surrender their homes). He questioned Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding this. The Imam said: "Do not be grieved, for there is a clear proof in this matter with which you can argue." He asked: "Which proof?" The Imam replied: "From the Book of Allah." He asked: "Where in the Word of Allah?" The Imam replied: "In this verse: 'Indeed, the first House established for mankind was that at Bakkah, blessed...'. Since Allah says: 'The first house built for the people was the Kaaba,' if they had built their houses before the construction of the Kaaba, the area around the Kaaba would belong to them. But if the Kaaba was prior, then this perimeter (as much as is needed by the pilgrims of the House of Allah) belongs to the Kaaba!"

Mansur ordered them to be brought and argued against them with these words. they were left speechless and said: "We will agree to whatever you wish."

Again, in the same Tafsir, it is narrated that a similar incident was repeated during the time of Mahdi al-Abbasi. Mahdi questioned the jurists of the time; they all said: "If the owners of the houses are not satisfied, usurped property cannot be included in the Masjid al-Haram." Ali ibn Yaqtin asked for permission to ask this question of Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام. Mahdi wrote to the governor of Medina to seek the solution to this problem from Imam Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام. The Imam replied:

"Write: In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. If the House of the Kaaba was built first and the people subsequently settled beside it, the surrounding space belongs to the House of the Kaaba. But if the settlement of the people there preceded the House of the Kaaba, they have more right to it."

When the response reached Mahdi al-Abbasi, he was so happy that he took the letter and kissed it. Then he ordered the houses to be demolished. The owners of the houses went to Imam Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام and requested him to write a letter to Mahdi so that he would pay the price of their houses. The Imam wrote in the letter: "Grant them something." He then satisfied them.

These two narrations contain a "subtle reasoning" that is also fully applicable to standard legal principles: when a temple like the House of the Kaaba is built on virgin land, it has priority over that land within the radius of its needs. Of course, until the day that this need becomes a necessity, others can also use its perimeter, but on the day an urgent need arises, the right of first priority can be exercised.

Distinctions of the House of the Kaaba

In these two verses, in addition to the distinction of being "the first place of worship," four other distinctions are mentioned for the Kaaba:

1- Mubarak (Blessed)

"Mubarak" means full of blessing and benefit. The Kaaba is blessed in this sense because it is located in one of the most blessed lands in the world, both spiritually and materially. The spiritual blessings of this land, the Divine attractions, and the movement, activity, and unity created under its shadow—especially during the Hajj rites—are hidden from no one. If one does not suffice with the outward aspects of the Hajj rites and revives its spirit and philosophy, then its true blessing will be even more apparent.

From a material perspective, although it is a dry land without water or vegetation and is by no means naturally suitable for living conditions, this city throughout history has always been one of the prosperous and vibrant cities and a center ready for life and even for trade.

2- Hudan lil-'Alamin (A Guidance for the Worlds)

The Kaaba is a source of guidance for the worlds. People from far and near cross lands and seas and are drawn to this great place of worship, participating in the magnificent Hajj rites that have remained prevalent since the time of Abraham. Even the pre-Islamic Arabs honored the Kaaba and performed the Hajj rites as the religion of Abraham, even though it had become mixed with superstitions. Under the shadow of those same imperfect rites, they would temporarily cease their wrongdoings to a great extent. In this way, everyone, even the idolaters, benefited from the guidance of this great House. The spiritual attraction of this land and this holy House is such that it involuntarily influences everyone.

3- Fihi Ayatun Bayyinatun Maqamu Ibrahim (In it are Clear Signs, the Standing Place of Abraham)

In this House, clear signs of God-worship, Monotheism, and spirituality are visible. Its endurance and survival throughout history against powerful enemies who intended to destroy it is one of these signs. The relics left by a great Prophet like Abraham عليه السلام beside it—such as Zamzam, Safa, Marwah, the Rukn [^1], the Hatim [^2], the Black Stone, and the Hijr Ismail [^3]—each of which is a living history of past centuries and illuminates great and eternal memories, are among the other signs.

Among these clear signs, the "Station of Abraham" (Maqam Ibrahim) is specifically mentioned, for it is the place where Abraham stood, either for the construction of the Kaaba, or for performing the Hajj rites, or for the general call to the people to perform this great ceremony. In any case, it is among the most important of the aforementioned signs and revives unique memories of sacrifices, sincerity, and gatherings.

There is discussion among commentators whether "Maqam Ibrahim" refers specifically to that spot where the special stone bearing the footprint of Abraham currently resides, or if it refers to the entire "Haram of Makkah" or "all the stations of Hajj." However, in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام recorded in the book al-Kafi, the first possibility is indicated.

4- Wa Man Dakhalahu Kana Amina (And whoever enters it shall be safe)

Abraham عليه السلام, after building the House of the Kaaba, requested the security of the city of Makkah from Allah and said: رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هَذَا الْبَلَدَ آمِناً (Ibrahim: 35): "My Lord, make this city a place of peace and security." Allah answered Abraham's prayer and made it a center of security, which is both a source of spiritual peace and social security for the people who come to it and take inspiration from it. Also, according to religious laws, its security is held in such high regard that any kind of war or fighting within it is forbidden.

Especially in Islam, the "Kaaba" is recognized as a sanctuary and refuge. Even the animals of this land must be secure in every respect, and no one should disturb them. Human individuals who take refuge in it are also safe; even if they are murderers or criminals, they cannot be touched. However, so that this special respect for the House of the Kaaba is not abused and the rights of the oppressed are not trampled, it is commanded that if criminals and offenders take refuge there, they should be restricted in terms of food and water until they are forced to leave the place and be brought to justice.

وَ لِلَّهِ عَلَى النَّاسِ حِجُّ الْبَيْتِ مَنِ اسْتَطاعَ إِلَيْهِ سَبِيلًا In this sentence, the command to perform Hajj is given to all people, and it is expressed as a debt and a Divine obligation upon the responsibility of all people, for He said: "وَ لِلَّهِ عَلَى النَّاسِ" (And for Allah upon the people...).

The word "Hajj" originally means "intention" (qasd). For this reason, a road or path is called "Mahajjah" because it leads a person to the destination, and a proof or argument is called "Hujjah" because it clarifies the intended point in a discussion. The reason these specific rites are called Hajj is that when moving to participate in these ceremonies, people "intend to visit the House of Allah," and for this reason, in the above verse, it is annexed to "the House" (the Kaaba).

As we have previously indicated, the ceremony of visiting the House of the Kaaba was first "officially" established in the time of Abraham, and then continued as a tradition even during the time of the pre-Islamic Arabs. In Islam, it was legislated in a more complete form, free from any superstitions of the Age of Ignorance [^1]. (Of course, from Nahj al-Balagha (the Khutbah al-Qasi'ah) and some narrations, it is well understood that the obligation of Hajj was legislated since the time of Adam عليه السلام, but its official establishment is mostly related to the time of Abraham عليه السلام).

Hajj becomes obligatory upon every human being who has the ability only once in a lifetime, and no more than this is understood from the above verse, for the ruling is absolute and compliance is achieved by performing it once.

The only condition mentioned in the verse for the obligation of Hajj is the issue of "capability" (Istita'ah), which is expressed by the phrase "مَنِ اسْتَطاعَ إِلَيْهِ سَبِيلًا" (whoever has the ability of a way to the House).

Of course, in Islamic narrations and books of jurisprudence, "capability" is interpreted as "having provisions, a means of transport, physical health, the path being open, and the ability to manage one's life upon returning from Hajj." In reality, all of these are included in the above verse, for capability originally means "power/ability," which encompasses all these matters.

Furthermore, it is understood from the above verse that this law, like other Islamic laws, is not exclusive to Muslims; rather, everyone is duty-bound to perform it. This is supported by the famous rule: "The disbelievers are obligated to the branches (Furu') just as they are obligated to the roots (Usul)." Although the condition for the validity of such acts and worships is that they first accept Islam and then perform them, it must be noted that the lack of acceptance of Islam does not eliminate their responsibility toward such duties.

Regarding the importance of this great ceremony, the philosophy of Hajj, and its individual and social effects, a detailed discussion was provided under verses 196 to 203 of Surah al-Baqarah [^1].

[^1]: Each of the four corners of the Kaaba is called a "Rukn." [^2]: "Hatim" refers to the space between the Black Stone and the door of the Kaaba. It is called Hatim because the crowding there is very intense, and it was the place of Adam's repentance. [^3]: "Hijr Ismail" is a specific area built in the form of a semi-circle on the northwest side of the Kaaba. [^1]: From some narrations, it is understood that this obligation in Islam was first legislated in the tenth year of the Hijra, and it was in that same year that the Prophet (s) commanded a group to announce this command everywhere and prepare the people for the pilgrimage to the House of Allah, although the Umrah rites were performed by the Prophet (s) and a group of Muslims before that. [^1]: Refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 2, page 23 onwards, and also Volume 1, page 394.

The Importance of Hajj

وَ مَنْ كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَنِيٌّ عَنِ الْعالَمِينَ At the end of the verse, to emphasize and state the importance of the matter of Hajj, it says: those who show ingratitude (Kufr) and ignore this Divine command and rise in opposition, in reality, they harm themselves, for Allah is self-sufficient, independent of all the worlds.

The word "Kufr" originally means "to cover," and from a religious technical perspective, it has a broad meaning. It includes any kind of opposition to the Truth, whether at the level of principles (Usul) or at the level of subsidiary commands (Furu'). If we observe that "Kufr" is often used in opposition to the principles, it is not evidence that it is exclusively for that meaning. For this reason, in the above verse, it is used regarding the "abandonment of Hajj." Thus, in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, "Kufr" in this verse is interpreted as "abandonment" (Tark) [^2]. In other words, disbelief and distancing oneself from the Truth—just like faith and turning toward the Truth—have stages and degrees, each of which has its own specific rulings. By paying attention to this reality, many problems that arise in the meaning of verses and narrations related to disbelief and faith are resolved. Therefore, if "Kufr" is used regarding usurers (Surah al-Baqarah: 275) and also regarding sorcerers (al-Baqarah: 102), it is for this purpose.

[^2]: Tahdhib, as quoted in Tafsir al-Safi under the verse.

In any case, two points are understood from the above verse:

First is the extraordinary importance of Hajj, such that its abandonment is described as "Kufr." The late Saduq in the book Man La Yahduruhu al-Faqih narrates from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that He said to Ali عليه السلام:

"O Ali! The one who abandons Hajj while he is capable is a disbeliever (Kafir), for Allah, Blessed and Exalted, says: 'And for Allah upon the people is the Hajj of the House, for whoever is able to find a way to it; and whoever disbelieves, then indeed Allah is free of need of the worlds.' O Ali! Whoever postpones Hajj until he dies, Allah will resurrect him on the Day of Judgment as a Jew or a Christian."

Second: The performance of this important Divine obligation, like all religious obligations and programs, is for the benefit of the people and for their cultivation, and it will have no effect on Allah, Who is independent of everyone.

Ayahs

قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِمَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱللَّهُ شَهِيدٌ عَلَىٰ مَا تَعْمَلُونَ (٩٨)
Say, ‘O People of the Book! Why do you deny the signs of Allah, while Allah is witness to what you do?’ (98)
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِمَ تَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ مَنْ ءَامَنَ تَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا وَأَنتُمْ شُهَدَآءُ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (٩٩)
Say, ‘O People of the Book! why do you bar the faithful from the way of Allah, seeking to make it crooked, while you are witnesses [to its truthfulness]? And Allah is not oblivious of what you do.’ (99)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن تُطِيعُوا۟ فَرِيقًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَرُدُّوكُم بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِكُمْ كَـٰفِرِينَ (١٠٠)
O you who have faith, if you obey a part of those who were given the Book, they will turn you back, after your faith, into faithless ones. (100)
وَكَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ وَأَنتُمْ تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱللَّهِ وَفِيكُمْ رَسُولُهُۥ ۗ وَمَن يَعْتَصِم بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ هُدِىَ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (١٠١)
And how would you be faithless while the signs of Allah are recited to you and His Apostle is in your midst? Whoever takes recourse in Allah is certainly guided to a straight path. (101)

Occasion of Revelation

From the collective accounts narrated in both Shi'a and Sunni books regarding the occasion of revelation for these verses, it is understood that a Jew named "Shas ibn Qays"—an old man with a darkened heart who was peerless in his disbelief and enmity—was passing by a gathering of Muslims one day.

He saw a group from the "Aws" and "Khazraj" tribes, who for years had engaged in bloody wars with one another, sitting together in ultimate purity and sincerity, creating a circle of intimacy. The fire of intense disputes that had blazed among them during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) had been completely extinguished.

He was deeply disturbed by seeing this scene and said to himself: "If these people continue on this path under the leadership of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family), the very existence of the Jews will be in total danger." At that moment, a plan occurred to him. He commanded a Jewish youth to join their gathering and remind them of the bloody events of "Bu'ath" (the location where the intense war between Aws and Khazraj took place) and to visualize those events before their eyes.

Coincidentally, this plan, which was skillfully executed by that Jewish youth, proved effective. A group of Muslims began discussing these past events upon hearing them, and some individuals from the Aws and Khazraj tribes even threatened each other with a renewal of those scenes. It was nearly time for the long-extinguished fire to flare up once again.

The news reached the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family). He immediately went to them with a group of the Muhajireen and awakened them with his effective advice and soul-stirring words.

When the crowd heard the Prophet's calming words, they turned back from their decision, laid down their weapons, embraced one another, and wept bitterly. They realized this was one of the plots of the enemies of Islam, and peace, purity, and reconciliation once again washed away the grudges that were about to be revived.

At this moment, the four aforementioned verses were revealed. In the first two verses, Allah rebukes the seductive Jews, and in the following two verses, He warns the Muslims.

Commentary

The Sowers of Discord

قُلْ يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ لِمَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِآياتِ اللَّهِ وَ اللَّهُ شَهِيدٌ عَلى‌ ما تَعْمَلُونَ‌:

In this verse, the address is directed toward the People of the Book—referring here to the Jews. Allah commands His Prophet to ask them, in a tone of blame and reproach, what their motivation is for disbelieving in the signs of Allah, while they know that Allah is aware of their actions.

The "signs of Allah" (آياتِ اللَّهِ) here refers either to the verses in the Torah regarding the signs of the Prophet of Islam, or to the collection of verses and miracles revealed to the Prophet of Islam that testified to his truthfulness.

قُلْ يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ لِمَ تَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ مَنْ آمَنَ تَبْغُونَها عِعوِجاً وَ أَنْتُمْ شُهَداءُ:

Then He reproaches them: if you yourselves are not willing to accept the truth, why do you insist on diverting others from the path of Allah and making the straight divine path appear crooked and incorrect in their eyes? This is while you should be the first group to say "Labbayk" (at your service) to this divine caller, because the glad tidings of this Prophet's appearance were previously given in your books, and you are witnesses to it (وَ أَنْتُمْ شُهَداءُ).

Therefore, why do you distance people from the straight divine path through poisonous propaganda, whispers, the instillation of doubts, and the lighting of forgotten fires of hatred? In addition to your own deviation, why do you carry the heavy burden of responsibility for the deviation of others?! Why?

وَ مَا اللَّهُ بِغافِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ‌:

At the end of the verse, He threatens them that Allah is never heedless of what you do.

Perhaps the expression "Allah is not heedless" is used here because the Jews, to advance their sinister goals, often resorted to secret plans and hidden conspiracies that would quickly affect heedless and uninformed individuals. Therefore, He says: even if some people, due to heedlessness, are influenced by your sinister conspiracies, Allah—who is aware of their hidden and manifest secrets—will not be heedless, and His punishment awaits you!

يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنْ تُطِيعُوا فَرِيقاً مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ يَرُدُّوكُمْ بَعْدَ إِيمانِكُمْ كافِرِينَ‌.

Then, turning the address to the deceived Muslims, He warns them that if they are influenced by the poisonous words of the enemy, allow them to penetrate their ranks, and act upon their whispers, it will not be long before they completely sever the cord of faith and return to disbelief. This is because the enemy first strives to ignite the fire of enmity among them and set them against each other; surely, they will not be content with just this and will continue their whispering until they completely alienate them from Islam.

From what has been said, it becomes clear that the "return to disbelief" mentioned in the above verse refers to "true disbelief and absolute alienation" from Islam. It is also possible that "disbelief" (kufr) refers to the enmities and hostilities of the Age of Ignorance, which itself is considered one of the branches and signs of disbelief, just as faith is the source of love and brotherhood, and disbelief is the source of division and enmity.

وَ كَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ وَ أَنْتُمْ تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْكُمْ آياتُ اللَّهِ وَ فِيكُمْ رَسُولُهُ‌:

Next, He asks the believers in a tone of wonder: how is it possible for you to take the path of disbelief while the Prophet is among you and the divine verses are constantly being recited to you, and the life-giving raindrops of revelation are falling upon your hearts?

This sentence actually points out that if others go astray, it is not very surprising; the surprise is how individuals who see the Prophet among them and are constantly in contact with the world of revelation could possibly go astray. Certainly, if such people go astray, they themselves are the primary culprits, and their punishment will be very painful.

وَ مَنْ يَعْتَصِمْ بِاللَّهِ فَقَدْ هُدِيَ إِلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ‌:

At the conclusion of these verses, He advises the Muslims that to save themselves from the whispers of the enemies and to be guided to the straight path, they must cling to the grace of the Lord and hold fast to His Pure Essence and the verses of the Holy Quran. He explicitly tells them: "Whoever holds fast to Allah will be guided to a straight path."

Among the points that draw attention in these verses is that in the first two verses, where the address is to the Jews, it is an indirect address, as He commands the Prophet to say these things to them; hence, it begins with the word "Qul" (Say). However, in the last two verses, where the address is to the believers, the address is direct and begins without the word "Qul." This is a sign of the ultimate grace and special attention of Allah toward His believing servants.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ حَقَّ تُقَاتِهِۦ وَلَا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَأَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (١٠٢)
O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him and do not die except as Muslims. (102)
وَٱعْتَصِمُوا۟ بِحَبْلِ ٱللَّهِ جَمِيعًا وَلَا تَفَرَّقُوا۟ ۚ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ كُنتُمْ أَعْدَآءً فَأَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِكُمْ فَأَصْبَحْتُم بِنِعْمَتِهِۦٓ إِخْوَٰنًا وَكُنتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَفَا حُفْرَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ فَأَنقَذَكُم مِّنْهَا ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ (١٠٣)
Hold fast, all together, to Allah’s cord, and do not be divided [into sects]. Remember Allah’s blessing upon you when you were enemies, then He brought your hearts together, so you became brothers with His blessing. And you were on the brink of a pit of Fire, whereat He saved you from it. Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you so that you may be guided. (103)

Circumstances of Revelation

We know that during the era of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), there were two large tribes in Medina named "Aws" and "Khazraj" between whom war, bloodshed, and discord had persisted for more than a hundred years! Every so often, they would suddenly fall upon one another, inflicting heavy loss of life and property.

One of the great successes of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) after the migration to Medina was that, through Islam, He established peace and sincerity between them, and through their union, a powerful front was created in Medina.

However, since the roots of the discord were exceptionally deep and strong, and the union was fresh and young, occasionally—due to various factors—forgotten disputes would flare up, only to be quickly extinguished under the light of Islamic teachings and the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) management.

In the preceding verses, we observed an example of the emergence of discord due to the provocations of wise enemies; however, these verses refer to another type of discord that arose due to ignorant friends and senseless prejudices.

It is said that one day, two individuals from the tribes of "Aws" and "Khazraj" named "Tha'labah bin Ghanam" and "As'ad bin Zurarah" stood before each other. Each began enumerating the honors that had been bestowed upon his tribe after Islam. Tha'labah said: "Khuzaimah bin Thabit (Dhu al-Shahadatayn) and Hanzalah (Ghasil al-Mala'ikah), each of whom are honors for the Muslims, are from us; likewise, 'Asim bin Thabit and Sa'd bin Mu'adh are from us."
