In response, As'ad bin Zurarah, who was from the Khazraj tribe, said: "Four people from our tribe performed a great service in the path of disseminating and teaching the Quran: Ubayy bin Ka'b, Mu'adh bin Jabal, Zayd bin Thabit, and Abu Zayd; furthermore, Sa'd bin 'Ubadah, the leader and orator of the people of Medina, is from us."

Gradually, the matter reached a critical point, and the tribes of both sides became aware of the situation. They took up arms and stood against each other. It was feared that the fire of war would once again flare up between them and the earth would be stained with their blood! News reached the Prophet; His Eminence immediately came to the scene and, with His specific eloquence and management, ended that dangerous situation and established peace and sincerity among them.

The above verses were revealed here and, in the form of a general decree, invited all Muslims to unity with an effective and emphatic expression.

Commentary

The Call to Piety

يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ حَقَّ تُقَاتِهِ:

In this verse, the call is first made to piety (taqwa) so that it may serve as a prelude to the call toward unity. In truth, the call to unity without seeking aid from an ethical and doctrinal root is ineffective or very weakly effective. For this reason, an effort is made in this verse to weaken the factors that create discord and division under the light of faith and piety. Therefore, addressing the believers, it says: "All of you fear Allah and fulfill the right of piety and godliness."

Regarding what is meant by "the right of piety" (haqqa tuqatih), there is much discussion among commentators. However, there is no doubt that the right of piety is the highest and most sublime degree of godliness, which includes abstaining from every kind of sin, disobedience, transgression, and deviation from the truth. Thus, it is narrated in the commentary Al-Durr al-Manthur from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and in the commentaries of Ayyashi and Ma'ani al-Akhbar from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) that in interpreting "the right of piety," they said:

ان يطاع فلا يعصى و يذكر فلا ينسى (و يشكر فلا يكفر)

Meaning: "The right of piety and godliness is that He is constantly obeyed and never disobeyed, that He is always remembered and never forgotten, and that you are grateful for His blessings and do not show ingratitude toward them."

It is obvious that performing this command, like all divine commands, depends on the extent of human ability. Therefore, the above verse has no contradiction whatsoever with verse 16 of Surah At-Taghabun, which says: فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُمْ "So fear Allah as much as you are able." Discussions regarding the conflict between these two verses and the abrogation of one by the other are entirely baseless.

Of course, the second verse is, in fact, an explanation of a condition—or what is termed a "specification" (takhsis)—of the first verse, restricting it to the extent of human ability. Since it appears that among the ancients, the word "abrogation" (naskh) was sometimes applied to "specification," it is possible that those who considered the second verse as abrogating the first meant that very "specification."

وَ لَا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَ أَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ:

This phrase is, in fact, a warning to the tribes of Aws and Khazraj and all Muslims of the world to be alert. Merely embracing Islam is not enough; what is important is to preserve one's faith and Islam until the final hours of life. They must not blow away their faith and pure deeds by rekindling the extinguished fires of the grudges of the era of Ignorance and following irrational prejudices, lest their end and the conclusion of their affair turn toward wretchedness. Therefore, He says with emphasis: "Be careful that you do not leave this world except with faith and Islam."

The Call Toward Unity

وَ اعْتَصِمُوا بِحَبْلِ اللَّهِ جَمِيعاً وَ لَا تَفَرَّقُوا:

In this verse, the final discussion, which is the "issue of unity and the struggle against any kind of division," is raised, and He says: "Cling to the rope of Allah all together, and do not become divided."

Regarding what is meant by "the rope of Allah" (habl Allah), commentators have mentioned various possibilities. Some say it refers to the Quran, some say Islam, and others have said it refers to the Family of the Prophet and the Infallible Imams.

In the narrations transmitted from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt, these various expressions are also seen. For example, in the commentary Al-Durr al-Manthur from the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and in the book Ma'ani al-Akhbar from Imam al-Sajjad (عليه السلام), it is narrated that they said: "The rope of Allah is the Quran." In the commentary of Ayyashi from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام), he said: "The rope of Allah is the Progeny of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله), whom the people are commanded to hold fast to."

However, neither these hadiths nor those interpretations contradict one another, because the "rope of Allah" refers to any means of connection with the Pure Essence of God, whether this means be Islam, the Quran, the Prophet, or His Ahl al-Bayt. In other words, all that has been said is gathered within the broad concept of "connection with God," which is derived from the meaning of "the rope of Allah."

Why the expression "the rope of Allah"?

An interesting point is that describing these matters as the "rope of Allah" actually points to a reality: that under normal conditions and without having a mentor and guide, man will remain at the bottom of the valley of nature and the dark well of rebellious instincts and ignorance. To be saved from this valley and to emerge from this well, he needs a strong cord and rope to cling to and come out. This strong cord is that very connection with God through the Quran, the bringer of the Quran, and His true successors, who raise people from low and base levels to the heaven of spiritual and material perfection.

Enemies of Yesterday and Brothers of Today

وَ اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ كُنْتُمْ أَعْداءً فَأَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِكُمْ فَأَصْبَحْتُمْ بِنِعْمَتِهِ إِخْواناً

Then the Quran points to the great blessing of unity and brotherhood and invites the Muslims to reflect on the sorrowful state of the past and compare that "division" with this "unity." It says: "Do not forget how you were enemies in the past, but Allah, under the light of Islam and faith, linked your hearts together, and you, the enemies of yesterday, became the brothers of today." It is noteworthy that the word "blessing" (ni'mah) is repeated twice in this sentence, thereby highlighting the importance of the gift of harmony and brotherhood.

Another point is that He attributes the matter of reconciling the hearts of the believers to Himself, saying: "Allah created affection between your hearts." With this expression, an "Islamic social miracle" is pointed out. For if we carefully consider the history of the prior enmity and hostility of the Arabs—how deep-rooted grudges had accumulated in their hearts over many long years, and how a minor and simple matter was enough to ignite the fire of a bloody war among them—especially considering that ignorant, illiterate, and semi-savage people are usually stubborn and inflexible individuals who are not easily willing to forget the smallest past issues, then the importance of this "great social miracle" of Islam becomes clear. It proves that it was not possible through ordinary and common means to create, within a few years, a single, united, and brotherly nation out of such a scattered, grudge-bearing, ignorant, and unaware people.

The Testimony of Historians and Scholars

The importance of the aforementioned subject (unity and brotherhood among the grudge-bearing Arab tribes) has not remained hidden from the eyes of scholars and historians, even non-Muslim ones, and all have mentioned it with great admiration. For example:

"John Davenport," the famous English scholar, writes: "...Muhammad, a simple Arab, transformed the scattered, small, naked, and hungry tribes of his country into a compact and disciplined society and introduced them among the nations of the earth with new qualities and ethics. In less than thirty years, this method and way defeated the Emperor of Constantinople and eliminated the Sultans of Persia. He conquered Syria, Mesopotamia, and Egypt, and extended the scope of his conquests from the Atlantic Ocean to the shores of the Caspian Sea and to the Jaxartes River." [^1]

"Thomas Carlyle" says: "Allah guided the Arabs through Islam from darkness toward light. From a silent and stagnant nation from which neither a voice came nor a movement was felt, He created a nation that was driven from obscurity toward fame, from lethargy toward awakening, from lowliness toward the heights, and from weakness and inability toward strength; their light shone from the four corners of the world. Not more than a century had passed since the declaration of Islam when the Muslims set one foot in India and the other in Andalusia, and finally, in this short period, Islam was radiating light over half the world." [^2]

"Dr. Gustave Le Bon" has confessed to this truth as such: "...Until the time of this astonishing event, namely 'Islam,' which suddenly showed us the Arab race in the garb of world-conquerors and creators of meaning, no part of Arabia was counted as part of the history of civilization, nor was there any sign of it in terms of science or religion." [^3]

"Nehru," the late Indian scholar and politician, writes in this regard: "...The story of the Arabs and the tale of how they rapidly expanded in Asia, Europe, and Africa and created a high and great culture and civilization is one of the wonders of human history. The new force and thought that awakened the Arabs and filled them with self-confidence and power was 'Islam'..." [^4]

[^1]: An Apology for Mohammed and the Koran: John Davenport, translated by Sayyid Ghulam-Reza Sa'idi, p. 77. [^2]: The Plans of Colonialism: Muhammad Mahmoud Sawwaf, p. 38. [^3]: The Civilization of the Arabs: Gustave Le Bon. [^4]: Glimpses of World History, Vol. 1, p. 317.

وَ كُنْتُمْ عَلى‌ شَفا حُفْرَةٍ مِنَ النَّارِ فَأَنْقَذَكُمْ مِنْها:

"Shafa" in its original linguistic sense refers to the edge of a well or a trench and the like. Perhaps the application of "shafah" to the "lip" is for this same reason. Likewise, the use of this word for recovery from illness is because the person is placed on the edge of "health and well-being."

In the above sentence, Allah says: "In the past, you were on the brink of a pit of fire into which you might have fallen at any moment, and everything of yours would have turned to ash! But Allah saved you and guided you from this precipice to the point of safety and security, which was the point of 'brotherhood and love'."

Whether "fire" (nar) in the verse refers to the fire of Hell or the fires of this world has been discussed among commentators. However, considering the verse as a whole, it seems that "fire" is a metaphor for the wars and conflicts that flared up among the Arabs at any moment for any excuse during the era of Ignorance. The Holy Quran reflects the dangerous conditions of the era of Ignorance with this sentence—that the danger of war and bloodshed threatened them at every moment—and Allah saved them from that situation under the light of Islam. Certainly, by being saved from the dangerous situation of the past, they were also saved from the burning fire of Hell.

كَذلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ آياتِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ:

At the end of the verse, for further emphasis, it says: "Thus Allah makes clear His signs to you so that you may be guided." Therefore, the ultimate goal is your guidance and salvation, and since your interests and destiny are at stake, you must give great importance to what has been said.

The Role of Unity in the Survival of Nations

Despite all the discussions regarding the miraculous effect of unity in advancing social goals and the honor of societies, it can be said that its true effect is still not fully recognized.

Today, massive dams have been erected in various parts of the world which are the source of the greatest industrial powers and have placed vast lands under their coverage of irrigation and light. If we think correctly, we see that this great power is nothing but the result of the joining together of the insignificant powers of raindrops. Then we become aware of the importance of unity and the collective efforts of human beings.

In numerous hadiths that have reached us from the Prophet and the great leaders of Islam, the necessity and importance of this subject have been pointed out with various expressions:

In one instance, the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) says:

المؤمن للمؤمن كالبنيان يشيد بعضه بعضا

"Believers are to one another like the parts of a building, each part of which holds the other part firmly."

And He also said:

المؤمنون كالنفس الواحدة

"The believers are like a single soul."

And He also says:

مثل المؤمنين فى توادهم و تراحمهم كمثل الجسد الواحد اذا اشتكى بعضه تداعى سائره بالسهر و الحمى‌

"The likeness of the believers in their mutual love and kindness is like the members of a single body; when some part of it suffers and falls into pain, the other members will have no rest or peace due to sleeplessness and fever." [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, Vol. 2, p. 450.

Ayahs

وَلْتَكُن مِّنكُمْ أُمَّةٌ يَدْعُونَ إِلَى ٱلْخَيْرِ وَيَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (١٠٤)
There has to be a nation among you summoning to the good, bidding what is right, and forbidding what is wrong. It is they who are the felicitous. (104)
وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ تَفَرَّقُوا۟ وَٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٠٥)
Do not be like those who became divided [into sects] and differed after manifest signs had come to them. For such there will be a great punishment (105)

Commentary

Invitation to Truth and the Struggle Against Corruption

وَ لْتَكُنْ مِنْكُمْ أُمَّةٌ يَدْعُونَ إِلَى الْخَيْرِ وَ يَأْمُرُونَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ وَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ‌.

The word "Ummah" (community) originally stems from the root "umm," meaning anything to which other things are joined. For this reason, "Ummah" refers to a group that possesses an aspect of unity among them, whether that unity is in terms of time, place, or goal and ideology. Therefore, scattered and dispersed individuals are not called an "Ummah."

Following the previous verses which described the issue of brotherhood and unity, this verse points to the matter of "Enjoining the Good" (Amr bi-l-Ma‘ruf) and "Forbidding the Evil" (Nahy ‘ani-l-Munkar), which in reality serves as a social shield for the protection of the population. If the issue of enjoining the good and forbidding the evil is absent, various factors that are enemies to the survival of "social unity" will consume the roots of society from within like termites and cause it to disintegrate. Therefore, maintaining social unity is impossible without public oversight!

In the above verse, it is commanded that there must always be an Ummah among the Muslims who perform these two great social duties: inviting people to goodness and restraining them from evil. At the end of the verse, it explicitly states that prosperity and salvation are only possible through this path.

Question

A question arises here: the literal meaning of "minkum ummah" (from among you a community) suggests that this Ummah constitutes only a part of the Muslim population, not all of them. Accordingly, the duty of enjoining the good and forbidding the evil would not have a general character but would be the duty of a specific group—even if the selection and training of this group is the duty of all people. In other words, these two duties would be a collective obligation (wajib kifa’i) rather than an individual one (wajib ‘ayni). However, it appears from other verses of the Quran that these two duties are general in nature; for instance, a few verses after this one, we read: "You were the best Ummah brought forth for mankind, for you enjoin the good and forbid the evil" (3:110). And in Surah Al-Asr, He says: "All mankind is in loss except those who believe and do righteous deeds and invite to the truth and recommend patience and steadfastness." According to these and similar verses, these two duties are not restricted to a specific group.

Answer

Careful reflection on the totality of these verses clarifies the answer to this question, as it can be inferred that "Enjoining the Good and Forbidding the Evil" has two stages: one is the "individual stage" where every person is duty-bound to personally oversee the actions of others, and the other is the "collective stage" where a community is duty-bound to join hands and cooperate to end social disorders.

The first part is the duty of the general public, and since it has an individual aspect, its scope is naturally limited to the individual's ability. However, the second part takes the form of a collective obligation (wajib kifa’i), and because it is collective, its scope of power is vast and is naturally considered among the functions of the Islamic government. These two forms of struggling against corruption and inviting toward the truth are considered masterpieces of Islamic law, defining the issue of division of labor within the organization of the Islamic government and the necessity of forming a group to oversee the social situation and government institutions.

In the past, in Islamic lands (and today in some Islamic countries like Hijaz), inspired by the above verse, there existed special organizations for fighting corruption and inviting toward social responsibilities called the "Office of Hisbah," and its officers were known as "Muhtasib" or "Enjoiners of Good." They were tasked with cooperating to fight any form of corruption and vice among the people, or any form of oppression and corruption within the government apparatus, as well as encouraging people toward good and commendable deeds.

Therefore, the existence of such a group with that vast power is in no way inconsistent with the general nature of the duty of enjoining the good and forbidding the evil within the individual's scope and limited power.

Since this discussion is among the important topics of the Holy Quran and is mentioned in numerous verses, it is necessary to mention several points here:

1- What are "Ma‘ruf" and "Munkar"?

"Ma‘ruf" linguistically means "known" (from the root ‘arafa) and "Munkar" means "unknown" or "unrecognized" (from the root inkar). Thus, good deeds are introduced as "known" matters, and ugly or undesirable deeds as "unknown" matters. This is because the pure human fitrah (innate nature) is familiar with the first category and unfamiliar with the second!

2- Is Enjoining the Good a Rational or Devotional Duty?

A group of Islamic scholars believes that the obligation of these two duties is proven only through transmitted evidence (naqli), and that reason does not command a person to stop another from a bad deed whose harm only affects the doer himself.

However, considering social bonds and the fact that no bad deed in human society remains limited to a specific point—but rather, whatever it may be, it can spread like fire to other points—the rational nature of these two duties becomes clear.

In other words: in society, there is no such thing as "individual harm" in the absolute sense; every individual harm has the potential to become a "social harm." For this reason, logic and reason allow the members of society to spare no effort in keeping their living environment clean from any corruption.

Coincidentally, some hadiths point to this subject. It is narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: "A sinful individual among the people is like someone who boards a ship with a group, and when they reach the middle of the sea, he takes an axe and begins to hole the spot where he is sitting. When others protest, he replies: 'I am only interfering with my own portion!' If the others do not stop him from this dangerous act, it will not be long before the seawater penetrates the ship and they all drown together."

With this beautiful example, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله visualized the rationality of the duty of enjoining the good and forbidding the evil, considering the individual's right to oversee society as a natural right arising from the interconnectedness of destinies.

3- The Importance of Enjoining the Good and Forbidding the Evil

In addition to numerous verses of the Holy Quran, many hadiths in authentic Islamic sources have been recorded regarding the importance of these two great social duties, pointing to the dangers and grim consequences that arise in society due to their abandonment. For example:

1- Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام says: "Enjoining the good and forbidding the evil are two great divine obligations through which other obligations are established, paths become secure, earnings become lawful, injustices are redressed, the earth becomes inhabited, justice is exacted from enemies, and affairs become upright." [^1]

2- The Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله says: "Whoever enjoins the good and forbids the evil is the vicegerent of Allah on His earth, the vicegerent of His Messenger, and the vicegerent of His Book." [^2] From this hadith, it is well understood that this great obligation is, above all, a divine program, and the mission of the prophets and the revelation of heavenly books are all part of this program.

3- A man came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—while he was sitting upon the pulpit—and asked: "Who is the best of people?" The Prophet replied: "The one who enjoins the good the most, forbids the evil the most, is the most God-fearing, and strives the most for the pleasure of Allah." [^3]

4- In another hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is narrated that he said: "You must enjoin the good and forbid the evil, or else Allah will set a tyrant over you who neither respects the elderly nor shows mercy to the young. Your righteous and pious ones will pray, but their prayers will not be answered; they will seek help from Allah, but He will not help them; they will even repent, but Allah will not forgive their sins." [^4] All of these are the natural reactions to the actions of a population that suspends this great social duty. Without public oversight, the course of affairs falls out of the hands of the righteous, and the wicked conquer the social arena. The statement in the hadith that even their repentance is not accepted is because repentance has no true meaning while they continue their silence in the face of corruption, unless they revise their conduct.

5- Ali عليه السلام says: "All righteous deeds and even Jihad in the way of Allah, compared to enjoining the good and forbidding the evil, are like a drop of saliva compared to a vast, deep sea!" [^5] All these emphases are because these two great duties are, in fact, the guarantors for the implementation of all other individual and social duties, and are considered their very soul and life. With their suspension, all laws and moral principles will lose their value.

4- Does Enjoining the Good Deprive One of Freedom?

In response to this question, it must be said that while collective living undoubtedly brings many benefits and blessings to human beings—and these advantages have even compelled man toward social life—it also brings certain limitations in return. Since the harm of these limitations is minor and negligible compared to the countless benefits of collective life, humanity has accepted social life and its limitations from the very beginning. Since in social life the destinies of individuals are linked, and individuals affect one another's fate, the right to oversee the actions of others is a natural right and a characteristic of collective living, as beautifully illustrated in the hadith previously quoted from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Therefore, performing this obligation is not only not contrary to individual freedoms but is a duty that individuals owe to one another.

5- Does Enjoining the Good Produce Chaos?

Another question raised here is: if every person is to interfere in the social situation and oversee one another's actions, will it not produce chaos and various conflicts in society, and contradict the principle of division of duties and responsibilities?

In response, it must be said: from the previous discussions, the truth became clear that enjoining the good and forbidding the evil has two stages. The first stage, which is general, has a limited scope and does not exceed reminding, advising, protesting, criticizing, and the like. Certainly, a vibrant society must have all its members feel such a responsibility toward corruption.

However, the second stage, which is specific to a certain group and is considered among the functions of the Islamic government, possesses very vast power. This means that if there is a need for stern action or even retaliation (qisas) and the implementation of legal punishments (hudud), this group has the authority to perform their duty under the supervision of the Sharia judge and the officials of the Islamic government.

Therefore, considering the different stages of enjoining the good and forbidding the evil and the limits and regulations of each, not only is no chaos produced in society, but society emerges from being a dead and stagnant community and transforms into a living one.

6- Enjoining the Good is Distinct from Violence

At the end of this discussion, it is necessary to note that in performing this divine obligation, inviting toward the truth, and fighting corruption, one must not forget compassion, good intentions, and purity of purpose. Except in cases of necessity, one should proceed through peaceful means; performing this duty should not be equated with violence.

Unfortunately, some individuals, when performing this duty in cases where it is not necessary, resort to violence and sometimes use foul and offensive language. Consequently, we see that such types of enjoining the good not only leave no positive effect but sometimes produce the opposite result. Meanwhile, the method of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the lifestyle of the Imams of Guidance علیهم السلام show that when they implemented these two duties, they mixed them with the utmost kindness and love. For this reason, even the most stubborn individuals would soon surrender before them.

In the commentary of "Al-Manar," we read under this verse: A young man came to the Prophet and said: "O Messenger of Allah, do you give me permission to commit adultery (zina)?" Upon hearing this, the people's voices rose in protest from all sides. But the Prophet, with calmness and gentleness, said: "Come closer." The youth came closer and sat before the Prophet. The Prophet affectionately asked him: "Would you like it if they did such a thing to your mother?" He said: "No, may I be sacrificed for you." He said: "Likewise, people do not wish for such a thing to happen to their mothers. Would you like it if they did such a thing to your daughter?" He said: "No, may I be sacrificed for you." He said: "Likewise, people do not wish for it regarding their daughters. Tell me, would you approve of it for your sister?" The youth again denied it (and completely regretted his question). The Prophet then placed his hand on the youth's chest, prayed for him, and said: "O Allah, purify his heart, forgive his sin, and guard his chastity from pollution." From then on, the most loathsome act to that youth was adultery! ... This was the result of gentleness and love in forbidding evil.

وَ لا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ تَفَرَّقُوا وَ اخْتَلَفُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ: In this verse, the discussion again revolves around the issue of unity and avoiding division and hypocrisy. This verse warns Muslims against following the path of division and disagreement like previous nations, such as the Jews and Christians, and thereby earning a great punishment for themselves. In reality, it invites them to study the history of those who came before and their painful fate after disagreement and division.

The insistence and emphasis of the Holy Quran in these verses regarding the avoidance of division and hypocrisy is an indication that this event would occur in their society in the future; for whenever the Quran insists heavily on warning against something, it points to its eventual occurrence and emergence.

The Prophet of Islam also made this prediction previously and explicitly informed the Muslims: "The Jewish people split into 71 sects after Moses, the Christians into 72 sects, and my Ummah will split into 73 sects after me." [^6] Apparently, the number 70 indicates a large quantity—a "multiplicative number"—rather than a literal count. That is, among the Jews, one group was on the truth and "many groups" were on falsehood; among the Christians, the false groups increased while the truth remained with one group; and among the Muslims, disagreements would increase even further.

And as the Holy Quran indicated and the Holy Prophet informed, the Muslims after his passing deviated from the straight path—which was only one path—and became scattered in religious beliefs and even within the religion itself, resorting to declaring one another disbelievers (takfir). It reached a point where sometimes the sword, and sometimes cursing and vilification, ruled among them. It went so far that some Muslims considered the life and property of one another lawful, and such enmity and hostility were created among Muslims that some were willing to join the disbelievers to fight against their own religious brothers!

Thus, the unity and oneness that were the secret of the success of the early Muslims were replaced by hypocrisy and disagreement. Consequently, their prosperous life turned into a wretched one, and they lost their former greatness.

أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ‌: Those who disagree in religion despite having clear proofs will be afflicted with a great and painful punishment.

Undoubtedly, the immediate result of disagreement and hypocrisy is humiliation and abasement. The secret of the humiliation and abasement of any nation must be sought in their disagreement and hypocrisy. A society whose foundation of power and pillars of solidarity are smashed by the axes of division will find its land forever the playground of foreigners and the territory of colonialist rule. Truly, what a great punishment! But the punishment of the Hereafter, as Allah has described in the Quran, will be exceptionally more severe than this punishment and awaits those who cause division and lean toward disagreement.

Through Sunni chains, it is reported in Ad-Durr al-Manthur, Jami' al-Usul, and Al-Milal wa al-Nihal.

Ayahs

يَوْمَ تَبْيَضُّ وُجُوهٌ وَتَسْوَدُّ وُجُوهٌ ۚ فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْوَدَّتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ أَكَفَرْتُم بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِكُمْ فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ (١٠٦)
on the day when [some] faces will turn white and [some] faces will turn black. As for those whose faces turn black [it will be said to them], ‘Did you disbelieve after your faith? So taste the punishment because of what you used to disbelieve.’ (106)
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ٱبْيَضَّتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ فَفِى رَحْمَةِ ٱللَّهِ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (١٠٧)
But as for those whose faces become white, they shall dwell in Allah’s mercy, and they will remain in it [forever]. (107)

Commentary

Radiant and Dark Faces

يَوْمَ تَبْيَضُّ وُجُوهٌ وَ تَسْوَدُّ وُجُوهٌ:

Following the warnings given in the previous verses regarding division, hypocrisy, and returning to the traces of the era of disbelief and ignorance (Jahiliyyah), these two verses point to their ultimate consequences. They illustrate how disbelief, division, hypocrisy, and a return to ignorance result in "blackness of face," while Islam, faith, unity, and sincerity lead to "whiteness of face."

The above verses state: On the Day of Resurrection, there will be faces that are radiant and faces that are dark and black. To those who have black and dark faces, it will be said: Why did you follow the path of disbelief after your faith? And why, after achieving unity under the light of Islam, did you choose the path of hypocrisy and ignorance? In contrast, the united believers will be immersed in the sea of Divine mercy, living eternally in that tranquil life.

We have reminded many times that the states and qualities of human life, as well as one's rewards and punishments in the next world, are the embodiment of one's actions, dispositions, and thoughts in this world. In other words: every deed performed by a human in this world leaves vast and extensive effects on the human soul that may not be easily perceived in this world. However, on the Day of Resurrection, after the transformations and evolutions that occur there, they will manifest in their true reality. Since the sovereignty and manifestation of the soul are greater there, its effects will even be reflected in the physical body.

Just as faith and unity are the cause of "whiteness of face" (honor) in this world, and conversely, a divided and faithless nation is a "black-faced" (disgraced) people, in the next world, this "metaphorical" blackness and whiteness of face from this world will take on a "literal" form. Their owners will be resurrected with white and brilliant faces, or black and dark ones.

This reality is also mentioned in other verses of the Quran. For instance, regarding those who repeatedly commit sins, we read: كَأَنَّما أُغْشِيَتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ قِطَعاً مِنَ اللَّيْلِ مُظْلِماً: "As if their faces had been covered with patches of the dense darkness of night" (Surah Yunus, Verse 27).

And regarding those who attribute lies to Allah, He says: وَ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ تَرَى الَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا عَلَى اللَّهِ وُجُوهُهُمْ مُسْوَدَّةٌ: "And on the Day of Resurrection you will see those who lied about Allah [with] their faces blackened" (Surah Az-Zumar, Verse 60). All of these are reflections of their actions in this world.

Ayahs

تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱللَّهِ نَتْلُوهَا عَلَيْكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ ظُلْمًا لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٠٨)
These are the signs of Allah, which We recite to you in truth, and Allah does not desire any wrong for the creatures. (108)
وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ (١٠٩)
To Allah belongs whatever there is in the heavens and the earth, and to Allah all matters are returned. (109)

Commentary

تِلْكَ آياتُ اللَّهِ نَتْلُوها عَلَيْكَ بِالْحَقِّ وَ مَا اللَّهُ يُرِيدُ ظُلْماً لِلْعالَمِينَ‌:

This verse refers to the various preceding discussions regarding unity, harmony, faith, disbelief, enjoining what is good and forbidding what is evil, and their results and consequences. It states: These are the signs of Allah which We recite to you in truth. Whatever befalls individuals as a result of violating these commands is the consequence of their own actions, and Allah does not desire injustice for anyone; rather, these sinister effects are exactly what they have prepared for themselves with their own hands.

وَ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ إِلَى اللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ الْأُمُورُ:

This verse, in reality, contains two proofs for the absence of any injustice or oppression from Allah's side. First, for a God who is the Owner of all existence and the beings of this world, injustice and oppression have no meaning. One commits an encroachment against another when they lack something that others possess.

Furthermore, injustice and oppression are meaningful regarding someone against whom an action might be taken without their consent. However, for Him to whom all affairs of existence return from beginning to end, and without whose permission no one can perform any action, injustice and oppression from His side are impossible.

Ayah

كُنتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ تَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَتَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ ۗ وَلَوْ ءَامَنَ أَهْلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُم ۚ مِّنْهُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَأَكْثَرُهُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (١١٠)
You are the best nation [ever] brought forth for mankind: you bid what is right and forbid what is wrong, and have faith in Allah. And if the People of the Book had believed, it would have been better for them. Among them [some] are faithful, but most of them are transgressors. (110)

Commentary

Once Again, Combating Corruption and Inviting to Truth

كُنْتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ تَأْمُرُونَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ‌ ...:

In this verse, the issue of enjoining what is good and forbidding what is evil, along with faith in Allah, is raised once again. As mentioned in the commentary of verse 104, this verse describes enjoining what is good and forbidding what is evil as a general and universal duty, whereas the previous verse described a specific stage of it as a private duty and a collective obligation (wajib kifa'i), the details of which we have already discussed extensively.

An interesting point to note is that in this verse, Muslims are introduced as the "best nation" mobilized for the service of human society. The reason given for them being the best nation is that "they enjoin what is good, forbid what is evil, and have faith in Allah." This itself demonstrates that the reform of human society is impossible without faith, inviting to the truth, and combating corruption. Furthermore, it can be inferred that these two great duties, with the breadth they possess in Islam, did not exist in previous religions.

As for why this nation must be the best of nations, it is also clear: because they possess the final heavenly religion, and the final religion—by account of evolution—is the most complete of them.

In the above verse, two other points should be noted: First, "كُنْتُمْ" (you were) is mentioned in the past tense, meaning you were the best nation in the past. Regarding the meaning of this phrase, although commentators have offered many possibilities, it seems more likely that the use of the past tense is for emphasis. There are many similar instances in the Holy Quran where certain subjects are mentioned in the form of the past tense to present them as an established reality.

Second, here, enjoining what is good and forbidding what is evil are prioritized over faith in Allah. This is a sign of the importance and greatness of these two major divine obligations. Additionally, the performance of these two obligations guarantees the spread of faith and the implementation of all individual and social laws; the guarantee of implementation practically takes precedence over the law itself.

Above all, if these two duties are not carried out, the roots of faith in hearts will also weaken, and its foundations will crumble. For these reasons, they have been prioritized over faith.

From this explanation, it also becomes clear that Muslims are considered an "excellent nation" only as long as they do not forget the invitation toward goodness and the struggle against corruption. The day these two duties are forgotten, they will neither be the best nation nor will they be of benefit to human society.

Furthermore, it should be noted that the addressees in this verse are the generality of Muslims, just as other Quranic addresses are. The possibility suggested by some that it is specific to the Emigrants (Muhajirun) or the early Muslims has no supporting evidence.

وَ لَوْ آمَنَ أَهْلُ الْكِتابِ لَكانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ مِنْهُمُ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَ أَكْثَرُهُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ‌.

Then, it points out that the benefits of a religion so clear and laws so magnificent cannot be denied by anyone. Therefore, "if the People of the Book (Jews and Christians) were to believe, it would be better for them. However, unfortunately, only a minority of them have cast aside ignorant prejudices and embraced Islam with open arms, while the majority of them have departed from the command of their Lord," even ignoring the glad tidings regarding the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that were in their own books, remaining persistent in their disbelief and prejudice.

Ayahs

لَن يَضُرُّوكُمْ إِلَّآ أَذًى ۖ وَإِن يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ يُوَلُّوكُمُ ٱلْأَدْبَارَ ثُمَّ لَا يُنصَرُونَ (١١١)
They will never do you any harm, except for some hurt; and if they fight you, they will turn their backs [to flee], then they will not be helped. (111)
ضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلذِّلَّةُ أَيْنَ مَا ثُقِفُوٓا۟ إِلَّا بِحَبْلٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَحَبْلٍ مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَبَآءُو بِغَضَبٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْمَسْكَنَةُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَقْتُلُونَ ٱلْأَنۢبِيَآءَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا عَصَوا۟ وَّكَانُوا۟ يَعْتَدُونَ (١١٢)
Wherever they are found, abasement is stamped upon them, except for an asylum from Allah and an asylum from the people. They have earned the wrath of Allah, and poverty has been stamped upon them. That, because they would deny the signs of Allah and kill the prophets unjustly. That, because they would disobey and commit transgression. (112)

Circumstances of Revelation

When some of the enlightened leaders of the Jews, such as Abdullah bin Salam, along with their companions, left their former faith and embraced Islam, a group of Jewish leaders came to them and began to reproach and blame them. They even threatened them, asking why they had abandoned the religion of their fathers and ancestors to embrace Islam.

The above verses were revealed as a source of comfort and glad tidings for them and for all other Muslims.

Commentary

لَنْ يَضُرُّوكُمْ إِلَّا أَذىً وَ إِنْ يُقاتِلُوكُمْ يُوَلُّوكُمُ الْأَدْبارَ ثُمَّ لا يُنْصَرُونَ

This verse gives glad tidings to the Muslims who were being pressured by their disbelieving kinsmen and were being rebuked or occasionally threatened for accepting Islam. It assures them that the opponents can never cause them any significant harm; any harm they do cause will be very minor and of little effect, not exceeding verbal abuse and the like.

In reality, these two verses contain several important prophecies and glad tidings for the Muslims, all of which were fulfilled during the time of the Noble Prophet (peace be upon him and his family):

  1. The People of the Book can never cause significant harm to the Muslims, and their injuries are minor, fleeting, and short-lived (لَنْ يَضُرُّوكُمْ إِلَّا أَذىً).
  2. Whenever they face the Muslims in battle, they will ultimately be defeated, final victory will belong to the Muslims, and no one will rise to support them (وَ إِنْ يُقاتِلُوكُمْ يُوَلُّوكُمُ الْأَدْبارَ ثُمَّ لا يُنْصَرُونَ).
  3. They will never stand on their own feet and will always remain humiliated and wretched, unless they revise their conduct and take the path of God, or resort to others and temporarily utilize their power (ضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الذِّلَّةُ أَيْنَ ما ثُقِفُوا...).

It did not take long for these three divine promises and glad tidings to be realized during the time of the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family). Specifically, the Jews of Hijaz (Banu Qurayza, Banu Nadir, Banu Qaynuqa, the Jews of Khaybar, and Banu al-Mustaliq), after extensive incitement against Islam, faced the Muslims on various battlefields and were ultimately defeated and dispersed.

ضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الذِّلَّةُ أَيْنَ ما ثُقِفُوا إِلَّا بِحَبْلٍ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ حَبْلٍ مِنَ النَّاسِ

The word "ثقفوا" (thuqifu) is originally derived from the root "ثقف" (thaqaf) and "ثقافة" (thaqafah), meaning to find something with skill; it is applied to anything that a person attains through precision and expertise. In the above sentence, the Holy Quran states that wherever they are found, the seal of humiliation has been stamped upon their foreheads! Although the name of the Jews is not explicitly mentioned in the above verses, based on the context within this verse and the preceding ones—as well as the similarity to verse 61 of Surah Al-Baqarah where the Jews are explicitly named—it is understood that this sentence here also refers to the Jews.

Then, at the end of this sentence, He says: only in two cases can they erase this seal of humiliation from their foreheads: first, by returning to and connecting with Allah and believing in His true religion (إِلَّا بِحَبْلٍ مِنَ اللَّهِ), or by dependency on people and relying on others (وَ حَبْلٍ مِنَ النَّاسِ).

Although commentators have offered various possibilities regarding these two expressions (حبل من الله and حبل من الناس), what has been stated is most consistent with the meaning of the verse. This is because when "a rope from Allah" (connection with God) is placed in contrast to "a rope from the people" (connection with people), it becomes clear that they refer to two different meanings, rather than one meaning faith and the other meaning the protection (dhimmah) granted by Muslims.

Therefore, the summary of the verse's concept is this: they must either revise their life program and return to Allah—cleansing the memory of malice, hypocrisy, and spite they have left in people's minds—or continue their hypocrisy-tainted life through dependency on this or that power.

وَ باؤُ بِغَضَبٍ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ ضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الْمَسْكَنَةُ

The word "باءوا" (ba'u) originally means "they returned and took up residence," and here it is a metaphor for becoming deserving; that is, the Jewish people, due to their wrongdoings, became deserving of divine punishment and chose the wrath of the Lord as their "abode."

"مسكنت" (maskanah) means wretchedness, especially severe wretchedness from which escape is difficult. It is originally derived from the root "سكون" (sukun - stillness), because the "miskin" (wretched person) is one who, due to weakness and need, is unable to move or stir.

Furthermore, it should be noted that "miskin" does not only mean one who is needy in terms of wealth and property; rather, any type of wretchedness mixed with weakness and inability is included in its concept. Some believe the difference between "maskanah" (wretchedness) and "dhillah" (humiliation) is that humiliation is imposed by others, while wretchedness refers to a person's internal state of "self-inferiority."

Thus, the above sentence says: due to their wrongdoings, the Jews were first rejected by others and fell under the wrath of Allah, and then gradually this became an inherent trait such that, despite all the resources they possess, they still feel a kind of internal insignificance. For this reason, no exception is seen at the end of this clause in the verse.

ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كانُوا يَكْفُرُونَ بِآياتِ اللَّهِ وَ يقتلون الأنبياء بغير حق...

At the end of the verse, the reason for this grim fate of the Jews is explained. He says that if they met such a fate, it was not because of their race or other characteristics, but because of the actions they committed. Firstly, they denied the signs of Allah; secondly, they persisted in killing divine leaders, the guides of the people, and the saviors of humanity—namely, the Prophets of the Lord; and thirdly, they were tainted with various sins, especially oppression, tyranny, and encroaching upon the rights and interests of other people. Certainly, any nation or people who commit such acts will share a similar fate.

The Dangerous Fate of the Jews

The eventful history of the Jews fully confirms what was said in the above verses, and their current situation also bears witness to this truth. What we read in the verses—"humiliation was stamped upon them"—is not a legislative decree as some commentators have suggested, but rather a creative (takwini) command and a decisive law of history. Any people who drown in sin, make the violation of others' rights part of their program, and strive to eliminate the leaders of human salvation will face such a fate, unless they revise their situation and turn back from this path, or continue their existence for a few days through dependencies they form with others. The events currently unfolding in Islamic countries, the specific positioning of Zionism against Muslims, their placement under the protective umbrella of others, and the various factors threatening their existence are all eloquent witnesses to the reality derived from these verses. Perhaps the bitter experiences of the past and the events that have recently changed the course of their history will cause them to revise their age-old programs, enter into peace and friendship with other nations, and lead a peaceful life with others based on respect for their rights and the cessation of aggression against them.

Ayahs

۞ لَيْسُوا۟ سَوَآءً ۗ مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أُمَّةٌ قَآئِمَةٌ يَتْلُونَ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ءَانَآءَ ٱلَّيْلِ وَهُمْ يَسْجُدُونَ (١١٣)
Yet they are not all alike. Among the People of the Book is an upright nation; they recite Allah’s signs in the watches of the night and prostrate. (113)
يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَيَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَيُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١١٤)
They have faith in Allah and the Last Day, and bid what is right and forbid what is wrong, and they are active in [performing] good deeds. Those are among the righteous. (114)
وَمَا يَفْعَلُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَلَن يُكْفَرُوهُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (١١٥)
Whatever good they do, they will not go unappreciated for it, and Allah knows well the Godwary. (115)

Occasion of Revelation

It is narrated that when Abdullah bin Salam, who was among the Jewish scholars, embraced Islam along with a group of others, the Jews—especially their leaders—became very upset by this event. They sought to accuse them of wickedness so that they would appear lowly in the eyes of other Jews, ensuring their action would not become a model for others. Consequently, the Jewish scholars spread the slogan among them that "only a group of our wicked ones have converted to Islam! If they were righteous people, they would not have abandoned the religion of their ancestors and would not have betrayed the Jewish nation." The above verses were revealed to defend this group.

Commentary

The Spirit of Truth-Seeking in Islam

لَيْسُوا سَواءً مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ أُمَّةٌ قائِمَةٌ يَتْلُونَ آياتِ اللَّهِ آناءَ اللَّيْلِ‌ ... ^1 Following the severe condemnations of the Jewish people in the preceding verses, the Quran, in this verse—to observe justice, respect the rights of worthy individuals, and declare the reality that not all of them can be viewed with the same eye—states:

"The People of the Book are not all alike; in contrast to the transgressors, there are those among them who are steadfast in obeying Allah and standing firm in faith, who constantly recite the verses of Allah in the depths of the night, and prostrate before the majesty of the Lord. They believe in Allah and the Last Day, and they rise to fulfill the duty of enjoining what is right and forbidding what is wrong, and they hasten to do good deeds; and ultimately, they are among the righteous and faithful."

In this way, the Quran refrains from condemning the Jewish race entirely or considering their blood as impure. Instead, it points only to their actions and speaks with honor and respect of those individuals who did not join the corrupt majority and surrendered to faith and truth. This is the method of Islam: in no instance does its struggle take on a racial or tribal color; rather, it revolves solely around the axis of beliefs, deeds, and the conduct of individuals.

Furthermore, it is understood from some narrations that those praised in these verses were not limited to Abdullah bin Salam and his companions from the Jewish people. Rather, forty Christians from Najran, thirty-two Christians from Ethiopia, and eight people from Rome who had accepted Islam by that time are also included in this verse. The expression "People of the Book," which is a broad term, also testifies to this fact.

وَ ما يَفْعَلُوا مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَلَنْ يُكْفَرُوهُ‌ This verse is, in fact, a complement to the previous verses, stating: "This group of the People of the Book will have a worthy reward for the good deeds they perform." That is to say, even if they committed transgressions in the past, now that they have revised their conduct and joined the ranks of the pious and God-fearing, they will see the result of their good deeds and will never experience "ingratitude" from Allah!

The use of the word "Kufr" (disbelief/denial) here is in contrast to "Shukr" (gratitude/appreciation), because gratitude originally means acknowledging a blessing, while "Kufr" and "Kufran" mean denying it. This means that Allah will never ignore their good deeds.

وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِالْمُتَّقِينَ‌ Although Allah is aware of all things, He states in this sentence: "Allah is well-aware of the righteous." It is as if this expression points to the fact that righteous individuals, though they are often in the minority—and specifically formed a weak minority among the Jews contemporary to the Prophet—and naturally such few individuals might not be noticed, they are never hidden from the sharp eye of the Lord's infinite knowledge. Allah is aware of them, and their good deeds, whether small or great, will never be lost.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَن تُغْنِىَ عَنْهُمْ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۚ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (١١٦)
As for the faithless, neither their wealth nor their children will avail them anything against Allah. They shall be the inmates of the Fire and remain in it [forever]. (116)
مَثَلُ مَا يُنفِقُونَ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا كَمَثَلِ رِيحٍ فِيهَا صِرٌّ أَصَابَتْ حَرْثَ قَوْمٍ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فَأَهْلَكَتْهُ ۚ وَمَا ظَلَمَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَلَـٰكِنْ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (١١٧)
The parable of what they spend in the life of this world is that of a cold wind that strikes the tillage of a people who wronged themselves, destroying it. Allah does not wrong them, but they wrong themselves. (117)

Commentary

إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَنْ تُغْنِيَ عَنْهُمْ أَمْوالُهُمْ وَ لا أَوْلادُهُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ شَيْئاً ...

The opposite of the believers and truth-seekers whose description appeared in the previous verse are the faithless and oppressive individuals described in these two verses. First, He says:

"Those who have taken the path of disbelief can never be protected from Allah's punishment by the shelter of their wealth or their numerous children," for on the Day of Resurrection, only pure deeds, sincere intentions, and true faith will be of use, not the material advantages of this world... "يَوْمَ لا يَنْفَعُ مالٌ وَ لا بَنُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ‌":

"The Day when neither wealth nor children will benefit, except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart" (Ash-Shu'ara, Verse 89).

But why does the verse mention only wealth and children among material possibilities? This is because the most important material assets are, firstly, human resources—which are mentioned as children—and secondly, economic capital. All other material possibilities stem from these two sources.

The Quran explicitly states: financial advantages and collective power alone cannot be considered a merit before Allah, and relying on them is a mistake, unless they are utilized in the correct paths under the light of faith and pure intention. Otherwise, the fate of their owners will be eternal punishment: (أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ‌) "Those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally."

مَثَلُ ما يُنْفِقُونَ فِي هذِهِ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا كَمَثَلِ رِيحٍ فِيها صِرٌّ ...

"Sirr" (صِرّ) and "Israr" (اصرار) come from the same root and carry the meaning of binding something with intensity. Here, it refers to the intensity found in a wind, whether it be in the form of a scorching wind or a cold, drying one.

In this verse, reference is made to the state of their hypocritical donations and spending (infaq), and through a striking parable, its fate is explained: The Quran likens the spending of the disbelievers to a fierce, scorching, or extremely cold and drying wind that blows upon a crop and destroys it. Naturally, the nature of a breeze is life-giving; the spring breeze caresses the blossoms, opens the buds, breathes spirit into the frame of the trees, and makes them fruitful. Spending (infaq), too, if it arises from the spring of sincerity and faith, is very beneficial and profitable; it both solves social problems and leaves a deep moral effect on the essence of the benefactor,

and it causes moral virtues and excellences to flourish in his heart.

However, if the gentle and life-giving breeze turns into a deadly, scorching, or extremely cold storm, it burns and withers every flower and plant it blows upon. Faithless and tainted individuals, because they lack a correct motive in their spending, the spirit of showing off and hypocrisy blows like a scorching and drying wind upon the field of their spending and renders it ineffective. Such spending neither solves a problem from a social perspective (as it is often spent on improper causes) nor will it have a moral result for the spender.

It is noteworthy that the Quran says in the above verse: "حَرْثَ قَوْمٍ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ‌" (the harvest of a people who have wronged themselves). This means the center where this scorching and drying wind blows is the cultivation of those who wronged themselves. This points to the fact that these cultivators did not exercise the necessary care in choosing the time and place for cultivation; they either sowed their seeds in a land exposed to such storms, or they chose a time that was the season for the poisonous winds (Samum), and in this way, they wronged themselves. Faithless individuals also wrong themselves in choosing the time and place of spending and waste their capital pointlessly.

From what was mentioned above, and considering the clues present in the verse, it becomes clear that this comparison is actually between two things: one is the comparison of their spending to untimely cultivation in an unsuitable place, and the other is the comparison of the motives for spending to cold and scorching winds. Therefore, the verse is not devoid of an implied meaning (taqdir), and the meaning of the phrase "مَثَلُ ما يُنْفِقُونَ‌" (the likeness of what they spend) is that the example of the motive of their spending is like a dry, cold, or scorching wind (reflect carefully).

A group of commentators have said that this verse refers to the wealth that the enemies of Islam spent to crush this religion, and through which they incited enemies against

the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, or the wealth that the Jews gave to their scholars in exchange for distorting the verses of the Heavenly Books.

However, it is clear that the verse has a broad meaning that includes these and others besides them.

وَ ما ظَلَمَهُمُ اللَّهُ وَ لكِنْ أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ‌:

In the end, He says that Allah has not wronged them; it is they themselves who wrong themselves and waste their capital in vain, for what result can a corrupt action have other than a corrupt effect?

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ بِطَانَةً مِّن دُونِكُمْ لَا يَأْلُونَكُمْ خَبَالًا وَدُّوا۟ مَا عَنِتُّمْ قَدْ بَدَتِ ٱلْبَغْضَآءُ مِنْ أَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَمَا تُخْفِى صُدُورُهُمْ أَكْبَرُ ۚ قَدْ بَيَّنَّا لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (١١٨)
O you who have faith! Do not take your confidants from others than yourselves; they will spare nothing to ruin you. They are eager to see you in distress. Hatred has already shown itself from their mouths, and what their breasts hide [within] is yet worse. We have certainly made the signs clear for you, should you exercise your reason. (118)
هَـٰٓأَنتُمْ أُو۟لَآءِ تُحِبُّونَهُمْ وَلَا يُحِبُّونَكُمْ وَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْكِتَـٰبِ كُلِّهِۦ وَإِذَا لَقُوكُمْ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَإِذَا خَلَوْا۟ عَضُّوا۟ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْأَنَامِلَ مِنَ ٱلْغَيْظِ ۚ قُلْ مُوتُوا۟ بِغَيْظِكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (١١٩)
Ah! You are the ones who bear love towards them, while they do not love you, though you believe in all the Books. When they meet you, they say, ‘We believe,’ but when they are alone, they bite their fingertips at you out of rage. Say, ‘Die of your rage!’ Indeed Allah knows well what is in the breasts. (119)
إِن تَمْسَسْكُمْ حَسَنَةٌ تَسُؤْهُمْ وَإِن تُصِبْكُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَفْرَحُوا۟ بِهَا ۖ وَإِن تَصْبِرُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ لَا يَضُرُّكُمْ كَيْدُهُمْ شَيْـًٔا ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطٌ (١٢٠)
If some good should befall you, it upsets them, but if some ill befalls you, they rejoice at it. Yet if you are patient and Godwary, their guile will not harm you in any way. Indeed Allah encompasses what they do. (120)

Occasion of Revelation

It has been narrated from Ibn Abbas that these verses were revealed when a group of Muslims maintained friendships with Jews due to kinship, neighborly ties, the right of fosterage (breastfeeding), or treaties made before Islam. They were so intimate with them that they would disclose the secrets of the Muslims to them. In this way, the Jewish people—who were staunch enemies of Islam and the Muslims while outwardly pretending to be their friends—became informed of the Muslims' secrets. The verse was revealed to warn that group of Muslims that since they (the Jews) do not share your faith, you must not take them as confidants of your secrets, for they spare no effort in causing you harm and corruption; they desire for you to always be in suffering and distress.

Commentary

Do Not Make Strangers Your Confidants

يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَتَّخِذُوا بِطَانَةً مِنْ دُونِكُمْ ...

"Bitanah" (بطانة) literally means the inner lining of a garment, and its opposite is "Zhiharah" (ظهارة), meaning the outer garment. Here, it is a metaphor for a "confidant" or "intimate friend."

"Khabal" (خبال) originally means the destruction of something, and it is often used to refer to damages that affect a person's intellect.

Following the verses that explained the relations between Muslims and disbelievers, this verse points to one of the sensitive issues and, through a subtle metaphor, warns the believers:

Do not choose friends and confidants from outside your own circle of faith, and do not inform strangers of your internal secrets and mysteries. This means that the disbelievers are not worthy of your friendship, and they should not be your friends or confidants. The disbelievers spare no effort in bringing harm and corruption upon the Muslims (لَا يَأْلُونَكُمْ خَبَالًا).

Never will their past records of friendship and companionship with you prevent them—due to the separation in religion and ideology—from harboring desires for your hardship and loss in their hearts; rather, their constant desire is for you to be in pain and trouble (وَدُّوا مَا عَنِتُّمْ). To prevent you from becoming aware of their inner thoughts and to keep their secrets from being revealed, they usually watch their words and behavior, speaking with caution and precision. However, despite this, signs of enmity and hatred are apparent from between the lines of their speech, and sometimes they unconsciously utter words that can be said to be like sparks from the hidden fire of their hearts, through which you can perceive their inner conscience (قَدْ بَدَتِ الْبَغْضَاءُ مِنْ أَفْوَاهِهِمْ).

This verse expresses a truth that the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, explained in his words where he says:

"مَا أَضْمَرَ أَحَدٌ شَيْئاً إِلَّا ظَهَرَ فِي صَفَحَاتِ وَجْهِهِ أَوْ فَلَتَاتِ لِسَانِهِ"

"No one conceals a secret in their inner self except that it becomes apparent through the expressions of their face or the slips of their tongue."

In short, Allah thereby shows the method of identifying the inner nature of enemies and informs of their inner conscience and hidden secrets, saying: what they have concealed of enmity and hatred in their hearts is far greater than what they utter with their tongues (وَمَا تُخْفِي صُدُورُهُمْ أَكْبَرُ).

Then He added: We have made these verses clear to you, so that if you reflect upon them,

you may distinguish your friend from your enemy through them and find the way to salvation from the evil of the enemies (قَدْ بَيَّنَّا لَكُمُ الْآيَاتِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ).

هَا أَنْتُمْ أُولَاءِ تُحِبُّونَهُمْ وَلَا يُحِبُّونَكُمْ وَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْكِتَابِ كُلِّهِ ...

In this verse, He says: You, O group of Muslims, love them because of kinship, proximity, or other reasons, unaware that they do not love you, while you believe in all the Books revealed by Allah (including your own Book and their heavenly Books), but they do not believe in your heavenly Book.

وَإِذَا لَقُوكُمْ قَالُوا آمَنَّا وَإِذَا خَلَوْا عَضُّوا عَلَيْكُمُ الْأَنَامِلَ مِنَ الْغَيْظِ:

This group of the People of the Book are hypocrites; when they meet you, they say, "We believe and we attest to your religion," but when they are alone, they bite their fingertips out of the intensity of malice, enmity, and rage. قُلْ مُوتُوا بِغَيْظِكُمْ.

Say, O Prophet: "Die in your rage!" and this grief will not leave you until the day of death.

إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ بِذَاتِ الصُّدُورِ:

You were not aware of their condition, but Allah is aware, "for Allah is Knower of the secrets within the breasts."

إِنْ تَمْسَسْكُمْ حَسَنَةٌ تَسُؤْهُمْ وَإِنْ تُصِبْكُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَفْرَحُوا بِهَا ...

In this verse, one of the signs of their malice and enmity is recounted: "If a victory, success, or good event happens to you, it grieves them; and if a misfortune befalls you, they rejoice at it."

وَإِنْ تَصْبِرُوا وَتَتَّقُوا لَا يَضُرُّكُمْ كَيْدُهُمْ شَيْئاً إِنَّ اللَّهَ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطٌ: "But if you persevere against their malice and remain God-wary and self-controlled, they cannot harm you in the least through their treacherous schemes, for Allah is fully encompassing of what they do."

Therefore, it is understood from the end of the verse that the security of Muslims against the sinister plots of enemies is conditional upon perseverance, vigilance, and having Taqwa (piety/God-wariness), and only in this case is their security guaranteed.

A Warning to Muslims

In this verse, Allah has warned the believers not to make their enemies part of their inner circle and not to reveal the secrets of the Muslims, their strengths, or their weaknesses to this group. This warning is general and universal, and Muslims must pay attention to this warning at all times and in every situation.

Unfortunately, however, many followers of the Quran have neglected this warning, and as a result, they have been afflicted with numerous disorders. Even now, there are enemies surrounding the Muslims who feign friendship and outwardly support the Muslims, but through the actions they display, it becomes clear that they are lying. Muslims are deceived by their outward appearance and trust them, whereas they want nothing for the Muslims except distress, misery, and ruin, and they do not hesitate to throw thorns in their path and cast their affairs into difficulty.

We need not look far; in recent years, Muslims were involved in two major wars with their enemies. In the first war, they suffered a very painful defeat, while in the recent war, a brilliant victory was granted to them, and almost all global events changed in their favor. The terrifying and daunting myth of the enemy's invincibility was buried forever on the very first day of the war in the Sinai Desert and the Golan Heights, and Muslims tasted victory for the first time in recent years.

What caused this transformation in such a short time? The answer to this question requires a long discussion, but certainly, one of the effective factors of that defeat and this victory was that in the first war, strangers—some of whom even spoke of friendship—were aware of their plans. But in the recent war, no one except two or three leaders of Islamic countries had knowledge of their plans, and this was itself a great secret of their victory and a living witness to the greatness of this heavenly command of the Quran!

Ayahs

وَإِذْ غَدَوْتَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ تُبَوِّئُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ مَقَـٰعِدَ لِلْقِتَالِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (١٢١)
When you left your family at dawn to settle the faithful in their positions for battle —and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (121)
إِذْ هَمَّت طَّآئِفَتَانِ مِنكُمْ أَن تَفْشَلَا وَٱللَّهُ وَلِيُّهُمَا ۗ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (١٢٢)
When two groups among you were about to lose courage—though Allah is their guardian, and in Allah let all the faithful put their trust. (122)

Commentary

وَ إِذْ غَدَوْتَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ تُبَوِّئُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ مَقاعِدَ لِلْقِتالِ وَ اللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ From here, verses begin that were revealed regarding an important and extensive Islamic event, namely the Battle of "Uhud." Evidence within the above verses suggests they were revealed after the Battle of Uhud, referring to a portion of the events of this harrowing war, a view held by most commentators.

At the beginning, it refers to the Prophet leaving Medina to select a campsite at the foot of Uhud, saying: "And remember, O Prophet, when you set out in the morning from your household to assign the believers to their posts for battle."

On that day, there was much discussion among the Muslims. As will be mentioned shortly in the account of the event of Uhud, there was a sharp disagreement among the Muslims regarding the choice of the battlefield—whether it should be inside or outside Medina. After sufficient consultation, the Prophet chose the opinion of the majority, a large number of whom were young men, and moved the campsite outside the city to the foot of Mount Uhud. Naturally, there were individuals among them who harbored hidden thoughts and, for various reasons, were not willing to express them. The phrase "وَ اللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ" (And Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing) likely refers to all of this, meaning: "Allah heard your words and was aware of the secrets within you."

إِذْ هَمَّتْ طائِفَتانِ مِنْكُمْ أَنْ تَفْشَلا ...

Then, referring to another aspect of this event, He says: "When two parties among you (who, according to historical accounts, were the 'Banu Salama' from the Aws tribe and the 'Banu Haritha' from the Khazraj tribe) were about to lose heart and turn back to Medina midway."

The reason for this decision might have been that they were supporters of the "war within the city" theory, and the Prophet had gone against their view. Additionally, as we shall mention:

"Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul," along with three hundred Jews who had joined the Islamic army, returned to Medina because the Prophet opposed their staying in the Islamic camp. This event weakened the resolve of those two Muslim parties to continue the path they had taken.

However, as inferred from the end of the verse, those two parties soon turned back from their decision and continued their cooperation with the Muslims. Therefore, the Quran says: وَ اللَّهُ وَلِيُّهُما وَ عَلَى اللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ: "And Allah was their Ally and Protector, and in Allah let the believers put their trust."

Furthermore, it should be noted that mentioning the story of Uhud following the verses regarding not trusting the disbelievers is a reference to a living example of this reality. For, as we mentioned and will explain in detail later, the Prophet did not allow the Jews—who had ostensibly risen to support him—to remain in the Islamic camp. This was because they were outsiders, and outsiders must not be confidants of secrets or a pillar of support for Muslims in such sensitive conditions.

The Battle of Uhud: The Origins of the Conflict

Here, it is necessary to first provide an overview of the events of Uhud: It is gathered from Islamic narrations and histories that when the Quraysh were defeated in the Battle of Badr and returned to Mecca having lost seventy killed and seventy taken prisoner, Abu Sufyan warned the people of Mecca not to let the women weep for those killed at Badr. He argued that tears dissipate grief and erase enmity and hatred toward Muhammad from their hearts. Abu Sufyan himself had vowed not to sleep with his wife until he had taken revenge on the killers of the Battle of Badr! In any case, the Quraysh tribe used every means at their disposal to incite the people to war against the Muslims, and the cry of "Revenge! Revenge!" echoed through the city of Mecca.

In the third year of the Hijra, the Quraysh set out from Mecca with the intention of fighting the Prophet, with three thousand cavalry and two thousand infantry, fully equipped. To ensure greater steadfastness on the battlefield, they also brought their great idols and their women with them.

Abbas's Timely Report

Abbas, the Prophet's uncle, had not yet embraced Islam and remained among the Quraysh following their faith. However, because he was very fond of his nephew, when he saw the powerful army of the Quraysh departing Mecca with the intent to fight the Prophet, he immediately wrote a letter and sent it to Medina via a man from the "Banu Ghifar" tribe.

Abbas's messenger rushed toward Medina. When the Prophet became aware of the situation, he met with "Sa'd ibn Abi [Waqqas]" and shared Abbas's report with him, making an effort to keep the matter hidden for a time.

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) Consults the Muslims

On the same day the letter from Abbas reached the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), he ordered several Muslims to go toward the Mecca-Medina road to gather information about the state of the Quraysh army.

The two scouts sent by Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) to gather information soon returned and informed the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) of the strength of the Quraysh forces, stating that this powerful army was under the command of Abu Sufyan himself.

After a few days, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) invited all the companions and the people of Medina and formed a meeting to address the situation, openly discussing the matter of defense with them. He then consulted the Muslims on whether to fight inside Medina or to go out of the city. Some said they should not leave Medina and should fight the enemy in the narrow streets of the city, as in that case, even weak men, women, and female servants could assist the army.

"Abdullah ibn Ubayy," after saying these words, added: "O Messenger of Allah! It has never been seen that we were inside our fortifications and homes and the enemy triumphed over us!"

This view was also considered by the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) due to the specific situation of Medina at that time; he too wanted to stay in Medina and confront the Quraysh inside the city. However, a group of young men and warriors opposed this view. "Sa'd ibn Mu'adh" and several men from the Aws tribe stood up and said: "O Prophet! In the past, no one among the Arabs had the power to covet us, even when we were polytheists and idolaters. Now that you are among us, how can they covet us? No, we must certainly go out of the city and fight the enemy. If any of us is killed, he has drunk the draught of martyrdom, and if anyone survives, he has attained the honor of Jihad in the way of Allah."

Such speeches and epic sentiments increased the supporters of leaving Medina to the point that "Abdullah ibn Ubayy's" plan fell into the minority.

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself, although he did not favor leaving Medina, respected this consultation and chose the opinion of the supporters of leaving the city. He went out of the city with one of the companions to prepare the campsite and chose a location at the foot of Mount Uhud, which held a sensitive position in terms of military conditions.

The Muslims Prepare for Defense

That day was Friday when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) conducted this consultation. Afterward, he stood to deliver the Friday prayer sermon. After praising and glorifying the One God, he informed the Muslims of the approach of the Quraysh army and said:

"If you are prepared for war with heart and soul and fight the enemies with such a spirit, Allah will certainly grant you victory." On that same day, he set out for the campsite with one thousand men from the Muhajirun and Ansar.

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) personally took command of the army. Before leaving Medina, he ordered three banners to be prepared: one for the Muhajirun and two for the Ansar.

The Prophet traveled the distance between Medina and Uhud on foot. Along the way, he inspected the ranks of the army and personally arranged and organized the ranks so they would move in a straight and direct line.

The famous historian "Halabi" writes in his book: Before the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) reached Uhud, while inspecting the army, he saw a group among them he had never seen before. He asked, "Who are these?" They replied, "A group of Jews who were allies of 'Abdullah ibn Ubayy' and have come to help the Muslims for this reason." The Prophet paused and said: "One cannot seek help from polytheists to fight polytheists, unless they become Muslims." The Jews did not accept this proposal and all returned to Medina. Thus, the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) force of one thousand was reduced by three hundred [1]. However, commentators have written that because "Abdullah ibn Ubayy's" proposal was not accepted, he withdrew his cooperation midway and returned to Medina with a group numbering three hundred.

In any case, after the necessary purging, the Prophet reached the foot of Mount Uhud with his forces, which numbered seven hundred. After performing the morning prayer, he arranged the ranks of the Muslims.

He appointed "Abdullah ibn Jubayr" with fifty skilled archers to be stationed at the mouth of the mountain pass, strictly instructing them not to move from their positions under any circumstances and to protect the rear of the army. He said: "Even if we pursue the enemy to Mecca, or if the enemy defeats us and forces us to retreat to Medina, do not leave your post."

On the other side, Abu Sufyan placed "Khalid ibn Walid" with two hundred elite soldiers to watch this pass and ordered: "Stay in ambush until the Muslim soldiers move away from this valley, then immediately attack the Islamic army from behind."

The Battle Began

The two armies lined up against each other, prepared for war. Each of these armies encouraged its men in its own way.

Abu Sufyan, in the name of the idols of the Kaaba and by drawing the attention of beautiful women, brought his warriors to a state of enthusiasm!

[1]: Sirah al-Halabiyyah, Vol. 2, account of the Battle of Uhud.

But the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), in the name of Allah and divine blessings, encouraged the Muslims to fight.

Now, the sound of "Allahu Akbar, Allahu Akbar" from the Muslims filled the entire plain and the slopes of Uhud. On the other side of the field, the women and daughters of the Quraysh sang poems, likely accompanied by tambourines and flutes, to stir the emotions and feelings of the Quraysh warriors.

After the battle began, the Muslims, with a fierce attack, were able to break the Quraysh army. They took to flight, and the soldiers of Islam pursued them.

Khalid ibn Walid, seeing the defeat of the Quraysh as certain, wanted to exit through the valley and attack the Muslims from behind, but the archers forced them to retreat.

This retreat of the Quraysh caused a group of new Muslims, thinking the enemy was defeated, to suddenly abandon their posts to collect spoils.
