Even the archers standing atop the mountain left their positions and rushed into the battlefield. No matter how much Abdullah ibn Jubayr reminded them of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) command, only a few—numbering about ten—remained at their sensitive post.

The result of disobeying the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) command was that "Khalid ibn Walid," with the other two hundred men in ambush, seeing the mountain pass empty of guards, quickly charged at "Abdullah ibn Jubayr" and killed him and his companions, then attacked the Islamic army from the rear.

Suddenly, the Muslims found themselves under the enemy's swords from all sides. Their order and coordination vanished. The fugitives of the Quraysh army, seeing the situation thus, returned and surrounded the Muslims in a circle. At this moment, the brave officer of Islam, Hamza, the Master of Martyrs, along with some other brave companions of the Prophet, drank the draught of martyrdom. Except for a limited number who surrounded their leader like moths, the rest fled in terror.

In this dangerous battle, the one who sacrificed more than anyone else and repelled every attack made by the enemy against the Prophet was Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام).

Ali (عليه السلام) fought with utmost bravery until his sword broke. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) gave his own sword, known as "Dhul-Fiqar," to Ali (عليه السلام). Finally, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) took cover in a spot, and Ali (عليه السلام) continued to defend him until, according to some historians, more than sixty wounds were inflicted upon his head, face, and body. It was at this time that the messenger of revelation said to the Prophet: "O Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله)! This is the meaning of self-sacrifice (muwasat)." The Prophet said: "Ali (عليه السلام) is from me and I am from him," and Gabriel added: "And I am from both of you!" Imam Sadiq (عليه السلام) says: The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) saw the messenger of revelation between the earth and the sky saying: "There is no sword but Dhul-Fiqar, and no brave youth but Ali." [1] In the meantime, a cry arose: "Muhammad has been killed!"

Who Cried Out Loudly that Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) was Killed?

Some biographers say: "Ibn Qami'ah," who struck down "Mus'ab," an Islamic soldier, thinking he was the Prophet, then shouted loudly: "By Lat and Uzza, Muhammad has been killed!" Whether this rumor came from the Muslims or the enemy, it was undoubtedly to the benefit of Islam and the Muslims. For the enemy, believing Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) was killed, left Uhud for Mecca. Otherwise, the victorious Quraysh army, who harbored the most intense hatred and enmity toward the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and had come with the intent to take revenge on him this time, would not have left Uhud without killing him.

The five-thousand-strong force of the Quraysh did not even want to stay until morning in the battlefield after their victory; they immediately took the road to Mecca and departed! The news of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) death caused even more instability among a group of Muslims. Those Muslims who were on the battlefield, to prevent the rest from scattering and to remove their instability and anxiety, took the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) up the mountain to show the Muslims that the Prophet was alive. The fugitives returned and gathered around him. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) reproached the fugitives for fleeing in such a dangerous situation. The Muslims, though ashamed, began to offer excuses, saying: "O Prophet of Allah, we heard the news of your death and fled out of intense fear."

Thus, in the Battle of Uhud, the Muslims suffered great financial and human losses; seventy Muslims were killed on the battlefield, and many lay wounded. However, the Muslims learned a great lesson from the defeat, which became a guarantee of their victory in future battlefields. In the coming verses, a broad analysis of the various effects of this great event will be conducted—God willing.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1, p. 497.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ نَصَرَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِبَدْرٍ وَأَنتُمْ أَذِلَّةٌ ۖ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (١٢٣)
Certainly Allah helped you at Badr, when you were weak [in the enemy’s eyes]. So be wary of Allah so that you may give thanks. (123)
إِذْ تَقُولُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَلَن يَكْفِيَكُمْ أَن يُمِدَّكُمْ رَبُّكُم بِثَلَـٰثَةِ ءَالَـٰفٍ مِّنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ مُنزَلِينَ (١٢٤)
When you were saying to the faithful, ‘Is it not enough for you that your Lord should aid you with three thousand angels sent down?’ (124)
بَلَىٰٓ ۚ إِن تَصْبِرُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ وَيَأْتُوكُم مِّن فَوْرِهِمْ هَـٰذَا يُمْدِدْكُمْ رَبُّكُم بِخَمْسَةِ ءَالَـٰفٍ مِّنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ مُسَوِّمِينَ (١٢٥)
Yes, if you are steadfast and Godwary, and should they come at you suddenly, your Lord will aid you with five thousand angels sent in [to the scene of battle]. (125)
وَمَا جَعَلَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا بُشْرَىٰ لَكُمْ وَلِتَطْمَئِنَّ قُلُوبُكُم بِهِۦ ۗ وَمَا ٱلنَّصْرُ إِلَّا مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (١٢٦)
Allah did not appoint it but as a good news for you and to reassure with it your hearts; and victory comes only from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise, (126)
لِيَقْطَعَ طَرَفًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَوْ يَكْبِتَهُمْ فَيَنقَلِبُوا۟ خَآئِبِينَ (١٢٧)
that He may cut down a section of the faithless, or subdue them, so that they retreat disappointed. (127)

Commentary

The Dangerous Phase of the War

After the Battle of Uhud ended, the victorious army of the polytheists quickly returned toward Mecca. However, along the way, the thought occurred to them: why did they leave their victory incomplete? It would be better to return to Medina, loot the city, crush the Muslims, and if Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله were still alive, kill him, so they could be forever relieved of the threat of Islam and the Muslims. Consequently, the order to return was issued. In reality, this was the most dangerous phase of the Battle of Uhud, as the Muslims had suffered enough casualties and wounds, and naturally, they had no readiness at that moment to renew the war. Conversely, the enemy, with high morale, could restart the battle and predict its final outcome.

This news soon reached the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and had it not been for his extraordinary courage and unique initiative—which was rooted in Divine revelation—perhaps the history of Islam would have ended right there.

The above verses were revealed regarding this sensitive stage, working to strengthen the morale of the Muslims. Following this, a general command was issued by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to move toward the polytheists. Even the wounded of the war (including Ali عليه السلام, who had more than sixty wounds on his body) prepared to fight the enemy and set out from Medina.

This news reached the ears of the leaders of the Quraysh, and they were struck with terror by this incredible morale of the Muslims! They thought perhaps a fresh force from Medina had joined the Muslims, and a new encounter might change the final result of the war to their detriment. Therefore, they decided that to preserve their victory, it was better to return to Mecca.

This is exactly what happened, and they quickly took the road to Mecca.

وَلَقَدْ نَصَرَكُمُ اللَّهُ بِبَدْرٍ وَأَنْتُمْ أَذِلَّةٌ We mentioned that these verses were actually revealed to strengthen the broken morale of the Muslims. First, they refer to the striking victory of the Muslims on the battlefield of Badr [^1] so that by recalling that memory, they might be encouraged about their future. Thus, He says:

"Allah granted you victory at Badr while you were weak compared to the enemy, and in terms of numbers and equipment, you were not comparable to them." (Your number was 313 with little equipment, while the polytheists were more than a thousand with abundant equipment.) Since this is the case, fear Allah and avoid repeating the opposition to the command of your leader, namely the Prophet, so that you may show gratitude for His various blessings. (فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ)

إِذْ تَقُولُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَلَنْ يَكْفِيَكُمْ أَنْ يُمِدَّكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ بِثَلَاثَةِ آلَافٍ مِنَ الْمَلَائِكَةِ... Then, He recalls the memory of aiding the Muslims on the field of Badr through the angels and says: "Do not forget that on that day the Prophet said to you: 'Is it not enough for you that your Lord should reinforce you with three thousand angels sent down?'"

بَلَىٰ إِنْ تَصْبِرُوا وَتَتَّقُوا وَيَأْتُوكُمْ مِنْ فَوْرِهِمْ [^2] هَٰذَا يُمْدِدْكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ بِخَمْسَةِ آلَافٍ مِنَ الْمَلَائِكَةِ مُسَوِّمِينَ. "Yes, today as well, if you show endurance and hasten to meet the army of the Quraysh,

[^1]: "Badr" was originally the name of a man who owned a water well in the land between Mecca and Medina; therefore, that land was named "Badr" after him. From a linguistic root, "Badr" means full and complete, which is why the moon on the fourteenth night is called "Badr" (full moon).

[^2]: "Fawr" originally means the boiling and bubbling of a pot and the like. For this reason, the word is used for actions performed with speed, as they occur quickly like the boiling and overturning of a pot's contents.

and practice piety, and do not oppose the Prophet's command as you did yesterday—if the polytheists should return to you in haste, Allah will reinforce you with five thousand angels, all having distinct marks."

وَمَا جَعَلَهُ اللَّهُ إِلَّا بُشْرَىٰ لَكُمْ وَلِتَطْمَئِنَّ قُلُوبُكُمْ بِهِ وَمَا النَّصْرُ إِلَّا مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ... "But be aware that the coming of the angels to aid you is only as a glad tiding for you and to reassure your hearts and strengthen your morale; otherwise, victory comes only from Allah, who is All-Mighty and All-Wise in all His actions." He knows the path to victory and has the power to execute it.

لِيَقْطَعَ طَرَفًا مِنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَوْ يَكْبِتَهُمْ فَيَنْقَلِبُوا خَائِبِينَ: Although commentators have various interpretations of this verse, given the path we have taken in interpreting the previous verses with the help of the verses themselves and existing histories, the interpretation of this verse is also clear. Allah says: "The reason you were promised that angels would be sent to aid you in a new encounter with the enemy is to cut off a part of the body of the polytheist army and return them in humiliation and disgrace." It should be noted that "Taraf" in the verse means a part or segment, and "Yakbitahum" is from the root "Kabt," meaning to repel by force accompanied by humiliation.

Here, questions arise regarding the nature of the angels' assistance, their support for the Muslims, and how this aid was manifested. God willing, we will provide a detailed answer to this in Surah Al-Anfal under verses 7 to 12.

Ayah

لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ شَىْءٌ أَوْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ أَوْ يُعَذِّبَهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ (١٢٨)
You have no hand in the matter, whether He accepts their repentance or punishes them, for they are indeed wrongdoers. (128)

Commentary

There is a great deal of discussion among commentators regarding the interpretation of this verse, but it is almost certain that the above verse was revealed after the Battle of "Uhud" and is related to its events; the preceding verses also confirm this fact.

As for the interpretation of the verse, two meanings attract the most attention:

First, this verse constitutes an independent sentence. Therefore, the phrase "أَوْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ" (or He turns to them in mercy) means "unless He turns to them in mercy." Thus, the overall meaning of the verse becomes: "Regarding their fate, nothing is in your hands, unless Allah wills to forgive them or punish them for the injustice they have committed." The "they" refers either to the disbelievers who dealt heavy blows to the Muslims in this battle and even broke the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله tooth and forehead, or to the Muslims who fled the battlefield and later regretted it after the war ended and asked the Prophet for forgiveness. The verse says: "Pardon, forgiveness, or their punishment and torment is in the hands of Allah, and even the Prophet does not do anything without the permission of the Lord."

The second interpretation is that "لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ شَيْءٌ" (Not for you is any decision) is a "parenthetical" sentence, and the phrase "أَوْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ" is conjoined to the phrase "أَوْ يَكْبِتَهُمْ" (or disgrace them) [from the previous verse]. In this case, this verse is considered a continuation of the previous verse. Thus, the combined meaning of the two verses would be:

"Allah will provide you with the means of victory and ordains one of four fates for the disbelievers: He either destroys a portion of the polytheists' army, or He forces them to retreat through this means, or He forgives them if they are worthy and repent, or He punishes them for their oppression. In short, He will deal with each group of them according to wisdom and justice, and you cannot make any decision regarding them on your own."

Regarding the occasion of revelation for this verse, several narrations have been cited. Among them is that after the Prophet’s tooth and forehead were broken in the Battle of "Uhud," and after all those severe blows were dealt to the Muslims, the Prophet became concerned about the future of the polytheists. He thought to himself how such a population could ever be guided and said:

"كيف يفلح قوم فعلوا هذا بنبيهم و هو يدعوهم الى ربهم" "How shall a people prosper who treat their Prophet in such a manner while he calls them toward their Lord?"

The above verse was revealed and comforted the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, stating that he is not responsible for their guidance, but rather his only duty is to convey the message to them.

Correcting a Misconception

Two points must be noted here:

  1. The famous commentator, the author of "Al-Manar," believes that this verse teaches Muslims a great lesson regarding the use of natural means for victory. That is, if Allah promises them victory, it does not mean that Muslims should forget natural means, military equipment, war plans, and the like, and sit idly by, always waiting for the Prophet's prayer for victory. Therefore, addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it says: "لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ شَيْءٌ," meaning victory has not been delegated to you; rather, it is by Allah's command, and Allah has established laws for it that must be utilized (and the Prophet's prayer, although effective and beneficial, is an exceptional matter and specific to certain cases).

While the statement of this author in this part is logical, it is in no way consistent with the end of the verse, which discusses the repentance or punishment of the disbelievers. Therefore, it cannot be considered the [primary] interpretation of the verse.

  1. This verse, which strips the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله of any independent authority regarding the pardon, forgiveness, or punishment of opponents, has no contradiction with the effectiveness of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله prayer, his pardon and forgiveness, or intercession and the like, as derived from other verses of the Quran. This is because the purpose of the above verse is that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله cannot do anything independently. However, by Allah's command and His permission, he can forgive, punish in the appropriate place, and provide the factors for victory. He can even, by the permission and leave of the Lord, bring the dead to life just as the Messiah عليه السلام did.

Those who have taken the phrase "لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ شَيْءٌ" and sought to deny the Prophet's ability in this matter have, in fact, forgotten other verses of the Quran.

The Holy Quran says in Surah An-Nisa, verse 64: "وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ إِذْ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ جَاءُوكَ فَاسْتَغْفَرُوا اللَّهَ وَاسْتَغْفَرَ لَهُمُ الرَّسُولُ لَوَجَدُوا اللَّهَ تَوَّابًا رَحِيمًا"

"And if, when they wronged themselves, they had come to you and asked forgiveness of Allah and the Messenger had asked forgiveness for them, they would have found Allah Accepting of repentance and Merciful."

According to this verse, the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله seeking of forgiveness is counted as one of the factors for the forgiveness of sins. In future discussions under the appropriate verses, we will further explain this matter.

Ayah

وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ يَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٢٩)
To Allah belongs whatever there is in the heavens and the earth: He forgives whomever He wishes and punishes whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (129)

Commentary

This verse is, in fact, an emphasis on the previous verse, stating: "Forgiveness and punishment are not in the hands of the Prophet; rather, they are by the command of Allah, to Whom belongs the sovereignty of whatever is in the heavens and the earth. Creation is His, and their ownership and governance also belong to Him."

Therefore, He is the One who can forgive sinners whenever He deems it appropriate, or punish them.

وَ اللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌.

While His punishment is severe, He is All-Forgiving and Most Merciful, and His mercy precedes His wrath.

It is worth mentioning here the statement of one of the Islamic scholars which, despite being concise, serves as an answer to certain questions. The esteemed commentator "Tabarsi," under this verse, narrates that a scholar was asked: "How does Allah punish His servants for their sins, despite His vast and infinite mercy?"

He replied: "The 'Mercy' of Allah does not negate His 'Wisdom,' for His mercy—unlike our sense of pity—does not stem from emotions or a softening of the heart. Rather, His mercy is always intertwined with wisdom, and wisdom necessitates that sinners (except in specific cases) receive their punishment."

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَأْكُلُوا۟ ٱلرِّبَوٰٓا۟ أَضْعَـٰفًا مُّضَـٰعَفَةً ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (١٣٠)
O you who have faith! Do not exact usury, twofold and severalfold, and be wary of Allah so that you may be felicitous. (130)
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱلنَّارَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أُعِدَّتْ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٣١)
Beware of the Fire, which has been prepared for the faithless, (131)
وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلرَّسُولَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (١٣٢)
and obey Allah and the Apostle so that you may be granted [His] mercy. (132)

Commentary

The Connection Between Quranic Verses

The preceding verses—as we have learned—pertain to the Battle of "Uhud," its related events, and the various lessons the Muslims derived from that incident. However, these three verses and the six verses following them contain a series of economic, social, and educational programs. After the conclusion of these nine verses, the events related to the Battle of Uhud are once again addressed.

This style of presentation might cause wonder for some, but by considering a fundamental principle, the reality of the matter becomes clear:

The Quran is not a classic book divided into chapters and sections with a specific compositional system intended between its parts. Rather, the Quran is a book that was revealed "nujuman" (gradually) over a period of twenty-three years according to various educational needs at different times and places. One day, the story of Uhud would occur, and various military programs would be announced within several verses; another day, a need would be felt for an economic issue such as usury (riba), a legal matter like the rules of marriage, or an educational and moral issue like repentance, and multiple verses would be revealed accordingly.

From this, it can be concluded that in many cases, there may not be a specific link between the preceding and succeeding verses. One should not, like some commentators, insist on finding a relationship between them in such instances. We should not burden ourselves by artificially creating a connection between matters where Allah did not intend a connection, for such an effort is inconsistent with the spirit of the Quran and the nature of its revelation during various events and according to diverse needs under separate conditions and circumstances.

Of course, all surahs and verses of the Quran are related from one perspective: they all pursue a high-level program of human development and education, and they were revealed to build a prosperous, aware, secure, and advanced society both materially and spiritually.

Therefore, if the aforementioned nine verses do not have a specific relationship with the verses before and after them, it is for this very reason.

The Prohibition of Usury in Several Stages

We know that the Quran's method in combating deep-rooted social deviations is to gradually prepare the ground and familiarize public opinion with their harms. Once the readiness to accept the final prohibition is achieved, it announces the law explicitly (especially in cases where the indulgence in sin is widespread and extensive).

Furthermore, we know that the Arabs during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) were heavily involved in usury, and the environment of Mecca, in particular, was a hub for usurers. Many of their social miseries stemmed from this ugly and oppressive practice. For this reason, to uproot usury, the Quran expressed the ruling of prohibition in four stages:

  1. In Surah Ar-Rum, verse 39, it first contents itself with a moral counsel regarding "riba," where it says: وَ ما آتَيْتُمْ مِنْ رِباً لِيَرْبُوَا فِي أَمْوالِ النَّاسِ فَلا يَرْبُوا عِنْدَ اللَّهِ وَ ما آتَيْتُمْ مِنْ زَكاةٍ تُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ اللَّهِ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُضْعِفُونَ. In this way, it announces that it is only from the perspective of short-sighted individuals that the wealth of usurers increases through taking interest; however, in the presence of Allah, nothing is added to them. Rather, it is Zakat and spending in the way of Allah that increases wealth.

  2. In Surah An-Nisa, verse 161, while criticizing the wrong customs and traditions of the Jews, it refers to their ugly habit of usury and says: وَ أَخْذِهِمُ الرِّبَوا وَ قَدْ نُهُوا عَنْهُ: "Another of their bad habits was that they took usury, even though they had been forbidden from it."

  3. In the verse under discussion, as we will mention during its commentary, the ruling of the prohibition of usury is explicitly stated, but it refers only to one type among the types of usury, which is the severe and flagrant kind.

  4. Finally, in Surah Al-Baqarah, verses 275 to 279, every form of usury is strictly declared forbidden and is described as being at war with Allah.

يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَأْكُلُوا الرِّبَا أَضْعَافًا مُضَاعَفَةً ...

We said that this verse refers to the prohibition of flagrant usury, expressed by the term "doubled and multiplied" (أَضْعَافًا مُضَاعَفَةً). What is meant by flagrant usury is that the capital moves in an exponential manner; that is, the interest in the first stage is added to the original capital, and the total becomes subject to usury. In this way, at every stage, "interest plus capital" forms a new capital. Consequently, in a short time, through the accumulation of interest, the total debt of the debtor increases to several times the original debt, and he is completely ruined in life.

As gathered from narrations and history, it was customary during the Age of Ignorance that if a debtor could not pay his debt at the end of the term, he would request the creditor to lend him the total of the interest and the original debt as a new capital and take interest on that! In our era as well, this extremely oppressive form of usury is prevalent among usurers.

وَ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ At the end of the verse, He says: "If you wish to be successful, you must practice Taqwa (God-wariness) and abstain from this sin."

وَ اتَّقُوا النَّارَ الَّتِي أُعِدَّتْ لِلْكافِرِينَ This verse again emphasizes the command of Taqwa and warns against the Fire that awaits the disbelievers.

From the expression "disbelievers" (كافِرِينَ), it is understood that usury is fundamentally incompatible with the spirit of faith, and usurers have a share of the Fire that awaits the disbelievers! It is also understood that the Fire of Hell was originally prepared for the disbelievers, and sinners and the disobedient will have a share of it to the extent that they resemble and align with the disbelievers.

وَ أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَ الرَّسُولَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ The threat of the previous verse is completed by the encouragement mentioned in this verse for the obedient, saying: "Obey Allah and the Prophet and abandon usury so that you may be included in Divine mercy."

Ayahs

۞ وَسَارِعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ مَغْفِرَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَجَنَّةٍ عَرْضُهَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ أُعِدَّتْ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٣٣)
Hasten towards your Lord’s forgiveness and a paradise as vast as the heavens and the earth, prepared for the Godwary (133)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ فِى ٱلسَّرَّآءِ وَٱلضَّرَّآءِ وَٱلْكَـٰظِمِينَ ٱلْغَيْظَ وَٱلْعَافِينَ عَنِ ٱلنَّاسِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٣٤)
—those who spend in ease and adversity, and suppress their anger, and excuse [the faults of] the people, and Allah loves the virtuous; (134)
وَٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا فَعَلُوا۟ فَـٰحِشَةً أَوْ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ ذَكَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فَٱسْتَغْفَرُوا۟ لِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَمَن يَغْفِرُ ٱلذُّنُوبَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ وَلَمْ يُصِرُّوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَعَلُوا۟ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (١٣٥)
and those who, when they commit an indecent act or wrong themselves, remember Allah, and plead [to Allah seeking His] forgiveness for their sins—and who forgives sins except Allah?—and who knowingly do not persist in what [sins] they have committed. (135)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ جَزَآؤُهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ وَجَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ وَنِعْمَ أَجْرُ ٱلْعَـٰمِلِينَ (١٣٦)
Their reward is forgiveness from their Lord, and gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever]. How excellent is the reward of the workers! (136)

Commentary

The Race in the Path of Prosperity

وَ سارِعُوا إِلى‌ مَغْفِرَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ‌ "Sāri‘ū" is derived from "Musāra‘ah," which means the effort and struggle of two or more people to overtake one another in reaching a goal. In righteous deeds, it is praiseworthy, and in evil deeds, it is reprehensible.

Following the previous verses that threatened evildoers with the punishment of Fire and encouraged the righteous toward Divine mercy, this verse likens the efforts and struggles of the righteous to a spiritual race whose ultimate goal is Divine forgiveness and the eternal blessings of Paradise. It says: Hasten to surpass one another to reach this goal.

In truth, the Quran utilizes a psychological point here: when a human performs a task alone, they usually do it without speed and in an ordinary manner. However, if it takes the form of a competition—especially one where a valuable prize has been set—they employ all their strength and energy and charge toward the goal with the utmost speed.

The reason the goal of this race is primarily set as "forgiveness" is that reaching any spiritual station is impossible without being forgiven and cleansed of sin. First, one must wash oneself of sin and then step into the station of proximity to the Lord!

وَ جَنَّةٍ عَرْضُهَا السَّماواتُ وَ الْأَرْضُ: The second goal of this spiritual race is Paradise—a Paradise whose width is the expanse of the heavens and the earth. (It should be noted that "width" [‘ard] in this verse does not refer to the geometric term as opposed to "length," but rather to its literal meaning, which is "expanse" or "vastness").

In this way, the Quran explicitly states: the vastness of Paradise is the same as the vastness of the heavens and the earth.

In verse 21 of Surah Al-Hadid, the same expression is seen with a slight difference: "سابِقُوا إِلى‌ مَغْفِرَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَ جَنَّةٍ عَرْضُها كَعَرْضِ السَّماءِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌" (Race toward forgiveness from your Lord and a Paradise whose width is like the width of the heaven and the earth). In that verse, instead of "Musāra‘ah," the word "Musābaqah" (racing/competition) is explicitly mentioned, and "Sama" (heaven) appears in the singular form with the definite article "Al," which here gives a general meaning, and the "Kaf of comparison" (like) is used. This means that while the verse under discussion explicitly says "the width of Paradise is the width of the heavens and the earth," the verse in Surah Al-Hadid says "its width is like the width of the heaven and the earth," and both expressions convey the same meaning.

At the end of the verse, it clarifies that this Paradise, with all its grandeur, has been prepared for the God-fearing (أُعِدَّتْ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ‌). Now, the following questions arise:

First: Are Paradise and Hell already created and do they have an external existence now, or will they be brought into being later in the light of people's deeds?

Second: If they are already created, where is their location (considering that the Quran says the width of Paradise is as large as the heavens and the earth)?

Are Paradise and Hell Currently in Existence?

Most Islamic scholars believe that these two currently have an external existence, and the apparent meanings of the Quranic verses also support this view. For example:

  1. In the verse under discussion and in numerous other verses, the term "u‘iddat" (prepared/made ready) or other expressions from the same root are used, sometimes regarding Paradise and sometimes regarding Hell. [^1] It is inferred from these verses that Paradise and Hell are already prepared, even though they expand as a result of the good and evil deeds of humans (reflect upon this).

  2. In the verses related to the Ascension (Mi'raj) in Surah An-Najm, we read: "وَ لَقَدْ رَآهُ نَزْلَةً أُخْرى‌ عِنْدَ سِدْرَةِ الْمُنْتَهى‌ عِنْدَها جَنَّةُ الْمَأْوى‌": "And he certainly saw him in another descent, at the Lote Tree of the Utmost Boundary, near it is the Garden of Abode" (An-Najm: 13-15). This expression is another proof of the current existence of Paradise.

  3. In Surah At-Takathur, verses 5, 6, and 7, He says: "كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ ثُمَّ لَتَرَوُنَّها عَيْنَ الْيَقِينِ‌": "No! If you only knew with the knowledge of certainty, you would surely see the Hellfire. Then you will surely see it with the eye of certainty."

In the narrations regarding the Ascension and other traditions, clear signs of this matter are also seen. [^2]

Where are Paradise and Hell?

Following the above discussion, the question arises: if these two currently exist, where are they?

This question can be answered in two ways:

First: Paradise and Hell are within the inner dimension (batin) of this world. We see this sky, earth, and various planets with our eyes, but we do not see the worlds that exist within this world. If we had a different vision and perception, we could see them right now. In this universe, there are many beings whose waves are not perceivable by our eyes. The verse "كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ‌" mentioned above also testifies to this reality. It is also understood from some hadiths that certain men of God possessed a perception and vision in this world such that they saw Paradise and Hell with their truth-seeking eyes.

An example can be given for this:

Suppose there is a powerful transmitter at one point on earth whose waves are spread across the entire globe with the help of space satellites, broadcasting a delightful melody of Quranic recitation with an exceptionally pleasant and soul-nurturing voice everywhere. At another point on earth, there is another transmitter of equal power broadcasting an extremely grating and disturbing sound on different waves everywhere.

When we are sitting in an ordinary gathering, we hear the voices of those around us talking, but we have no knowledge of those two sets of "soul-nurturing" and "distressing" waves that are within our environment and have filled everywhere. However, if we had a receiver whose frequency matched one of those two transmitters, they would immediately become manifest to us, yet our auditory system is normally incapable of perceiving them.

Although this example is not perfect in all aspects, it seems effective for visualizing how Paradise and Hell exist within the inner dimension of this world.

Second: The world of the Hereafter, Paradise, and Hell encompass this world; so to speak, this world is located within the "womb" or interior of that world. Just like the world of the fetus which is inside the world of this life; for we know the world of the fetus is an independent world for itself, yet it is not separate from this world we are in, but rather situated within it. The world of this life has the same relationship to the world of the Hereafter—it is situated within it.

And if we see the Quran saying the width of Paradise is as large as the heavens and the earth, it is because man knows nothing more vast than the heavens and the earth to be used as a scale of measurement. Therefore, to depict the vastness and grandeur of Paradise, the Quran has likened it to the expanse of the heavens and the earth. There was no other way, just as if a child in the mother's womb had intellect and we wanted to speak to him, we would have to speak with a logic that is perceivable for him in that environment.

From what we have said, the answer to the question "If the width of Paradise is as large as the earth and heavens, then where is Hell?" also becomes clear. According to the first answer, Hell is also located within this very world, and its existence within this world does not contradict the existence of Paradise within it (just as mentioned in the example of the sound transmitter waves). According to the second answer—that Paradise and Hell encompass this world—the answer is even clearer, for Hell can encompass this world, and Paradise can encompass Hell and be even vaster than it.

The Profile of the God-fearing

الَّذِينَ يُنْفِقُونَ فِي السَّرَّاءِ وَ الضَّرَّاءِ ...

Since the previous verse promised eternal Paradise to the God-fearing, this verse introduces the God-fearing and mentions five of their sublime and human qualities:

  1. They spend in all circumstances, whether they are in ease and abundance or in distress and deprivation (الَّذِينَ يُنْفِقُونَ فِي السَّرَّاءِ وَ الضَّرَّاءِ). By this action, they prove that the spirit of helping others and doing good has permeated their souls, and for this reason, they take action to do this under any conditions. It is clear that spending only in times of abundance is not a sign of the complete penetration of the sublime quality of generosity into the depths of the human soul; but those who take action to help and give in all circumstances show that this quality is deep-rooted in them.

It might be asked: how can a person in poverty spend?

The answer to this is clear: firstly, even poor individuals can spend in the way of helping others according to their ability. Secondly, spending (infaq) is not limited to wealth and property but includes every God-given blessing, whether it be wealth, knowledge, or other favors. In this way, Allah wants to instill the spirit of sacrifice, selflessness, and generosity even in the souls of the needy so they may remain far from the many moral vices that stem from "niggardliness."

Those who consider small acts of spending in the way of Allah as insignificant do so because they examine each one separately. Otherwise, if we put these small contributions together—for example, if every person in a country, whether poor or rich, spent a negligible amount to help Allah's servants and for social goals—great things could be achieved through it. Furthermore, the spiritual and moral effect of spending does not depend on the volume or amount of the spending and reaches the spender in any case.

It is noteworthy that "spending" is mentioned here as the first prominent quality of the God-fearing, because these verses state the opposite of the qualities mentioned in the previous verses regarding usurers and exploiters. Additionally, giving up wealth and property, especially in times of both ease and hardship, is the clearest sign of the station of Taqwa (God-wariness).

  1. They control their anger (وَ الْكاظِمِينَ الْغَيْظَ). "Kāzm" literally means to tie the neck of a water-skin that has been filled with water, and it is used metaphorically for those who are filled with rage and anger but refrain from acting upon it. "Ghayz" means the intensity of anger and the state of inflammation and extraordinary spiritual excitement that comes over a person after observing unpleasant things. The state of rage and anger is among the most dangerous states, and if left unchecked, it manifests as a type of madness and loss of all nervous control. Many crimes and dangerous decisions, for which a person must pay the penalty and fine for a lifetime, are committed in such a state. Therefore, in the above verse, the second prominent quality of the God-fearing is introduced as swallowing one's anger. The Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله says: "Whoever swallows his anger while he has the power to act upon it, Allah fills his heart with security and faith." This hadith shows that swallowing anger has an extraordinary effect on the spiritual perfection of man and the strengthening of the spirit of faith.

  2. They pardon the faults of people (وَ الْعافِينَ عَنِ النَّاسِ‌). Swallowing anger is very good, but it is not sufficient on its own, for it might not uproot malice and enmity from the human heart. In this case, to end the state of enmity, "swallowing anger" must be accompanied by "pardon and forgiveness." Therefore, following the sublime quality of self-restraint and swallowing anger, the issue of pardon and forgiveness is mentioned. Of course, what is meant is the pardon and forgiveness of those who deserve it, not bloodthirsty enemies whose pardon would lead to their further audacity and boldness.

  3. They are doers of good (وَ اللَّهُ يُحِبُّ الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌). Here, a stage higher than pardon is pointed out, arranged like a series of evolutionary steps: a person must not only swallow their anger and wash away malice from their heart through pardon and forgiveness, but by doing good in response to evil (where appropriate), they should also burn the roots of enmity in the heart of the other party and make their heart kind toward them so that such a scene is not repeated in the future. In short, first comes the command for self-restraint against anger, then the command to wash one's own heart, and then the command to wash the heart of the other party.

In a hadith narrated in both Shi'a and Sunni books under the above verse, we read that one of the maidservants of Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, while pouring water over the Imam's hands, dropped the water vessel, injuring the Imam's body. The Imam عليه السلام raised his head in anger. The maidservant immediately said: "Allah says in the Quran: وَ الْكاظِمِينَ الْغَيْظَ." The Imam said: "I have swallowed my anger." She said: "وَ الْعافِينَ عَنِ النَّاسِ‌." He said: "I have forgiven you, may Allah forgive you." She said again: "وَ اللَّهُ يُحِبُّ الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌." The Imam said: "I have set you free for the sake of Allah." [^1] This hadith is a living witness that each of these three stages is higher than the previous one.

  1. They do not persist in sin (وَ الَّذِينَ إِذا فَعَلُوا فاحِشَةً ...). "Fāhishah" is from the root "fuhsh" and "fahshā’," meaning any very ugly act, and it is not limited to acts contrary to chastity, for it originally means "exceeding the limit," which includes every sin. In the above verse, another quality of the God-fearing is mentioned: "In addition to the previous positive qualities, if they commit a sin, they soon remember Allah and repent, and they never persist in sin." From the expression used in this verse, it is understood that as long as a person remembers Allah, they do not commit sin; they commit sin when they completely forget Allah and negligence (ghaflah) envelops their entire being. However, this forgetfulness and negligence do not last long in God-fearing individuals; they soon remember Allah and compensate for the past. They feel that they have no refuge except Allah and must seek the forgiveness of their sins only from Him (وَ مَنْ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ‌): "And who can forgive sins except Allah?"

It should be noted that in the verse, in addition to the term "fāhishah," "wronging oneself" is also mentioned (أَوْ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ‌). The difference between the two might be that "fāhishah" refers to major sins and "wronging oneself" refers to minor sins.

At the end of the verse, for emphasis, it says: "وَ لَمْ يُصِرُّوا عَلى‌ ما فَعَلُوا وَ هُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ‌": "And they do not persist in what they have done while they know."

Under this verse, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that he said: "Persistence (israr) is that a person commits a sin and does not seek forgiveness from Allah and does not think of repentance; that is persistence in sin." [^1] In the book "Amali al-Saduq," a meaningful hadith is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, the summary of which is: "When the above verse was revealed and gave the tidings of Divine forgiveness to repentant sinners, Iblis became severely distressed. He called all his companions with a loud voice to a meeting. They asked him the reason for this call. He expressed his concern over the revelation of this verse. One of his companions said: 'I will neutralize the effect of this verse by inviting humans to this and that sin.' Iblis did not accept his proposal. Another made a similar proposal which was also not accepted. Meanwhile, an old, experienced devil named 'Waswas al-Khannas' said: 'I will solve the problem.' Iblis asked: 'How?' He said: 'I will contaminate the children of Adam with sins through promises and desires, and when they commit a sin, I will make them forget the remembrance of Allah and returning to Him.' Iblis said: 'This is the way!' and he entrusted him with this mission until the end of the world." It is clear that forgetfulness is the result of negligence and satanic whisperings, and only those who surrender themselves to him fall victim to it, cooperating closely with "Waswas al-Khannas"! But vigilant and faithful men are completely watchful; whenever a mistake escapes them, they wash away its effects from their heart and soul at the first opportunity with the water of repentance and seeking forgiveness, and they close the windows of their hearts to Satan and his army—for they do not enter through the closed doors of the heart.

أُولئِكَ جَزاؤُهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ وَ جَنَّاتٌ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها.

In this verse, the reward of the God-fearing whose qualities were mentioned in the previous two verses is explained, which consists of "forgiveness from their Lord and a Paradise beneath whose trees rivers flow (and the water never stops for a moment), a Paradise in which they will abide eternally."

In truth, here first the spiritual blessings, "forgiveness," the cleansing of the heart and soul, and spiritual perfection are mentioned, then the material blessings are pointed out, and at the end, it says: وَ نِعْمَ أَجْرُ الْعامِلِينَ‌: "And how excellent is the reward of the [righteous] workers"—those who are people of action and men of the field, not the passive and lazy individuals who always flee from their commitments and responsibilities.

[^1]: Refer to the verses: At-Tawbah: 89, At-Tawbah: 100, Al-Fath: 6, Al-Baqarah: 24, Al-Imran: 131, Al-Imran: 133, Al-Hadid: 21.

[^2]: It should be noted that the Paradise of the other world currently under discussion is different from the Paradise in which Adam was, which existed before the creation of Adam.

[^1]: Refer to Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur and Nur al-Thaqalayn under the verse in question.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Ayyashi, under the verse.

Ayahs

قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ سُنَنٌ فَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (١٣٧)
Certain [Divine] precedents have passed before you. So travel through the land and observe how was the fate of the deniers. (137)
هَـٰذَا بَيَانٌ لِّلنَّاسِ وَهُدًى وَمَوْعِظَةٌ لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٣٨)
This is an explanation for mankind, and a guidance and advice for the Godwary. (138)

Commentary

Analysis: The History of Predecessors

The Holy Quran links past eras with the present time, and the present with past history. It considers the intellectual and cultural connection of the current generation with those of the past as necessary and essential for understanding truths. This is because through the connection and intertwining of these two times (past and present), the duty and responsibility of future generations become clear. In the above verse, It says:

قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ سُنَنٌ فَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ...

"Allah had established traditions (Sunan) among past nations; these traditions are never exclusive and are implemented as a series of vital laws for everyone—past and future." In these traditions, the progress and transcendence of faithful, striving, united, and vigilant individuals are foreseen. Likewise, the defeat and destruction of scattered, faithless nations mired in sin are also predicted, as recorded in the history of humanity.

Indeed, history holds vital importance for every nation. History recounts for us the moral characteristics, the good and bad deeds, and the thoughts of those who came before us. It shows the causes of the fall and prosperity, the success and failure of societies across different eras and centuries. In reality, the history of predecessors is a mirror of the spiritual and moral life of human societies and a warning for those to come.

For this reason, the Holy Quran commands Muslims: "Go and travel upon the earth and look closely at the remnants of the predecessors, the past nations, the rulers, and the stubborn and tyrannical Pharaohs. See what was the end of those who disbelieved, who rejected the Prophets of Allah, and who laid the foundations of oppression and corruption on earth? And where did their affairs ultimately lead?"

The remnants of the past are cautionary events for future generations, and by benefiting from them, people can become aware of the correct path of life and existence.

Tourism (Travel through the Earth)

The remnants that have remained in various parts of the earth from ancient times are living and eloquent historical documents. We benefit from them even more than from written history. The remains from past eras show us the forms, images, and imprints of the soul, heart, thoughts, power, greatness, and insignificance of nations, whereas history only visualizes the events that occurred and their dry, soulless images.

Indeed, the ruined palaces of tyrants, the amazing structures of the Pyramids of Egypt, the Tower of Babel, the palaces of Khosrow (Kasra), the remnants of the civilization of the people of Saba, and hundreds of similar examples scattered across the corners of the world—each, despite their silence, possesses a thousand tongues and speaks volumes. It is here that perceptive poets, when standing before the ruins of these palaces, felt a profound tremor in their souls and composed stirring verses. Just as "Khaqani" and other famous poets heard these voices with the ear of the soul from within the particles of the fallen palace of Khosrow and the like, and composed them into literary masterpieces:

He laughed at my eye, asking why it weeps here? They should laugh at the eye that does not turn tearful here! Jamshid became a lesson for the people, the crown lost from his head, His head became the brick atop the wine jar; beware, O wise head, beware! This is the counsel of the silent ones, if you seek the counsel of the tongue, Go and read the verse of "Ourathna" (We made them inherit) from the school of the Quran! [^1]

Studying a single line of these living histories is equivalent to studying a thick volume of history. The effect this study has on awakening the human soul and spirit is unparalleled by anything else. For when we stand before the remnants of the past, it is as if the ruins suddenly come to life and the decayed bones rise from beneath the soil, resuming their former hustle and bustle. Once again, we look and see them all silent and forgotten. The comparison of these two states shows how short-sighted autocratic individuals are, who commit thousands of crimes to reach very fleeting desires.

Therefore, the Holy Quran commands Muslims to engage in travel and tourism upon the earth, to see the remnants of the predecessors in the heart of the earth or upon the soil with their own eyes, and to take heed from observing them.

Yes, tourism exists in Islam as well, and great importance is given to it. However, it is not like the pleasure-seeking tourists of today, but rather for the purpose of researching and investigating the remains and fates of predecessors and observing the signs of Allah's greatness in different parts of the world. This is exactly what the Quran calls "Sair fil-Ardh" (traveling through the earth) and has commanded in several verses, including:

  1. "قُلْ سِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ": "Say: Travel through the earth and see how was the end of the criminals" (An-Naml: 69).

  2. "قُلْ سِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ بَدَأَ الْخَلْقَ": "Say: Travel through the earth and see how He originated the creation" (Surah Al-Ankabut, Verse 20).

  3. "أَ فَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَتَكُونَ لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ يَعْقِلُونَ بِها..." (Surah Al-Hajj, Verse 46) and other verses...

This verse says: This spiritual tourism and travel through the earth makes the human heart wise, the eye seeing, and the ear hearing, and grants liberation from lethargy and stagnation.

Unfortunately, this vital Islamic instruction, like many other forgotten instructions, is ignored by Muslims. Even a group of Islamic scholars and thinkers seem to have frozen time and space within their own intellectual environment; they live in a world other than this one, are unaware of the social transformations of the world, and have occupied themselves with minor and low-impact matters that hold little value compared to fundamental and essential tasks.

In a world where even Christian Popes and Cardinals—who, after centuries of isolation, seclusion, and cutting ties with the outside world—now engage in travel through the earth to understand the needs of the time, should not Muslims act upon this explicit command of the Quran? Should they not bring themselves out of the narrow straits of a limited intellectual environment so that a transformation and movement may emerge in the world of Islam and Muslims?

هذا بَيانٌ لِلنَّاسِ وَ هُدىً وَ مَوْعِظَةٌ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ In this verse, He says: "What has been said in the above verses is a clear statement for all people, and a means of guidance and an admonition for all the God-fearing." This means that while these statements have a universal and public aspect, only the God-fearing and purposeful individuals draw inspiration from them and are guided.

[^1]: - "Khaqani," in his famous qasida while shedding tears before the Arch of Ctesiphon (Iwan Mada'in), says: He laughed at my eye, asking why it weeps here? / They should laugh at the eye that does not turn tearful here! And the famous scholar, the late Sheikh Abivardi, in his famous qasida before Persepolis (Takht-e Jamshid), says: Jamshid became a lesson for the people, the crown lost from his head / His head became the brick atop the wine jar; beware, O wise head, beware! / This is the counsel of the silent ones, if you seek the counsel of the tongue / Go and read the verse of "Ourathna" from the school of the Quran!

Ayahs

وَلَا تَهِنُوا۟ وَلَا تَحْزَنُوا۟ وَأَنتُمُ ٱلْأَعْلَوْنَ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٣٩)
Do not weaken or grieve: you shall have the upper hand, should you be faithful. (139)
إِن يَمْسَسْكُمْ قَرْحٌ فَقَدْ مَسَّ ٱلْقَوْمَ قَرْحٌ مِّثْلُهُۥ ۚ وَتِلْكَ ٱلْأَيَّامُ نُدَاوِلُهَا بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَيَتَّخِذَ مِنكُمْ شُهَدَآءَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٤٠)
If wounds afflict you, like wounds have already afflicted those people; and We make such vicissitudes rotate among mankind, so that Allah may ascertain those who have faith, and that He may take martyrs from among you, and Allah does not like the wrongdoers. (140)
وَلِيُمَحِّصَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَيَمْحَقَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٤١)
And so that Allah may purge [the hearts of] those who have faith and that He may wipe out the faithless. (141)
أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَن تَدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَلَمَّا يَعْلَمِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ جَـٰهَدُوا۟ مِنكُمْ وَيَعْلَمَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (١٤٢)
Do you suppose that you would enter paradise, while Allah has not yet ascertained those of you who have waged jihad and He has not ascertained the steadfast? (142)
وَلَقَدْ كُنتُمْ تَمَنَّوْنَ ٱلْمَوْتَ مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَلْقَوْهُ فَقَدْ رَأَيْتُمُوهُ وَأَنتُمْ تَنظُرُونَ (١٤٣)
Certainly you were longing for death before you had encountered it. Then certainly, you saw it, as you were looking on. (143)

Occasion of Revelation

Multiple narrations have been recorded regarding the occasion of the revelation of these verses. From the sum of these reports, it is understood that these verses are a continuation of the previous verses concerning the Battle of Uhud. In reality, these verses serve as an analysis of the outcomes of the Battle of Uhud and the factors behind its occurrence, serving as a great lesson for the Muslims. Simultaneously, they act as a means of consolation, comfort, and spiritual strengthening for them. As previously mentioned, the Battle of Uhud ultimately ended in defeat due to the disobedience and lack of military discipline of a group of Muslim soldiers, and several prominent figures and distinguished personalities of Islam, including Hamza, the uncle of the Prophet, drank the nectar of martyrdom on this field.

That same night, the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) went among the fallen with his companions. To honor the souls of the martyrs, He sat by each of their bodies, weeping and seeking forgiveness for them. Subsequently, the bodies of all of them were buried at the foot of Mount Uhud amidst profound grief. At these sensitive moments, when the Muslims were in dire need of both spiritual strengthening and gaining moral lessons from the results of the defeat, the above verses were revealed.

Commentary

The Outcomes of the Battle of Uhud

وَ لا تَهِنُوا وَ لا تَحْزَنُوا وَ أَنْتُمُ الْأَعْلَوْنَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ

"وَ لا تَهِنُوا" (Do not weaken) is derived from the root "wahn," which literally means any kind of weakness, whether in the body and physique or in will and faith.

In this verse, the Muslims are first warned not to allow weakness to creep into them, nor to grieve or despair of final victory because of losing one battle. This is because vigilant individuals learn lessons from defeats just as they benefit from victories; in the light of defeat, they discover the weak points that were the source of the failure and, by rectifying them, prepare themselves for the ultimate victory.

The phrase "وَ أَنْتُمُ الْأَعْلَوْنَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ" (And you are the superior ones if you are believers) is a very profound statement. It means that your defeat was, in reality, due to losing the spirit of faith and its effects. Had you not disregarded the command of Allah and the Prophet on this field, you would not have met such a fate. Furthermore, do not grieve; if you remain firm on the path of faith, the final victory belongs to you, and defeat in one field does not mean final defeat in the war.

إِنْ يَمْسَسْكُمْ قَرْحٌ فَقَدْ مَسَّ الْقَوْمَ قَرْحٌ مِثْلُهُ

"Qarh" refers to a wound inflicted on the body due to contact with an external factor. In this verse, another lesson for achieving final victory is given to the Muslims: you should not be less than your enemies. They suffered a heavy and severe defeat from you at the Battle of Badr, losing seventy killed and many wounded and captured, yet they did not give up. In this field, they compensated for their defeat due to your negligence. If you have suffered a defeat in this field, you must not give up until you compensate for it. Therefore, He says: "If a wound has touched you, a similar wound has already touched the [disbelieving] people." So, what is the reason for your weakness and grief?

Some commentators believe the verse refers to the wounds inflicted on the disbelievers at the field of Uhud. However, firstly, those wounds were not like the wounds of the Muslims; thus, it does not align with the word "mithluhu" (similar to it). Secondly, it does not harmonize with the subsequent sentence, the explanation of which follows.

وَ تِلْكَ الْأَيَّامُ نُداوِلُها بَيْنَ النَّاسِ وَ لِيَعْلَمَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ يَتَّخِذَ مِنْكُمْ شُهَداءَ:

In this part, reference is first made to one of the Divine Sunnahs (laws): that in human life, bitter and sweet events occur, none of which are permanent. Defeats, victories, powers, greatness, and weaknesses are all in a state of flux. Therefore, one should not consider a defeat in one field and its effects as permanent; rather, by examining the factors and motives of the defeat, one should utilize the law of change and transform it into victory. The world has its ups and downs, and life is in a state of transformation, and "Allah alternates these days among the people" so that the law of perfection may manifest through these events. [^1]

Then, He points to the result of these unpleasant events, saying: "وَ لِيَعْلَمَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا..." (And so that Allah may know those who believe...). These occur so that the true believers may be distinguished from those who merely claim faith. In other words, until painful events occur in the history of a nation, the ranks will not be clearly demarcated, for victories are sleep-inducing and deceptive, while defeats are awakening for prepared individuals and reveal true values.

In the phrase "وَ يَتَّخِذَ مِنْكُمْ شُهَداءَ" (And that He may take martyrs from among you), He says: One of the results of this painful defeat was that you should offer martyrs and sacrifices in the way of Islam, so you may know that you did not acquire this pure religion cheaply to lose it cheaply in the future. A nation that does not offer sacrifices for its sacred goals will always hold them in low esteem; but when it offers sacrifices, both that nation and its future generations will look upon those goals with a sense of greatness.

It is possible that "shuhada" here means "witnesses," meaning Allah wanted to take witnesses from you through this event—witnesses to how disobedience leads to painful defeats. These witnesses will be teachers for the people in the future when facing similar events ahead.

At the end of the verse, He says that Allah does not love the wrongdoers, and therefore, He will not support them.

The Field of Cultivation and Training

وَ لِيُمَحِّصَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ...

"Liyumahhisa" is derived from "tamhis," which means to purify something from any defect.

And "yamhaqa" is from "mahq," which means the gradual reduction of something. For this reason, the night at the end of the lunar month is called "muhaq," because the moon's light gradually diminishes and disappears.

In this verse, reference is made to another natural outcome of the defeat at the Battle of Uhud: that such defeats reveal the weak points and defects of groups and serve as an effective means to wash away these flaws. The Quran says that Allah intended to purify the believers in this battlefield and show them their weak points. They had to be placed in such a crucible of trial for future victories and to measure the caliber of their character. As Ali (peace be upon him) says: "In the fluctuation of circumstances, the core essence of men is known." They become aware of the caliber of their own character.

It is here that sometimes certain defeats are so constructive that their impact on the destiny of human societies is far greater than deceptive outward victories.

Interestingly, the author of the Al-Manar commentary narrates from his teacher, Muhammad Abduh, the great Mufti of Egypt, that he saw the Prophet in a dream and He said to him: "If I had been given the choice between victory and defeat at the field of Uhud, I would have preferred defeat specifically for that field," because this defeat became a constructive factor in the history of Islam.

"وَ يَمْحَقَ الْكافِرِينَ" (And that He may destroy the disbelievers): This phrase is actually a result of the preceding one. For when the believers are purified in the furnace of events, they gain sufficient readiness to gradually eliminate polytheism and disbelief and to cleanse their society of these impurities. That is, one must first be purified and then purify others.

In reality, just as the moon, with its specific allure and charm, gradually loses its light and enters the "muhaq" (waning phase), so too the glory and grandeur of disbelief and polytheism and their supporters decline and vanish through the refinement and purification of the Muslims.

أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَنْ تَدْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ وَ لَمَّا يَعْلَمِ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا مِنْكُمْ وَ يَعْلَمَ الصَّابِرِينَ

Here, the Quran utilizes the event of Uhud to correct a conceptual error among the Muslims, saying: Did you think that you could enter the lofty Paradise without Jihad and steadfastness in the way of Allah? Did you imagine that attaining the depths of that spiritual bliss is possible merely by adopting the name of a Muslim or by a belief without action? If it were so, the matter would be very simple; but it has never been thus. Until real beliefs are implemented in the field of action, no one will benefit from those felicities. It is here that the ranks must be distinguished, and the mujahideen and the patient ones must be identified from the worthless individuals.

Empty Words

وَ لَقَدْ كُنْتُمْ تَمَنَّوْنَ الْمَوْتَ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ تَلْقَوْهُ فَقَدْ رَأَيْتُمُوهُ وَ أَنْتُمْ تَنْظُرُونَ

After the Battle of Badr and the honorable martyrdom of a group of Muslims, some would sit in gatherings and constantly wish for martyrdom, saying: "If only this honor had been our lot at the field of Badr as well." As is usual, among them were those who were sincere and those who were pretenders and liars, or those who were mistaken in their self-assessment. However, it was not long before the terrifying Battle of Uhud occurred. The true mujahideen fought with extraordinary courage, drank the nectar of martyrdom, and reached their desire. But a group of the liars, when they saw the signs of defeat in the army of Islam, fled for fear of being killed. This verse rebukes them, saying: You were the ones who nurtured the wish for death and martyrdom in the way of Allah in your hearts; so why did you flee when you saw your "beloved" with your own eyes before you?

A Brief Analysis of the Causes of the Defeat at Uhud

In the above verses, interesting expressions are observed, each of which unveils one of the secrets of the defeat at Uhud. In short, several important factors joined hands to bring about this tragic yet lesson-filled event:

  1. A miscalculation that had arisen for some new Muslims in understanding Islamic concepts caused them to imagine that merely expressing faith was sufficient for victory, and that Allah was supposed to support them in all battlefields through unseen aid. Thus, they forgot the Divine Sunnah regarding natural factors of victory, choosing correct plans, and preparing necessary means.

  2. Lack of military discipline and opposition to the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) explicit command for the archers to remain in their sensitive position was another major factor for this defeat.

  3. The worldliness of a group of novice Muslims who preferred collecting war booty over pursuing the enemy; they laid down their arms and rushed so as not to fall behind others. This was the third factor of defeat, so they might learn that in the way of Allah and during holy Jihad, these matters must be entirely forgotten.

  4. The pride resulting from the brilliant victory at the field of Badr, to the extent that they had dismissed the thought of the enemy's power and considered his equipment insignificant, was the fourth factor of defeat.

These were the weak points that had to be washed away in the boiling water of this defeat.

[^1]: "Ayyam" is the plural of "yawm," meaning day, and sometimes the eras of nations' victories are also called "ayyam." "Nudawiluha" is from "mudawalah," meaning to circulate something among a group, one after another.

Ayahs

وَمَا مُحَمَّدٌ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ ۚ أَفَإِي۟ن مَّاتَ أَوْ قُتِلَ ٱنقَلَبْتُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَعْقَـٰبِكُمْ ۚ وَمَن يَنقَلِبْ عَلَىٰ عَقِبَيْهِ فَلَن يَضُرَّ ٱللَّهَ شَيْـًٔا ۗ وَسَيَجْزِى ٱللَّهُ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (١٤٤)
Muhammad is but an apostle; [other] apostles have passed before him. If he dies or is slain, will you turn back on your heels? Anyone who turns back on his heels, will not harm Allah in the least, and soon Allah will reward the grateful. (144)
وَمَا كَانَ لِنَفْسٍ أَن تَمُوتَ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ كِتَـٰبًا مُّؤَجَّلًا ۗ وَمَن يُرِدْ ثَوَابَ ٱلدُّنْيَا نُؤْتِهِۦ مِنْهَا وَمَن يُرِدْ ثَوَابَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ نُؤْتِهِۦ مِنْهَا ۚ وَسَنَجْزِى ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (١٤٥)
No soul may die except by Allah’s leave, at an appointed time. Whoever desires the reward of this world, We will give him of it; and whoever desires the reward of the Hereafter, We will give him of it; and soon We will reward the grateful. (145)

Occasion of Revelation

This verse also pertains to another incident from the Battle of Uhud, which is as follows:

While the fire of war was raging intensely between the Muslims and the idolaters, a cry suddenly rang out and someone shouted: "I have killed Muhammad... I have killed Muhammad!"

This happened exactly at the moment when a man named "Amr ibn Qami'ah al-Harithi" threw a stone at the Prophet ﷺ. His forehead and tooth were broken, His lower lip was split, and blood covered His face [^1]. At this point, the enemy intended to kill the Prophet ﷺ, but Mus'ab ibn Umayr, one of the standard-bearers of the Islamic army, blocked their attacks until he himself was killed in the process. Since he bore a great resemblance to the Prophet ﷺ, the enemy imagined that the Prophet ﷺ had fallen in blood and dust; therefore, they announced this news loudly throughout the entire camp.

The spread of this news had a positive effect on the morale of the idolaters, but it created a strange instability among the Muslims. A group, which formed the majority, became frantic and quickly fled the battlefield. Some even thought of turning back from the religion of Islam and seeking protection from the leaders of the idolaters now that the Prophet ﷺ was killed. However, standing against them was a devoted and steadfast minority, such as Ali عليه السلام, Abu Dujana, Talhah, and some others, who called the rest to perseverance. Among them, Anas ibn Nadr came forward and said: "O people! If Muhammad ﷺ is killed, the God of Muhammad is not killed. Go and fight, and drink the draught of martyrdom for the same goal for which the Prophet was killed." After delivering these words, he attacked the enemy until he was killed. Soon, however, it became clear that the Prophet ﷺ was alive and that the news had been a mistake or a lie. The above verse was revealed regarding this matter, severely rebuking the first group.

Commentary

Personality Cult is Forbidden

وَ ما مُحَمَّدٌ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ الرُّسُلُ أَ فَإِنْ ماتَ أَوْ قُتِلَ انْقَلَبْتُمْ عَلى‌ أَعْقابِكُمْ‌:

[^1]: In some historical accounts, it is mentioned that these injuries were the result of an attack by several individuals.

Using the events of the Battle of Uhud, the verse teaches the Muslims another truth: that Islam is not a religion of personality cults. Even if the Prophet ﷺ had tasted the draught of martyrdom on that field, the duty of the Muslims was undoubtedly to continue the struggle. This is because Islam does not end with the death or martyrdom of the Prophet ﷺ; rather, it is a true religion that will remain eternal forever.

The issue of personality cults is one of the greatest dangers threatening goal-oriented struggles. Dependence on a specific person—even if it be the Final Prophet—means the end of effort and striving for progress upon the loss of that person. Such dependence is one of the clear signs of a lack of social maturity.

The Prophet's ﷺ struggle against personality cults is another sign of His truthfulness and greatness. If He had risen for His own sake, He would have needed to reinforce the idea in people that everything depends on His existence and that if He were gone, everything would end. However, true leaders like the Noble Prophet ﷺ never encourage people toward such thoughts; rather, they fight against them vigorously. They tell them: "Our goal is higher than ourselves and will never be destroyed by our destruction." Therefore, the Quran explicitly states in the above verse:

"Muhammad is no more than a messenger; messengers have passed away before him. If he dies or is killed, will you then turn back on your heels?" It is noteworthy that to describe this regression, the phrase "انْقَلَبْتُمْ عَلى‌ أَعْقابِكُمْ‌" is used. "A'qab" is the plural of "aqib," meaning the heel of the foot. Thus, the phrase means "you turn back on your heels," which is a vivid image of regression and reactionism in its true sense, though it is more explicit and clearer than the word "reactionism" (irtija').

وَ مَنْ يَنْقَلِبْ عَلى‌ عَقِبَيْهِ فَلَنْ يَضُرَّ اللَّهَ شَيْئاً Then He says: Those who turn back and return to the era of disbelief and idolatry only harm themselves, not Allah. By this act, they not only halt the wheels of their own prosperity but also quickly lose what they have gained.

وَ سَيَجْزِي اللَّهُ الشَّاكِرِينَ‌ At the end of the verse, it refers to the minority who, despite all the difficulties and the spread of the news of the Prophet's martyrdom at Uhud, did not abandon Jihad. It praises their efforts and introduces them as the "grateful ones"—those who used blessings in the way of Allah—and says: Allah will give a good reward to these grateful ones.

The lesson this verse teaches regarding the struggle against personality cults is a lesson for all Muslims in all centuries and ages. They must learn from the Quran that goal-oriented matters should never be dependent on a person or persons; rather, they must revolve around a series of eternal principles and structures so that the work does not stop with the change of individuals or their death, even if it be the great Prophet of God. Fundamentally, this is the secret to the survival of a religion or an organization. Therefore, programs and structures that are dependent on a person are considered unhealthy and unnatural structures that will soon disintegrate.

Unfortunately, on the whole, most structures in Islamic societies are still dependent on individuals, and for this reason, they fall apart very quickly. Muslims, inspired by the above verse, must lay the foundations of their various institutions in such a way that capable individuals are fully utilized, yet the institutions remain independent of their specific persons.

وَ ما كانَ لِنَفْسٍ أَنْ تَمُوتَ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ كِتاباً مُؤَجَّلًا As we mentioned, the baseless rumor of the Prophet's martyrdom at Uhud terrified many Muslims to the point that they fled the battlefield, and some even wanted to turn back from Islam. In the above verse, to admonish and awaken this group, He says: Death is in the hand of Allah and by His command, and for everyone, a term (ajal) has been decreed from which they cannot escape. Therefore, if the Prophet ﷺ had tasted the draught of martyrdom on this field, it would have been nothing but the fulfillment of a divine law; nevertheless, Muslims should not be terrified by it or abandon the struggle.

On the other hand, fleeing from the battlefield cannot prevent the arrival of the appointed term, just as participating in the field of Jihad does not bring a person's term forward. Therefore, fleeing from the field of Jihad to save one's life is futile.

Regarding the meaning of "ajal" (term), as well as "certain" (hatmi) and "suspended" (mu'allaq) terms and the difference between them, we will discuss it in detail under the second verse of Surah Al-An'am, God willing.

وَ مَنْ يُرِدْ ثَوابَ الدُّنْيا نُؤْتِهِ مِنْها وَ مَنْ يُرِدْ ثَوابَ الْآخِرَةِ نُؤْتِهِ مِنْها: At the end of the verse, He says: Human effort and striving are never wasted. If someone's goal is only material and worldly results—and like some of the warriors at Uhud, they strive only for booty—they will eventually obtain a share of it. But if the goal was higher, and efforts were employed in the path of eternal life and human virtues, they will also reach their goal. Therefore, since reaching either the world or the hereafter requires effort, why should a person not employ their existential capital in the second path, which is a sublime and lasting path? Then He emphasizes once more: "We shall soon reward the grateful." (وَ سَنَجْزِي الشَّاكِرِينَ‌).

It is noteworthy that in the previous verse, this phrase was mentioned in the third person (He will reward), while here it is in the first person (We shall reward). This expresses the ultimate emphasis of the divine promise to give them a reward. In simple terms, Allah says: "I am the guarantor of their reward."

In the commentary of Majma' al-Bayan, under this verse, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that: On the day of Uhud, Ali عليه السلام received sixty-one wounds. The Prophet ﷺ ordered "Umm Sulaym" and "Umm Atiyyah" to treat His injuries. Before long, they anxiously reported to the Prophet: "The condition of Ali's body is such that whenever we bind one wound, another opens, and the wounds on His body are so numerous and dangerous that we fear for His life." The Prophet and a group of Muslims entered Ali's house to visit Him while His body was a mass of wounds and injuries. The Prophet ﷺ stroked His body with His blessed hand and said: "One who suffers such in the way of Allah has fulfilled the highest degree of his responsibility!" And the wounds the Prophet ﷺ touched were soon healed. Ali عليه السلام said at that moment: "Praise be to Allah that despite all this, I did not flee and did not turn my back to the enemy." Allah appreciated His efforts and referred to it (and to the sacrifices of other exemplary mujahideen of Uhud) in two verses of the Quran. In one instance He says: "وَ سَيَجْزِي اللَّهُ الشَّاكِرِينَ‌" and in another instance He says: "وَ سَنَجْزِي الشَّاكِرِينَ‌".

Ayahs

وَكَأَيِّن مِّن نَّبِىٍّ قَـٰتَلَ مَعَهُۥ رِبِّيُّونَ كَثِيرٌ فَمَا وَهَنُوا۟ لِمَآ أَصَابَهُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا ضَعُفُوا۟ وَمَا ٱسْتَكَانُوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (١٤٦)
How many a prophet there has been with whom a multitude of godly men fought. They did not falter for what befell them in the way of Allah, neither did they weaken, nor did they abase themselves; and Allah loves the steadfast. (146)
وَمَا كَانَ قَوْلَهُمْ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا ٱغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَا وَإِسْرَافَنَا فِىٓ أَمْرِنَا وَثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَنَا وَٱنصُرْنَا عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٤٧)
All that they said was, ‘Our Lord, forgive us our sins and our excesses in our affairs, make our feet steady, and help us against the faithless lot.’ (147)
فَـَٔاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ثَوَابَ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَحُسْنَ ثَوَابِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٤٨)
So Allah gave them the reward of this world and the fair reward of the Hereafter; and Allah loves the virtuous. (148)

Commentary

The Mujahideen of Old

وَ كَأَيِّنْ مِنْ نَبِيٍ‌ ...

"Ka-ayyin" (كأين) means "how many" or "many a." It is said to be originally composed of the "Kaf of comparison" (کاف تشبیه) and the interrogative "Ayy" (ای), which have merged into a single word, losing their original individual meanings to take on a new meaning equivalent to "how many."

"Ribbiyyun" (ربيون) is the plural of "Ribbi" (on the pattern of milli). It refers to someone whose connection and bond with Allah is firm; one who is faithful, scholarly, steadfast, and sincere.

Following the events of Uhud, the above verse encourages Muslims toward courage, sacrifice, and perseverance by recalling the bravery, faith, and steadfastness of the mujahideen and companions of past Prophets. Simultaneously, it reproaches those who fled the battlefield of Uhud, stating: There were many Prophets who had fighting God-worshippers in the ranks of their companions.

It then describes their conduct and speech as follows:

They rose to assist their Prophets and never became weak or feeble due to the heavy casualties, severe wounds, and grueling hardships they endured in the way of Allah. They never supplicated, wailed, or showed humility and submission before the enemy, nor did they surrender (مَا ضَعُفُوا وَ مَا اسْتَكانُوا). Naturally, Allah loves such individuals who do not abandon their resistance (وَ اللَّهُ يُحِبُّ الصَّابِرِينَ‌).

Whenever they occasionally faced difficulties against the enemy due to mistakes, weaknesses, or lapses, instead of abandoning the field to the enemy, surrendering, or entertaining thoughts of apostasy and returning to disbelief, they would turn to the threshold of Allah. While seeking pardon and forgiveness for their sins, they would request patience, steadfastness, and firm-footedness from the presence of Allah, saying: "رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لَنا ذُنُوبَنا وَ إِسْرافَنا فِي أَمْرِنا وَ ثَبِّتْ أَقْدامَنا وَ انْصُرْنا عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْكافِرِينَ‌": "Our Lord! Forgive us our sins and our excesses in our affairs, make our foothold firm, and grant us victory over the disbelieving people." With this mindset and action, they would soon receive their reward from Allah—both the reward of this world, which was victory over the enemy, and the reward of the Hereafter (فَآتاهُمُ اللَّهُ ثَوابَ الدُّنْيا وَ حُسْنَ ثَوابِ الْآخِرَةِ).

At the end of the verse, He counts them among the doers of good, stating: (وَ اللَّهُ يُحِبُّ الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌) "And Allah loves the doers of good."

In this way, He provides a living lesson for the new Muslims regarding the program of the mujahideen of previous nations, their ultimate end, and how they confronted and overcame difficulties, thereby nurturing them for future battlefields.

In the above verses, in addition to what has been mentioned, other points attract attention:

  1. "Sabr" (patience), as we have previously noted, means steadfastness. Therefore, in this verse, it is placed in contrast to "weakness" and "submission." Furthermore, the patient and the doers of good are placed in the same rank; for at the end of one verse He says, "وَ اللَّهُ يُحِبُّ الصَّابِرِينَ‌" (And Allah loves the patient), and in the next verse He says, "وَ اللَّهُ يُحِبُّ الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌" (And Allah loves the doers of good). This indicates that doing good is impossible without steadfastness, because every doer of good faces thousands of problems; if they lack steadfastness, they will soon abandon their work.

  2. True mujahideen, instead of attributing their defeat to others or relating it to imaginary and mysterious factors, search for its source within themselves and think of compensating for their own mistakes.
