They do not even utter the word "defeat"; instead, they mention "excess" and "undue haste." This is contrary to us today, as we try to ignore the weak points that are the source of failures and defeats, attributing them all to external and foreign factors, and consequently, we never think of compensating for our mistakes or removing our weak points.

  3. In the above verses, the reward of this world is expressed as "ثَوابَ الدُّنْيا" (reward of the world), but the reward of the Hereafter is expressed as "حُسْنَ ثَوابِ الْآخِرَةِ" (the excellence of the reward of the Hereafter). This indicates that the reward of the Hereafter differs greatly from the reward of this world; for whatever the reward of this world may be, it is ultimately mixed with perishability and certain unpleasantries that are the nature of this worldly life, whereas the reward of the Hereafter is entirely "Hasan" (excellent), pure in every respect, and far removed from any distress.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن تُطِيعُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يَرُدُّوكُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَعْقَـٰبِكُمْ فَتَنقَلِبُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ (١٤٩)
O you who have faith! If you obey the faithless, they will turn you back on your heels, and you will become losers. (149)
بَلِ ٱللَّهُ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ ۖ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلنَّـٰصِرِينَ (١٥٠)
Indeed, Allah is your Master, and He is the best of helpers. (150)
سَنُلْقِى فِى قُلُوبِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱلرُّعْبَ بِمَآ أَشْرَكُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِۦ سُلْطَـٰنًا ۖ وَمَأْوَىٰهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ ۚ وَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٥١)
We shall cast terror into the hearts of the faithless because of their ascribing partners to Allah, for which He has not sent down any authority, and their refuge shall be the Fire, and evil is the [final] abode of the wrongdoers. (151)

Commentary

Repeated Warnings

يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنْ تُطِيعُوا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا ...

These verses, like the preceding ones, were revealed after the Battle of Uhud to analyze the events of the war; the context of both the previous and current verses bears witness to this fact.

It appears that after the conclusion of the Battle of Uhud, the enemies of Islam, under the guise of advice and compassion, were sowing seeds of discord among the Muslims through a series of poisonous propaganda. Utilizing the unfavorable psychological state of some Muslims, they attempted to make them cynical toward Islam. Perhaps the Jews and Christians also cooperated with the hypocrites in this regard, just as they had attempted to weaken the morale of the Muslims on the battlefield of Uhud by fueling the baseless rumor of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله death.

The first verse warns the Muslims and cautions them against following them, stating: If you follow the disbelievers, they will turn you back on your heels, and after traversing the glorious path of spiritual and material perfection under the light of Islamic teachings, they will cause you to fall back to the starting point—the point of disbelief and corruption. At that moment, the greatest loss will afflict you; for what loss is greater than for a human to exchange Islam for disbelief, felicity for wretchedness, and truth for falsehood?

Then, It emphasizes that you have the highest Supporter and the best Helper, saying:

"بَلِ اللَّهُ مَوْلاكُمْ وَ هُوَ خَيْرُ النَّاصِرِينَ": "Nay, Allah is your Patron, and He is the best of helpers." He is a Helper who is never defeated, and no power can equal His power, whereas other helpers may be subject to defeat and destruction.

سَنُلْقِي فِي قُلُوبِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الرُّعْبَ.

Here, It refers to the miraculous rescue of the Muslims after the Battle of Uhud, recounting one of the instances of His support and His loathing for the enemies. It encourages the Muslims regarding the future and promises victory. As we mentioned in the story of Uhud, although the idolaters of Mecca had achieved a significant victory in the battle and the army of Islam had apparently disintegrated, they should not have harbored the slightest hesitation in returning to the battlefield to eliminate the remaining strength of the Muslims, or even to plunder Medina and kill the Prophet himself—especially after they realized the rumor of his martyrdom was baseless.

However, Allah cast a strange fear and terror into their hearts—an irrational fear that is a characteristic of disbelief, idolatry, and superstition. It permeated their entire being to the extent that we read in narrations: when they returned from Uhud and reached the outskirts of Mecca, they had the exact appearance and demeanor of a defeated army.

The above verse says: We will soon cast terror into the hearts of those who disbelieve (just as We cast it at the end of the Battle of Uhud, an example of which you saw with your own eyes). Therefore, be hopeful for your future.

Interestingly, It explains the reason for casting terror and fear into their hearts as follows:

"بِما أَشْرَكُوا بِاللَّهِ ما لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِ سُلْطاناً": "Because they associated with Allah that for which He has sent down no authority." In reality, people who are superstitious and do not follow logic or proof—who turn a straw into a mountain in their minds and consider wood and stone as their deity and lord—are extremely weak in the face of events. This is because they very quickly fall victim to miscalculations. If a minor incident occurs in their lives—for instance, if they hear that the Muslims of Medina, along with the wounded from the battlefield, are returning to the field of Uhud once more—this matter appears immense in their eyes and they become severely terrified by it. Just as in today's world, we observe powerful individuals who are terrified by the smallest incident and make mountains out of molehills because they have not chosen a firm support for themselves in life.

وَ مَأْواهُمُ النَّارُ وَ بِئْسَ مَثْوَى الظَّالِمِينَ:

These individuals have wronged themselves and their society; therefore, they shall have no abode but the Fire, and wretched is the dwelling place of the wrongdoers.

Victory Through the Enemy's Fear

In several narrations, we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله used to say: "One of the distinctions Allah has granted me is that He has given me victory by casting fear into the hearts of the enemy." [1] This point refers to one of the important factors of victory in wars, which is particularly noted today: that one of the most vital factors for victory is the soldier's morale. The high morale of soldiers influences victory to an extent that their quantity, quality, or type of weaponry does not. By strengthening the spirit of faith, the love for Jihad, the honor of martyrdom, and reliance on Allah, the All-Powerful Benefactor, Islam cultivated this spirit in its warriors in the most sublime manner. Meanwhile, the superstitious idolaters—whose support was lifeless, will-less idols, who did not believe in the Resurrection or life after death, and whose thoughts were contaminated by superstitions—possessed a weak and feeble morale. One of the effective factors in the Muslims' victory over them was this very difference in morale.

[1]: Refer to the book Al-Khisal and Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ صَدَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَعْدَهُۥٓ إِذْ تَحُسُّونَهُم بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا فَشِلْتُمْ وَتَنَـٰزَعْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَمْرِ وَعَصَيْتُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَآ أَرَىٰكُم مَّا تُحِبُّونَ ۚ مِنكُم مَّن يُرِيدُ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَمِنكُم مَّن يُرِيدُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ ۚ ثُمَّ صَرَفَكُمْ عَنْهُمْ لِيَبْتَلِيَكُمْ ۖ وَلَقَدْ عَفَا عَنكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٥٢)
Allah certainly fulfilled His promise to you when you were slaying them with His leave, until you lost courage, disputed about the matter, and disobeyed after He showed you what you loved. Some among you desire this world, and some among you desire the Hereafter. Then He turned you away from them so that He might test you. Certainly He has excused you, for Allah is gracious to the faithful. (152)
۞ إِذْ تُصْعِدُونَ وَلَا تَلْوُۥنَ عَلَىٰٓ أَحَدٍ وَٱلرَّسُولُ يَدْعُوكُمْ فِىٓ أُخْرَىٰكُمْ فَأَثَـٰبَكُمْ غَمًّۢا بِغَمٍّ لِّكَيْلَا تَحْزَنُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَاتَكُمْ وَلَا مَآ أَصَـٰبَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٥٣)
When you were fleeing without paying any attention to anyone, while the Apostle was calling you from your rear, He requited you with grief upon grief, so that you may not grieve for what you lose nor for what befalls you, and Allah is well aware of what you do. (153)
ثُمَّ أَنزَلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ٱلْغَمِّ أَمَنَةً نُّعَاسًا يَغْشَىٰ طَآئِفَةً مِّنكُمْ ۖ وَطَآئِفَةٌ قَدْ أَهَمَّتْهُمْ أَنفُسُهُمْ يَظُنُّونَ بِٱللَّهِ غَيْرَ ٱلْحَقِّ ظَنَّ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِيَّةِ ۖ يَقُولُونَ هَل لَّنَا مِنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلْأَمْرَ كُلَّهُۥ لِلَّهِ ۗ يُخْفُونَ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِم مَّا لَا يُبْدُونَ لَكَ ۖ يَقُولُونَ لَوْ كَانَ لَنَا مِنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ شَىْءٌ مَّا قُتِلْنَا هَـٰهُنَا ۗ قُل لَّوْ كُنتُمْ فِى بُيُوتِكُمْ لَبَرَزَ ٱلَّذِينَ كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَتْلُ إِلَىٰ مَضَاجِعِهِمْ ۖ وَلِيَبْتَلِىَ ٱللَّهُ مَا فِى صُدُورِكُمْ وَلِيُمَحِّصَ مَا فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (١٥٤)
Then He sent down to you safety after grief—a drowsiness that came over a group of you—while another group, anxious only about themselves, entertained false notions about Allah, notions of [pagan] ignorance. They say, ‘Do we have any role in the matter?’ Say, ‘Indeed the matter belongs totally to Allah.’ They hide in their hearts what they do not disclose to you. They say, ‘Had we any role in the matter, we would not have been slain here.’ Say, ‘Even if you had remained in your houses, those destined to be slain would have set out toward the places where they were laid to rest, so that Allah may test what is in your breasts, and that He may purge what is in your hearts, and Allah knows well what is in the breasts.’ (154)

Commentary

Defeat After Victory

In the account of the Battle of Uhud, we mentioned that at the beginning of the war, the Muslims fought with a specific unity and courage, and soon achieved victory. The enemy's army was scattered, and a wave of joy spread throughout the ranks of Islam. However, the disobedience of a group of archers stationed at the pass of Mount "Aynayn" under the leadership of "Abdullah bin Jubayr," their abandonment of that critical stronghold, and their preoccupation—along with others—with gathering spoils, caused the tide to turn, and a severe defeat was inflicted upon the Muslim army.

When the Muslims returned to Medina having suffered heavy casualties and losses, they said to one another: "Did Allah not promise us victory and triumph? So why were we defeated in this battle?" The above verses respond to them and explain the causes of the defeat. Now we return to the commentary on the details of the verses:

وَ لَقَدْ صَدَقَكُمُ اللَّهُ وَعْدَهُ إِذْ تَحُسُّونَهُمْ [1] بِإِذْنِهِ حَتَّى إِذا فَشِلْتُمْ 2 In this phrase, the Quran says: Allah's promise regarding your victory was completely true, and for this reason, you were victorious at the start of the battle and scattered the enemy by Allah's command. This promise of victory continued as long as you did not abandon steadfastness and obedience to the Prophet's command. The defeat began from the moment that weakness and disobedience overcame you. That is, if you imagined that the promise of victory was unconditional, you were gravely mistaken; all promises of victory are conditional upon following Allah's command.

Regarding where Allah had promised the Muslims victory in this battle, there are two possibilities: first, it refers to the general promises repeatedly given by Allah to the believers regarding victory over enemies; second, that the Messenger of Allah—whose promise is the promise of Allah—had explicitly promised the Muslims victory in this field before the start of the Battle of Uhud.

وَ تَنازَعْتُمْ فِي الْأَمْرِ وَ عَصَيْتُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما أَراكُمْ ما تُحِبُّونَ From this phrase, which refers to the situation of the archers on Mount Aynayn, it is clearly understood that the archers at the mountain pass disagreed about abandoning their post, and a large group committed disobedience and opposition (the expression "عصيتم" meaning "you disobeyed" indicates that the majority had turned to opposition). Therefore, the Quran says: after witnessing that striking victory which you desired, you took the path of disobedience. In reality, you exerted the necessary effort to achieve victory, but you did not show steadfastness in maintaining it; and maintaining victories is always more difficult than achieving them.

مِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُرِيدُ الدُّنْيا وَ مِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُرِيدُ الْآخِرَةَ At this moment, a group of you desired the world and the gathering of spoils, while another group, such as "Abdullah bin Jubayr" himself and a few steadfast archers, desired the Hereafter and divine rewards.

ثُمَّ صَرَفَكُمْ عَنْهُمْ لِيَبْتَلِيَكُمْ At this point, the tide turned, and Allah changed your victory into defeat to test you, admonish you, and nurture you.

وَ لَقَدْ عَفا عَنْكُمْ وَ اللَّهُ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ Then Allah forgave all these disobediences and sins of yours, even though you deserved punishment, for Allah does not withhold any kind of grace from the believers.

إِذْ تُصْعِدُونَ [1] وَ لا تَلْوُونَ عَلى‌ أَحَدٍ وَ الرَّسُولُ يَدْعُوكُمْ فِي أُخْراكُمْ 2 In this verse, Allah reminds the Muslims of the closing scene of Uhud and says: Remember when you were scattering in every direction and fleeing, not looking back at anyone to see how your other brothers were faring, while the Prophet was calling out to you from behind: "O servants of Allah! To me! To me! For I am the Messenger of Allah!" But none of you paid attention to his words.

فَأَثابَكُمْ غَمًّا بِغَمٍ At this time, grief and sorrow came upon you one after another: grief for the defeat, for the loss of a group of brave officers and soldiers, for the wounded, and for the rumor of the Prophet's martyrdom and the reality of his injuries. All of these were the result of that disobedience.

لِكَيْلا تَحْزَنُوا عَلى‌ ما فاتَكُمْ وَ لا ما أَصابَكُمْ This flood of grief and sorrow rushed toward you so that you would no longer grieve over the loss of war spoils, nor be distressed by the wounds that afflict you in the field of battle on the path to victory.

وَ اللَّهُ خَبِيرٌ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ Allah was aware of your deeds and well knows the state of the obedient ones and the true mujahideen, as well as those who fled. Therefore, none of you should deceive yourselves or claim anything contrary to what occurred on the field of Uhud. If you truly belong to the first group, thank Allah; otherwise, repent for your sins.

[1]: "تحسونهم" is derived from the root "حس" (hiss) and means to destroy someone's senses and kill them; i.e., you were killing them.

[1]: "تصعدون" is from the root "اصعاد" which, according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, means walking on level ground or toward an elevation, whereas "صعود" is specific to going upward. Perhaps the use of this term in the verse is because some of the fugitives went up the mountain while others scattered across the plain. 2: "اخريكم" means "ورائكم": behind you.

The Whispers of Ignorance

ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ الْغَمِّ أَمَنَةً نُعاساً [1] The night after the Battle of Uhud was a painful and highly anxious night. The Muslims anticipated that the victorious soldiers of the Quraysh would return to Medina once more to crush the last of the Muslim resistance, and perhaps scattered news of the idolaters' decision to return had also reached them. Certainly, if they had returned, the most dangerous stage of the war would have occurred.

In the midst of this, the true mujahideen and the repentant ones—who regretted fleeing at Uhud, trusted in the Lord's grace, and were heartened and reassured by the Prophet's promises regarding the future—experienced a peaceful and soothing sleep amidst this general anxiety and terror, while their battle dress was on them and their weapons were by their side. However, the hypocrites and those weak in faith and fearful remained awake all night amidst a mass of distressing thoughts, and without intending to, they acted as guards for the true believers. The above verse describes the events of that night and says:

"Then, after all that grief and sorrow of the day of Uhud, He sent down security upon you. This security was a light slumber that overcame a group of you, but there was another group who thought only of their own lives and considered nothing but their own salvation, and for this reason, they had completely lost their peace."

This is one of the important effects of faith: that the believer enjoys a delightful tranquility even in the life of this world, the taste of which those without faith, the hypocrites, or those weak in faith never experience. Then the Holy Quran proceeds to describe the conversations and thoughts of the hypocrites and those of weak faith who stayed awake that night, saying:

"يَظُنُّونَ بِاللَّهِ غَيْرَ الْحَقِّ ظَنَّ الْجاهِلِيَّةِ": They harbored incorrect thoughts about Allah, similar to the thoughts of the era of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) before Islam. In their minds, they entertained the possibility that the Prophet's promises were false, and they said to one another or to themselves: "هَلْ لَنا مِنَ الْأَمْرِ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ": "Is it possible, with this heart-wrenching situation we see, that victory will be ours?" Meaning, it is very unlikely or impossible. The Quran says in response to them: "قُلْ إِنَّ الْأَمْرَ كُلَّهُ لِلَّهِ": "Say: Yes, victory is in the hand of Allah, and if He wills and sees you worthy, He will grant it to you."

They were not willing to express all that they held in their hearts because they feared being placed in the ranks of the disbelievers (يُخْفُونَ فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ ما لا يُبْدُونَ لَكَ).

It seems they imagined that the defeat at Uhud was a sign of the falsity of the religion of Islam, and thus they said: "لَوْ كانَ لَنا مِنَ الْأَمْرِ شَيْ‌ءٌ ما قُتِلْنا هاهُنا": "If we were on the right path and had a share in victory, we would not have suffered so many casualties here."

Allah points to two matters in response to them: First, do not imagine that anyone can escape death by fleeing from the battlefield or from the hardships they must rush to meet. Those whose appointed time has come, even if they remain in their homes, death would have marched to their beds and killed them there (قُلْ لَوْ كُنْتُمْ فِي بُيُوتِكُمْ لَبَرَزَ الَّذِينَ كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَتْلُ إِلى‌ مَضاجِعِهِمْ).

Fundamentally, a nation that is doomed to defeat due to the weakness of its majority will eventually taste death. How much better it is to see it in the field of Jihad and under the sword-strokes of the enemy in an honorable struggle, rather than having them burst into one's home and destroy them with humiliation in their bed.

Second, these events must occur so that everyone reveals what is in their heart and the ranks become distinct. Furthermore, individuals are gradually nurtured, their intentions purified, their faith strengthened, and their hearts cleansed (وَ لِيَبْتَلِيَ اللَّهُ ما فِي صُدُورِكُمْ وَ لِيُمَحِّصَ ما فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ).

At the end of the verse, it says: "وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ": Allah knows the secrets within the breasts, and for this reason, He does not look only at people's actions, but He also wants to test their hearts and purify them from any pollution of polytheism, hypocrisy, doubt, and hesitation.

[1]: "امنة" means safety/security, and "نعاس" refers to a light sleep or slumber.

Ayah

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ تَوَلَّوْا۟ مِنكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْتَقَى ٱلْجَمْعَانِ إِنَّمَا ٱسْتَزَلَّهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ بِبَعْضِ مَا كَسَبُوا۟ ۖ وَلَقَدْ عَفَا ٱللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ (١٥٥)
Those of you who fled on the day when the two hosts met, only Satan had made them stumble because of some of their deeds. Certainly, Allah has excused them, for Allah is all-forgiving, all-forbearing. (155)

Commentary

Sin is the Source of Other Sins

إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَوَلَّوْا مِنْكُمْ‌ ...

This verse, which again refers to the events of the Battle of Uhud, reveals another reality to the Muslims: that the lapses which overcome a person due to Satanic whispers and lead him into sins are the result of unsuitable spiritual foundations created within the person by previous sins, which have paved the way for further transgressions. Otherwise, Satanic whispers have no effect on pure hearts that do not bear the traces of former sins. Therefore, He says:

"Those of you who fled on the day of Uhud, Satan caused them to slip because of some of what they had earned (their previous deeds), but Allah has already forgiven them; indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Forbearing." In this way, He teaches them that to achieve victory in the future, they must first strive to discipline themselves and cleanse their hearts of sin.

It is possible that the "sin" they previously committed refers to the sin of worldliness, the gathering of spoils, and disobeying the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله command in the heat of the battle, or other sins committed before the event of Uhud that had weakened the power of faith within them.

The great commentator, the late Tabarsi, narrates under this verse from "Abu al-Qasim Balkhi" that on the day of Uhud, all the Muhajirin and Ansar fled except for thirteen people (who, including the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, numbered fourteen). Of these thirteen, eight were from the Ansar and five were from the Muhajirin. There is disagreement regarding the specific identities of these individuals, except for Ali عليه السلام and Talhah, whom everyone unanimously agrees did not flee.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَقَالُوا۟ لِإِخْوَٰنِهِمْ إِذَا ضَرَبُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَوْ كَانُوا۟ غُزًّى لَّوْ كَانُوا۟ عِندَنَا مَا مَاتُوا۟ وَمَا قُتِلُوا۟ لِيَجْعَلَ ٱللَّهُ ذَٰلِكَ حَسْرَةً فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (١٥٦)
O you who have faith! Do not be like the faithless who say of their brethren, when they travel in the land or go into battle, ‘Had they stayed with us they would not have died or been killed,’ so that Allah may make it a regret in their hearts. But Allah gives life and brings death, and Allah watches what you do. (156)
وَلَئِن قُتِلْتُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْ مُتُّمْ لَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَحْمَةٌ خَيْرٌ مِّمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ (١٥٧)
If you are slain in the way of Allah, or die, forgiveness and mercy from Allah are surely better than what they amass. (157)
وَلَئِن مُّتُّمْ أَوْ قُتِلْتُمْ لَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (١٥٨)
If you die or are slain, you will surely be mustered toward Allah. (158)

Commentary

The Exploitation by the Hypocrites

يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا ...

The event of Uhud was of extraordinary importance for the Muslims from two perspectives: First, it was a full-length mirror that could reflect the true face of the Muslims of that time, compelling them to reform their condition and eliminate their points of weakness. For this reason, the Quran emphasizes it tremendously, drawing educational lessons from it in many verses we have previously read and will read in the future.

On the other hand, this event prepared the ground for the poisonous propaganda of enemies and hypocrites. Consequently, many verses were revealed to neutralize these toxic efforts, including the verses mentioned above.

To crush the destructive activities of the hypocrites and to warn the Muslims, these verses first address the believers, saying: "Do not be like those who disbelieved, who say of their brothers when they travel through the land or go forth to fight and are killed: 'If they had stayed with us, they would not have died or been killed.'"

Although they utter these words in the guise of compassion, they have no intention other than poisoning your morale, and you must not be influenced by these toxic words or utter such sentences.

لِيَجْعَلَ اللَّهُ ذَلِكَ حَسْرَةً فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ:

If you, the believers, are influenced by their misleading words and repeat the same things, your morale will naturally weaken, and you will refrain from going to the field of Jihad or traveling in the way of Allah. Thus, they would achieve their goal. However, do not do this; go to the field of Jihad with strong morale so that this regret remains in the hearts of the hypocrites forever.

Then, the Quran provides three logical responses to their poisonous propaganda:

1- Life and death are in the hands of Allah in every circumstance, and traveling or being present on the battlefield cannot change its definitive course, and Allah is well-aware of all the deeds of His servants. (وَاللَّهُ يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ وَاللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ).

2- Furthermore, if you die or are killed in the way of Allah, and—as the hypocrites imagine—a premature death overtakes you, you have lost nothing. For the forgiveness and mercy of your Lord are better than all the wealth that you or the hypocrites might accumulate for yourselves by continuing to live. (وَلَئِنْ قُتِلْتُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَوْ مُتُّمْ لَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَرَحْمَةٌ خَيْرٌ مِمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ).

Fundamentally, these two should not even be compared. However, in the face of the lowly thoughts that prioritized a few days of life and wealth accumulation over the honor of Jihad and martyrdom, there was no way but to say: what you gain through martyrdom or dying in the way of Allah is better than what the disbelievers accumulate through a wretched life mixed with lusts and worldliness.

3- Above all, death does not mean annihilation and non-existence, which you dread so much; rather, it is a gateway toward another life on a much vaster scale, intertwined with eternity. (وَلَئِنْ مُتُّمْ أَوْ قُتِلْتُمْ لَإِلَى اللَّهِ تُحْشَرُونَ) "And whether you die or are killed, unto Allah you will be gathered."

It is noteworthy that in the above verses, dying during travel is mentioned alongside martyrdom in the way of Allah. This is because the reference is to travels undertaken in the way of Allah and for Allah, such as traveling toward the battlefield or missionary journeys and the like. Since travel at that time was fraught with difficulties, dangers, and numerous diseases, the mortality rate during travel was sometimes no less than the mortality rate on the battlefield.

As for the possibility suggested by some commentators that "travel" here refers to commercial trips, it is very far from the meaning of the verse. This is because the disbelievers would never regret such a thing; rather, it was their way of accumulating wealth. Furthermore, this subject would have no effect on weakening the morale of the Muslims after the Battle of Uhud, nor would the Muslims' lack of alignment with the disbelievers in this regard cause them any regret. Therefore, it apparently refers to dying during the journey toward the field of Jihad or other Islamic programs.

Ayahs

فَبِمَا رَحْمَةٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ لِنتَ لَهُمْ ۖ وَلَوْ كُنتَ فَظًّا غَلِيظَ ٱلْقَلْبِ لَٱنفَضُّوا۟ مِنْ حَوْلِكَ ۖ فَٱعْفُ عَنْهُمْ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ وَشَاوِرْهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَمْرِ ۖ فَإِذَا عَزَمْتَ فَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُتَوَكِّلِينَ (١٥٩)
It is by Allah’s mercy that you are gentle to them; had you been harsh and hardhearted, they would have surely scattered from around you. So excuse them and plead for forgiveness for them, and consult them in the affairs, and once you are resolved, put your trust in Allah. Indeed Allah loves those who trust in Him. (159)
إِن يَنصُرْكُمُ ٱللَّهُ فَلَا غَالِبَ لَكُمْ ۖ وَإِن يَخْذُلْكُمْ فَمَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَنصُرُكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ ۗ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (١٦٠)
If Allah helps you, no one can overcome you, but if He forsakes you, who will help you after Him? So in Allah let all the faithful put their trust. (160)

Commentary

The Decree of General Amnesty

فَبِما رَحْمَةٍ مِنَ اللَّهِ لِنْتَ لَهُمْ Although a series of general instructions are given to the Prophet ص in this verse, and its content consists of general and fundamental programs, in terms of its revelation, it concerns the incident of "Uhud." This is because after the Muslims returned from Uhud, those who had fled from the battle gathered around the Prophet ص and, while expressing remorse, requested pardon and forgiveness.

In this verse, Allah issued the command for their general amnesty to the Prophet ص, and the Prophet ص accepted the repentant wrongdoers with open arms.

In the aforementioned verse, reference is first made to one of the extraordinary moral merits of the Prophet ص, stating: By the mercy and grace of your Lord, you became gentle with the people, whereas if you had been rude, harsh-hearted, and severe, they would have dispersed from around you.

"Fazz" (فظ) in linguistics refers to someone whose speech is sharp and coarse, and "Ghaliz al-Qalb" (غليظ القلب) refers to someone who is hard-hearted and shows no flexibility or affection in practice. Therefore, although both words signify harshness, one is mostly used for harshness in speech and the other for harshness in action. In this way, Allah points to the complete leniency of the Prophet ص and his flexibility toward ignorant and sinful individuals.

فَاعْفُ عَنْهُمْ وَ اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ Then He commands him to overlook their shortcomings, include them in his pardon, and seek forgiveness for them. This means: "Pass over the disloyalties they showed you and the calamities they brought upon you in this war regarding your own right, and I shall intercede for them with you; and regarding the oppositions they showed toward My command, you be their intercessor and seek My forgiveness for them."

In other words, "Pardon what pertains to your right, and I shall forgive what pertains to My right." The Prophet ص acted upon Allah's command and included them all in his general amnesty.

It is clear that this was one of the manifest instances of pardon, gentleness, and flexibility. Had the Prophet ص done otherwise, the grounds for the people's dispersal would have been fully prepared. A people who had suffered such a crushing defeat and had given so many dead and wounded (even though they themselves were considered the primary cause of it all) were in dire need of affection, consolation, and a balm for their physical and emotional wounds, so that all these injuries might heal quickly and they would be ready for future events.

This verse points to one of the important qualities necessary for any leader, which is the matter of forgiveness, gentleness, and flexibility toward those who have committed a violation and later regretted it. It is obvious that a person in a position of leadership, if he is harsh, ill-tempered, inflexible, and lacking the spirit of forgiveness, will soon face failure in his programs, and people will scatter from him, hindering him from his duty of leadership. For this reason, Ali عليه السلام says in one of his short maxims:

"The tool of leadership is a broad chest (patience and tolerance)."

The Command to Consult

وَ شاوِرْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْرِ After the decree of general amnesty, to revive their personality and renew their intellectual and spiritual life, He commands: "Consult with the Muslims in affairs and seek their views and opinions."

This command is because—as we mentioned—before the start of the Battle of Uhud, the Prophet ص consulted with his companions on how to face the enemy, and the majority opinion was that the camp should be at the foot of Uhud, and we saw that this opinion did not yield a satisfactory result. Here, the thought occurred to many that in the future, the Prophet ص should not consult with anyone. The Quran responds to this way of thinking and commands: "Consult with them again, even if the result of consultation in some cases is not beneficial." This is because, from a general perspective, its benefits overall far outweigh its harms, and the effect it has on nurturing the individual and society and elevating their character is higher than all of these.

Now let us see on what subjects the Prophet ص consulted with the people.

Although the word "al-Amr" (الامر) in "شاوِرْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْرِ" has a broad meaning and includes all affairs, it is certain that the Prophet ص never consulted with the people regarding Divine laws (Ahkam); rather, in those matters, He was purely a follower of revelation.

Therefore, the subject of consultation was only the manner of executing commands and the way to implement Divine laws. In other words, the Prophet ص never consulted in legislation (law-making) and only sought the opinion of Muslims in the manner of executing the law. Thus, sometimes when the Prophet ص proposed a suggestion, the Muslims would first ask: "Is this a Divine decree and a law that is not subject to opinion, or does it relate to the method of applying the laws?" If it was of the second type, they would express their views, and if it was of the first type, they would submit.

As in the Battle of Badr, the army of Islam, according to the Prophet's ص command, wanted to camp at a certain spot. One of the companions named "Hubab ibn Mundhir" said: "O Messenger of Allah ص, is this place you have chosen for the camp based on Allah's command such that changing it is not permissible, or is it based on your own discretion?"

The Prophet ص said: "There is no specific command regarding it." He replied: "For this and that reason, this is not a suitable place for the camp; command the army to move from here and choose a place near the water." The Holy Prophet ص liked his view and acted according to his opinion. [1]

[1]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 4, p. 200.

The Importance of Consultation in Islam

The subject of consultation is regarded with special importance in Islam. Even though the Holy Prophet ص, regardless of heavenly revelation, possessed such a powerful intellect that He had no need for consultation, He held consultation sessions regarding the public affairs of Muslims that involved the execution of Divine laws (not legislation) for two reasons: first, to draw the Muslims' attention to the importance of consultation so they would make it a fundamental part of their life programs; and second, to nurture the power of thought and reflection in individuals. He especially valued the opinions of experts, to the extent that He would sometimes set aside His own view out of respect for them, as we observed in the Battle of Uhud. It can be said that one of the factors of the Prophet's ص success in advancing Islamic goals was this very subject.

Fundamentally, people who carry out their important tasks through consultation and mutual advice, and whose experts sit for deliberation, are less likely to fall into error. Conversely, individuals who are afflicted with autocracy of opinion and consider themselves independent of the thoughts of others—no matter how intellectually extraordinary they may be—often fall into dangerous and painful mistakes.

Furthermore, autocracy of opinion kills the personality within the masses, halts thoughts, and destroys ready talents; thus, the greatest human capital of a nation is lost.

Additionally, one who consults with others in performing his tasks is less likely to be envied if he meets with success, because others consider his victory their own, and usually, a person does not feel envy toward a task he performed himself. And if he happens to face failure, the tongue of protest, blame, and reproach of the people is closed to him, for no one protests the result of their own work; not only will they not protest, but they will also show sympathy and care.

Another benefit of consultation is that a person will realize the value of individuals' personalities and the extent of their friendship or enmity toward him, and this recognition paves the way for his victory. Perhaps the consultations of the Prophet ص, despite the extraordinary intellectual power He possessed, were for the sake of all these aspects combined.

In Islamic traditions, great emphasis has been placed on consultation: In a Hadith from the Holy Prophet ص, it is narrated that He said: "No servant was ever wretched through consultation, and none was ever happy through autocracy of opinion." [1]

In the words of Ali عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever is autocratic in his opinion perishes, and whoever consults with great men shares in their intellects." [2]

It is also narrated from the Prophet ص that He said: "When your leaders are the best among you, and your wealthy are the generous among you, and your affairs are conducted through consultation among you, then the surface of the earth is better for you than its belly (i.e., you are worthy of life). But if your leaders are the wicked among you, and your wealthy are the stingy among you, and your affairs are not conducted through consultation, then the belly of the earth is better for you than its surface." [3]

With whom should we consult?

It is certain that not everyone can be a consultant, for sometimes they have weaknesses that make consulting them a cause of misery and backwardness. As Ali عليه السلام says: Do not consult with three groups:

1- "Do not include a miser in your consultation, for he will turn you away from generosity and frighten you with poverty."

2- "And do not consult a coward, for he will weaken you in [performing] affairs."

3- "And do not consult a greedy person, for he will make greed seem fair to you through injustice." [4]

The Duty of the Consultant

Just as Islam has given emphatic commands regarding seeking consultation, it has also emphasized to those who are consulted that they should not withhold any goodwill. Betrayal in consultation is considered one of the great sins. Even this rule holds true regarding non-Muslims; that is, if a person accepts a request for consultation from a non-Muslim, he has no right to betray him in the consultation or to express anything other than what he perceives to be correct.

In the Treatise on Rights (Risalat al-Huquq) narrated from Imam Sajjad Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, he says: "The right of the one who seeks your consultation is that if you have an opinion for him, you should advise him, and if you do not know, you should guide him to someone who does. And the right of the consultant over you is that you should not suspect him in what does not agree with your opinion." [5]

The Shura of Umar

A group of Sunni commentators and scholars, upon reaching the above verse, have referred to the six-member council (Shura) of Umar for choosing the third caliph and, through detailed statements, have considered it compliant with the above verse and the narrations on consultation.

Although a full explanation of this discussion belongs to books of theology (Aqa'id), it is necessary here to briefly mention a few points:

Firstly: The selection of the Imam and the successor of the Prophet ص must only be from the Lord, for he, like the Prophet ص, must possess qualities such as infallibility (Ismah) and the like, the recognition of which is only in the hand of Allah. In other words, just as a Prophet cannot be appointed through consultation, the selection of an Imam is also not possible through consultation.

Secondly: The aforementioned six-member council never complied with the standards of consultation. For if the intention was consultation with the general Muslims, what was the meaning of restricting it to six people?! And if the goal was consultation with the thinkers of the Ummah, the thinkers were not limited to these six; highly capable individuals like Salman—who was a consultant to the Prophet ص himself—Abu Dharr, Miqdad, Ibn Abbas, and their like were outside the circle of this consultation. Therefore, restricting the consultants to those six resembles a political factionalism more than a consultative body. And if the intention was to select influential people for consultation so that their vote would be accepted by others, it was still not correct, for personalities like Sa'd ibn Ubadah, who was the absolute leader of the Ansar, and Abu Dharr al-Ghifari, who was the great figure of the Banu Ghifar tribe, and others like them were excluded from this consultation.

Thirdly: We know that very harsh and heavy conditions were set for this consultation, and opponents were threatened with death, whereas such a thing does not exist in the consultative programs of Islam.

The Stage of Final Decision

فَإِذا عَزَمْتَ فَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللَّهِ Just as one must show gentleness and flexibility during consultation, one must be decisive at the time of making the final decision. Therefore, after holding the consultation and the result becoming clear, one must set aside all doubt, hesitation, and scattered opinions and decide with firmness. This is exactly what is expressed in the above verse as "Azm" (عزم), which is a firm decision.

It is noteworthy that in the above phrase, the matter of consultation is mentioned in the plural form (شاورهم), but the final decision is placed solely upon the Prophet ص and mentioned in the singular form (عزمت).

This difference in expression points to an important point: the study and investigation of various aspects of social issues must be done collectively, but when a plan is approved, a single will must be employed for its execution. Otherwise, chaos will arise, for if the execution of a program is carried out by multiple leaders without inspiration from a single supervisor, it will certainly face conflict and failure. For this reason, in today's world as well, consultation is done collectively, but its execution is entrusted to governments whose organizations are managed under one person.

Another important point is that the above phrase says: At the time of the final decision, you must have trust (Tawakkul) in Allah. This means that while providing the ordinary means and tools, do not forget to seek help from the infinite power of the Lord.

Of course, the meaning of Tawakkul is not that a person should disregard the means and causes of victory that Allah has placed at his disposal in the material world. As narrated in a Hadith from the Holy Prophet ص, when an Arab had not tied the leg of his camel and left it unguarded, considering this an act of trust in Allah, He said to him: "Tie it and then trust."

Rather, the intention is that a person should not be imprisoned within the four walls of the material world and the limits of his own power and ability, but should fix his eyes on the support and grace of the Lord. This special attention grants a person peace, assurance, and extraordinary spiritual and moral strength, which will have a great effect when facing difficulties. (A further explanation regarding the issue of Tawakkul and its relationship with the use of the means of the natural world will, God willing, be read under the verse: وَ مَنْ يَتَّقِ اللَّهَ يَجْعَلْ لَهُ مَخْرَجاً [Surah At-Talaq, Verse 2]).

إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُتَوَكِّلِينَ At the end of the verse, He commands that the believers must rely only on Allah, for Allah loves those who trust [in Him].

Additionally, it is understood from this verse that Tawakkul must certainly come after consultation and the use of all facilities available to a person.

The Result of Trust

إِنْ يَنْصُرْكُمُ اللَّهُ فَلا غالِبَ لَكُمْ وَ إِنْ يَخْذُلْكُمْ فَمَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يَنْصُرُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ

In this verse, which completes the previous one, the reason for trusting in Allah is explained: His power is the highest of powers; if He undertakes to support anyone, no one can overcome him. Just as if He withdraws His support from someone, no one is capable of supporting him. One from whom all victories originate in this way must be relied upon, and help must be sought from Him.

This verse encourages the believers to rely on the invincible power of Allah in addition to preparing all kinds of outward means.

In reality, the address in the previous verse was to the Holy Prophet, giving Him commands. But in this verse, the address is to all believers, telling them: Like the Prophet, they must rely on the Pure Essence of Allah. Therefore, at the end of the verse, we read: وَ عَلَى اللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ: "And upon Allah let the believers rely."

It goes without saying that Allah's support, or His withdrawal of support toward the believers, is not without reason; it occurs based on merits and worthiness. Those who trample Allah's command and neglect to provide material and spiritual forces will never be included in His help. Conversely, those who rise to fight the enemy with compact ranks, pure intentions, firm resolves, and the preparation of all necessary means, the hand of the Lord's support will be behind them.

[1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi. [2]: Nahj al-Balagha. [3]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi. [4]: Nahj al-Balagha, Command to Malik al-Ashtar. [5]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 405.

Ayah

وَمَا كَانَ لِنَبِىٍّ أَن يَغُلَّ ۚ وَمَن يَغْلُلْ يَأْتِ بِمَا غَلَّ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ ثُمَّ تُوَفَّىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا كَسَبَتْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (١٦١)
A prophet may not breach his trust, and whoever breaches his trust will bring his breaches on the Day of Resurrection; then every soul shall be recompensed fully for what it has earned, and they will not be wronged. (161)

Commentary

Every Form of Treachery is Forbidden

وَ ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَغُلَ‌ Considering that the above verse was revealed following the verses of "Uhud," and according to a narration cited by a group of early commentators, this verse responds to the baseless excuses of some of the warriors at Uhud. To explain: when some of the archers at Uhud wanted to abandon their critical outpost to collect spoils of war, their commander ordered them not to move from their positions, assuring them that the Messenger of Allah would not deprive them of the spoils. However, those worldly-minded individuals, to hide their true motives, said, "We fear the Prophet might overlook us in the distribution of the spoils, so we must secure our own share." They said this, abandoned the outposts, and began collecting spoils, which led to those painful events.

The Quran responds to them by saying: Did you imagine that the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) would betray you, while it is impossible for any Prophet to commit treachery?

وَ ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَغُلَ‌ [^1] In this verse, Allah declares the sacred station of the Prophets to be entirely free from treachery and states that such a thing is fundamentally unworthy of the station of Prophethood; that is, treachery is incompatible with Prophethood. If a Prophet were treacherous, he could no longer be trusted in delivering the Divine message and propagating the laws.

It goes without saying that the verse negates every form of treachery from the Prophets, whether it be treachery in distributing spoils, or in safeguarding people's trusts, or in receiving Revelation and conveying it to the servants of Allah.

It is strange for someone who considers the Prophet to be the trustworthy recipient of Allah's Revelation to then entertain the possibility that the Prophet—God forbid—would issue an unjust ruling regarding war spoils and deprive him of his right.

Of course, it is clear that treachery is not permissible for anyone, whether a Prophet or otherwise. However, since the discussion of those making excuses at the Battle of Uhud was about the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), the verse first speaks of the Prophets and then adds:

وَ مَنْ يَغْلُلْ يَأْتِ بِما غَلَّ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ: "And whoever betrays shall bring what he betrayed on the Day of Resurrection." He will carry what he misappropriated on his own shoulders as evidence of his crime, or bring it with him to the scene of the Gathering, and in this way, he will be disgraced before everyone.

Some commentators have said: The meaning of carrying it on the shoulders or bringing it along is not that he will literally carry the exact object he misappropriated, but rather it refers to carrying the responsibility for it. However, considering the issue of the "embodiment of deeds" (tajassum al-a'mal) on the Day of Judgment, there is no need for such an interpretation. Rather, as the literal wording of the verse testifies, the actual objects misappropriated will be on the shoulders of the traitors or with them as evidence of their crime.

ثُمَّ تُوَفَّى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما كَسَبَتْ وَ هُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ‌: "Then every soul shall be paid in full what it has earned, and they shall not be wronged." This means people will find their deeds exactly as they were, and for this reason, no injustice or oppression will be done to anyone, because everyone receives what they themselves have acquired, whether good or bad.

The above verse and the Hadiths issued by the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) in condemnation of treachery had a remarkable effect on the upbringing of the Muslims. They were trained such that often the slightest treachery, especially regarding war spoils and public property, did not occur. It was such that they would bring valuable yet small items—in which treachery would not have been difficult—completely untouched to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) or the rulers who came after him, in a manner that amazed every observer. These were the same wild and predatory Arabs of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) who, under the light of Islamic teachings, had reached this level of human refinement.

It was as if they saw the scene of the Resurrection before their eyes, where treacherous people carry the property they misappropriated on their shoulders in front of everyone. This very faith warned them to abandon even the thought of treachery.

Tabari narrates in his history that when the Muslims entered Madain and began collecting spoils, one of the Muslims brought a very valuable item to the official in charge of collecting spoils. They were astonished upon seeing it and said: "We have never seen anything so precious." Then they asked him: "Have you taken anything from it?"

He said: "By Allah, if it were not for 'Allah,' I would never have brought it to you." They realized that this man possessed a special spiritual character and asked him to identify himself. He replied: "No, by Allah, I will never identify myself so that you may praise me, nor will I tell anyone else so they may commend me; rather, I thank Allah and am satisfied with His reward." [^1]

[^1]: This word is derived from "ghalal," meaning treachery. "Ghalal" originally means the gradual and secret penetration of water into the roots of trees. Since treachery occurs secretly and gradually, it is called "ghulul." If internal heat resulting from thirst is called "ghalil," it is for the same reason.

Ayahs

أَفَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ رِضْوَٰنَ ٱللَّهِ كَمَنۢ بَآءَ بِسَخَطٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَمَأْوَىٰهُ جَهَنَّمُ ۚ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٦٢)
Is he who follows [the course of] Allah’s pleasure like him who earns Allah’s displeasure and whose refuge is hell, an evil destination? (162)
هُمْ دَرَجَـٰتٌ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ (١٦٣)
They have ranks with Allah, and Allah watches what they do. (163)

Commentary

Those Who Did Not Participate in Jihad

أَ فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ رِضْوانَ اللَّهِ In the preceding verses, various aspects of the Battle of "Uhud" and its outcomes were discussed.

[^1]: - Tarikh al-Tabari, Vol. 4, p. 16.

Now it is the turn of the hypocrites and the weak-of-faith believers who, by following them, failed to appear on the battlefield. Indeed, we read in narrations that when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله issued the command to march toward "Uhud," a group of hypocrites refrained from attending the field under the pretext that they were not certain a war would actually occur, and some weak-of-faith Muslims joined them. The above verse describes their fate and says: "Is one who follows the pleasure of Allah like one who draws the wrath of Allah upon himself and whose refuge is Hell? And wretched is the destination." Then He says: هُمْ دَرَجاتٌ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ "They are [varying] degrees in the sight of Allah." This indicates that not only is there a difference between the comfort-seeking hypocrites and the mujahideen, but each individual within these two ranks will have a specific degree before Allah based on the extent of their sacrifice and devotion, or their hypocrisy and enmity toward the Truth—degrees that start from zero and extend beyond what can be imagined.

It is noteworthy that in a narration from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, it is stated: "Every degree is as the distance between the heaven and the earth." [^1] In another hadith, it is mentioned that the inhabitants of Paradise see those in the Illiyyin (highest) degrees just as a star is seen in the sky. [^2] However, it should be noted that "darajah" (degree) is usually applied to steps by which a person ascends to a high point, whereas the steps used to descend to a deep point are called "dark" (rhyming with marg). Therefore, regarding the Prophets, we read in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 253: وَ رَفَعَ بَعْضَهُمْ دَرَجاتٍ ("and He raised some of them in degrees"), while regarding the hypocrites, we read in Surah An-Nisa, verse 145:

[^1]: - Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 406.

[^2]: - Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, under the mentioned verse.

إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ فِي الدَّرْكِ الْأَسْفَلِ مِنَ النَّارِ ("Indeed, the hypocrites will be in the lowest depths of the Fire"). But in the verse under discussion, since both groups are mentioned together, the side of the believers has been favored in the terminology, and the word "darajah" (degree) is used for both (this style of expression is known in literary terminology as taghlib).

At the end of the verse, He says: وَ اللَّهُ بَصِيرٌ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ: "And Allah is All-Seeing of what they do." He knows well which degree each person deserves according to their intention, faith, and action.

An Effective Educational Method

In the Holy Quran, many truths regarding religious, moral, and social knowledge are presented in the form of a question, and both sides of the issue are left to the listener so that he may choose one through his own reflection. This method, which should be called the "indirect method," has an extraordinary effect on the impact of educational programs. This is because a human being usually values his own thoughts and perceptions of various issues more than anything else. When an issue is presented as a definitive and dogmatic statement, he sometimes shows resistance and views it as an alien thought. However, when it is presented as a question and he hears the answer from within his own conscience and heart, he considers it his own thought and discernment, viewing it as a "familiar idea," and thus does not resist it. This style of teaching is especially effective against stubborn individuals as well as children.

The Quran makes extensive use of this method, a few examples of which we mention here:

1- قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الَّذِينَ يَعْلَمُونَ وَ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ: "Say, are those who know equal to those who do not know?" [^1]

2- قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الْأَعْمى‌ وَ الْبَصِيرُ أَ فَلا تَتَفَكَّرُونَ: "Say, is the blind equivalent to the seeing? Do you not then reflect?" [^2]

3- قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الْأَعْمى‌ وَ الْبَصِيرُ أَمْ هَلْ تَسْتَوِي الظُّلُماتُ وَ النُّورُ: "Say, is the seeing equal to the blind? Or is darkness equal to light?" [^3]

Ayah

لَقَدْ مَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذْ بَعَثَ فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِّنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَإِن كَانُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ لَفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (١٦٤)
Allah certainly favoured the faithful when He raised up among them an apostle from among themselves to recite to them His signs and to purify them and teach them the Book and wisdom, and earlier they had indeed been in manifest error. (164)

Commentary

The Greatest Blessing of Allah

لَقَدْ مَنَّ اللَّهُ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذْ بَعَثَ فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِنْ أَنْفُسِهِمْ‌ In this verse, the discussion turns to the greatest Divine blessing, namely the blessing of the "Mission of the Prophet of Islam."

[^1]: - Surah Az-Zumar, Verse 9.

[^2]: - Surah Al-An'am, Verse 50.

[^3]: - Surah Ar-Ra'd, Verse 16.

In reality, this is a response to the questions that crossed the minds of some new Muslims after the Battle of Uhud, asking: why should we be so afflicted with difficulties and calamities? The Quran tells them: if you have suffered losses on this path, do not forget that Allah has bestowed upon you the greatest blessing; He has raised a Prophet who purifies and trains you, and prevents you from manifest deviations.

No matter how much effort you exert to preserve this great blessing, and whatever price you pay, it is still insignificant.

It is noteworthy that the mention of this blessing begins with the phrase "لَقَدْ مَنَّ اللَّهُ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌" (Allah surely conferred a favor upon the believers). At first glance, it might be thought that this expression is unseemly [as if reminding someone of a favor], but when we return to the original root of the word "manna" (favor/obligation), the matter becomes completely clear. As Raghib states in the book Al-Mufradat: this word is originally derived from "mann," referring to the stones used for weighing. For this reason, every heavy and precious blessing is called a "minnah." If this takes a practical form—meaning someone actually grants a great favor to another—it is entirely beautiful and valuable. However, if someone magnifies their own small deed through speech and throws it in people's faces, it is a very ugly act. Therefore, the "favor" (minnah) that is rebuked refers to magnifying blessings in speech, but the "favor" that is beautiful is the granting of great blessings.

In the above verse, Allah says: The Lord conferred a favor upon the believers, meaning He practically placed a great blessing at their disposal.

As for why only the believers are mentioned, while the mission of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is for the guidance of all humanity, it is because, in terms of result and impact, only the believers utilize this great blessing and practically claim it for themselves.

Then He says: One of the advantages of this Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is that he is from their own kind and from the human race (مِنْ أَنْفُسِهِمْ‌), not from the genus of angels or the like. This is so he can precisely understand human needs and requirements, and feel their pains, difficulties, calamities, and life issues, and proceed to train them accordingly. Furthermore, the most important part of the educational program of the Prophets is their practical propagation; meaning their actions are the best role model and means of education. This is because one can propagate through the "language of action" better than any other language, and this is only possible if the propagator is of the same genus as those being addressed, possessing the same physical characteristics, instincts, and spiritual structure. If the Prophets were, for example, of the genus of angels, the question would remain for the people: if they do not sin, is it not because they lack human lust, anger, needs, and various instincts? In this way, the program of practical propagation would be rendered ineffective. Therefore, the Prophets were chosen from the human race with the same needs and instincts so they could be a role model for everyone.

يَتْلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ آياتِهِ وَ يُزَكِّيهِمْ وَ يُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتابَ وَ الْحِكْمَةَ Then He says: This Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله implements three important programs regarding them: first, reciting the verses of the Lord to them and acquainting their ears and thoughts with these verses; second, teaching, meaning entering these truths into the depths of their souls; and following that, the purification of souls and the cultivation of moral and human virtues. However, since the primary and ultimate goal is purification and training (tarbiyah), it is mentioned in the verse before teaching (ta'lim), even though in the natural process of education, teaching precedes training.

A population that is completely distant from human truths cannot be easily trained; rather, their ears must be acquainted with Divine words for a time to remove the fear they previously had, then enter the stage of fundamental teaching, and subsequently reap its educational fruit.

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse that purification (tazkiyah) refers to cleansing them from the filth of polytheism, false and superstitious beliefs, and ugly animalistic traits. For as long as the human essence is not purified from these contaminations, it is impossible for it to be ready to learn the Divine Book and true wisdom and knowledge. Just as if you do not clear a tablet of ugly markings, it will never be ready to accept beautiful inscriptions. For this reason, purification in the above verse is mentioned before the teaching of the Book and Wisdom—meaning the lofty and high Islamic sciences.

وَ إِنْ كانُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ‌ The importance of a great blessing becomes clear when we compare the time of enjoying it with the times before it and realize the distance between the two. In the above phrase, the Quran says: take a look at the era before Islam and see what state and what day you were in, and from where you reached where you are now.

It is noteworthy that the Quran describes the state of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) as "ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ‌" (manifest error). This is because there are various types of error and deviation; some means of deviation are such that a person cannot easily understand their falsehood, while sometimes it is such that anyone with a modicum of intellect and perception would immediately realize it.

The people of the world, especially the people of the Arabian Peninsula at the time of the mission of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, were in manifest error. Misery and wretchedness, ignorance and lack of knowledge, and various spiritual contaminations had encompassed all parts of the world at that time, and this disordered state was hidden from no one.

Ayah

أَوَلَمَّآ أَصَـٰبَتْكُم مُّصِيبَةٌ قَدْ أَصَبْتُم مِّثْلَيْهَا قُلْتُمْ أَنَّىٰ هَـٰذَا ۖ قُلْ هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ أَنفُسِكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٦٥)
What, when an affliction visits you—while you have inflicted twice as much—do you say, ‘How is this?’! Say, ‘This is from your own souls.’ Indeed Allah has power over all things. (165)

Commentary

Another Analysis of the Battle of Uhud

This verse is another analysis of the event of Uhud. To explain: a group of Muslims were grieved and anxious over the painful outcomes of the battle, and they repeatedly expressed this sentiment. In the above verse, Allah reminds them of three points:

1- You should not be anxious about the outcome of a single battle; rather, you must calculate all your encounters with the enemy together. If a calamity befell you in this field, in another field (the field of the Battle of Badr), you inflicted twice as much upon the enemy. This is because in Uhud, they martyred seventy of you while taking no prisoners, whereas in Badr, you killed seventy of them and took seventy others as captives.

(أَ وَ لَمَّا أَصابَتْكُمْ مُصِيبَةٌ قَدْ أَصَبْتُمْ مِثْلَيْها) In reality, the phrase "قَدْ أَصَبْتُمْ مِثْلَيْها"—meaning "you struck the enemy with twice as much"—serves as an answer that has been placed before the question.

2- You say: "Where did this calamity come from?" (قُلْتُمْ أَنَّى هذا). But, O Prophet, tell them: "This calamity originated from your own selves, and you must search for the factors of defeat within yourselves" (قُلْ هُوَ مِنْ عِنْدِ أَنْفُسِكُمْ‌).

It was you who, by disobeying the command of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, abandoned the sensitive stronghold of Mount Aynayn; it was you who, before finishing the battle and finalizing its fate, turned to collecting spoils; and it was also you who, during the enemy's counter-attack, abandoned the field and fled from the battle. It was these sins and weaknesses of yours that caused that defeat and all those casualties.

3- You should not be anxious about the future, for Allah is All-Powerful and Capable of all things. If you compensate for your points of weakness, you will be covered by His support (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

Ayahs

وَمَآ أَصَـٰبَكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْتَقَى ٱلْجَمْعَانِ فَبِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٦٦)
What befell you on the day when the two hosts met, was by Allah’s permission, so that He may ascertain the faithful, (166)
وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱلَّذِينَ نَافَقُوا۟ ۚ وَقِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعَالَوْا۟ قَـٰتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أَوِ ٱدْفَعُوا۟ ۖ قَالُوا۟ لَوْ نَعْلَمُ قِتَالًا لَّٱتَّبَعْنَـٰكُمْ ۗ هُمْ لِلْكُفْرِ يَوْمَئِذٍ أَقْرَبُ مِنْهُمْ لِلْإِيمَـٰنِ ۚ يَقُولُونَ بِأَفْوَٰهِهِم مَّا لَيْسَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَكْتُمُونَ (١٦٧)
and ascertain the hypocrites. [When] they were told: ‘Come, fight in the way of Allah, or defend [yourselves], they said, ‘If we knew any fighting, we would have surely followed you.’ That day they were nearer to unfaith than to faith. They say with their mouths what is not in their hearts, and Allah knows well whatever they conceal. (167)

Commentary

The Ranks Must Be Distinguished

The above verse points out the fact that every calamity (such as the calamity of Uhud) that occurs is not without cause; rather, it is a means of testing to separate the ranks of the true Mujahideen from the hypocrites or those with weak faith. Therefore, in the first part of the verse, It says: وَ ما أَصابَكُمْ يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعانِ فَبِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ‌: "What befell you on the day the two armies met [at Uhud] was by Allah's leave."

That day, when the Muslim forces clashed with the idolaters, what happened to you was by Allah's command and took place according to His will and desire. This is because every event, according to the general laws of creation, has a specific cause and reason, and fundamentally, the world is established upon a series of causes and effects. This is a fixed and eternal principle. Based on this principle, any army that shows weakness on the battlefield, sets its heart on wealth and spoils, and forgets the instructions of its compassionate commander, is destined for defeat. Therefore, the meaning of "Allah's leave" (اذن الله) is His will and decree, which is reflected in the universe in the form of the law of causality.

In the second part of the verse, It says: وَ لِيَعْلَمَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَ لِيَعْلَمَ الَّذِينَ نافَقُوا:

Another effect of this war was that the ranks of the believers and the hypocrites would be distinguished from one another, and the people of faith would be known from those of weak faith.

Generally, in the event of Uhud, three distinct groups emerged among the Muslims:

The first group consisted of a limited number of individuals who persevered until the final moments and stood firm against the masses of enemies until their last breath; some drank the nectar of martyrdom, and others sustained heavy wounds.

The second group experienced wavering and anxiety in their hearts; they could not maintain steadfastness until the final moment and chose the path of flight.

The third group was the group of hypocrites who, along the way, withdrew from participating in the war using excuses that will be mentioned and returned to Medina. These were Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul and three hundred of his companions. Had it not been for the severe trial of Uhud, the ranks would never have been so clearly distinguished, and individuals would not have been placed in specific ranks according to their unique characteristics. Otherwise, anyone might have claimed to be the best person of faith.

In reality, the verse points to two things: first, the efficient cause of the defeat at Uhud, and second, its final cause and ultimate result.

It is also necessary to note that the above verse says: لِيَعْلَمَ الَّذِينَ نافَقُوا ("that He might make known those who acted hypocritically") and does not say li-ya'lama al-munafiqin ("that He might make known the hypocrites"). In other words, hypocrisy is mentioned as a "verb" (an action) rather than an "adjective" (a fixed trait). This expression is likely because hypocrisy had not yet become a fixed attribute in all of them; hence, we read in the history of Islam that some of them later succeeded in repenting and joined the ranks of the believers.

Then, the Quran describes the conversation that took place between some Muslims and the hypocrites before the war as follows: وَ قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعالَوْا قاتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَوِ ادْفَعُوا: Some of the Muslims (who, according to Ibn Abbas, was Abdullah ibn Amr ibn Haram), when he saw that Abdullah ibn Ubayy had withdrawn himself and his companions from the army of Islam and decided to return to Medina, said: "Come, either fight for the sake of Allah and in His way, or at least defend against the danger that threatens your homeland and your kinsmen."

But they resorted to a hollow excuse and said: لَوْ نَعْلَمُ قِتالًا لَاتَّبَعْناكُمْ‌: "If we knew there would be a fight, we would certainly have followed you." We think this affair will end without war and bloodshed.

According to another interpretation, the hypocrites said: "If we considered this to be a 'war,' we would have cooperated with you, but in our view, this is not war; rather, it is a kind of suicide. Because of the imbalance seen between the army of Islam and the disbelievers, fighting them is not rational, especially since the Muslim camp is situated in an unsuitable location."

In any case, these were nothing more than excuses. Both the occurrence of the war was certain, and the Muslims were initially victorious; if defeat overtook them, it was due to their own mistakes and wrongdoings. Allah says: They were lying: هُمْ لِلْكُفْرِ يَوْمَئِذٍ أَقْرَبُ مِنْهُمْ لِلْإِيمانِ‌: "They were on that day nearer to disbelief than to faith." Incidentally, from this phrase, it is understood that disbelief and faith have degrees that depend on a person's belief and manner of action.

يَقُولُونَ بِأَفْواهِهِمْ ما لَيْسَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ‌: "They say with their mouths what is not in their hearts." Their intention was completely contrary to what they were saying. They refrained from participating in the field due to stubbornness regarding their own proposal to fight inside Medina, or fear of the enemy's strikes, or a lack of interest in Islam. وَ اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يَكْتُمُونَ‌: "And Allah is most knowing of what they conceal." He has both pulled back the curtain from their faces in this world, showing their true countenances to the Muslims, and He will settle their account in the Hereafter.

Ayah

ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوا۟ لِإِخْوَٰنِهِمْ وَقَعَدُوا۟ لَوْ أَطَاعُونَا مَا قُتِلُوا۟ ۗ قُلْ فَٱدْرَءُوا۟ عَنْ أَنفُسِكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (١٦٨)
Those who said of their brethren, while they themselves sat back: ‘Had they obeyed us, they would not have been killed.’ Say, ‘Then keep death off from yourselves, if you are truthful.’ (168)

Commentary

The Groundless Claims of the Hypocrites

In addition to withdrawing from the Battle of Uhud and attempting to weaken the morale of others, the hypocrites, upon the return of the mujahideen, began to reproach them, saying: "If they had followed our command, they would not have suffered such casualties."

In the above verse, the Quran responds to their baseless claims and says: الَّذِينَ قالُوا لِإِخْوانِهِمْ وَ قَعَدُوا... "Those who remained behind and said of their brothers, 'If they had obeyed us, they would not have been killed.' Say to them: 'Then avert death from yourselves, if you are truthful.'"

This means that, in reality, by making such a claim, you consider yourselves knowledgeable of the Unseen and aware of future events. Anyone who is such should be able to predict and neutralize the causes and factors of their own death. Do you possess such power?! Furthermore, if you are not killed in the field of Jihad and in the path of honor and glory, will you have eternal life? Can you keep death away from yourselves forever? Therefore, since you cannot abolish the certain law of death, why die in bed with humiliation? Why not drink the nectar of martyrdom with honor in the field of Jihad against the enemy?

In the above verse, there is another point that must be noted:

And that is that the believers are referred to as "brothers," while the believers are never truly the brothers of the hypocrites. This is a form of reproach toward them, implying: "You considered the believers your brothers; why then did you withdraw your support from them at these critical moments?" Therefore, immediately after the expression "إِخْوانِهِمْ" (their brothers), the phrase "قَعَدُوا" (they remained behind) is mentioned. Does a person claim brotherhood and then immediately fail to support his brother?!

Ayahs

وَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أَمْوَٰتًۢا ۚ بَلْ أَحْيَآءٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ (١٦٩)
Do not suppose those who were slain in the way of Allah to be dead; no, they are living and provided for near their Lord, (169)
فَرِحِينَ بِمَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَيَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يَلْحَقُوا۟ بِهِم مِّنْ خَلْفِهِمْ أَلَّا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (١٧٠)
exulting in what Allah has given them out of His grace, and rejoicing for those who have not yet joined them from [those left] behind them, that they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (170)
۞ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِنِعْمَةٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَفَضْلٍ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٧١)
They rejoice in Allah’s blessing and grace, and that Allah does not waste the reward of the faithful. (171)

Commentary

The Ever-Living

Some commentators believe that the above verses were revealed concerning the martyrs of Uhud, while others consider them to be about the martyrs of Badr. However, the truth is that the connection between these verses and the preceding ones indicates they were revealed after the event of Uhud. Nevertheless, the theme and content of the verses are general and encompass all martyrs, including the fourteen martyrs of Badr. Therefore, it is narrated in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that he said: "The verses were revealed concerning both the martyrs of Uhud and Badr." [^1] Ibn Mas'ud narrates from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that Allah addressed the souls of the martyrs of Uhud and asked them: "What do you desire?" They replied: "O Lord! What higher desire could we have, while we are immersed in Your eternal blessings and dwell in the shade of Your Throne? Our only request is to return to the world once more and be martyred in Your way again." Allah said: "My irrevocable decree is that no one shall return to the world again." They said: "Since it is so, our request is that You convey our greetings to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and inform our survivors of our state, giving them glad tidings of our condition so they may not worry." At this moment, the above verses were revealed.

In any case, it appears that a group of weak-faithed individuals, after the event of Uhud, would sit and express regret over their friends and relatives who were martyred at Uhud, lamenting why they died and were lost. Especially when they encountered a blessing and saw their places empty, they would become more upset, saying to themselves: "Here we are in comfort and blessing, while our brothers and children are asleep in graves, their hands cut off from everything."

Such thoughts and words, besides being incorrect and not corresponding with reality, were not without effect in weakening the morale of the survivors.

The above verses draw a line of cancellation through such thoughts and mention the high and lofty station of the martyrs, saying: "And do not think of those who are killed in the way of Allah as dead." Here, the speech is addressed only to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله so that others may take account of themselves.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Ayyashi, as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 409.

It says: O Prophet! Never imagine that those who were killed in the way of Allah are dead; "rather, they are alive, receiving provision near their Lord." (بَلْ أَحْياءٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ)

The meaning of life here is that same "Barzakh life" (purgatory life) which souls possess in the world after death, not a physical and material life. Although the Barzakh life is not exclusive to martyrs—as many other people also possess a Barzakh life [^1]—since the life of martyrs is an exceptionally sublime life mixed with various spiritual blessings, and furthermore, they are the subject of the verse, only they are mentioned. They are so immersed in the gifts of spiritual life that it is as if the life of other inhabitants of Barzakh is nothing in comparison to theirs.

Then, referring to a corner of the many merits and blessings of the Barzakh life of martyrs, it says: "Rejoicing in what Allah has given them of His bounty." (فَرِحِينَ بِما آتاهُمُ اللَّهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ)

Another reason for their happiness is for their fellow mujahideen who have not yet drunk the draught of martyrdom in the battlefield and have not joined them; for they clearly see their stations and rewards in that world and thus become cheerful and glad, as the Quran says: "And they rejoice in those who have not yet joined them from behind them." (وَ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِالَّذِينَ لَمْ يَلْحَقُوا بِهِمْ مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ) [^2] Following that, it says: "that there shall be no fear upon them, nor shall they grieve." (أَلَّا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ لا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ) This means the martyrs sense that their mujahideen brothers, after death, will have no grief regarding what they left behind in the world, nor any fear of the Day of Resurrection and its terrifying events.

[^1]: According to some researchers: Barzakh life is for two groups: the righteous who are at a high level, and the wicked who are in an exceptionally bad state.

[^2]: Istibshar means becoming happy due to receiving glad tidings or witnessing a blessing for oneself or friends; it does not mean giving glad tidings.

This sentence may have another interpretation as well: that the martyrs, in addition to being happy by witnessing the stations of their mujahideen brothers who have not yet joined them, themselves have no fear of the future nor grief over the past. [^1] "They rejoice in a grace from Allah and bounty." (يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِنِعْمَةٍ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ فَضْلٍ) This verse is, in fact, an emphasis and further explanation regarding the glad tidings that martyrs receive after being killed. They become happy and joyful for two reasons:

First, because they receive Allah's blessings—not only His blessings but also His "bounty" (fadl), which is the increase and repetition of blessings, encompasses them.

Second, they see that Allah does not waste the reward of the believers—neither the reward of the martyrs nor the reward of the truthful mujahideen who did not drink the draught of martyrdom ("and that Allah does not waste the reward of the believers"). In reality, what they had heard before, they now see manifest there.

Evidence for the Survival of the Soul

Among the verses of the Quran that explicitly indicate the survival of the soul are the above verses concerning the life of martyrs after death.
