The suggestion by some that "life" here is meant in a metaphorical sense—referring to the remaining effects of their efforts and their name and reputation—is very far from the meaning of the verse and is inconsistent with any of the phrases in the above verses, including the martyrs receiving provision and their joy from various aspects. Furthermore, the above verses are clear evidence for the issue of Barzakh and Barzakh blessings, the explanation of which will come, Allah willing, under the noble verse: "And behind them is a barrier (Barzakh) until the Day they are resurrected." (Surah Al-Mu'minun, Verse 100).

[^1]: In other words, according to the first interpretation, the pronouns in "there shall be no fear upon them, nor shall they grieve" refer to those remaining in the world, and according to the second interpretation, to the martyrs themselves.

The Reward of the Martyrs

Much has been said about the importance of the station of martyrs, and every nation and people holds a special respect for its martyrs. However, without exaggeration, the respect that Islam has accorded to the martyrs in the way of Allah is unparalleled. The following narration is a clear example of the respect Islam holds for martyrs; it was under the light of these teachings that a limited, backward population gained such power and strength that they brought the greatest empires of the world to their knees.

Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام narrates from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, that while he was delivering a sermon and encouraging the people to Jihad, a young man stood up and said: "O Commander of the Faithful! Describe for me the virtue of the warriors in the way of Allah." The Imam replied: "I was riding the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله mount behind him as we were returning from the Battle of Dhat al-Salasil, and I asked the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله the same question you have asked me."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "When the warriors decide to participate in the field of Jihad, Allah decrees for them freedom from the fire of Hell.

And when they take up arms and prepare for the field, the angels take pride in their existence.

And when their wives, children, and relatives bid them farewell, they emerge from their sins... From this moment, they do nothing except that its reward is multiplied, and for every day, the reward of the worship of a thousand worshippers is recorded for them...

And when they face the enemies, the people of the world cannot comprehend the extent of their reward.

And when they step into the field for battle and spears and arrows are exchanged, and hand-to-hand combat begins, the angels surround them with their wings and pray to Allah that they remain steadfast in the field. At this moment, a caller cries out:

‘Paradise is under the shade of swords.’

At this time, the blows of the enemy upon the body of the martyr are easier and more pleasant than drinking cool water on a hot summer day.

And when the martyr falls from his mount, before he even reaches the ground, the Houris of Paradise hasten to welcome him and describe for him the great spiritual and material blessings Allah has prepared for him.

And when the martyr is placed upon the ground, the earth says: ‘Welcome to the pure soul that flies from a pure body; glad tidings be to you,

Indeed, for you is what no eye has seen, no ear has heard, and has never occurred to the heart of any human.’

And Allah says: ‘I am the guardian of his survivors; whoever pleases them has pleased Me, and whoever angers them has angered Me...’" [^1]

[^1]: What is mentioned above is a summary of a narration that the great Islamic commentator, the late Tabarsi, narrated in Majma' al-Bayan under the above verse.

Ayahs

ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَجَابُوا۟ لِلَّهِ وَٱلرَّسُولِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَآ أَصَابَهُمُ ٱلْقَرْحُ ۚ لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٧٢)
Those who responded to Allah and the Apostle [even] after they had been wounded—for those of them who have been virtuous and Godwary there shall be a great reward. (172)
ٱلَّذِينَ قَالَ لَهُمُ ٱلنَّاسُ إِنَّ ٱلنَّاسَ قَدْ جَمَعُوا۟ لَكُمْ فَٱخْشَوْهُمْ فَزَادَهُمْ إِيمَـٰنًا وَقَالُوا۟ حَسْبُنَا ٱللَّهُ وَنِعْمَ ٱلْوَكِيلُ (١٧٣)
—Those to whom the people said, ‘All the people have gathered against you, so fear them.’ That only increased them in faith, and they said, ‘Allah is sufficient for us, and He is an excellent trustee.’ (173)
فَٱنقَلَبُوا۟ بِنِعْمَةٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَفَضْلٍ لَّمْ يَمْسَسْهُمْ سُوٓءٌ وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ رِضْوَٰنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٧٤)
So they returned with Allah’s blessing and grace, untouched by any harm. They pursued the pleasure of Allah, and Allah is dispenser of a great grace. (174)

Commentary

The Battle of Hamra al-Asad

We mentioned that at the conclusion of the Battle of Uhud, the victorious army of Abu Sufyan quickly set out toward Mecca after their triumph. When they reached the land of "Rauha," they deeply regretted their actions and decided to return to Medina to annihilate the remaining Muslims. This news reached the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and He immediately ordered His army from Uhud to prepare for another battle. Specifically, He commanded that the wounded from the Battle of Uhud join the ranks of the army. One of the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله says:

"I was among the wounded, but my brother's wounds were even more severe and critical than mine. We decided to reach the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله by any means necessary. Since my condition was slightly better than my brother's, whenever he lagged behind, I would carry him on my back, and with great difficulty, we reached the army." In this manner, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the army of Islam reached a place called "Hamra al-Asad," which was eight miles from Medina, and set up camp.

News of this reached the Quraysh army, and they were struck with terror, especially by this extraordinary resistance and the participation of the wounded in the battlefield. Perhaps they thought a fresh army from Medina had also joined them.

At this moment, an event occurred that further weakened their morale and crushed their resistance. A man from the polytheists named "Ma'bad al-Khuza'i" was traveling from Medina toward Mecca. Witnessing the state of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and His companions deeply moved him. His human emotions were stirred, and he said to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Seeing your condition is very painful for us; it would have been better for us if you had rested." He said this and moved on, reaching Abu Sufyan's army in the land of "Rauha." Abu Sufyan asked him about the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. He replied: "I saw Muhammad with a massive army, the likes of which I have never seen before! They are in pursuit of you and are advancing rapidly!"

Anxious and agitated, Abu Sufyan said: "What are you saying? We killed them, wounded them, and scattered them!" Ma'bad al-Khuza'i replied: "I do not know what you did; I only know that a vast and massive army is currently in pursuit of you!"

Abu Sufyan and his companions made the final decision to retreat quickly and return to Mecca. To ensure the Muslims would not pursue them and to give themselves enough time for the retreat, they asked a group from the tribe of Abd al-Qays—who were passing by on their way to Medina to buy wheat—to deliver a message to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muslims: that Abu Sufyan and the idolaters of Quraysh were rapidly approaching Medina with a massive army to finish off the remaining companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

When this news reached the Prophet and the Muslims, they said: حَسْبُنَا اللَّهُ وَ نِعْمَ الْوَكِيلُ: "Allah is sufficient for us, and He is the best Disposer of affairs." However, no matter how long they waited, there was no sign of the enemy army. Therefore, after staying for three days, they returned to Medina. The above verses refer to this incident [1]: الَّذِينَ اسْتَجابُوا لِلَّهِ وَ الرَّسُولِ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما أَصابَهُمُ الْقَرْحُ لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا مِنْهُمْ وَ اتَّقَوْا أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ:

"Those who responded to the call of Allah and the Messenger after they had suffered injuries [at Uhud]—for those of them who did good and feared Allah, there is a great reward."

From the fact that the above verse restricts the "great reward" to a specific group, it becomes clear that even among them, there were individuals who lacked complete sincerity. It is also possible that the expression "minhum" (from among them) refers to the fact that some of the warriors of Uhud had refrained from participating in this field under some pretext.

Then, the Quran describes one of the living signs of their steadfastness and endurance as follows:

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir al-Manar, and other books.

الَّذِينَ قالَ لَهُمُ النَّاسُ إِنَّ النَّاسَ قَدْ جَمَعُوا لَكُمْ فَاخْشَوْهُمْ فَزادَهُمْ إِيماناً وَ قالُوا حَسْبُنَا اللَّهُ وَ نِعْمَ الْوَكِيلُ: "Those to whom the people (referring to the caravan of Abd al-Qays, and according to one narration, Nu'aym ibn Mas'ud, who brought this news) said: 'The enemy army has gathered against you, so fear them.' But it only increased them in faith, and they said: 'Allah is sufficient for us, and He is the best Guardian.'"

Following this manifest steadfastness, faith, and endurance, the Quran states the result of their action: فَانْقَلَبُوا بِنِعْمَةٍ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ فَضْلٍ: "So they returned with grace and bounty from Allah." What grace and bounty could be greater than this: that without entering into a dangerous confrontation with the enemy, the enemy fled from them, and they returned to Medina safe and without trouble? (The difference between "Ni'mah" [grace/blessing] and "Fadl" [bounty] may be that Ni'mah is a reward according to merit, while Fadl is an addition beyond merit).

Then, as an emphasis, He says: لَمْ يَمْسَسْهُمْ سُوءٌ: "No harm touched them" in this event, while وَ اتَّبَعُوا رِضْوانَ اللَّهِ: "they pursued the pleasure of Allah and followed His command," وَ اللَّهُ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَظِيمٍ: "and Allah is the Possessor of great bounty" which awaits true believers and sincere mujahideen.

The Rapid Impact of Divine Training

Comparing the morale of the Muslims in the Battle of Badr with their morale in the incident of Hamra al-Asad, as described, evokes human wonder. How could a defeated group, lacking high morale and sufficient numbers, and with so many wounded, undergo such a transformation in such a short period—perhaps not even a full twenty-four hours—and become ready to pursue the enemy with firm resolve and excellent morale? To the extent that the Quran says about them: when the news of the enemy's gathering for an attack reached them, not only did they not fear, but their faith and consequently their steadfastness increased. This is the characteristic of faith in a goal: the more a person sees difficulties and calamities approaching, the more his endurance and steadfastness grow, and in truth, all his spiritual and material forces are mobilized to face the danger. This extraordinary transformation in such a short interval acquaints one with the speed and depth of the educational impact of the Quranic verses and the captivating and effective words of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, which in itself reaches the level of a miracle.

Ayah

إِنَّمَا ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ يُخَوِّفُ أَوْلِيَآءَهُۥ فَلَا تَخَافُوهُمْ وَخَافُونِ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٧٥)
That is only Satan frightening [you] of his followers! So fear them not, and fear Me, should you be faithful. (175)

Commentary

This verse is a continuation of the verses revealed regarding the Battle of "Hamra al-Asad," and the word "ذلكم" (that) refers to those who were trying to frighten the Muslims with the power of the Quraysh army in order to weaken their morale. Therefore, the meaning of the verse is as follows: the action of Nu'aym ibn Mas'ud or the caravan of Abd al-Qays is merely a satanic act intended to frighten the friends of Satan. This means that such whisperings only affect those who are among the allies and friends of Satan; however, individuals with faith and steadfastness are never influenced by these whisperings. Consequently, since you are not followers of Satan, you must not be shaken by these whisperings.

The characterization of Nu'aym ibn Mas'ud or the caravan of Abd al-Qays as "Satan" is either because:

Their action was truly a satanic act performed under his inspiration; for in the Quran and traditions, every ugly and wrongful act is usually called a satanic act, as it is carried out through the whisperings of Satan.

Or, the term "Satan" refers to these individuals themselves. This is one of the instances where "Satan" is applied to a human manifestation, as the word "Satan" has a broad meaning that includes all deceivers, whether human or non-human. As we read in Surah Al-An'am, verse 112: وَ كَذلِكَ جَعَلْنا لِكُلِّ نَبِيٍّ عَدُوًّا شَياطِينَ الْإِنْسِ وَ الْجِنِ‌:

"And thus We have made for every prophet an enemy—devils from among mankind and jinn."

وَ خافُونِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ‌: At the end of the verse, He says: "And fear Me, if you are believers." This means that faith is incompatible with fearing anyone other than Allah.

Similarly, we read elsewhere: فَمَنْ يُؤْمِنْ بِرَبِّهِ فَلا يَخافُ بَخْساً وَ لا رَهَقاً:

"And whoever believes in his Lord will not fear any loss or burden" (Surah Al-Jinn, verse 14).

Therefore, if fear of other than Allah arises in a heart, it is a sign of the lack of perfection in faith and the influence of satanic whisperings. For we know that in the vast universe of existence, the only refuge is Allah, and He alone is the Essential Cause, while others have no power in the face of His power.

Fundamentally, if believers compare their Guardian, who is Allah, with the guardian of the polytheists and hypocrites, who is Satan, they will know that the latter have no power against Allah. For this reason, they should not have the slightest fear of them. The conclusion of this statement is that wherever faith penetrates, bravery and courage will also penetrate alongside it.

Ayahs

وَلَا يَحْزُنكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْكُفْرِ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ لَن يَضُرُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ شَيْـًٔا ۗ يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ أَلَّا يَجْعَلَ لَهُمْ حَظًّا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٧٦)
Do not grieve for those who are active in unfaith; they will not hurt Allah in the least: Allah desires to give them no share in the Hereafter, and there is a great punishment for them. (176)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱشْتَرَوُا۟ ٱلْكُفْرَ بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ لَن يَضُرُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ شَيْـًٔا وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١٧٧)
Those who have bought unfaith for faith will not hurt Allah in the least, and there is a painful punishment for them. (177)

Commentary

Consolation to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)

وَ لا يَحْزُنْكَ الَّذِينَ يُسارِعُونَ فِي الْكُفْرِ — The address in this verse is to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and following the painful event of Uhud, Allah consoles him: O Prophet! Do not be grieved by seeing a group of people surpassing one another in the path of disbelief, as if they are in a race with each other, because:

They can never cause any harm to Allah (إِنَّهُمْ لَنْ يَضُرُّوا اللَّهَ شَيْئاً); rather, they only harm themselves in this pursuit. Fundamentally, benefit and harm, or profit and loss, pertain to beings whose existence is not from themselves. But for the Eternal and Everlasting Allah, who is Self-Sufficient in every respect and whose existence is infinite, what effect can the disbelief or faith of people, and their efforts and struggles in this path, have upon Him? It is they who achieve perfection under the shelter of faith and suffer decline and fall because of disbelief.

Furthermore, their wrongdoings will not be forgotten, and they will face the consequences of their actions.

يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ أَلَّا يَجْعَلَ لَهُمْ حَظًّا فِي الْآخِرَةِ وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ: "Allah intends to leave them free in this path, so that they may traverse the way of disbelief so rapidly that they have not the slightest share in the Hereafter; rather, a great punishment awaits them."

In reality, the verse says: If they surpass one another in the path of disbelief, it is not because Allah is unable to stop them; rather, Allah has given them freedom of action to do whatever they can, and the result is their complete deprivation of the blessings of the next world. Therefore, the verse not only does not imply predestination (Jabr) but is one of the proofs of the freedom of will.

Then, in the following verse, the matter is presented more broadly, stating:

إِنَّ الَّذِينَ اشْتَرَوُا الْكُفْرَ بِالْإِيْمانِ لَنْ يَضُرُّوا اللَّهَ شَيْئاً: This means that not only those who move rapidly in the path of disbelief are like this, but all those who have taken the path of disbelief in any way—losing faith and purchasing disbelief in exchange for it—will never harm Allah, and the harm of it will fall upon themselves.

And at the end of the verse, He says: وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ: "And for them is a painful punishment." This difference in expression—where "a great punishment" (عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ) was mentioned in the previous verse and "a painful punishment" (عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ) is mentioned here—is because the former group was advancing in the path of disbelief with greater speed.

Ayah

وَلَا يَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَا نُمْلِى لَهُمْ خَيْرٌ لِّأَنفُسِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا نُمْلِى لَهُمْ لِيَزْدَادُوٓا۟ إِثْمًا ۚ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (١٧٨)
Let the faithless not suppose that the respite that We grant them is good for their souls: We give them respite only that they may increase in sin, and there is a humiliating punishment for them. (178)

Commentary

The Heavy-Laden

وَ لا يَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّما نُمْلِي‌ [^1] لَهُمْ‌ ...

Following the consolation and comfort offered to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) regarding the excessive efforts and struggles of the enemies of Truth in the preceding verses, Allah turns the discourse toward them in this verse, speaking of the grim fate that awaits them. (This verse, in fact, completes the discussions related to the event of Uhud and the incidents following it; for in one place the discourse was addressed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), in another to the believers, and here the discourse is addressed to the polytheists).

The above verse warns them that they must never take the resources Allah has placed at their disposal, the victories they occasionally achieve, and the freedom of action they possess as evidence of being righteous and upright individuals, or as a sign of Allah’s pleasure with them.

To explain: It is understood from the verses of the Holy Quran that Allah awakens sinful individuals—provided they are not too deeply mired in sin—through "wake-up calls" and the natural consequences of their actions, or sometimes through punishments proportionate to the deeds they have committed, leading them back to the path of Truth. These are people who still possess the capacity for guidance and are included in Allah’s grace; in reality, their punishments and hardships are considered a blessing for them. As we read in the Quran:

ظَهَرَ الْفَسادُ فِي الْبَرِّ وَ الْبَحْرِ بِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِي النَّاسِ لِيُذِيقَهُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي عَمِلُوا لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ‌: "Corruption has appeared throughout the land and sea by [reason of] what the hands of people have earned so He may let them taste part of [the consequence of] what they have done that perhaps they will return [to righteousness]." (Surah Ar-Rum, Verse 41).

However, those who drown in sin and transgression, reaching the final stage of rebellion and disobedience, Allah leaves them to themselves and, so to speak, gives them rein so that their backs become heavy with the burden of sin and they become deserving of the maximum punishment.

These are people who have destroyed all bridges behind them, leaving no way to return; they have torn the veil of modesty and shame and have completely lost the merit and capacity for Divine guidance.

The above verse emphasizes this meaning, stating: Let not those who disbelieve think that the respite We grant them is good for them; rather, We grant them respite only that they may increase in sin and rebellion, and for them is a humiliating punishment.

In the sermon delivered by the courageous lady of Islam, Zainab al-Kubra (عليه السلام), in Damascus before the tyrannical and autocratic government, we read her citing this verse against the rebellious Yazid—who was a clear manifestation of the irredeemable sinner—where she says:

"Do you rejoice today and think that because you have narrowed the expanse of the world for us and closed the horizons of heaven against us, leading us as captives from land to land, it is a sign of your power? Or that you have power and status before Allah while we have no path to Him?! You are mistaken; Allah has given you this opportunity and freedom only so that your back may become heavy with the burden of sin, and a painful punishment awaits you...

By Allah, if the course of life's events has brought me as a captive woman to the foot of your throne, do not imagine that you have the slightest character or value in my eyes. I consider you small, base, and worthy of every kind of contempt, blame, and reproach... Do whatever is in your power; by Allah, you shall never be able to extinguish our light, nor shall you erase our eternal revelation and the religion of Truth. You shall perish, and this brilliant star shall continue to shine."

Answer to a Question

The above verse simultaneously answers a question that exists in many minds: why is it that a group of oppressors and sinful, tainted individuals are so immersed in blessings and do not face punishment?

The Quran says: These are incorrigible individuals who, according to the law of creation and the principle of free will and choice, have been left to themselves to reach the final stage of their fall and deserve the maximum punishment.

Furthermore, it is understood from some verses of the Quran that Allah sometimes grants such individuals abundant blessings, and when they are drowned in the pleasure of victory and joy, He suddenly takes everything from them, so they may experience the maximum agony in this very worldly life; for being separated from such a luxurious life is extremely distressing. As we read:

فَلَمَّا نَسُوا ما ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ فَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ أَبْوابَ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ حَتَّى إِذا فَرِحُوا بِما أُوتُوا أَخَذْناهُمْ بَغْتَةً فَإِذا هُمْ مُبْلِسُونَ‌: "So when they forgot that by which they had been reminded, We opened to them the gates of every [good] thing until, when they rejoiced in that which they were given, We seized them suddenly, and they were [then] in despair." (Surah Al-An'am, Verse 44).

In reality, such persons are like someone who oppressively climbs a tree; the higher he goes, the happier he becomes, until he reaches the top of the tree, when suddenly a storm blows and he falls from that height in such a way that all his bones are shattered.

A Literary Point

From what we have said in the commentary of the verse, it becomes clear that the "Lam" (letter L) in "لِيَزْدادُوا إِثْماً" (that they may increase in sin) is the "Lam of Consequence" (Lam al-Aqibah), not the "Lam of Purpose" (Lam al-Ghayah). To explain: sometimes the "Lam" in the Arabic language is used for something that is desired and sought by a person, such as: ...لِتُخْرِجَ النَّاسَ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ: "[This is] a Book which We have revealed to you, [O Muhammad], that you might bring mankind out of darknesses into the light" (Surah Ibrahim, Verse 1).

It is obvious that the guidance of people is desired and beloved by Allah.

But sometimes the word "Lam" is used in a context where the outcome is not the person's goal or desire, but is the result of their action, such as: لِيَكُونَ لَهُمْ عَدُوًّا وَ حَزَناً: "And the family of Pharaoh picked him up [out of the river] so that he might become to them an enemy and a [cause of] sorrow" (Surah Al-Qasas, Verse 8).

Certainly, they did not pick him up from the water for this purpose, but this matter was the result of their action.

These two different expressions are seen not only in Arabic literature but also in the literature of other languages. From here, the answer to another question becomes clear: why did Allah say: "لِيَزْدادُوا إِثْماً" (We want their sins to increase)? This objection only holds if the "Lam" were the "Lam of Cause and Purpose," not the "Lam of Consequence and Result." Therefore, the meaning of the verse becomes: We give them respite, the end and consequence of which is that their backs become heavy with the burden of sin. Thus, the above verse is not only not an argument for predestination (Jabr), but rather an argument for the freedom of will.

[^1]: Numli is from the root imla', meaning to provide aid, which in many instances has come to mean granting respite, as that itself is considered a type of aid. In the above verse, it carries this same meaning; i.e., We grant them respite.

Ayah

مَّا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيَذَرَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَنتُمْ عَلَيْهِ حَتَّىٰ يَمِيزَ ٱلْخَبِيثَ مِنَ ٱلطَّيِّبِ ۗ وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُطْلِعَكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْغَيْبِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَجْتَبِى مِن رُّسُلِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۚ وَإِن تُؤْمِنُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ فَلَكُمْ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٧٩)
Allah will not leave the faithful in your present state, until He has separated the bad ones from the good. Allah will not acquaint you with the Unseen, but Allah chooses whomever He wishes from His apostles. So have faith in Allah and His apostles; and if you are faithful and Godwary, there shall be a great reward for you. (179)

Commentary

The Muslims are Purified

Prior to the event of Uhud, the issue of the "hypocrites" was not widely discussed among the Muslims; for this reason, they mostly considered the "disbelievers" as their enemies. However, after the defeat at Uhud, the temporary weakening of the true Muslims, and the preparation of the ground for the activities of the hypocrites, they realized they had more dangerous enemies whom they must watch closely—and those were the hypocrites. This was one of the most important outcomes of the event of Uhud.

The above verse, which is the final verse discussed here regarding the event of Uhud, expresses this reality as a general law, stating: مَا كَانَ اللَّهُ لِيَذَرَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ عَلَىٰ مَا أَنْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ حَتَّىٰ يَمِيزَ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ: "It is not for Allah to leave the believers in the state you are in until He separates the 'khabith' (evil/impure) from the 'tayyib' (good/pure)." This is a universal and general decree and is considered an eternal Sunnah (tradition) of the Lord: that anyone who claims faith and carves out a place for themselves among the ranks of the Muslims will not be left alone; rather, through successive trials from Allah, their inner secrets will eventually be revealed.

At this point, a question might arise (and according to some narrations, such a question was indeed raised among the Muslims): since Allah is aware of everyone's inner secrets, what prevents Him from informing the people of their status, so that through the knowledge of the Unseen (Ghayb), the believer may be distinguished from the hypocrite?

The second part of the verse, وَ مَا كَانَ اللَّهُ لِيُطْلِعَكُمْ عَلَى الْغَيْبِ, answers this question: "Allah will never grant you access to the hidden secrets and the knowledge of the Unseen." This is because awareness of hidden secrets—contrary to what many imagine—does not solve problems for the people; rather, in many cases, it leads to chaos, the disintegration of social bonds, the extinguishing of the flames of hope, and the destruction of effort and endeavor among the masses.

Most importantly, the value of individuals must be clarified through their actions, not through any other means; and the matter of the Lord's trial and examination is nothing other than this. Therefore, the only way to identify individuals is through their deeds. [1] Then, He excludes the Messengers of Allah from this decree and says: وَلَٰكِنَّ اللَّهَ يَجْتَبِي مِنْ رُسُلِهِ مَنْ يَشَاءُ: "But Allah chooses from His Messengers whom He wills." Whenever He wills, He selects from among His Prophets and places at their disposal a portion of His infinite "knowledge of the Unseen" and the inner secrets of the people—knowledge that is necessary for the perfection of their leadership. However, in any case, the general, universal, and eternal law for identifying individuals remains their actions.

From this phrase, it is understood that the Prophets do not possess knowledge of the Unseen inherently; it is also understood that they know a portion of the secrets of the Unseen through Divine instruction. Therefore, there are individuals who become aware of the Unseen, and the extent of their awareness depends on the Will of Allah.

It goes without saying that the meaning of "Will" and "Desire" of Allah in this verse, as in other verses, refers to "Will joined with Wisdom." That is, Allah grants awareness of the secrets of the Unseen to whomever He deems worthy and as His Wisdom necessitates.

[^1]: Under the verse "وَلَنَبْلُوَنَّكُمْ بِشَيْءٍ مِنَ الْخَوْفِ وَالْجُوعِ..." (Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 155) regarding the trials of Allah, we have discussed this question in detail and answered that Divine trials are, in reality, a form of nurturing and training, not a means of inquiry or gathering information. For further explanation, refer to that discussion.

فَآمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَإِنْ تُؤْمِنُوا وَتَتَّقُوا فَلَكُمْ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ: At the end of the verse, He points out that since the field of life is a field of trial and the separation of the pure from the impure and the believer from the hypocrite, then in order for you to emerge successfully from this crucible of trial, "believe in Allah and His Messengers." However, He does not stop at merely believing, but says: "If you believe and practice Taqwa (piety/God-consciousness), a great reward awaits you."

A noteworthy point in the verse is that the believer is referred to as "tayyib" (pure/good). We know that "pure" refers to something that remains in its original state of creation, and external or alien things have not made it "khabith" (impure/evil). Pure water, a pure garment, and the like, are things that external contaminating factors have not reached. From this, it is inferred that having faith is the primordial nature (Fitrah) and the original creation of man.

Ayah

وَلَا يَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ بِمَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ هُوَ خَيْرًا لَّهُم ۖ بَلْ هُوَ شَرٌّ لَّهُمْ ۖ سَيُطَوَّقُونَ مَا بَخِلُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ وَلِلَّهِ مِيرَٰثُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (١٨٠)
Let the stingy not suppose that [their grudging] what Allah has given them out of His bounty is good for them; no, it is bad for them. They will be collared with what they grudge on the Day of Resurrection. To Allah belongs the heritage of the heavens and the earth, and Allah is well aware of what you do. (180)

Commentary

The Heavy Collar of Captivity

وَ لا يَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ بِما آتاهُمُ اللَّهُ‌ ...

The above verse explains the fate of the miserly on the Day of Resurrection—those who strive in accumulating and preserving wealth and refrain from spending it in the way of Allah's servants.

Although the verse does not explicitly mention Zakat or obligatory financial dues, in the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt علیهم السلام and the statements of the commentators, the verse has been specifically applied to those who withhold Zakat. The emphasis and severity found in the verse also serve as evidence that it does not refer to recommended (mustahabb) charity.

The verse states: "Those who are stingy and do not give from what Allah has granted them out of His bounty in His way, let them not think that it is good for them; rather, it will end up being to their detriment."

Then, it describes their fate on the Day of Resurrection as follows: سَيُطَوَّقُونَ ما بَخِلُوا بِهِ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ: "Soon, the wealth they were stingy with will be hung around their necks like a collar."

From this phrase, it is understood that wealth for which the obligatory dues have not been paid, and from which society has not benefited—being used only for personal whims, sometimes for insane expenditures, or accumulated without reason while no one benefits from it—will, like other evil human deeds, be manifested on the Day of Resurrection according to the law of the "Embodiment of Deeds" (Tajassum al-A'mal). It will take the form of a painful means of punishment.

The embodiment of such wealth as a collar placed around the neck points to the reality that man will bear the full weight of the responsibility for it without benefiting from its effects. Vast amounts of wealth that are gathered and kept in an obsessive manner and are not at the service of society are nothing but chains and a prison for the owner. For we know that personal enjoyment of wealth and riches has certain limits; beyond that, it results in nothing but a type of captivity and useless burden, unless one enjoys its spiritual blessings and places it in the path of positive works.

Such wealth will not only be a heavy collar around the necks of its owners in the Hereafter, but it is also so in this world; however, it is manifest in the Resurrection and more hidden here. What madness and folly is greater than for a person to shoulder the numerous responsibilities of acquiring wealth, plus the immense responsibilities and hardships required for its preservation, accounting, maintenance, and defense, while benefiting from none of it? Is the collar of captivity anything other than this?!

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام: "Whoever does not pay the Zakat of his wealth, Allah will transform that wealth into collars of fire. Then it will be said to him: 'Just as you would not part with this wealth in the world at any price, now take it and place it around your neck!'"

It is noteworthy that in the verse, wealth is referred to as "بِما آتاهُمُ اللَّهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ" (that which Allah has given them of His bounty), the meaning of which is that the true Owner of wealth and sources of income is Allah, and whatever has been given to anyone is from His grace and generosity. Therefore, there is no place for anyone to be stingy in spending in the way of the true Owner!

Some commentators believe that the concept of this phrase is general and includes all divine favors, even knowledge and science, but this possibility does not align with the apparent expressions of the verse.

Then the verse points to another key point and says: وَ لِلَّهِ مِيراثُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ: meaning that this wealth, whether it is spent in the way of Allah and His servants or not, will eventually be separated from its owners, and Allah will be the Inheritor of all the heritage of the heavens and the earth. Since this is the case, how much better it is that before being separated from it, they benefit from its spiritual blessings, rather than only its regret and responsibility.

And at the end of the verse, He says: Allah is aware of your deeds; if you are stingy, He knows, and if you use it to help human society, He knows that as well, and He will give everyone an appropriate reward (وَ اللَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ).

Ayahs

لَّقَدْ سَمِعَ ٱللَّهُ قَوْلَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ فَقِيرٌ وَنَحْنُ أَغْنِيَآءُ ۘ سَنَكْتُبُ مَا قَالُوا۟ وَقَتْلَهُمُ ٱلْأَنۢبِيَآءَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ وَنَقُولُ ذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلْحَرِيقِ (١٨١)
Allah has certainly heard the remark of those who said, ‘Allah is poor and we are rich.’ We will record what they have said, and their killing of the prophets unjustly, and We shall say, ‘Taste the punishment of the burning. (181)
ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ (١٨٢)
That is because of what your hands have sent ahead, and because Allah is not tyrannical to His servants.’ (182)

Circumstances of Revelation

The verse was revealed regarding the rebuke and censure of the Jews. Ibn Abbas says:

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله wrote a letter to the Jews of "Bani Qaynuqa," inviting them to establish prayer, pay the Zakat, and give a loan to Allah (this phrase refers to spending in the way of Allah, expressed in this manner to stir the maximum human emotion).

The Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله messenger entered a house that was the center of religious instruction for the Jews, known as "Bayt al-Midras," and handed the letter to "Phineas," a great Jewish scholar. After studying the letter, he said in a mocking tone: "If your words are true, then it must be said that Allah is poor and we are rich and self-sufficient! For if He were not poor, He would not ask us for a loan!" (referring to the verse: “Who is it that will lend Allah a goodly loan...”) [^1]. Furthermore, he added: "Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله believes that Allah has forbidden you from usury, yet He Himself promises you usury and increase in return for your spending!" (referring to the verse: “...and He gives increase for charities”) [^2]. Later, Phineas denied having said such things, at which point the above verses were revealed. [^3]

Commentary

The first verse says: "Allah has certainly heard the statement of those who said, 'Indeed, Allah is poor, and we are rich.'" If they deny it before the people, it cannot be denied before Allah, who hears all speech. He even hears extremely weak or very powerful sound waves that human ears are incapable of perceiving. (لَقَدْ سَمِعَ اللَّهُ قَوْلَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ فَقِيرٌ وَ نَحْنُ أَغْنِياءُ).

Therefore, their denial is futile. Then He says: “We will record what they said” (سَنَكْتُبُ ما قالُوا).

[^1]: Surah Al-Hadid, Verse 11. [^2]: Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 276. [^3]: Asbab al-Nuzul by Al-Waqidi, page 99, and Tafsir Ruh al-Bayan under this same verse.

It is obvious that "writing" here does not mean writing as we do on a page of paper; rather, it refers to the preservation of the effects of the action, which, according to the law of the conservation of "energy-matter," will always remain in the universe. Even the writing of Allah's angels is a form of preserving the action that is superior to any conventional writing.

Then He says: We will not only record this blasphemous statement of theirs, but We also record “their killing of the prophets without right” (وَ قَتْلَهُمُ الْأَنْبِياءَ).

This means that the struggle of the Jewish community and their alignment against the prophets is not new. This is not the first time the Jews have mocked a prophet; they have many such crimes throughout their history. For a people who reached such a level of audacity and insolence as to kill a group of Allah's prophets, what surprise is there that they utter such blasphemous words?! It might be said that the killing of the prophets was not related to the Jews of the Prophet of Islam's صلى الله عليه وآله era, but as we have stated previously, this attribution is because they were pleased with the actions of their ancestors, and for this reason, they share in the responsibility.

However, the recording and preservation of their deeds is not without purpose; it is so that on the Day of Resurrection, We may place it before them and say: "Now taste the punishment of the Burning Fire" (وَ نَقُولُ ذُوقُوا عَذابَ الْحَرِيقِ).

This painful punishment, the bitterness of which you are now tasting, is the result of your own actions. It was you who wronged yourselves; Allah will never wrong anyone. (ذلِكَ بِما قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ).

In principle, if criminals like you did not see the punishment for your deeds and were placed in the same rank as the righteous, that would be the ultimate injustice. And if Allah did not do this, He would be "Zallam" (a great oppressor).

In Nahj al-Balagha, it is narrated from Ali عليه السلام that he said:

"By Allah, no people were ever stripped of the comforts of life except because of the sins they committed," then the Imam cited this very phrase, saying: "Because Allah is not an oppressor to His servants (لأنَّ اللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ), and He does not strip blessings from worthy individuals!" This verse is among those that, on one hand, negate the doctrine of the determinists (Jabriyyun) and, on the other hand, generalize the principle of justice regarding Allah's actions.

To explain: The above verse explicitly states that every form of punishment or reward from Allah is due to the actions that people have performed by their own will.

(ذلِكَ بِما قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ): "That is for what your hands have put forth." [^1] On the other hand, the verse explicitly says: "Allah never commits injustice, and His law of reward revolves around the axis of absolute justice." This is exactly what the Adliyyah (those who believe in Justice, namely the Shi'a and a group of Sunnis called the "Mu'tazilites") believe.

However, in contrast to them, a group of Sunnis called the "Ash'arites" hold a surprising belief. They say: "In principle, injustice cannot be conceived regarding God, and whatever He does is the essence of justice. Even if He were to send all the righteous to Hell and all the

[^1]: Attributing human actions to the "hand" is because most tasks are performed with the hand; otherwise, this ruling is not exclusive to the actions of the hand. Thus, in the Persian language as well, we say "such a task was done by the hand of so-and-so," while his hand might not have had any involvement at all.

oppressors to Paradise, He has committed no injustice, and no one can question Him!"

The above verse completely rejects such beliefs and says: If Allah were to punish individuals without them having committed a wrong act, He would be "Zalim" (unjust), or rather "Zallam" (grossly unjust).

The word Zallam is an intensive form meaning "one who oppresses greatly." The choice of this word here—despite Allah not permitting even the slightest injustice—is perhaps because if He were to compel people toward disbelief and sin, create the motives for evil deeds within them, and then punish them for the crimes they were forced to commit, He would not have committed a small injustice, but would indeed be "Zallam"!

Ayahs

ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَهِدَ إِلَيْنَآ أَلَّا نُؤْمِنَ لِرَسُولٍ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَنَا بِقُرْبَانٍ تَأْكُلُهُ ٱلنَّارُ ۗ قُلْ قَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِّن قَبْلِى بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَبِٱلَّذِى قُلْتُمْ فَلِمَ قَتَلْتُمُوهُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (١٨٣)
Tell those who say, ‘Allah has pledged us not to believe in any apostle unless he brings us an offering consumed by fire,’ ‘Apostles before me certainly did bring you manifest signs and what you speak of. Then why did you kill them, if you are truthful?’ (183)
فَإِن كَذَّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كُذِّبَ رُسُلٌ مِّن قَبْلِكَ جَآءُو بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَٱلزُّبُرِ وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُنِيرِ (١٨٤)
But if they deny you, [other] apostles have been denied before you, who came with manifest signs, holy writs, and an illuminating scripture. (184)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of Jewish leaders came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "You claim that Allah has sent you to us and has revealed a Book to you, while Allah has taken a covenant from us in the Torah not to believe in anyone who claims prophethood unless he offers a sacrifice for us and a fire (a lightning bolt) comes from the sky and consumes it. If you do this, we will believe in you." The above verses were revealed and provided them with an answer.

Commentary

The Pretexts of the Jews

الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَهِدَ إِلَيْنا ...

The Jews, in order to avoid accepting Islam, brought forward strange excuses, including the one mentioned in the above verse. They said: "Allah has taken a covenant from us not to accept the call of any prophet until he brings us a sacrifice which the fire consumes."

Commentators have stated: The Jews claimed that Divine prophets, to prove their truthfulness, must necessarily possess this specific miracle: they must sacrifice an animal, and it must be burned before the people by a lightning bolt from the sky.

If the Jews truly sought this as a miracle, rather than out of stubbornness and pretext-seeking, it would have been one thing. However, their past history, as well as their various interactions with the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, clearly proved this reality: that their intention was never to investigate the truth. Rather, every day they would propose a new demand to escape accepting Islam in the face of environmental pressure and the clear arguments of the Quran; and even if it were performed, they still would not have believed. This is because they had read all the signs of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله in their own books, yet they still refused to accept the truth.

The Quran says in response to them: قُلْ قَدْ جاءَكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِنْ قَبْلِي بِالْبَيِّناتِ وَ بِالَّذِي قُلْتُمْ فَلِمَ قَتَلْتُمُوهُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ‌: "Say to them in response to these pretexts: A group of the Prophets of the Children of Israel came before me and brought clear signs, and even brought such a sacrifice for you. If you are truthful, why did you not believe in them and why did you kill them?" (This refers to Zechariah, John, and a group of other Prophets of the Children of Israel who were murdered by their own hands).

Some recent commentators (such as the author of Tafsir al-Manar) have suggested another possibility regarding the issue of the sacrifice, which can be summarized as follows: Their intention was not that an animal be slaughtered and a fire miraculously descend from the sky to burn it. Rather, the intention was that in their religious instructions, there was a type of sacrifice called the "burnt offering"; they would slaughter an animal and burn it with specific ceremonies, the details of which are mentioned in the first chapter of the Book of Leviticus in the Torah.

They claimed that God had made a covenant with them that this instruction of the "burnt offering" would exist in every heavenly religion, and since it is not in the religion of Islam, we will not believe in you! However, this possibility is very unlikely in the interpretation of the verse. Firstly, because this phrase in the above verse is conjoined to "clear signs" (al-bayyinat), which testifies that the intention is a miraculous act, which does not align with this interpretation. Secondly, killing an animal and then burning it is a superstitious act and cannot be part of the heavenly instructions of the Prophets.

فَإِنْ كَذَّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كُذِّبَ رُسُلٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ‌: In this verse, Allah comforts His Prophet: if this group does not accept your words, do not be distressed, for this matter has a long history; Prophets came before you and they were belied.

جاؤُ بِالْبَيِّناتِ وَ الزُّبُرِ وَ الْكِتابِ الْمُنِيرِ: While those Prophets also brought with them clear signs and manifest miracles (al-bayyinat), firm and sublime books (al-zubur), and illuminating books (al-kitab al-munir).

It should be noted that "zubur" is the plural of "zabur," meaning a book written with firmness, because this root originally means writing—but not just any writing, rather writing that is accompanied by firmness.

As for the difference between "al-zubur" and "al-kitab al-munir," despite both being types of books, it may be because the former refers to the books of the Prophets before Moses عليه السلام, and the latter refers to the Torah and the Gospel, as the Quran in Surah Al-Ma'idah, verses 44 and 46, refers to them as "light": إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَا التَّوْراةَ فِيها هُدىً وَ نُورٌ ... وَ آتَيْناهُ الْإِنْجِيلَ فِيهِ هُدىً وَ نُورٌ.

Some commentators have suggested the possibility that Zabur is only applied to that part of the heavenly books containing exhortations and advice (just as the Psalms attributed to David, which are currently available, are entirely exhortations), while "Heavenly Book" or "Illuminating Book" is applied to that part which contains rulings, laws, and individual and social instructions.

Ayah

كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذَآئِقَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۗ وَإِنَّمَا تُوَفَّوْنَ أُجُورَكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۖ فَمَن زُحْزِحَ عَنِ ٱلنَّارِ وَأُدْخِلَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ فَقَدْ فَازَ ۗ وَمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَآ إِلَّا مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْغُرُورِ (١٨٥)
Every soul shall taste death, and you will indeed be paid your full rewards on the Day of Resurrection. Whoever is delivered from the Fire and admitted to paradise has certainly succeeded. The life of this world is nothing but the wares of delusion. (185)

Commentary

The Universal Law of Death

Following the discussion regarding the obstinacy of the opponents and the faithless, this verse refers to the universal law of death and the destiny of people in the Resurrection, serving both as a consolation for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the believers, and as a warning to the sinful opponents!

This verse first points to a law that governs all living beings in the world and states that all living things will, sooner or later, taste death (كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ).

Although many people prefer to forget their own perishability, this is a reality that even if we forget it, it will never forget us. The life of this world eventually has an end, and a day will come when death seeks out everyone, and they will be forced to depart from this world.

The meaning of "nafs" (soul/self) in the verse refers to the combination of body and soul, although sometimes in the Quran, nafs is applied solely to the spirit. The expression "tasting" refers to a complete perception; for sometimes a person sees food with their eyes or touches it with their hand, but none of these constitute a complete perception until they taste it with their sense of taste. It is as if, in the system of creation, death is ultimately a kind of "food" for man and living beings.

وَ إِنَّما تُوَفَّوْنَ أُجُورَكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ ...

Then it says: After the life of this world, the stage of reward and punishment for deeds begins; here is the place of action without recompense, and there is the place of recompense without action.

The phrase "tuwaffawn," which means "to be paid in full," indicates that the wages and rewards of man are paid completely on the Day of Resurrection. Therefore, there is no contradiction in a person seeing a portion of the results of their deeds and their reward or punishment in the world of Barzakh (the world that is an intermediary between this world and the Hereafter), because this reward and punishment in Barzakh is not complete.

فَمَنْ زُحْزِحَ عَنِ النَّارِ وَ أُدْخِلَ الْجَنَّةَ فَقَدْ فازَ

The word "zuhziha" originally means for a person to gradually remove and distance themselves from the influence of the gravity and pull of something, and "faza" originally means to be saved from destruction and to reach the beloved.

The above sentence says: Those who distance themselves from the influence of the gravity of the fire of Hell and enter Paradise have been saved and have found their beloved and desired goal.

It is as if Hell, with all its power, attracts humans toward itself, and truly, the factors that pull man toward it have a strange attraction. Do not fleeting whims, illicit sexual pleasures, and unlawful positions and wealth possess an attraction for every human being?! Furthermore, it is inferred from this expression that if people do not strive and do not keep themselves away from the gravity of these deceptive factors, they will gradually be pulled toward it.

However, those who, through discipline, practice, and education, gradually control themselves and reach the station of the "Serene Soul" (al-nafs al-mutma'innah), are considered the true survivors and feel security and peace.

وَ مَا الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا مَتاعُ الْغُرُورِ

This sentence completes the previous discussion and says: The life of this world is nothing but a deceptive enjoyment. This life and its distracting factors have a special allure from afar, but when a person attains them and touches them from up close, they usually appear hollow; and the meaning of "mata' al-ghurur" (deceptive enjoyment) is nothing other than this.

Additionally, material pleasures seem pure from a distance, but when a person approaches them, they see they are tainted with various types of distress, and this is another one of the deceptions of the material world.

Likewise, man often does not pay attention to their perishability, but soon realizes how quickly they vanish and fade away.

Of course, these expressions appear repeatedly in the Quran and narrations, and the goal of all of them is one thing: that man should not make the material world and its pleasures his ultimate goal, the result of which is drowning in various crimes and distancing oneself from truth and human perfection. However, using the material world and its blessings as a means to achieve human perfection is not only not condemned but is necessary and essential.

Ayah

۞ لَتُبْلَوُنَّ فِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِكُمْ وَأَنفُسِكُمْ وَلَتَسْمَعُنَّ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوٓا۟ أَذًى كَثِيرًا ۚ وَإِن تَصْبِرُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ ٱلْأُمُورِ (١٨٦)
You will surely be tested in your possessions and your souls, and you will surely hear much affront from those who were given the Book before you and from the polytheists; but if you are patient and Godwary, that is indeed the steadiest of courses. (186)

Occasion of Revelation

When the Muslims migrated from Mecca to Medina and were separated from their homes and livelihoods, the polytheists extended their hands in aggression toward their property, seizing it for themselves. Furthermore, they did not hesitate to inflict verbal and physical abuse upon anyone they could reach.

Upon arriving in Medina, the Muslims were subjected to the vilification and harassment of the Jews of Medina. Specifically, one of them named Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf—a foul-mouthed and spiteful poet—constantly lampooned the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muslims through his poetry and incited the polytheists against them. He even went so far as to make Muslim women and girls the subjects of his erotic verses and flirtations. In short, his insolence reached such a point that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was compelled to issue the order for his execution, and he was killed at the hands of the Muslims.

According to narrations cited by commentators, the above verse refers to these events and encourages the Muslims to continue their resistance.

Commentary

Do Not Grow Weary of Resistance

لَتُبْلَوُنَّ فِي أَمْوالِكُمْ وَ أَنْفُسِكُمْ "You will surely be tested in your possessions and in yourselves." Fundamentally, this world is an arena of trial, and one must inevitably prepare oneself to face difficult and unpleasant events and occurrences. This is, in fact, a warning and a call to readiness for all Muslims, so they do not imagine that the hardships of their lives have ended, or that, for instance, with the death of Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf—the aggressive and seditious foul-mouthed poet—they will no longer face distress or "wounds of the tongue" from the enemy.

For this reason, He says: وَ لَتَسْمَعُنَّ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ وَ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا أَذىً كَثِيراً "And you will surely hear from those who were given the Scripture before you and from those who associate others with Allah much abuse."

The matter of hearing abusive words from the enemy, although part of the trials mentioned at the beginning of the verse, is explicitly stated here due to the extraordinary impact this issue has on the souls of sensitive and honorable people. As the famous saying goes: "The wounds of the sword may heal, but the wounds of the tongue do not!"

وَ إِنْ تَصْبِرُوا وَ تَتَّقُوا فَإِنَّ ذلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ Here, He describes the duty Muslims have in the face of such difficult and painful events, stating: "If you persevere, remain patient, and practice piety and God-wariness, this is among the matters whose [positive] outcome is clear, and therefore every rational person should resolve to undertake it."

"Azm" (عزم) linguistically means "firm resolve," and it is sometimes applied to anything that is solid and firm. Therefore, "مِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ" means worthy actions upon which a person must resolve, or it refers to any firm and reliable undertaking.

The pairing of "patience" (صبر) and "piety" (تقوى) in the verse likely suggests that some individuals, while showing endurance and patience, may open their mouths in ingratitude and complaint. However, true believers always blend patience and endurance with piety and remain distant from such behaviors.

Ayah

وَإِذْ أَخَذَ ٱللَّهُ مِيثَـٰقَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ لَتُبَيِّنُنَّهُۥ لِلنَّاسِ وَلَا تَكْتُمُونَهُۥ فَنَبَذُوهُ وَرَآءَ ظُهُورِهِمْ وَٱشْتَرَوْا۟ بِهِۦ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا ۖ فَبِئْسَ مَا يَشْتَرُونَ (١٨٧)
When Allah made a covenant with those who were given the Book: ‘You shall explain it for the people, and you shall not conceal it,’ they cast it behind their backs and sold it for a paltry gain. How evil is what they buy! (187)

Commentary

وَ إِذْ أَخَذَ اللَّهُ مِيثاقَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ لَتُبَيِّنُنَّهُ لِلنَّاسِ وَ لا تَكْتُمُونَهُ‌ Following the mention of some of the transgressions of the People of the Book, this verse points to another of their ugly deeds, which was the concealment of truths. It says: And remember when Allah took a covenant from those who were given the Scripture to make the verses of the Book clear to the people and never to conceal them.

It is noteworthy that the phrase "لتبيننه" (you shall certainly make it clear), despite being accompanied by the Lam of oath and the Nun of heavy emphasis—conveying the utmost level of stress—is further reinforced by the phrase "و لا تكتمونه" (and do not conceal it), which also commands the avoidance of concealment. From the sum of these expressions, it is understood that Allah, through the previous prophets, had taken the most emphatic covenant from them to explain the truths. Yet, despite all this, they betrayed this firm divine covenant and concealed the truths of the Heavenly Books. Therefore, He says:

فَنَبَذُوهُ وَراءَ ظُهُورِهِمْ‌ "But they threw it behind their backs." This phrase is a beautiful metaphor for failing to act upon something and consigning it to oblivion. This is because when a person wants to make a program the criterion for their actions, they place it before them and look at it continuously. However, whenever they do not wish to act upon it and want to forget it entirely, they remove it from their sight and cast it behind them.

وَ اشْتَرَوْا بِهِ ثَمَناً قَلِيلًا فَبِئْسَ ما يَشْتَرُونَ‌ This phrase points to their intense worldliness and intellectual degradation, saying: "And they exchanged it for a small price. And wretched is that which they purchased." If they had received a vast fortune in exchange for this great crime (concealing the truths), one might say the magnitude of that wealth had blinded and deafened them. But the wonder lies in the fact that they sold all of this for a meager gain (of course, this refers to the actions of their low-minded scholars).

The Great Duty of Scholars

Although the above verse was revealed regarding the scholars of the People of the Book (Jews and Christians), it is, in reality, a warning to all scholars and religious authorities that they are duty-bound to strive in explaining and clarifying Divine commands and religious knowledge. Allah has taken an emphatic covenant from all of them in this regard.

Attention to the root word "تبيين" (clarification/making clear) used in the verse shows that the intent is not merely the recitation of Allah's verses or the publication of Heavenly Books. Rather, the intent is to place their truths nakedly and clearly at the disposal of the people so that the masses may become aware of them with clarity and so that these truths may reach their souls and spirits. Those who fall short in clarifying, explaining, interpreting, and enlightening the Muslims are subject to the same fate that Allah has described for the Jewish scholars in this verse and similar ones.

It is narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: من كتم علما عن اهله الجم يوم القيامة بلجام من نار "Whoever conceals knowledge from those who are worthy of it (and in need of it), Allah will bridle him on the Day of Resurrection with a bridle of fire."

Hasan ibn Ammar says: One day I went to "Zuhri" after he had stopped narrating Hadith to the people, and I said to him: "Relate to me the traditions you have heard." He said to me: "Do you not know that I no longer narrate Hadith for anyone?" I said: "In any case, either you narrate a Hadith to me, or I shall narrate a Hadith to you." He said: "You narrate." I said: It is narrated from Ali عليه السلام that he used to say: ما اخذ اللَّه على اهل الجهل ان يتعلموا حتى اخذ على اهل العلم ان يعلموا "Allah did not take a covenant from the ignorant to learn until He had taken a covenant from the learned to teach!" When I read this soul-shaking Hadith to him, he broke his silence and said: "Now listen so that I may tell you," and in that same sitting, he narrated forty traditions to me! [1]

[1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi and Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan under the mentioned verse. The text of the Hadith narrated from Ali عليه السلام also appears in the Short Sayings of Nahj al-Balagha. For more information on the betrayal of the scholars of the People of the Book, refer to verses 79 and 174 of Surah Al-Baqarah and verses 71 to 77 of Surah Al-Imran.

Ayahs

لَا تَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَفْرَحُونَ بِمَآ أَتَوا۟ وَّيُحِبُّونَ أَن يُحْمَدُوا۟ بِمَا لَمْ يَفْعَلُوا۟ فَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّهُم بِمَفَازَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١٨٨)
Do not suppose those who brag about what they have done, and love to be praised for what they have not done—do not suppose them saved from punishment and there is a painful punishment for them. (188)
وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٨٩)
To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and Allah has power over all things. (189)

Occasion of Revelation

Traditionists and commentators have cited several occasions of revelation for the above verse. Among them is that a group from the Jews, when they distorted and concealed the verses of their own Divine Books and, in their own imagination, gained results from this action, were very happy and rejoiced in what they had done. At the same time, they loved for people to regard them as scholars, learned men, supporters of the religion, and duty-conscious individuals. The above verse was revealed and responded to their false delusions.

Others have said: The verse is about the hypocrites; whenever one of the Islamic battles arose, they would refrain from participating in the battlefield using various excuses. When the warriors returned from Jihad, they would swear oaths that if they had not had an excuse, they would never have abandoned Jihad. Despite this, they expected to be praised for a "deed not done," just like the self-sacrificing Mujahideen! The verse was revealed and responded to this misplaced expectation. [^1]

Commentary

The Self-Satisfied

لا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ يَفْرَحُونَ بِما أَتَوْا وَ يُحِبُّونَ أَنْ يُحْمَدُوا بِما لَمْ يَفْعَلُوا Evildoers are of two types: one group is truly ashamed of their actions and commits ugliness and sins due to the rebellion of their instincts. The salvation of this group is very easy, because they always regret after committing a sin and are subjected to the reproach of their awakened conscience.

But there is another group who not only feel no shame but are so arrogant and self-satisfied that they are happy with their ugly and disgraceful actions and even boast about them. Higher than that, they desire that people praise and laud them for good deeds they have never performed. The above verse says:

"Do not think that such people, who rejoice in their evil deeds and love to be appreciated for what they have not done, are far from the punishment of the Lord and will find salvation." Rather, salvation is for those who, at the very least, are ashamed of their bad deeds and regret that they have not done good deeds.

وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ Not only are such self-satisfied and arrogant people not among those who find salvation, but—

[^1]: Asbab al-Nuzul by al-Waqidi regarding the verse, Tafsir al-Manar, and Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan.

—a painful punishment awaits them.

It is possible to infer from this verse that joy and delight for a good deed that a person has been granted the success to perform (if it is in moderation and does not become a source of pride and conceit) is not blameworthy. Similarly, the desire for encouragement and appreciation for good deeds that have been performed—again, if it is within the limits of moderation and is not the primary motive for one's actions—will not be censured, as these are human instincts. However, despite this, the friends of Allah and individuals who stand at high levels of faith keep themselves away even from such joy and such appreciation.

They always see their deeds as insignificant and less than the required measure, and they feel themselves falling short in the face of the Greatness of the Lord.

Furthermore, it should not be imagined that the above verse is exclusive to the hypocrites who existed at the dawn of Islam or those like them; rather, all individuals who, in our age and time, are in various social conditions and are happy with their evil deeds, or provoke people to appreciate them through the pen or speech for deeds they have not performed, are all included in this verse.

Such individuals not only have a painful punishment in the next world awaiting them, but in the life of this world as well, they will be afflicted by the anger of the people, separation from Allah's creation, and various types of disorders.

وَ لِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ "To Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth, and Allah is Powerful over all things." This verse is a glad tiding for the believers and a threat to the disbelievers. That is, there is no reason for the believers to enter through deviant paths for their progress or to be encouraged for a deed they have not done; they can continue their progress under the shadow of the power of the Lord of the heavens and the earth by using legitimate and correct methods. Likewise, the evildoers and hypocrites who wish to reach a position by using these deviant paths should not imagine that they will escape the punishment of the Lord Who governs the entire existence.

Ayahs

إِنَّ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱخْتِلَـٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (١٩٠)
Indeed in the creation of the heavens and the earth and the alternation of night and day, there are signs for those who possess intellect. (190)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَذْكُرُونَ ٱللَّهَ قِيَـٰمًا وَقُعُودًا وَعَلَىٰ جُنُوبِهِمْ وَيَتَفَكَّرُونَ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ رَبَّنَا مَا خَلَقْتَ هَـٰذَا بَـٰطِلًا سُبْحَـٰنَكَ فَقِنَا عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ (١٩١)
Those who remember Allah standing, sitting, and lying on their sides, and reflect on the creation of the heavens and the earth [and say], ‘Our Lord, You have not created this in vain! Immaculate are You! Save us from the punishment of the Fire. (191)
رَبَّنَآ إِنَّكَ مَن تُدْخِلِ ٱلنَّارَ فَقَدْ أَخْزَيْتَهُۥ ۖ وَمَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِنْ أَنصَارٍ (١٩٢)
Our Lord, whoever that You make enter the Fire will surely have been disgraced by You, and the wrongdoers will have no helpers. (192)
رَّبَّنَآ إِنَّنَا سَمِعْنَا مُنَادِيًا يُنَادِى لِلْإِيمَـٰنِ أَنْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِرَبِّكُمْ فَـَٔامَنَّا ۚ رَبَّنَا فَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَا وَكَفِّرْ عَنَّا سَيِّـَٔاتِنَا وَتَوَفَّنَا مَعَ ٱلْأَبْرَارِ (١٩٣)
Our Lord, we have indeed heard a summoner calling to faith, declaring, ‘‘Have faith in your Lord!’’ So we believed. Our Lord, forgive us our sins and absolve us of our misdeeds, and make us die with the pious. (193)
رَبَّنَا وَءَاتِنَا مَا وَعَدتَّنَا عَلَىٰ رُسُلِكَ وَلَا تُخْزِنَا يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ إِنَّكَ لَا تُخْلِفُ ٱلْمِيعَادَ (١٩٤)
Our Lord, give us what You have promised us through Your apostles, and do not disgrace us on the Day of Resurrection. Indeed You do not break Your promise.’ (194)

The Importance of These Verses

All verses of the Quran are of great importance, as they are all the Word of Allah and were revealed for the education and salvation of humanity. However, among them, some possess a particular brilliance. Among these are the five aforementioned verses [3:190-194], which are among the soul-stirring passages of the Quran. They represent a collection of religious knowledge blended with the tender tone of supplication and prayer, in the form of a heavenly melody. Consequently, special importance has been given to these verses in hadiths and narrations.

Ata ibn Abi Rabah says: "One day I went to Aisha and asked her, 'What was the most wondrous thing you saw from the Prophet of Islam during your life?' She replied, 'Everything about the Prophet was wondrous. But the most amazing of all was this: One night, while the Prophet was at my house, he went to rest. Before he had even settled, he rose, dressed, performed ablution, and stood for prayer. He wept so much during his prayer and in that state of special divine attraction that the front of his garment became soaked with his tears. Then he placed his head in prostration and wept so much that the ground became wet with his tears. He remained in this state of agitation and weeping until dawn. When Bilal called him for the morning prayer, he saw the Prophet weeping.'"

He asked, "Why do you weep so? Are you not the recipient of Allah's grace?" He replied:

أَفَلَا أَكُونُ لِلَّهِ عَبْدًا شَكُورًا

"Should I not be a grateful servant of Allah? Why should I not weep? Tonight, Allah has revealed soul-stirring verses to me." Then he began to recite the five aforementioned verses. At the end, he said:

وَيْلٌ لِمَنْ قَرَأَهَا وَ لَمْ يَتَفَكَّرْ فِيهَا

"Woe to the one who recites them and does not reflect upon them." [^1] This final sentence, which commands people with great emphasis to reflect while reciting these verses, has been narrated in numerous reports with various wordings.

[^1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, under the commentary of these verses.

In a narration from Ali عليه السلام, it is mentioned that whenever the Messenger of Allah rose for the night prayer, he would first use the miswak (tooth-stick), then look toward the sky and murmur these verses. [^1] In the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, it is also commanded that whoever rises for the night prayer should recite these verses. [^2] Nawf al-Bakali, who was among the close companions of Ali عليه السلام, says: "One night I was in his service; sleep had not yet overcome my eyes when I saw the Imam rise and begin reciting these verses. Then he called me and said: 'O Nawf! Are you asleep or awake?'"

I replied, "I am awake and watching the scene of the sky." He said: "Blessed are those who did not accept the pollutions of the earth and advanced toward this path of the sky (they leaped out of the four walls of the material world, and their lofty spirits journey through the kingdom of the heavens)."

Commentary

The Brightest Path to Knowing God

إِنَّ فِي خَلْقِ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ... The verses of the Quran are not merely for reading; rather, they were revealed for the understanding and perception of the people. Reciting and reading the verses is a prelude to reflection. Therefore, in the above verse, it first points to the greatness of the creation of the heavens and the earth, saying: "In the creation of the heavens and the earth, and the alternation of night and day, there are clear signs for those of understanding and thinkers." [^3] In this way, it attracts and draws people to reflect upon this great creation,

[^1] and [^2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn and Majma' al-Bayan.

[^3]: The expression Ulul-Albab (those of understanding), seen in this verse and several other verses of the Holy Quran, is a subtle reference to the possessors of intellect, because lubb originally means the "pure essence" of anything, and the pure essence of human existence is his intellect, thought, and brain.

so that everyone, according to the measure of their capacity and reflection, may take a share from this boundless ocean and be quenched by the pure spring of the secrets of creation. Truly, the world of creation, with its ingenious patterns, beautiful and enchanting designs, and the staggering systems that govern them, is an extraordinarily great book, where every letter and word is a very clear proof of the existence and oneness of the Creator of the universe. [^1]

الَّذِينَ يَذْكُرُونَ اللَّهَ قِياماً وَ قُعُوداً... The enchanting and wondrous design seen in every corner of this world and across the expanse of existence so attracts the hearts of the possessors of intellect that in all their states—whether standing, sitting, or while resting in bed lying on their sides—they are in remembrance of the Originator of this system and its marvelous secrets. Thus, the verse says: "The wise are those who remember Allah while standing, sitting, and lying on their sides," and they reflect upon the secrets of the heavens and the earth; meaning they are always and in every state immersed in this life-giving reflection.

In this verse, remembrance (dhikr) is mentioned first, followed by reflection (fikr). This means that the mere remembrance of Allah is not enough; rather, this remembrance will yield valuable fruits when it is blended with reflection. Similarly, reflection on the creation of the heavens and the earth will lead nowhere if it is not blended with the remembrance of Allah. How many scientists there are who, in their astronomical studies and reflections regarding the creation of celestial bodies, see this wondrous system, but because they do not remember Allah, do not wear the glasses of monotheism (Tawhid), and do not look at them from the angle of identifying the Source of existence, they do not derive the necessary educational and human results from it.

[^1]: The meaning of the alternation of night and day and its secrets was discussed in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 164, in Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 1, page 410. For further explanation regarding the "Argument from Design," which is the clearest proof of knowing God, refer to the books: The Creator of the World, The Enigma of Existence, and In Search of God.

It is like someone who eats food that only strengthens his body but has no effect on strengthening his thought, intellect, and spirit.

رَبَّنا ما خَلَقْتَ هذا باطِلًا... Reflection and thought regarding the secrets of creation and the earth give man a special awareness, and its first effect is the realization that creation is not in vain. For when man sees a purpose in every small entity of this great world, can he believe that the totality of the world is purposeless? In the specific structure of the parts of a plant's body, we see clear purposes; the human heart, its chambers, and its valves each have a program and a goal; the structure of the layers of the eye is each for a specific intent; even the eyelashes and fingernails each have a specific role. Is it possible for the particles of a being to each have a specific goal, yet the whole has no goal whatsoever?!

Therefore, the wise, noting this reality, raise this murmur:

"Our Lord! You have not created this grand system in vain." O Lord! This infinitely large world and this wondrous system have all been created based on wisdom, benefit, and a correct goal; they are all signs of Your Oneness, and You are sanctified from futile and vain actions.

فَقِنا عَذابَ النَّارِ... After confessing to the existence of a goal in creation, the possessors of intellect and wisdom immediately remember their own creation and realize that man, who is the fruit of this universe, was not created in vain, and there was no goal other than his education, cultivation, and perfection. He was not created only for the fleeting and low-value life of this world; rather, he has another abode ahead of him where the reward and punishment for deeds will be placed before him. At this moment, they become aware of their responsibilities and ask Allah for the success to fulfill them so they may be safe from His punishment. Thus they say: "Our Lord! Save us from the punishment of the Fire."

رَبَّنا إِنَّكَ مَنْ تُدْخِلِ النَّارَ فَقَدْ أَخْزَيْتَهُ وَ ما لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ أَنْصارٍ "Our Lord, indeed whomever You admit to the Fire, You have disgraced him, and for the wrongdoers there are no helpers."

From this sentence, it is understood that the wise fear disgrace even more than they fear the fire of Hell. Such is the state of people of character; they are willing to endure all kinds of pain and discomfort provided their dignity and honor remain intact. Therefore, the most painful punishment of the Resurrection for this group is that very disgrace in the presence of Allah and the servants of Allah.

The point hidden in the phrase "and for the wrongdoers there are no helpers" is that:

The wise, after becoming acquainted with the educational goals of man, reach the reality that the only means of victory and salvation for man is his own deeds and actions. Therefore, the wrongdoers cannot have any helper, because they have lost the primary helper, which is pure action. The emphasis on the word "wrong" (zulm) is either due to the importance of this sin among all sins, or because all sins return to wrongdoing and oppression against oneself.

Of course, this verse does not contradict the issue of intercession (shafa'ah) in its correct sense, because as we said in the discussion of intercession, it requires a specific readiness in the one being interceded for, and this readiness is found in the light of some good deeds.

رَبَّنا إِنَّنا سَمِعْنا مُنادِياً يُنادِي لِلْإِيمانِ أَنْ آمِنُوا بِرَبِّكُمْ فَآمَنَّا The possessors of intellect and wisdom, after perceiving the goal of creation, also realize this point: that they can never traverse this path full of ups and downs without divine leaders. For this reason, they are always waiting to hear the voice of the callers to true faith, and as soon as they hear their first call, they hasten toward them. After the necessary inquiry and investigation, they respond to their invitation and believe with all their being.

Thus, they say to the presence of their Lord: "Our Lord! We heard the voice of the caller to monotheism inviting us toward faith, and following that, we believed."

رَبَّنا فَاغْفِرْ لَنا ذُنُوبَنا وَ كَفِّرْ عَنَّا سَيِّئاتِنا وَ تَوَفَّنا مَعَ الْأَبْرارِ "Our Lord! Now that it is so and we have believed with all our being, yet since we are exposed to the storms of various instincts, sometimes slips occur from us and we commit sins. O Allah! Forgive us, pardon our sins, cover our lapses, and cause us to die with the righteous and in their way of life."
