They are so connected to the human community and so averse to isolation and self-centeredness that they ask Allah not only that their life be with the righteous and the pure, but that their death—whether natural death or martyrdom in the way of Allah—takes place among the righteous and according to their way, for dying among the wicked is a double death.

A question arises here: what is the meaning of asking for the forgiveness of "sins" (dhunub), the covering of "evil deeds" (sayyi'at), and their pardon?

With reference to other verses of the Quran, the answer to this question becomes clear. From verse 31 of Surah An-Nisa: "If you avoid the major sins which you are forbidden, We will remove from you your lesser sins..." it is understood that sayyi'at refers to minor sins. Therefore, the wise first ask Allah for the pardon of major lapses, followed by the request for the removal of the effects of minor sins.

رَبَّنا وَ آتِنا ما وَعَدْتَنا عَلى‌ رُسُلِكَ‌ ...

In the final stage, after traversing the path of monotheism, faith in the Resurrection, responding to the call of the prophets, and fulfilling their duties and responsibilities, they make a request to their God, saying: "Now that we have fulfilled our covenant, O Lord, grant us what You promised and gave glad tidings of through Your messengers, and do not disgrace us on the Day of Resurrection, for You never fail in Your promise."

The emphasis on the phrase "not being disgraced" once again underscores the reality that because of the importance they place on their character, they consider disgrace to be among the most painful punishments, and thus they point specifically to it.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 3, p. 219

It is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "Whoever is faced with an important matter and says 'Our Lord' (Rabbana) five times, Allah will deliver him from what he fears and grant him what he hopes for." They asked, "How should he say 'Rabbana' five times?" He replied, "Recite these verses [3:190-194] which contain five instances of 'Rabbana,' and following that is the response of the Lord, for He says: 'So their Lord responded to them...'" It goes without saying that the real and deep effect of these verses occurs when the human tongue is in harmony with the heart and action, and the content of the verses—which conveys the way of thinking of the wise, their love for Allah, and their attention to responsibilities and performing good deeds—becomes settled in their souls, and they attain that same state of humility and submissiveness that the wise believers attained during their supplication with their Lord.

Ayah

فَٱسْتَجَابَ لَهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ أَنِّى لَآ أُضِيعُ عَمَلَ عَـٰمِلٍ مِّنكُم مِّن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ ۖ بَعْضُكُم مِّنۢ بَعْضٍ ۖ فَٱلَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا۟ وَأُخْرِجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِمْ وَأُوذُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِى وَقَـٰتَلُوا۟ وَقُتِلُوا۟ لَأُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ وَلَأُدْخِلَنَّهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ثَوَابًا مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عِندَهُۥ حُسْنُ ٱلثَّوَابِ (١٩٥)
Then their Lord answered them, ‘I do not waste the work of any worker among you, whether male or female; you are all on the same footing. So those who migrated and were expelled from their homes, and were tormented in My way, and those who fought and were killed—I will surely absolve them of their misdeeds and I will admit them into gardens with streams running in them, as a reward from Allah, and Allah—with Him is the best of rewards.’ (195)

Occasion of Revelation

This verse is a continuation of the previous verses regarding the possessors of intellect and the result of their deeds, and the beginning of the verse with the "conjunction of consequence" (fa) is the clearest evidence of this connection. Nevertheless, certain occasions of revelation have been mentioned for this verse in narrations and the words of commentators, which, of course, do not contradict the verse's continuity with the preceding ones.

Among these, it is narrated that Umm Salama (one of the wives of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله) said to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "In the Quran, there is much discussion about Jihad, Hijrah (migration), and the sacrifices of men; do women have a share in this part as well?" The above verse was revealed and answered this question.

It is also narrated that when Ali عليه السلام migrated from Mecca to Medina with the "Fawātim" (Fatima bint Asad, Fatima the daughter of the Prophet, and Fatima the daughter of Zubayr), and Umm Ayman—another of the believing women—joined them along the way, the above verse was revealed.

As we mentioned, the existence of these occasions of revelation for the verse does not contradict its connection to the previous verses, just as there is no contradiction between these two occasions of revelation themselves.

Commentary

The Result of the Program of the Wise

In the past five verses, a summary of faith, practical programs, the requests of the possessors of thought and intellect, and their supplications were expressed. In this verse, He says: فَاسْتَجابَ لَهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ: "So their Lord responded to them immediately." The use of the expression "their Lord" (rabbuhum) signifies the ultimate grace and mercy of the Lord toward them. Then He adds:

أَنِّي لا أُضِيعُ عَمَلَ عامِلٍ مِنْكُمْ:

Lest there be any misunderstanding and the connection between a person's victory and salvation and their deeds be severed, He immediately says: "I never let the work of any worker among you go to waste." In this sentence, reference is made both to the essence of the deed and to the doer of the deed, so it becomes clear that the main axis for the acceptance and answering of prayer is righteous deeds arising from faith, and those requests are answered immediately which are accompanied by righteous action.

مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى‌ بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ: And so that it is not imagined that this divine promise is exclusive to a specific group, He explicitly states: "This worker, whether male or female, makes no difference," because "you are all related to one another in creation," "some of you are born from others"—women from men and men from women.

The phrase بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ (you are of one another) may also indicate that you are all followers of one religion and supporters of one truth and cooperate with one another; therefore, there is no reason for Allah to discriminate among you.

فَالَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا وَ أُخْرِجُوا مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ وَ أُوذُوا فِي سَبِيلِي وَ قاتَلُوا وَ قُتِلُوا لَأُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ.

Then the conclusion is drawn: "Therefore, all those who migrated in the way of Allah, and were driven out of their homes and homelands, and were harmed in My way, and fought and were killed—the first favor from Allah toward them will be that Allah has sworn He will forgive their sins and make the endurance of these hardships and sufferings an expiation for their sins so that they become entirely purified from sin."

وَ لَأُدْخِلَنَّهُمْ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ: Then He says: In addition to forgiving their sins, I will certainly admit them into Gardens beneath whose trees rivers flow, filled with all kinds of blessings.

ثَواباً مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ وَ اللَّهُ عِنْدَهُ حُسْنُ الثَّوابِ: This is a reward given to them by Allah in appreciation of their sacrifice, and the best of rewards and wages are with the Lord—indicating that divine rewards cannot be fully described for the people of this world; they only need to know that it is higher than any other reward.

From the above verse, it is clearly understood that one must first be purified from sins in the light of righteous deeds, then enter the realm of proximity to the Lord, Paradise, and His blessings. For at the beginning He says: لَأُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ (I will surely expiate from them their misdeeds) and then وَ لَأُدْخِلَنَّهُمْ جَنَّاتٍ (and I will surely admit them into gardens...). In other words, Paradise is the abode of the pure, and until one becomes pure, they shall find no way into it.

The Spiritual Value of Man and Woman

The above verse, like many other verses of the Holy Quran, considers women and men equal before God and in reaching spiritual stations under similar conditions. It never considers the difference in gender, physical structure, and consequently some differences in social responsibilities as a reason for a difference between the two in terms of achieving human perfection. Rather, it places both on exactly the same level in this regard, and therefore mentions them together. This subject is exactly like the fact that for administrative discipline, one person is chosen as the head and another as the deputy or member; the head must have more ability or wider experience and information in their work, but this difference and hierarchy is never a reason for the human personality and existential value of the head to be greater than that of the deputy or employees.

The Holy Quran explicitly says: وَ مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى‌ وَ هُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُولئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ يُرْزَقُونَ فِيها بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ (Surah Ghafir, Verse 40): "Whoever does a righteous deed, whether male or female, and is a believer—those will enter Paradise, being provided therein without account (and enjoying the spiritual and physical blessings of that world)." And in another verse we read: مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى‌ وَ هُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَياةً طَيِّبَةً وَ لَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْرَهُمْ بِأَحْسَنِ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ (Surah An-Nahl, Verse 97): "Whoever does righteousness, whether male or female, while he is a believer—We will surely cause him to live a good life, and We will surely give them their reward according to the best of what they used to do."

These verses and many others were revealed in an era and time when many nations of the world doubted the humanity of the female gender and considered her a cursed being, a source of sin, deviation, and death!

Many ancient nations even believed that a woman's worship was not accepted before God. Many Greeks considered woman an impure being and a product of the devil's work. The Romans and some Greeks believed that fundamentally, woman does not possess a human soul, and therefore the human soul is exclusively reserved for men.

Interestingly, until recently, Christian scholars in Spain were debating whether a woman, like a man, has a human soul and whether her soul will remain eternal after death or not. After discussions, they reached the conclusion that because a woman's soul is a "barzakh" (intermediary) between a human soul and an animal soul, it is not eternal, except for the soul of Mary [1]! From here it becomes clear how far from the truth it is when some uninformed individuals sometimes accuse Islam of being a religion for men and not women. Generally, if in some Islamic laws differences are seen in terms of social responsibilities due to the physical and emotional differences that exist between men and women, it in no way harms the spiritual value of women. In this regard, men and women do not differ from each other, and the doors of happiness are equally open to both, as we read in the verse under discussion: بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ "You are all of the same kind and one society."

[1]: Refer to the books of "Westermarck," "Apology to Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله)," "Women's Rights in Islam," and other books related to human beliefs and opinions.

Ayahs

لَا يَغُرَّنَّكَ تَقَلُّبُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْبِلَـٰدِ (١٩٦)
Never be misled by the bustle of the faithless in the towns. (196)
مَتَـٰعٌ قَلِيلٌ ثُمَّ مَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۚ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمِهَادُ (١٩٧)
It is a trivial enjoyment; then their refuge is hell, and it is an evil resting place. (197)
لَـٰكِنِ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ رَبَّهُمْ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا نُزُلًا مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ لِّلْأَبْرَارِ (١٩٨)
But those who are wary of their Lord—for them shall be gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever], a hospitality from Allah; and what is with Allah is better for the pious. (198)

Occasion of Revelation

Many of the polytheists of Mecca were merchants and acquired considerable wealth through this trade, living in luxury and ease. Similarly, the Jews of Medina were skilled in commerce and often returned from their trading journeys with full hands. Meanwhile, the Muslims at that time were in great distress regarding their material situation due to specific living conditions, including the migration from Mecca to Medina and the economic blockade by powerful enemies, living in hardship. Comparing these two states raised a question for some: why do the faithless live in such luxury and ease, while the faithful live in suffering, torment, poverty, and distress?

The above verses were revealed and provided the answer to this question. [^1]

Commentary

A Distressing Question

The question raised in the above occasion of revelation for a group of Muslims during the Prophet's time is a general and universal question for many people in every age and era. They often compare the affluent and luxurious lives of the defiant, the transgressors, the tyrants, and the dissolute with the hardship-filled lives of a group of believers. They ask why those people, despite all their crimes and impurities, have a comfortable life, while these, despite having faith and piety, live in hardship. Sometimes, this issue creates doubt and hesitation in those with weak faith.

If this question is carefully examined and the factors on both sides are analyzed, it has clear answers, some of which the above verse points to, and others we can discover through careful study.

The verse says: لا يَغُرَّنَّكَ تَقَلُّبُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي الْبِلادِ ("Let not the strutting of those who disbelieve through the land deceive you..."). Although the addressee in the verse is the person of the Prophet, it is clear that the intended audience is the general body of Muslims.

Then He says: مَتاعٌ قَلِيلٌ ("It is but a brief enjoyment"). These victories and unconditional material gains are fleeting and meager successes.

ثُمَّ مَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَ بِئْسَ الْمِهادُ ("Then their ultimate abode is Hell—and what an evil resting place!"). Following these victories, the ominous consequences and their responsibilities will overtake them, and their place is Hell; what an evil place and unsuitable resting spot.

The above verse actually points to two key points:

First, many of the victories of the transgressors and oppressors have limited dimensions, just as the deprivations and discomforts of many believers are also limited. We can observe a living example of this in the situation of the Muslims at the dawn of Islam and their enemies. Because the Islamic government at that time was like a newly sprouted sapling and was threatened by powerful enemies who attacked it like a storm, it was very restricted—especially since the migration of the Meccan Muslims, who were in the minority, had completely stripped them of their possessions. This situation was not unique to them; rather, all supporters of a fundamental and spiritual revolution in a corrupt society will face a period of severe deprivation.

However, we know that this situation did not last long. The Islamic government developed firm roots, its branches became powerful and strong, a flood of wealth poured into the Islamic land, and the stubborn enemies who were in luxury were brought to ruin. This is exactly what is referred to by the phrase "brief enjoyment" (متاع قليل) in the verse.

Second, the material successes of a group of faithless individuals are due to the fact that they recognize no constraints or conditions for themselves in accumulating wealth. They amass wealth through any means, whether legitimate or illegitimate, even by sucking the blood of the destitute. In contrast, believers have limitations—and must have them—to observe the principles of truth and justice. Therefore, the state of these two cannot be compared. The believers feel a sense of responsibility, while the faithless feel no responsibility for themselves. Since the world is a world of choice and free will, Allah has left both groups free so that ultimately, each reaches the result of their own actions. This is what is pointed to in the verse: ثُمَّ مَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَ بِئْسَ الْمِهادُ.

Strengths and Weaknesses

Another cause for the progress of some faithless individuals and the backwardness of a group of believers is that the first group, despite lacking faith, sometimes possesses strengths through which they achieve remarkable victories. The second group, despite having faith, possesses weaknesses that cause their backwardness.

For example: we know individuals who, despite being estranged from God, are serious, determined, persistent, steadfast, and coordinated with one another in their life's work, and are aware of the conditions of their time.

These individuals will certainly achieve victories in material life, and in reality, they implement a series of authentic religious programs without attributing them to religion.

In contrast, there are individuals who adhere to religious beliefs but have forgotten many of its practical instructions. They are lethargic, lacking courage, devoid of steadfastness and persistence, and are completely scattered and divided. Certainly, they will face successive failures in life. But these failures are not because of their faith, but because of the weaknesses they possess. They sometimes imagine that merely by performing prayers and fasting, they should be victorious in all matters, whereas religion has brought a series of practical programs for progress in life, the forgetting of which is accompanied by failure and disappointment.

In short, each of these two groups has strengths and weaknesses, each of which has effects; however, during calculation, these effects are sometimes confused with one another.

For instance: a faithless individual possesses seriousness and persistence. Because he is faithless, he lacks peace of heart and soul, a high human goal, and pure human emotions; but because he is serious and steadfast, he advances in material life.

Here, some ask why this faithless individual has succeeded in life?

It is as if they imagine the factor of victory was something else. This subject is true for an individual and can also be applied at the level of a country.

Furthermore, the three factors mentioned for the victory of the faithless and the failure of some believers do not all apply in one place; rather, each is specific to a particular case.

لَكِنِ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا رَبَّهُمْ لَهُمْ جَنَّاتٌ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها In the previous verse, the end of the faithless was described, and in this verse, the end of the pious is stated: "But those who feared their Lord will have gardens beneath which rivers flow, abiding eternally therein." In exchange for these hardships (where they observed the standards of truth and justice to reach material assets, or were displaced from their homelands and placed under social and economic blockade due to faith in God), Allah places at their disposal gardens of Paradise beneath whose trees rivers flow, and they shall remain there forever.

نُزُلًا مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ وَ ما عِنْدَ اللَّهِ خَيْرٌ لِلْأَبْرارِ "Nuzul" linguistically means something prepared for the reception of a guest, and some have said it is the first thing with which a guest is welcomed (like a drink or fruit brought for a guest upon arrival). Therefore, the above verse says: the gardens of Paradise with all those material blessings are the first means of welcoming the pious. But the more important and higher reception is those spiritual and intellectual blessings, which are pointed to by the phrase: وَ ما عِنْدَ اللَّهِ خَيْرٌ لِلْأَبْرارِ ("And what is with Allah is best for the righteous").

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, al-Manar, and al-Mizan.

Ayah

وَإِنَّ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَمَن يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ خَـٰشِعِينَ لِلَّهِ لَا يَشْتَرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (١٩٩)
Indeed among the People of the Book there are some who have faith in Allah and in what has been sent down to you, and in what has been sent down to them. Humble toward Allah, they do not sell the signs of Allah for a paltry gain. They shall have their reward near their Lord; indeed Allah is swift at reckoning. (199)

Occasion of Revelation

According to most commentators, this verse was revealed concerning the believers among the People of the Book—those who abandoned undue prejudices and joined the ranks of the Muslims, constituting a significant number of Christians and Jews. However, according to a group of commentators, the verse was revealed regarding the Negus (al-Najashi), the people-loving ruler of Abyssinia, although its concept is broad.

In the ninth year of the Hijra, in the month of Rajab, the Negus passed away. News of his death reached the Prophet ﷺ on the same day through divine inspiration. The Prophet ﷺ said to the Muslims: "One of your brothers has passed away outside the land of Hijaz; come, let us offer prayers for him in honor of the services he rendered to the Muslims." Some asked: "Who is he?"

He replied: "The Negus." Then, accompanied by the Muslims, he went to the Baqi' cemetery and offered prayers for him from afar, seeking forgiveness for him, and commanded his companions to do the same. Some of the hypocrites said: "Muhammad ﷺ offers prayers for a disbelieving man whom he has never seen, while he [the Negus] never accepted his religion." The above verse was revealed and answered them. [^1] From this narration, it is understood that the Negus had fully accepted Islam, even if he did not manifest it publicly.

Commentary

The People of the Book are Not All Alike

وَ إِنَّ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ لَمَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ‌ ...

We have previously stated that in its discussions regarding other religions, the Quran never views everyone with the same eye and does not adopt a tone of hostility toward a race or group. Rather, Its judgment is based on their conduct and programs; therefore, It does not forget the share of the faithful and righteous minorities who exist within a misguided majority. Here too, after rebuking many of the People of the Book for concealing Allah's signs and for their rebellion and transgression—as mentioned in previous verses—It speaks of a minority that responded to the Prophet's call, describing five distinguished attributes for them:

  1. يُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ: They are those who believe in Allah with all their heart and soul.

  2. وَ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ: They believe in the Quran and what has been revealed to you Muslims.

  3. وَ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ: Their faith in the Prophet of Islam actually stems from their sincere faith in their own Heavenly Book and the glad tidings contained within it. Therefore: "They believe in what was revealed to them."

  4. خاشِعِينَ لِلَّهِ: They are submissive and humble before the command of Allah, and it is this very humility that has become the motive for true faith and has separated them from ignorant prejudices.

  5. لا يَشْتَرُونَ بِآياتِ اللَّهِ ثَمَناً قَلِيلًا: They never sell the divine verses for a meager price, and they are not like some Jewish scholars who distorted Allah's verses to preserve their status, continue their rule over the people, and take bribes. It is obvious that they would not sell them for any price, let alone a meager one; the mention of a "meager price" is to indicate that they are not like that group of low-minded, world-worshipping scholars.

Furthermore, fundamentally, whatever a human receives in exchange for Allah's verses is worthless.

أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ: These people, possessing such a clear and vibrant program and sublime human qualities, shall have their reward with their Lord. The expression "their Lord" (ربهم) points to the ultimate grace and mercy of the Lord toward them, and also indicates that Allah nurtures and assists them on the path of guidance.

إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ: Allah is swift in taking account of His servants: neither do the righteous face any difficulty in receiving their reward, nor is the punishment of the wicked delayed.

This sentence is both a glad tiding for the righteous and a threat to the evildoers. [^2]

[^1]: Asbab al-Nuzul by al-Wahidi. [^2]: For a more detailed explanation of the commentary on this phrase, refer to verse 202 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱصْبِرُوا۟ وَصَابِرُوا۟ وَرَابِطُوا۟ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (٢٠٠)
O you who have faith! Be patient, stand firm, and close [your] ranks, and be wary of Allah so that you may be felicitous. (200)

Commentary

This verse is the final verse of Surah Al-Imran, containing a comprehensive four-article program for all Muslims; for this reason, it begins with the address: "O you who have believed!"

1- "Patiently endure" (Isbiru): The first article of this program, which guarantees the honor and victory of Muslims, is steadfastness, patience, and resistance against events. This, in truth, forms the primary root of every material and spiritual victory. Whatever is said regarding its role and importance in individual and social progress is insufficient. This is the same quality that Ali (عليه السلام) described in his short aphorisms as being like the head to the body: (إِنَّ الصَّبْرَ مِنَ الْإِيمَانِ كَالرَّأْسِ مِنَ الْجَسَدِ) "Indeed, patience is to faith as the head is to the body."

2- "Excel in patience" (Sabiru): This comes from Musabarah (from the Mufa'alah pattern), meaning patience and steadfastness in the face of the patience and steadfastness of others. Therefore, the Quran first commands individuals with faith to be steadfast (which includes every kind of struggle against the self and the difficulties of life), and in the second stage, it commands steadfastness against the enemy. This indicates that until a nation is victorious in the struggle against the self and in rectifying internal weaknesses, its victory over the enemy is impossible. Most of our defeats against enemies are due to the defeats we have suffered in the struggle against our own selves and in rectifying our own weaknesses. Furthermore, from this command (Sabiru), it is understood that however much the enemy increases their steadfastness, we too must increase our own persistence and endurance.

3- "Be on guard" (Rabitu): This phrase is derived from the root Ribat, which originally means tying something in a place (like tying a horse in a location). For this reason, a caravanserai is called a Ribat. Rabt al-qalb means the tranquility of the heart and peace of mind, as if it is tied to a place. Murabatah has come to mean guarding the borders, because soldiers, mounts, and war equipment are kept in that location. This phrase commands Muslims to be in a state of readiness against the enemy and to maintain constant vigilance over the borders and frontiers of Islamic countries, so they are never caught by surprise attacks. It also commands them to be in a state of constant readiness and vigilance against the attacks of Satan and rebellious desires, so they are not taken by surprise. Hence, in some narrations from Ali (عليه السلام), this phrase has been interpreted as being mindful of and waiting for prayers one after another; for one who keeps his heart and soul awake through this continuous and successive worship is like a soldier who has taken a stance of readiness against the enemy. In short, Murabatah has a broad meaning that includes every kind of readiness to defend oneself and the Islamic society. In Islamic jurisprudence, under the chapter of Jihad, there is a discussion titled Murabatah, meaning readiness to protect the borders against a potential enemy attack, for which specific rulings have been stated (refer to books of jurisprudence for more information). In some narrations, scholars and thinkers are also called Murabit. Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), according to a narration, says: (عُلَمَاءُ شِيعَتِنَا مُرَابِطُونَ فِي الثَّغْرِ الَّذِي يَلِي إِبْلِيسَ وَ عَفَارِيتَهُ وَ يَمْنَعُونَهُ عَنِ الْخُرُوجِ عَلَى ضُعَفَاءِ شِيعَتِنَا وَ عَنْ أَنْ يَتَسَلَّطَ عَلَيْهِمْ إِبْلِيسُ) [^1] "The scholars of our followers are like border guards stationed at the frontier facing Iblis and his demons, preventing them from attacking those of our followers who lack the strength to defend themselves, and preventing Iblis from gaining mastery over them." In the continuation of this Hadith, their status and position are considered superior and higher than the officers and border guards who fight against the invasion of the enemies of Islam. This is because they are the guardians of Islamic beliefs and culture, while the latter are the protectors of geographical borders. Certainly, a nation whose ideological and cultural borders are attacked by the enemy and cannot defend them well will, in a short time, also be defeated politically and militarily.

4- "And fear Allah" (Wa-ttaqu-llah): Finally, the last command, which casts a shadow over all previous commands like an umbrella, is the command to piety. Steadfastness, mutual patience, and guarding the borders must be mixed with piety and God-wariness, and must be kept far from any kind of selfishness, hypocrisy, or personal motives. "That you may succeed" (La'allakum tuflihun): In the shadow of implementing these four commands, you can achieve salvation; without them, there will be no path toward success.

Question

Sometimes the question is asked: why do some sentences in the Quran begin with the word La'alla (perhaps/maybe), such as the phrase "that you may succeed," "that you may become pious," or "that you may receive mercy," while the word La'alla implies a kind of doubt which is far from the station of Allah, the All-Knowing? This phrase has incidentally become a pretext for some enemies of Islam who say: [^1]: Ihtijaj al-Tabarsi, Chapter One. Islam does not give anyone a promise of definitive salvation, and its promise is mixed with doubt because many of these promises begin with the word La'alla.

Answer

In fact, this expression is one of the signs of the greatness, realism, and truthfulness of the Holy Quran. The Quran uses this word in places where achieving a result requires certain conditions, and through the word La'alla, a brief reference is made to those conditions. For example, remaining silent while hearing the verses of the Quran and listening to their content is not alone sufficient for a person to receive Divine mercy; rather, understanding the verses and implementing them is also necessary. Therefore, the Quran says: "And when the Quran is recited, listen to it and be silent that you may receive mercy" (Al-A'raf: 204). If the Quran had said "you will definitely receive mercy," it would have been far from realism, because as we mentioned, this matter has other conditions. But when it says "perhaps," the role of the other conditions is preserved. However, lack of attention to this reality has led to criticism of these verses, and even some of our scholars have come to believe that La'alla in such cases does not mean "perhaps," whereas this claim is also a kind of deviation from the apparent meaning without evidence (reflect on this). In the verse under discussion, although four important articles of the highest Islamic instructions are mentioned, the word La'alla is used so that other constructive Islamic programs are not neglected.

In any case, if Muslims today were to implement the above verse as an Islamic motto in their life program, many of the problems they currently face would be solved. Today, painful blows are struck against the body of Islam and Muslims due to the violation or forgetting of all or some of these four instructions. If the spirit of steadfastness and persistence is revived in Muslims, if Muslims show more effort and endeavor in response to the increased efforts of the enemies, and if, according to the command of Murabatah, they maintain sufficient vigilance over geographical, cultural, and ideological borders and always remain in a state of readiness against the plots of enemies, and in addition to all this, they remove sin and corruption from their society through individual and social piety, their victory will be guaranteed. O Allah! Grant us all the success to apply these life-giving instructions of Your Heavenly Book in our lives, and include us in Your infinite mercy and grace!

Surah An-Nisa (Madani - 177 Verses)

Before entering into the commentary of the verses of this Surah, a few points are necessary to mention:

1- The Place of Revelation of Surah An-Nisa All the verses of this Surah (with the exception of verse 58, according to some commentators) were revealed in Medina, and in terms of the order of revelation, it is placed after Surah Al-Mumtahanah. We know that the current order of the Surahs of the Quran does not correspond to the order of their revelation; many Surahs revealed in Mecca are at the end of the Quran, and many revealed in Medina are at the beginning. Of course, as we mentioned at the beginning of the first volume, there is evidence that the collection of the Surahs of the Quran in its current form took place during the time of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself. Therefore, during the collection of the Quran, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself, for various reasons including the importance of the subjects and their natural order, ordered the Surahs to be arranged according to the state we see now, the first being Surah Al-Hamd and the last being Surah An-Nas, without a single word or even a letter being added or removed from the verses or Surahs. In terms of the number of words and letters, this Surah is the longest Surah of the Quran after Surah Al-Baqarah and contains 177 verses. Since many discussions regarding the rulings and rights of women are raised in it, it has been named Surah "An-Nisa" (The Women).

2- The Contents of this Surah As mentioned, this Surah was revealed in Medina, meaning at a time when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was engaged in establishing the Islamic government and creating a healthy human society. For this reason, many laws effective in making society healthy were revealed in this Surah. On the other hand, since the individuals who formed the fabric of this fledgling society were the idol-worshippers of yesterday with all the pollutions of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), the residues of the past had to be removed from their minds and souls before anything else, and replaced with the laws and programs necessary for rebuilding a worn-out society. Generally, the various discussions we read in this Surah include: 1- Invitation to faith and justice and cutting off friendly relations with persistent enemies. 2- A portion of the history of predecessors to become more familiar with the fate of unhealthy societies. 3- Support for those in need of help, such as orphans, and the necessary instructions for the preservation and care of their rights. 4- The law of inheritance based on a natural and just method, in contrast to the very ugly form it had at that time where weak individuals were deprived under various pretexts. 5- Laws related to marriage and programs for preserving public chastity. 6- General laws for the protection of public property. 7- Control, maintenance, and improvement of the first unit of society, namely the family environment. 8- Mutual rights and duties of members of society toward one another. 9- Identification of the enemies of the Islamic society and a call to vigilance for Muslims against them. 10- Islamic government and the necessity of obeying the leader of such a government. 11- Encouraging Muslims to struggle against known enemies. 12- Identifying enemies who occasionally had underground activities. 13- The importance of migration (Hijrah) and its necessity when faced with a corrupt and impenetrable society. 14- Further discussions on inheritance and the necessity of distributing accumulated wealth among heirs.

3- The Virtue of Reciting this Surah The Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), according to a narration, said: "Whoever recites Surah An-Nisa, it is as if he has given charity in the way of Allah to the extent of every Muslim who inherits according to the contents of this Surah, and he will also be given the reward of someone who has freed a slave." It is obvious that in this narration and all similar narrations, the intention is not merely the reading of the verses; rather, reading is a prelude to understanding, and that in turn is a prelude to implementing it in individual and social life. It is certain that if Muslims take inspiration from the contents of the verses of this Surah in their lives, they will have all these rewards in addition to its worldly results.
